scale.sourceNote: AideDD dataF.js: `factorDist` and comment `1052 px = 500 miles`

zones.name: Anauroch
zones.txt: The Anauroch is a sandy desert formed due to phaerimm magic in the former region of Netheril. It is commonly divided into three general regions: the northern High Ice (including the Frozen Sea), the middle Plain of Standing Stones, and the Sword in the south. For a millennium after the creation of the Anauroch desert, the nomadic Bedine were its primary humanoid inhabitants. Then, in the mid-1300s DR, the Zhentarim invaded the desert with plans of creating a permanent trade route. Some decades later in 1372 DR, the Shadovar returned from the Plane of Shadow with the goal of restoring the land of Netheril, until the fall of their flying city called Shade in 1487. Below the dunes of Anauroch lurks what remains of the evil phaerimm, a race of spellcasters with near-unrivaled mastery of the Art.

zones.name: Arcane Empires
zones.txt:
The Arcane Empires refer to three distinct realms in eastern Faerûn: Aglarond, Rashemen, and Thay. They are ruled by powerful spellcasters. While each nation practices various forms of magic, they each favor a specific arcane tradition: Aglarond is a land of sorcery, Rashemen is governed through warlock pacts, and Thay is the domain of wizardry. In these lands, magical training is a definitive sign of high status and power, as the common folk are generally not spellcasters themselves.

Realms:

  • Aglarond
  • Rashemen
  • Thay

zones.name: Forgotten Lands
zones.txt:
Located in Faerûn's remote northeast, the Forgotten Lands are a diverse region characterized by vast mineral wealth, buried ancient empires, and long-lost magical secrets. This territory stretches from the Sea of Fallen Stars in the south to the frozen expanse of the Great Glacier in the north, where merchant-princes and archdruids struggle for control over a harsh but bounteous wilderness. In the northern part of the region, realms such as Damara, Narfell, and the Great Dale have reputations for brutal climates, rocky soil, and a lack of creature comforts. However, beneath this 'crusty exterior', the land teems with river-size veins of gold, gemstone-lined caverns, and buried cities.

Realms:

  • Damara
  • Great Dale
  • Impiltur
  • Narfell
  • Thesk
  • Vaasa
  • The Vast

zones.name: Heartlands
zones.txt:
The Heartlands is a central region of Faerûn defined by a mix of powerful, structured kingdoms, independent frontier settlements, and ancient, monster-infested ruins. The rich, powerful realms of Cormyr and Sembia surround the frontier settlements of the Dalelands and a legendary elven ruin.

Realms:

  • Cormyr
  • The Dalelands
  • The Moonsea
  • Sembia

zones.name: Lands of Intrigue
zones.txt:
The Lands of Intrigue consist of the wealthy mercantile realms of Amn, Calimshan, and Tethyr, located in southwestern Faerûn. While these nations have not engaged in open warfare with one another for centuries, they share a long history of political schemes, espionage, and aggressive trade deals. This region is characterized by a 'live and let live' attitude toward religion but a deep suspicion regarding the motives of neighbors and internal rivals alike.

Realms:

  • Amn
  • Calimshan
  • Tethyr

zones.name: The North
zones.txt:
The North is a vast, harsh, and beautiful wilderness where civilization survives only through fear, toil, and blood. It is a land defined by violent extremes, characterized by fertile river valleys and mineral-rich mountains that are constantly threatened by frost giants, dragons, and the relentless reach of winter. In this region, nothing lasts forever, and survival is a day-to-day concern for every inhabitant.

Realms:

  • Icewind Dale
  • Kingdom of Many-Arrows
  • Lords' Alliance
  • Luskan
  • Northern dwarfholds
  • Savage Frontier

zones.name: Old Empires
zones.txt:
The Old Empires of Faerûn—comprising Chessenta, Mulhorand, and Unther—are the most ancient extant civilizations on the continent, boasting a history that spans over three thousand years. Unlike any other region, these realms are uniquely defined by the presence of living gods who walk among mortals, personally issuing divine mandates and leading their followers through cycles of golden ages and devastating wars.

Realms:

  • Chessenta
  • Mulhorand
  • Unther

zones.name: Sword Coast
zones.txt:
The Sword Coast is a vast and cosmopolitan region along the western edge of Faerûn, famous for its massive independent city-states, bustling trade routes, and a history of world-shaking threats. Unlike other regions defined by rigid borders, the Sword Coast is a collection of diverse settlements tied together by the Lords' Alliance to ensure mutual security and economic prosperity.

Realms:

  • Elturgard
  • Lords' Alliance
  • Najara

zones.name: Vilhon Reach
zones.txt:
The Vilhon Reach is a subtropical region located southwest of the Sea of Fallen Stars, characterized by a profound respect for nature and a widespread distrust of arcane magic. Influenced heavily by the Emerald Enclave, which maintains its headquarters on the mystic island of Ilighôn, the region has been shaped by a history of natural disasters and the devastating magical plague known as the Rotting War.

Realms:

  • Chondath
  • Sespech
  • Shining Plains
  • Turmish

markers.name: Airspur
markers.textHtml: The ancient Airspur was a powerful trading port city. It was destroyed during the Spellplague, and rebuilt as a new city that drifts above the shores of Wizards' Reach on an island of floating stone. It is a highly cosmopolitan and multiracial city, with an impressively high population of orcs, half-orcs and genasi. The Church of Talos – locally worshipped as Bhaelros – is powerful here, to the extent of ruling the city at several points in its history. Airspur is a long-standing political rival of neighboring Cimbar.

markers.name: Akanax
markers.textHtml: This large city is in essence a large military camp. In fact, Akanax is a harsh, warlike city known for its talented smiths, its rigid military structure, and its intolerance of outsiders. Structures within Akanax lacked ornamentation, and the majority of its population lived in tents surrounding the city.

markers.name: Alaghôn
markers.textHtml: Alaghôn, the capital of Turmish, is an ancient city, a trade center and the most popular port on the southern fringes of the Inner Sea. There were numerous stonemasons involved in the creation of the stone buildings, vaults, houses, and drains that comprise Alaghôn. This construction created 'a thousand thousand' hiding places that are popular places for children to play. A strong elf presence exists in Alaghôn, but there is not a segregated population of elves within the city, for they live comfortably around humans.

markers.name: Almraiven
markers.textHtml: Almraiven weathered countless disasters and was rebuilt many times, but the city was relatively unscathed by the Spellplague. Archmages worked tirelessly to protect the city from djinn and efreet threats.

markers.name: Amah
markers.textHtml: Amah is a city-state of the Free Cities of the Vilhon Reach that maintains neutrality between Turmish and Chondath. Along with the cities of Nleeth, and Reth, they are sometimes referred to as the Southsands Games. Amah was orignally settled in 320 DR to be used as a neutral training center for Turmish and Chondath armies. As in the late 14th century, Amah and Nleeth were still used as training centers for outside armies, however bloodshed of past was abandoned. The visiting armies of Turmish and Chondath clashed in competitions of combat mettle and tactics.

markers.name: Amruthar
markers.textHtml: Amruthar has a population of more than 41,000 residents (80% human, 8% half-elf, 6% halfling). It is a metropolis that maintains its status as an independent city despite being located in the center of the Thayan tharch of Lapendrar. Its independence was established when the satrap Sekhotet aided the Red Wizards in their rebellion against Mulhorand in exchange for the right to rule the city as he pleased. While Sekhotet's lineage has ended, the zulkirs of Thay continue to honor the original agreement with his successors. But despite this formal independence, the ruler is widely recognized as a puppet of the zulkirs. The city's aristocracy is well-compensated for its complacency, and in return, they surrender a portion of Amruthar's financial transactions to Thay. The city's internal political landscape is defined by three competing factions: the Realists (the largest group, they seek to replace the Hierarch but believe the city must maintain its relationship with Thay to survive), the Independents (they advocate for severing all ties with Thay to become a true city-state), and the Westerners (the smallest faction, they wish to bring the city under the protection of Aglarond).

markers.name: Ankhapur
markers.textHtml: This city is primarily known for its pearl industry, which involves many of its citizens. The mirror industry is also one of the cities' traditional claims to fame. Vorbel wagon wheels are a specific type manufactured here. Unlike all other cities along the Arnaden, Ankhapur alone was not completely resettled by Calishites, having retained their Jhaamdathan roots and appearing more like a city of the Vilhon Reach or Tethyr.

markers.name: Arabel
markers.textHtml: Arabel is a fortified city standing at an important crossroads and a major trade center. Arabel is a merchant city, primarily known for its jewelry. Other major exports include coal, horses, dry red wine, black beer known as Bitter Black, and cheese. The city is the major source of coal for all of Cormyr, obtaining it from the nearby Gnoll Pass. Several major trading companies have outposts within the city, and the city also has a number of powerful local merchants.

markers.name: Arrabar
markers.textHtml: Arrabar is the capital city of Chondath and one of the primary port cities of the Vilhon Reach, known for its crime and corruption. The city is an ancient and sprawling port megalopolis, though the people keep it tidy, clean, and maintained. One of the most distinctive features of Arrabar is greenery. The city is filled with lush gardens, exotic plants, vines crawling up trellises, etc. The people of Arrabar largely engage in trade, crafting, fishing, and mercenary work as part of mercenary companies.

markers.name: Athkatla
markers.textHtml: Athkatla, the City of Coin, is the capital of the mercantile nation of Amn. It's one of the wealthiest countries in all of Faerûn and a city where everything—and everyone—has a price. It is the seat of the Council of Five. Laws are lax concerning morality but strict concerning trade. Notably, 'unlicensed' magic is strictly banned here, enforced by the feared Cowled Wizards.

markers.name: Baldur's Gate
markers.textHtml:
The grim, crowded city of Baldur's Gate sits alongside the River Chionthar. Its nobles, called patriars, reside in the Upper City while most of the rest of the populace lives in the Lower City. Refugees and the destitute make what homes they can in the Outer City. A short way to the east sits the walled district of Little Calimshan, a thriving neighborhood of immigrants from that southern realm. The Council of Four governs Baldur's Gate, led by Grand Duke Ulder Ravengard.

MAP OF BALDUR'S GATE

markers.name: Berdusk
markers.textHtml: Berdusk is known as the Jewel of the Vale as it sits on both the Uldoon Trail and the River Chionthar in Sunset Vale. Berdusk's buildings are tall, steep-roofed, made primarily of stone, and built very close together within the city's high, stone walls. Over the course of the 15th century DR, Berdusk grew enough that many of its buildings and houses ended up outside of these walls. Berdusk is an important trading center, its economy and culture centered around the amount of goods that pass through the city with caravans and travelers. Thanks to the efforts of the Harpers in keeping the city free of danger, as well as the affinity of the city's ruler for the arts, Berdusk is also home to a thriving community of artisans and craftsmen.

markers.name: Bezantur
markers.textHtml: Bezantur, also known as the City of a Thousand Temples or the Citadel of Thieves, is a massive metropolis and the undisputed commercial heart of Thay with a population of more than 136,000 residents (81% human, 8% gnoll, 6% orc). It is the capital of the tharch of Priador and the largest port in the Red Wizards' domain. It is by far the wealthiest city in Thay, fueled by a massive influx of gold, goods, and slaves from Thayan enclaves across the Sea of Fallen Stars. It maintains a legion of nearly 5,000 troops, including gnolls, humans, and darkenbeasts. The city's wealth is managed through the Guild of Foreign Trade. The guild issues licenses and oversees the export of magic items. Yellow-sailed ships known as dromonds depart with potions and scrolls, returning heavily laden with bulkier goods and gold. Bezantur is famous for its religious openness, specifically regarding evil deities. While the worship of Mystra and Azuth is strictly forbidden to protect the Red Wizards' monopoly on arcane knowledge, other gods flourish as Umberlee and Mask.

markers.name: Bildoobaris
markers.textHtml: Bildoobaris, also simply called the Trade Fair, is a large tent city and the unofficial capital of Narfell. Though certainly not a permanent location, the Trade Fair is home to upwards of 30,000 Nar every summer as members of the various tribes meet on the vast plains. The plains are covered for miles in every direction with thousands of hide tents while the Nar welcome merchants from the west to trade goods. This is the only time of the year when foreigners are welcomed in Narfell.

markers.name: Calimport
markers.textHtml: Built by genies long ago, Calimport is the capital city of Calimshan. For most of its existence, it was by far the largest city in all of Faerûn. One could find anything they want in Calimport, if they are willing to pay a high enough price. Calimport has more to offer in sales than even Waterdeep.

markers.name: Caer Calidyrr
markers.textHtml: Caer Callidyrr is a sprawling, walled port city that stands in the shadow of the massive and splendid castle that shares the city's name. It is the capital city of the Ffolk people on the Moonshaes island of Alaron, as well as the Kingdom of Callidyrr, and the seat of the High Kings and Queens of the Ffolk. The city is a busy, bustling hub of trade and barter. It is the biggest and the wealthiest city of the Moonshaes, with a small flotilla of fishing vessels entering and leaving the bay daily. The city's shipyards construct sturdy seafaring vessels, and docks provide anchorage to many trade galleons and longships.

markers.name: Caer Corwell
markers.textHtml: A small city and castle that served as the capital of the Ffolk kingdom of Corwell through the late 14th century DR. It is small enough to be located mostly on a hill leading down to the port. The city is overlooked by a castle located on a tall rocky cliff that north of the waters of the harbor. However, Caer Corwellfell into ruin following the conquest of Gwynneth and expulsion of the humans by the fey of Sarifal. It lays abandoned as of the late 15th century DR.

markers.name: Caer Westphal
markers.textHtml: Caer Westphal is located in a small cove that serves as a natural harbor on the southern coast of the island of Snowdown. As the largest city on the island, it was the seat of power for the Kingdom of Snowdown and subsequently the seat of Amn's military occupation of Snowdown after its seizure in the early 15th century DR. Under Amnian occupation, Westphal and the rest of Snowdown only do business swith that nation. Because of this, its harbor is typically full of ships flying the colors of Amn. Each day, a small squadron of these ships leave Westphal for Athkatla, loaded with goods and resources.

markers.name: Calaunt
markers.textHtml: Calaunt has long been an independent city-state, but by 1479 DR, it had united with Ravens Bluff and Tantras in the nation of Vesperin. It is governed by the Supreme Scepter and the Merchant Dukes, who form an oppressive and kleptocratic regime. Calaunt is one of the wealthiest cities in the Vast, having earned this position thanks to its monopoly on all legal trade in and out of the River Vesper; the iron grip of the local thieves’ guild, the Shadowcloaks', on fencing, piracy, and smuggling in the city; and ruthless competition with Ylraphon. Nevertheless, it remains a place of squalor and poverty. Corruption remains rife, and money, influence, and blackmail can see dodgy deeds go unhindered. The city's major industry is tanning, with several large tanneries located there and polluting the harbor water. The smell of Calaunt's tanneries can be detected for miles around, and, in the heat of summer, the foul reek swamps all other smells.

markers.name: Cedarspoke
markers.textHtml: Cedarspoke, also known as Cedarsproke, is the only settlement in the Gulthmere Forest. It is a small independent city inhabited and ruled by druids. The residents of Cedarsproke are simple folk who seek to live off land and druids who rarely interact with the Emerald Enclave, the major power in the Vilhon Reach. Silvanites of Cedarsproke live in harmony with Nobanion, the Lion King of the Vilhon Reach, Dragon Coast, and Shining Plains. Merchants and traders occasionally use Cedar River to transport their goods into Cedarsproke. The religious significance bring a high number of pilgrims to the druid stronghold every year, and many adventurers travel to Cedarsproke in search of ancient lore and history.

markers.name: Cimbar
markers.textHtml: Cimbar is a city-state and the former capital of Chessenta. The city is ruled by the red dragon Tchazzar, and its navy is second in size only to the navy of Thay when compared to other fleets of the Inner Sea. Cimbar is also known as a city of philosophy and art, and its University is said to be the greatest center of learning in all the Realms.

markers.name: City of the Werecrocodiles
markers.textHtml: The City of the Werecrocodiles is a half-sunken settlement sitting on a large island in the middle of the western fork of the Adder River where it meets the coast. It was constructed out of mud bricks, but never fully completed. Many of the buildings and walls are half-submerged and crumbling. The eastern portion of the city is more complete, with short square buildings huddled together in a semblance of city blocks connected by muddy canals. This area contains a great pyramid, from which the High Priest of Sebek rules the city. The city is large and sprawling, far larger than necessary to accommodate the werecrocodile and wererat communities living in it.

markers.name: Colletro
markers.textHtml: One of the city-states of the Blade Kingdoms, in the Akanapeaks' rich Valley of Umbricci.

markers.name: Crimmor
markers.textHtml: Crimmor, also known as the City of Caravans or the Caravan Capital of Amn, is a fortified city and the focal point of practically all trades between the Sword Coast North and the Lands of Intrigue. The constant stream of travelers makes it the most diverse Amnian city. Built on the southern shore of the Alandor River, Crimmor is a city constantly bustling with life. A strong stink of ox dung fills the streets, courtesy of the unusual quantity of wagons roaming them. Caravaneers set camp outside of the city, filling the southern lands with tents and cattle. Most of the city is built from stone, from the buildings to the cobbled streets. Buildings are tall and narrow, touching each other as to not waste any space.

markers.name: Daerlun
markers.textHtml: Daerlun declared itself a neutral independent city-state in 1439 DR, rather than be absorbed by Cormyr or stay under the direct influence of Sembia. It is the most heavily fortified city in Sembia, and boasts the tallest curtain wall on all of Toril. This 500-foot-tall (150 m) wall is made of magically hard obsidian and was erected by the Netherese Empire in 1382 DR. Patrols out of Daerlun—mounted on flying griffons or veserabs—range as far as the Vast Swamp, the Thunder Peaks, and the southern Dales.

markers.name: Darmshall
markers.textHtml: Darmshall serves as a critical hub for both the farming and the mining communities of southern Vaasa, and it is as much a fortress as it is a city. It is surrounded by capable stone walls lined with siege weaponry. Beneath the city is a huge storage facility for food and supplies to enable the city to withstand long periods of isolation. During the reign of the Warlock Knights in the late 15th century DR, the town is occupied by the Knights' forces and otherwise is mostly populated by human serfs working as farmers or ranchers. It is notable for having a fairly small non-human population relative to other cities in Vaasa.

markers.name: Darromar
markers.textHtml: Darromar, formerly known as Ithmong, is a metropolis and the capital of Tethyr. The city is divided into quarters by the River Ith (east–west) and the Ithal Road (north–south), with the Ithal Bridge in the center.

markers.name: Delthuntle
markers.textHtml: A commercial center and port in Aglarond, Delthuntle primarily makes a profit through trade with the genasi nation of Akanûl, especially its capital, Airspur. This trade with Akanûl helps give the city one of the highest non-native genasi populations throughout Faerûn. There is also a large halfling community in Delthuntle.

markers.name: Derlusk
markers.textHtml: Derlusk is a city of music: bards are highly regarded, and every inn, tavern, tankard-house, and fencing club has its own musical entertainment. Also full of scriveners, bookbinders and book collectors, Derlusk is the city to come to if you sought an old, rare book. Evidence of wealth is everywhere in the city, with many of the stout stone buildings appearing as a beautiful vista of slate roofs and shimmering lanterns at night. The city's port, one of the busiest in the Border Kingdoms, has several rival shipyards, best known for building swift, sleek 'wave runner' coastboats.

markers.name: Dilpur
markers.textHtml: Dilpur was a rather insignificant location until its population swelled with refugees from neighboring Sarshel when that city fell into decline after a drop-off in trade from Damara in the 14th century.

markers.name: Djerad Thymar
markers.textHtml: This fortress-city was the capital of two former dragonborn kingdoms, Tymanther and Tymanchebar. With the return of Gilgeam in the Second Sundering, the armies of Unther have shattered the power of Tymanther, but Djerad Thymar still stands as the sole surviving bastion of the dragonborn in the Old Empires. It is renowned throughout Faerûn for its credible size, unique shape, and virtually impenetrable architecture. Unique in its design, the entire city is one colossal structure carved into the face of a mountain, its basis an immense block of granite, on top of which sit a huge number of pillars supporting a giant, hollow flat-topped pyramid. Djerad Thymar is also known across Faerûn for its military schools on tactics, engineering, and unconventional warfare—particularly dragon-fighting.

markers.name: Duhlnarim
markers.textHtml: [not Duhlnarimm] A port city on the Shalane River, ruled by a baron. The mercantile H'Krabil clan operated from Duhlnarim around 1370 DR.

markers.name: Eartheart
markers.textHtml: Eartheart is the primary settlement of the gold dwarves in East Rift in the late 15th century DR and their cultural and spiritual heart. It used to house great temples and shrines to all the dwarven pantheon and dwarf heroes but after the great collapse much was lost. Yet the city is still renowned for its magnificent architecture and temples. A lot of the higher levels are filled with sunlight, while in the lower levels the perpetual darkness is fended off with dim watch lanterns and magical lights. These are meant to guard against off the monsters and drow that come up from the Underdark. Ringing Eartheart is a curtain wall interspersed with great towers known as the Riftedge Towers, each with a garrison of soldiers.

markers.name: Eshpurta
markers.textHtml: Eshpurta is a fortified, bustling military trade town in eastern Amn. It sits at the confluence of Trifin Creek and the River Rimril where they merge to later join the Amstel River. Eshpurta is known as the 'Shield City' because it has more troops stationed there than anywhere else in Amn. The business of the city largely revolves around the military with hundreds of blacksmiths, weaponsmiths, armorers, leatherworkers, stablekeepers, etc. Large mining operations pull iron and nickel out of the Troll Mountains to feed the smelters.

markers.name: Elbulder
markers.textHtml: Elbulder was established as a logging town for Sespech on the River Arran. Elbulder's lumber was sent up River Arran to the Vilhon Reach and onwards to the Sea of Fallen Stars. However, during the Rotting War, the disapproval of the Emerald Enclave meant that the logging operation could not continue. But Elbulder remained active as a trading town after the lumbering industry was abandoned. The Old Road brings a constant stream of traders, travelers, and adventurers to support Elbulder's economy.

markers.name: Elturel
markers.textHtml: Elturel is a city-state situated atop a bluff with a cliff dominating the River Chionthar, and surrounded by a stout stone wall. In the mid–14th century DR, it was a center for agriculture and trade in the region, and was renowned for its elite mounted defenders, the Hellriders. In the late 15th century DR, it is the capital of the realm of Elturgard, a theocracy of Torm the True and defended by its paladin knighthood, the Order of the Companion. Elturgard's laws are rigid, intolerant, and persecute evil with inquisitorial zeal, leading to a host of problems.

markers.name: Eltabbar
markers.textHtml: Eltabbar is the former capital of Thay, on the edge of Lake Thaylambar within the tharch that shared its name, and the nation's second-largest city with a population of more than 123,000 residents (83% human, 8% gnoll, 7% orc). Its financial assets are immense. Security is maintained by a massive City Guard which includes a dedicated force of over 1,600 gnolls. Eltabbar is the hub of the Thayan slave trade. Every year, thousands of individuals are brought to the city's markets to be auctioned off on bloodstained public blocks. Most slaves purchased here face a grim future of backbreaking labor and a rapid death. During the decade-long War of the Zulkirs, Eltabbar remained a seat of power for the Council of Zulkirs as they fought against Szass Tam's forces of northern Thay.

markers.name: Erebos
markers.textHtml: A large city that has trade relationships with the lycanthrope nation of Sebakar. In the 15th century DR, the red dragon Tchazzar appeared again in the Realms and claimed Erebos as his own domain.

markers.name: Escalant
markers.textHtml: Escalant, located within the tharch of same name, is a significant coastal city with a population of more than 28,000 residents (82% human, 5% gnoll, 5% half-elf, 5% half-orc). It is considered the second most important Thayan port, surpassed only by Bezantur. Escalant is quite prosperous and its docks are regularly visited with ships from Chessenta, Mulhorand, Turmish, and beyond. To enforce this and maintain order, several barracks are stationed throughout the city, each containing one hundred gnolls. These 'Thayan brutes' patrol the streets and are known to make brutal examples of criminals and tax cheats. The Tharchion's Guard is a substantial military force. This includes various tiers of fighters and warriors, bolstered by 620 gnolls without formal class levels. The Shadowmasters is a powerful thieves' guild within the city.

markers.name: Esmeltaran
markers.textHtml: Esmeltaran is a large city in central Amn that served as its capital for much of its history. By the late 15th century DR, halflings had become the dominant folk in the city. Esmeltaran is dominated by tourism for the wealthy, as it is warm in winter, possesses beautiful architecture, and actively puts out advertising that ensures it is the most highly-regarded spot in all of Amn. It is filled with boarding houses, inns, festhalls, theaters, clubs, and villas that catered to these visitors. The nearby lake Esmel is a spot for expensive health spas and lake bathing, recreational boating and fishing, as well as a quite lucrative commercial fishing.

markers.name: Evereska
markers.textHtml: Evereska is a secreted city within a fabled valley in the Western Heartlands, one of the last realms of the elves in the North. It is situated on a large stone pedestal, formed by the marble cliffs known as the Three Sisters, that rise nearly 1,000 ft (300 m) from the grassland below. Beyond the city are a grouping of flat farmlands known as the Meadow, the edge of which marks the border of the city's magical protection. The wall is surrounded by the Vine Vale, a stretch of terraced vineyards and orchards obscured by mists that shelter the Evereska from outsiders. Founded in secret during the Founding Time, Evereska became a haven for the remaining Fair Folk in Faerûn following the Retreat. It is the epitome of elven culture and beauty, and nearly no outsiders are considered welcome within. As of the late 14th century DR, Evereska was only known to most humans by mere rumor or legend.

markers.name: Everlund
markers.textHtml: Everlund (formerly Besander's Blockhouse) is a city of merchant traders and caravan teams and one of the greatest supporters of the league of the Silver Marches. Situated on the River Rauvin, Everlund is the second largest city in the Silver Marches and one of its most active mercantile communities.

markers.name: Elversult
markers.textHtml: [not Eversult] Elversult was founded at the confluence of a number of freshwater ponds set between rolling hills and forested ridges. A rocky tor known as Temple Hill in the center of Elversult overlooks the city environs, which lacking walls sprawled beyond the woodlands. Elversult is an important trading and mercantile center that facilitates both honest and illegitimate trade across the Eastern Heartlands and beyond. Beyond its reputation as a great trade city and hotspot of the latest trends, Elversult is known across nearly all of Faerûn as the birthplace of the renewed church of Amaunator and the god's second sun that shines bright in the skies above.

markers.name: Furthinghome
markers.textHtml: Furthinghome is a trading port city in Aglarond and one of the oldest cities in the nation. It is made up of some buildings in the traditional Aglarondan weathered wood style and others made from stone. It also contains a large slum where the buildings are simple huts, populated by those settlers who were driven out of the Yuirwood, and most of which work at the docks or as laborers or drivers. The port in Furthinghome is small and shallow, which limits the size of vessels that can dock. Merchants import spices, textiles, tools, and weapons, and export fish and a small amount of timber from the Yuirwood, limited in quantity by its half-elf occupants. The city is also known for its horticulture.

markers.name: Gheldaneth
markers.textHtml: Gheldaneth is the second largest city in Mulhorand and the only one in Mulhorand to survive the Spellplague after 1385 DR. The city houses many tieflings, descendants of slaves who made pacts with dark forces.

markers.name: Goliad
markers.textHtml: Goliad is a small high-walled city and the seat of power for the Duchy of Brandiar. Its critical location at the junction of Damara's two main rivers makes it highly influential. The village was rebuilt at least twice since its founding. It was here that two major historical battles between the evil forces of Vaasa and the people of Damara took place.

markers.name: Hardcastle
markers.textHtml: Little more than a fortified trading camp, Hardcastle is strategically built at the crossroads of three major caravan routes between the Great Rift, Unther, and the Vilhon Reach. It quickly became a hub for trade.

markers.name: Helgabal
markers.textHtml: [not Heliogabalus] Lord Yarin Frostmantle renamed the city of Heliogabalus as Helgabal, and rules both the city and the kingdom from his palace as a tyrannical king. The city sits at or near the intersection of every major trade artery in Damara, making it a critical lynchpin in the kingdom's economy. Various independent merchant and trade guilds allow Helgabal to heavily influence the supply of goods to all of Damara, and it is the farthest stop north for most merchants traveling into the Bloodstone Lands. However, by 1479 DR, the fortunes of Helgabal had begun to dwindle as southern merchants hesitated to venture into the domain of the unpredictable and despotic King Frostmantle. The city is home to several thieves guilds, and there is also a population of celebrated dwarven craftsfolk in the city.

markers.name: Hilbrand
markers.textHtml: Hilbrand, also known as Tilbrand, is a walled port city. It remained an independent city until the mid–14th century DR when it was forced to surrendered to Thayan invaders during the Salamander War of 1357 DR. However, although nominally a Thayan vassal, the city continues to rule itself and simply pays tribute to Thay. With years, it has grown increasingly confident that Thay is not willing to risk a military venture to punish the city for failing to pay, and so it has begun to give only some of the tribute demanded of it. In that time, it has also grown closer with Aglarond and with the influential Waverunner clan, both of whom help to defend the city.

markers.name: Hillsfar
markers.textHtml: Hillsfar is a walled city, fortified, and is the military force of the Red Plumes who ensure the city's security from foreign forces. In the 15th century DR, the military is known as the Tower Guard and is supplemented by a powerful mages guild. Although trade in the Moonsea is typically carried out through rivalries and competition, almost all the goods of the region pass through Hillsfar in the hands of middlemen and intermediary merchants. The city was once a port where stolen and illicit goods were traded, but this is no longer the case. By the late 15th century DR, the city is ruled by a senate of thirty-one wealthy merchants and guildmasters. However, since the senate could rarely agree on anything, actual rule of the city falls to the First Lord Torin Nomerthal.

markers.name: Hlammach
markers.textHtml: Hlammach was a bustling, walled port city which, like many of Impiltur's grand cities, was struck hard by the magical alterations of the Spellplague and the recession of the waters of the Sea of Fallen Stars during the late 14th century DR. Before that time, Hlammach was home to much of the realm's great wealthy and old guard of noble families, and the royal mint of Impiltur was located here, receiving many of its gold and silver shipments from the mines of the Earthfast Mountains.

markers.name: Hlondeth
markers.textHtml: Hlondeth or the City of Serpents is an ancient and powerful independent city-state, with a longtime association with serpents. It is ruled by the Extaminos yuan-ti family. Hlondeth is walled and known for its distinctive architecture, reminiscent of snake burrows, graceful round towers with round doorways. The wide use of beautiful, emerald-hued stone from nearby quarries and the city's tall, graceful arches and coils give Hlondeth a unique appearance. Hlondeth is a very prosperous city and active in trade and commerce with its neighbors. The people of Hlondeth value trade, ritualized combat, and nature. Magic-users are treated with suspicion, and the city's kobold population is kept in an enslaved state. Hlondeth welcomes visitors of most races with open arms, and the city itself is considered a pleasant place. Its yuan-ti inhabitants do not discriminate or mistreat humans.

markers.name: Hulburg
markers.textHtml: The city is situated between two rocky headlands, Keldon Head and Easthead. By the late 15th century DR, Spellplague-changed land is common in the vicinity of Hulburg and unnatural green hills rise just to the east of the city. Hulburg was destroyed in 1347 DR by a horde of orcs in alliance with forces of Zhentil Keep. It remained in ruins for decades, but from the late 14th century on, the city slowly repopulated, and as of the late 15th century DR, it has several thousand inhabitants, while still occupying only a fraction of the area of historical Hulburg. There are many vacant lots in the city, and new construction is built on top of the foundations and basements of ruined buildings.

markers.name: Immilmar
markers.textHtml: Capital of Rashemen. The Iron Lord, the nominal leader of the nation, rules from a mighty castle raised centuries ago by hathran magic and warded with spells. Beyond the castle, citizens dwell in A-frame timber lodges. The city is insular, and visitors are encouraged to leave as quickly as possible.

markers.name: Immurk's Hold
markers.textHtml: Immurk's Hold is the largest city found within the Pirate Isles on the Sea of Fallen Stars. It was named for the pirate Immurk, who founded it in 1182 DR. Practically any form of trade associated with the sea is conducted within Immurk's Hold. A set of pirate traditions, known as Immurk's Code, serves as the accepted code of conduct in the city.

markers.name: Innarlith
markers.textHtml: Innarlith is a city-state that has political control over the surrounding areas, including as far north as the southern edge of the Nagaflow river and as far south as the Firesteap Mountains. The head of the government is called the Ransar, an elected ruler for life. The Ransar presides over a senate comprising of the aristocracy, who also hold their positions for life. The city often has a bad smell to it, owing to the wind that blows in sulfurous air from the Lake of Steam. The lake keeps the city damp and warm most of the year.

markers.name: Iriaebor
markers.textHtml: Iriaebor, also known as the Overland City and the City of a Thousand Spires, is a large city standing on a sprawling ridge above the river Chionthar, on the southern end of the Dusk Road in Sunset Vale. Iriaebor carries the caravan trade on the Dusk Road over the rapids and rough water of the upper Chionthar river, and link up with the Trader's Road. While it retains its status as an independent city-state, it remains within the realm of influence of the 15th century nation of Elturgard. And while considered to be one of the most densely-populated and economically influential locales in western Faerûn, it is believed its true potential as a great power is stymied by the long history of intrigue and conflict that emerged amongst the various mercantile groups, influential Iriaeban families and other factions.

markers.name: Ithmong
markers.textHtml: Ithmong stands on the Lapal Way on top of a cliff that stands 50 feet (15 m) high at the head of Ithmong Bay and beside Ithmong Peninsula. Owing to its position, Ithmong suffers the majority of storms that traverse the Shining Sea, hence its title of 'Stormwracked City'. Ithmong's docks are constructed inside a series of a dozen linked seacaves at the base of the cliffs. Together, they give the city an underground port district. A high volume of goods from the Lapaliiyan hinterlands and Halruaa pass through these, making it the second richest city in the realm. The civic deity of Ithmong is Bhaelros, also known as Talos, the Storm Lord.

markers.name: Keczulla
markers.textHtml: Keczulla is a prosperous city that is the home of diverse and rich mining industry. Keczulla's gem mines are at the center of the town's culture, religion, and trade. Apart from gem mining, Keczulla has electrum mines outside of the city, iron mines, salt mines, among many others. The city is also known for the blistering sun heat that reach its peak in highsun, especially harshly felt during the markets.

markers.name: Keltar
markers.textHtml: Keltar, one of the oldest cities in Faerûn, is famous for its cattle trade and for being the site of the House of the Broken God. This temple to Ilmater, the largest in all of Faerûn, covers the entirety of the city's northwest district. The city contains a massive sluice gate to divert the waters of the Calim River and boasts many canals flowing through its easternmost districts. The majority of the city is on the west side of the river, but the cattle processing centers are on the east side. All kinds of livestock are traded, herded, slaughtered, and packed here. Keltar is also well known for its leather trade, especially its fine saddles.

markers.name: Kingsbay
markers.textHtml: A small port town comprising about one hundred cottages clustered around the coast at the base of a steep, rocky bluff. Kingsbay was one of the two most important ports for maritime trade in all of Corwell, with the other being Caer Corwell on the western coast. Fishing was a major industry, and the whole town smelled of fish. However, Kingsbay fell into ruin following the conquest of Gwynneth and expulsion of the humans by the fey of Sarifal. By the late 15th century DR, it is occupied by escaped Ffolk slaves, and is one of only two such free human ports on the island.

markers.name: Khôltar
markers.textHtml: Khôltar is a small city on the edge of the Great Rift, known as the Iron City because of its 30 m tall double walls covered in iron. Inside the walls, most structures are solid keeps of stone, cylindrical or rectangular, between five and nine stories tall. A constant stream of lumber flows into the city to feed the forges that work the iron into the primary export: ironmongery. From the constant forge smoke to the open sewers to the garbage between the walls, it is difficult to find any clear air in the Iron City. Nighttime is almost as busy—the skyline is lit by the fires of many forges and traffic is guided by burning braziers. The Iron City is a no-nonsense, business-oriented community that values hard work and has little tolerance for laziness.

markers.name: Lachom
markers.textHtml: Lachom is a peaceful, free city that works the bountiful fields between Deepwing and the Cloven Mountains, with Surkh as an important trade partner. Since gaining independence from Chondath in the 10th century DR in the wake of the Rotting War, Lachom is ruled by a Mayor; and like most free cities of the Vilhon Reach, it celebrates an annual festival that commemorates the shattering of Chondath.

markers.name: Laothkund
markers.textHtml: This city came under Thayan control in 1357 DR, during the Salamander War. The city became Laothkund the Drowned by 1396 DR, after the seismic shifts from the Spellplague caused the land to sink beneath the waves. By 1479 DR, the submerged and silted streets are populated only by fish and sea monsters. Much of the city's old wealth have yet to be plundered.

markers.name: Lasdur
markers.textHtml: An independent city-state.

markers.name: Llorbauth
markers.textHtml: Llorbauth is the capital of Erlkazar and the largest city in the kingdom. The city has official trade deals with Nimpeth, Ormpetarr, and several other ports in the Vilhon Reach, by means of the Deepwash and the Wintercloak River. A full 40 percent of the nation's sea trade comes through this city, and the king controls trade coming in and out of the port and the majority of the agricultural trade as well. Llorbauth is famous for a particular beer brewed in the city, known as Old One Eye.

markers.name: Lushpool
markers.textHtml: Lushpool, known as the City of Shimmering Pools, stands on the Lapal Way, by the mouth of Talar Bay in the sheltered lee of the Ithmong Peninsula. It is built upon a hill cut with seven great terraces. All over the city are splendid hanging gardens and pools draped in mosses, and the city is famed for its beauty. Several rare species of freshwater fish are bred in the city's pools. Their roe are delicacies all around Faerûn and the city's primary export, bringing Lushpool most of its riches. The Eselemaa yuan-ti also trade illicit goods through Lushpool. The civic deity of the city is Eldath.

markers.name: Luskan
markers.textHtml:
The City of Sails

Ruler: the five High Captains of the Ships (Beniago Kurth, Barri Baram, Dagmaer Suljack, Throa Taerl and Hartouchen Rethnor).

markers.name: Luthcheq
markers.textHtml: Luthcheq is a port city and the capital city of Chessenta. Luthcheq had a martial culture that valued physical fitness and the military arts. Prior to 1479 DR, mages were persecuted and forced to live in a ghetto.

markers.name: Lyrabar
markers.textHtml: The capital of Impiltur is a long and narrow port city, stretching over a mile along the coast. It houses a large fleet of ships, both military and mercantile. The port is well-protected by the royal warships. Lyrabar boasts numerous rival sailing academies, which host regattas and other contests of skill year-round. Lyrabar also hosts the headquarters of the Warswords, chainmail-clad soldiers responsible for patrolling Impiltur's trade roads.

markers.name: Marsember
markers.textHtml: Marsember, also known as the City of Spices, is a metropolis and the busiest port in Cormyr. The main trades in Marsember are spices—the local merchant companies traveled great distances to acquire these substances—and fish trade.

markers.name: Melvaunt
markers.textHtml: Melvaunt, also known as the City of Swords, is a walled city of smithing and mercantile services, as well as factories serving as the port of entry to many travelers coming to the region. Melvaunt is a cold and severe city, beneath the oppressive clouds of heavy smoke that constantly billow from its many manufactories. Beneath the smoke in the sky and fog that comes up from the sea, Melvaunt appears as if were lit by several hundred points of light. These lights are actually the fires of city' great many forges and Realms-renowned smithies. Crime runs rampant through Melvaunt, as the city's nobles are far more concerned with their financial interests than the Melvauntian citizens.

markers.name: Memnon
markers.textHtml: Memnon, a fishing town, and a key port of trade for Calimshan, was the former capital of the efreeti kingdom of Memnonnar. Nearly all caravaneers on the Trade Way stop there for at least a day, regardless if they are traveling north or south, as Memnon is on the edge of the Calim Desert.

markers.name: Messemprar
markers.textHtml: The large city of Messemprar is the finest and most hospitable settlement in Unther, and the only city in Unther not presently under the thumb of the God-king Gilgeam. Instead, a coalition of anti-Gilgeam mages called the North Wizards controls the settlement. The North Wizards foster a brand of democracy otherwise unknown in the Old Empires. A prime port, Messemprar is beset on all sides by would-be conquerors: war ships from Chessenta, the armies of Mulhorand, and Inner Sea pirates. Adventurers are in constant demand to protect the city's independence.

markers.name: Mimph
markers.textHtml: Mimph is a busy port city with its harbor chuck-full of ships nestled close to each other. Most buildings in Mimph are made out hand-raved of pale gray granite and are shaped like sharp-angled small, simple squares. Mimph and its closest naval neighbor, Arrabar, share certain animosity towards each other. Many merchants conduct business in only one of the two cities at the time not to provoke the other. It is common for the Arrabarran vessels to board ships heading to Mimph and claiming their cargo. Despite lacking land trade routes, Mimph receives merchants who dislike doing business with Chondath. These caravans leave the Old Road after reaching Ormpetarr and travel through roadless territory or take boats across the Nagawater.

markers.name: Mintar
markers.textHtml: Mintar is a large city-state that was conquered by a faction of the Church of Bane led by Teldorn Darkhope in the mid-14th century DR, and is ruled as a theocratic dictatorship. A resistance movement exists among the populace, referred to by some as the 'Pursuers of Freedom' due to the central role of the Pursuers of Pure Knowledge, the Oghmanytes of Mintar. Before its conquest by Banites, Mintar produced boats and ships, seafood, wagons, and wine. They were the primary shipbuilders of the region. All of these goods continued to be produced following the Banite takeover, but ceased to be exported.

markers.name: Mirabar
markers.textHtml: A mining city of great wealth, occupied by shield dwarves who live underground near to their workshops, which produce metal goods for export in the region and beyond. The humans above cooperate with the dwarves to handle the mining, move the ore to market, and defend the city against magical threats. Mirabar is ruled by a hereditary marchion, but the true power is in an elected assembly called the Council of Sparkling Stones, a mixed dwarven and human group that meets once a year to determine production quotas. On the surface, Mirabar is populated with squat stone buildings and a few stone towers. The area around the city is dotted with open mines and heaps of rock. Roads lead to its major mines in the Spine of the World mountains. Making maps of the city is illegal for security purposes, because the city is concerned about intelligence getting into the hands of Mirabar's enemies.

markers.name: Mordulkin
markers.textHtml: Mordulkin is a wealthy mage-ruled city-state on the Bay of Chessenta, one of the largest port facilities in Chessenta, a center of worship of the god Azuth and a haven for mages. But, by 1479 DR, the city-state ruled by House Jedea is in ruins. Its nearest neighbour and rival is Luthcheq.

markers.name: Mourktar
markers.textHtml: Mourktar is an independent city-state, that later became the capital of Threskel, before Threskel itself was subjugated as a vassal state of Chessentan the late 15th century DR. The city houses a grand port that greatly benefits the many farmers, herders, and miners of the region. Mourktar is also home to a great many mercenaries and merchants, all of whom regularly benefit when conflicts arise in the surrounding lands.

markers.name: Mulmaster
markers.textHtml: While it is a bustling and lively metropolis, Mulmaster exemplifies the worst consequences that arise when merciless commercial enterprise, unchecked greed and unabashed imperialist ideals take hold throughout a populace. Built within the depression formed by an arc of overbearing peaks, the city's buildings and winding streets stretch on a slant down towards the harbor. The buildings nearest the docks are the most dilapidated and dangerous, full of poverty and rife with crime. Further up towards the city's walls are the more affluent areas of the city, set along the mountainside. Governance of Mulmaster is carried out by a ruling council of sixteen individuals known as the Blades, also known as the Council of Blades. At their disposal are the Hawks, a clandestine group of spies and assassins; the Cloaks, Mulmaster's official mage guild; and the Soldiery, the city's armed forces. By the late 15th century DR, the church of Bane has become the dominant faith in the city.

markers.name: Murann
markers.textHtml: Murann, known as the Sailors' City, was a port city and the second-largest city of Amn. The city's economy was based on sea trade, including shipbuilding, whaling, and a well-established transoceanic trade with Maztica. This was until 1371 DR, when it was conquered by the ogres and orcs of the Sothillisian Empire and became the capital of the newly established kingdom of Muranndin.

markers.name: Murghyr
markers.textHtml: Murghyr is the capital city of the country of Murghôm, an inland nation of farmers and horsemen in East Faerûn. Before the 15th century DR, it was theoretically a subjugated territory of Mulhorand. However, Murghôm was an independent nation, rival to Semphar. In the 15th century DR, Murghôm became a nation ruled by dragons.

markers.name: Myratma
markers.textHtml: Myratma's strong resemblance to Calimshan is obvious. It's the main port for agricultural goods to and from the inlands.

markers.name: Myrinjar
markers.textHtml: Myrinjar, also known as The Watched City, is the Capital and main seat of power of the small Border Kingdom of Adaerglast. It is a small, very prosperous city crowded with narrow buildings standing up to four stories. Myrinjar is situated atop fine sand deposits, which requires the Lord High Mages to use 'forcebinding' spells to prevent cellar and whole buildings from collapsing due to shifting sands. This location has led to a thriving glass-blowing industry, and the city is famous for its fine glassware and bottles, as well as its thriving cork and wax industry, both used in the sealing of bottles. Wizards and magical items are forbidden in Myrinjar.

markers.name: Myth Dyraalis
markers.textHtml: Myth Dyraalis is a city of elves, gnomes, and other sylvan creatures that thrives hidden for untold millennia within the Forest of Mir, protected from the outside world by a magical field, a subject of fables, legends, and tavern talk. The city's center is dominated by the 'Mother Tree', a gargantuan, hollowed trunk tree whose roots spread all across the radius of the city.

markers.name: Nathlekh
markers.textHtml: Nathlekh was built around a granite plug, atop which stood the Pride of Nobanion, a temple devoted to the King of Lions, seemingly as an extension of the rock. Nathlekh was originally populated by feline lycanthropes worshiping Nobanion and Sharess. It had a significant population of mundane cats (including lions) and its mysterious rulers were known as the Cat Lords. However, settled by more and more immigrants from Kara-Tur over the centuries, by 1479 DR, the city had become populated almost entirely by Shou and the small nation of Nathlan had grown up around it. The citizens of Nathlekh are suspicious of outsiders. They forbid anyone not of Shou descent (especially non-humans) from taking up residence in the city and restrict visitors to a single, special section of the city.

markers.name: Neldorild
markers.textHtml: Neldorild was the fourth largest city in Mulhorand in 1357 DR. Compared to the other major cities of Mulhorand, Neldorild is a new city.

markers.name: Nethra
markers.textHtml: Under the name of Nethrar, this city was the capital of the kingdom of Galadorna until its fall in 764 DR. It is today an independent city-state.

markers.name: Neverwinter
markers.textHtml:
The City of Skilled Hands was once known for its master crafters, fine goods, and lavish gardens heated year-round by the Neverwinter River, flowing from Mount Hotenow. When the volcano erupted and a great chasm to the Underdark opened, Neverwinter was nearly destroyed. Now, with the volcano's wrath spent and the chasm sealed, the city is rebuilding itself under the rule of Lord Dagult Neverember.

MAP OF NEVERWINTER

markers.name: Nimpeth
markers.textHtml: Nimpeth is one of the Free Cities of the Vilhon Reach and, just like other city-states of the Vilhon Reach, exists in a constant state of conflict, diplomatic or military. The city was renowned for its slave and mercenary trade, but in the chaos of Spellplague, slavery collapsed in the city, and by the late 15th century, it never recovered. Another export important export from Nimpeth is wine: the lands around Nimpeth hold numerous vineyards producing some of the best wine in Faerûn. Another big contributor to Nimpeth's economy is its proximity to the trade road known as the Golden Road. Most of Nimpeth's city walls and buildings were constructed by slave labor, including the city's seat of power – the Citadel of Carifar. The most impressive achievement of slave labor lays to the city's north, in the Broken Hills. The rugged hills were turned into sprawling agricultural wonder – the Woren Vineyards.

markers.name: Nleeth
markers.textHtml: Nleeth is a Turmish-sponsored city-state, one of the Free Cities of the Vilhon Reach. It was established in 320 DR, alongside the cities of Amah and Reth, to be used as training settlements for the Chondathan and Turmish armies. Nleeth was used as Turmish host, while Reth housed Chondathan troops. Amah was the neutral territory between two opponent cities. The training exercises started attracting spectators from across the Vilhon Reach, and tourism became a major part of Nleeth's economy.

markers.name: Nonthal
markers.textHtml: Nonthal is reputed for being the most peaceful and stable city in the nation of Turmish. The city's main trade is cattle. Nonthal is surrounded by a strong aroma of manure, slaughterhouses, and the chemical stench of its many tanneries. Notably, Nonthal has a significant gnomish population. Many of them are illusionists by trade and make a living entertaining and pranking travelers.

markers.name: Ordulin
markers.textHtml: Ordulin was the capital of Sembia, until it was destroyed in a planar rift summoned by a Sharran priestess in 1374 DR. There was a constant ominous cloud above the city until 1484 DR.

markers.name: Ormath
markers.textHtml: Of the three cities it shares the Shining Plains with, Ormath is the most prone to conflict over the slightest transgression. The city keeps watch over Pikemen's Folly, the road connecting Ormath, Lheshayl, and Hlondeth, and controls the intersection the city was built around, namely the junction between the Folly and the north road. The city frequently conducts patrols along roads, removing any threats to the safety of the land.

markers.name: Ormpetarr
markers.textHtml: Ormpetarr is the capital city of Sespech, a port city on the eastern shores of the Nagawater, and an ancient metropolis protected by a thick wall. Ormpetarr was a banking center and handled all trade on the Golden Road and a busy caravan stop. It was surrounded by lush fertile fields growing grains for export. Other major exports included fish, and horses. Between the Spellplague and the Second Sundering however, the city became a center for Scar Pilgrimages from whence travelers ventured into the rain Wilds in hopes of acquiring a spellscar. At that time, Ormpetarr existed thanks to pilgrims and travelers that passed through the region. Its streets were dotted with establishments that catered to pilgrims.

markers.name: Ormpur
markers.textHtml: Ormpur is an independent city-state, one of the Cities of the Seabreeze, and is often treated as part of the realm of Lapaliiya. Ormpur is named the City of Saffron for good reason: Ormpur Bay is the only place in Faerûn where the crocus flower grows in abundance. From this flower, the rare spice saffron is produced in Ormpur. The city keeps a reserve stockpile of this valuable and much-sought-after spice, as well as stocks of other spices, all well guarded. Notably, the city has a long-established alliance with a clutch of chromatic dragon wyrmlings, and with it, they field wyrmling-mounted cavalry. Tiamat the Dragon Queen is the official civic deity of Ormpur.

markers.name: Orofin
markers.textHtml: Orofin, formerly known as Orolin the City of Magicians, was an oasis-city in Anauroch that dated back to the ancient realm of Asram. It was considered to be one of the greatest cities of the Lost Kingdoms of north Faerûn, before falling to ruin and languishing for centuries, and finally rebuilt by the descendants of its original inhabitants. It is now home to ethnic Bedine who refer to themselves as 'Netherese'. The city sits in a wide, grass-covered vale within a natural ridge formation. The fortress of Orofin, in the center of the city, is a grand tower surrounded by a spacious courtyard.

markers.name: Palischuk
markers.textHtml: Palischuk is a fortress town rebuilt in the mid-to-late 14th century DR by half-orcs, which grew into a bustling metropolis and the most important trade hub in Vaasa by the late 15th century DR. The largest major community in eastern Vaasa, the city lays upon the northern trade routes that connect Maur-Eturo and the south with Ironspur, Ishe, and Frohm's Rest in the far north and The Reach trail to the west. The majority of its residents are half-orc or orc. In the late 15th century DR, just 1 in 5 of the local population is human.

markers.name: Phsant
markers.textHtml: Phsant is a prosperous city of merchants and the most common place to stop on the journey between Telflamm and Mulsantir, whilst lying in the most densely populated part of Thesk. It is well known as the site of the defeat of the Tuigan Horde in the 14th century DR and for its multicultural diversity, with large populations of both Shou and orc peoples. The orc soldiers who'd settled in and around Phsant adjusted surprisingly well, learning to curb their more violent habits, and they became accepted members of the populace. Thesk was one of the places most welcoming of orcs in the Realms.

markers.name: Port Kir
markers.textHtml: Port Kir, also known as the the Seacity of Tethyr, is a port city and center of guild activities in the nation, standing along the Trade Way. Naval businesses are its main industry. Numerous fishers, whalers, and shellfish trappers prosper in Port Kir. In the summer, fishing booms, and the town's population grows drastically with seasonal workers, merchants, and visitors. During that time, Port Kir produces fresh, canned, cured, salted fish and shellfish. Another industry of note is shipbuilding.

markers.name: Port Nyanzaru
markers.textHtml: This port city is the major trading center for all of Chult. It is located at the mouths of the River Soshenstar and the River Tiryki, and built atop four hills. It was designed with defense as a priority due to the many pirates in the waters of the Shining Sea. It is protected by a high stone wall that encompasses the inner wards of the city, while the slums remain outside and relatively undefended. After becoming an Amnian colony in the late 14th century DR, Port Nyanzaru regained independence due to the influence of a consortium of Chultan traders headed by the seven Merchant Princes and backed by the Triceratops Society. Abundant wealth, as well as both mundane and exotic goods, pass through Port Nyanzaru. The city's main exports are resources from the jungle and the mountains, including jewels, gold, timber, exotic plants, and colorful textile dyes.

markers.name: Praka
markers.textHtml: Praka is one of the larger cities in the Barony of Ostel, second in size only to Heliogabalus, and known for its stout outer walls and mighty towers. Many skilled artists and craftsmen make their home in Praka. Inspiration from their skills can be seen throughout the city.

markers.name: Procampur
markers.textHtml: One of the oldest extant settlements along the Sea of Fallen Stars, Procampur was built atop an underground dwarven town called Proeskampalar. Procampur is known for its walled city districts, with slate-roofed buildings. More than its handsome architecture, though, Procampur is reputedly the single richest independent city along the northern Inner Sea. Hundreds of gem cutters and goldsmiths call the place home, and it would be a popular target for pirates if not for its incomparable fortifications and vigilant local government.

markers.name: Proskur
markers.textHtml: Since its founding, Proskur was an independent trade city with a historical associated with mercantile groups and thieves guild, and was even a haven for the Red Wizards. The city was annexed by the increasingly-powerful Cormyr in 1405 DR however. In the years that followed Proskur underwent a change to become a place of just laws and honest trade. Proskur is one of the stops along the trade route of the Thousandheads Trading Coster, and a variety of exotic and useful items and substances can be found there. The heart, eyes, and brains of catoblepas are imported from the Farsea Marshes. A small merchant company in the city dispatches trade caravans loaded up with maxoris, a preservative and spice.

markers.name: Purskul
markers.textHtml: A granary city and an important caravan stop. Its large half-orc population (15-30%) dates back to 1235 DR, when the armies of Amn captured many orcs and forced them to work at labor camps in Purskul. Those who worked the hardest were granted their freedom. Their descendants made up the later orcish population. The half-orc inhabitants generally work as caravan guards and mercenaries.

markers.name: Pyarados
markers.textHtml: Pyarados is a lawless city located in the eastern frontier of Thay. The city is divided into two zones: the 'Bright Heart', a luxurious and orderly interior for the elite, and the 'Grimshield', a muddy, dangerous slum serving as a base for miners and prospectors. It is a wild city of adventurers that serves as a base of operations for those who sought to explore the rugged Sunrise Mountains and lands further east. It has a population of 50,000.

markers.name: Raven's Bluff
markers.textHtml: Ravens Bluff, also known as the Living City, is a port metropolis in the nation of Vesperin. During the late 14th century DR, Ravens Bluff was a rapidly-growing, young city, rife with opportunities for adventure to be found, intrigue to be unraveled and riches to be earned. A beacon of civilization in an uncivilized land, it appealed to the local aristocracy just as much as it did to 'frontier folk' looking for new opportunities in life. This reputation carried on into the 15th century, as Ravens Bluff eclipsed Calaunt both in size and attraction. Ravens Bluff has a thriving, serviced-based economy. Individual laborers, craftspeople and shopkeepers hold a great number of small businesses, linked together by the city's numerous guilds. Ravens Bluff owns a large part of its success and wealth to trade outside the city. Large trading groups, the 16 Merchant Houses, manage the laborious task of managing goods in and out of the city, which long serves as the basis for the Ravenian economy.

markers.name: Ravensburg
markers.textHtml: Ravensburg is the capital and largest city of the Duchy of Carmathan. The city serves as a massive hub for the exporting of agricultural goods grown in the hugely productive fields of Carmathan. Long, squat warehouses in which the produce is stored are spread out for miles around the city center. Craftsfolk specializing in agricultural leather and metal goods comprise a bulk of the city's own trade, and each spring the city hosts a market for the famous Carmathan Horse-Ox, a massive breed of horse perfect for pulling plows.

markers.name: Reth the Half-Ruin
markers.textHtml: Reth is a high-walled trading port city. The council of merchants that governs the city keeps tariffs and taxes low, so the city attracts high numbers of trade ships. One of its most renowned structures is a grand amphitheater used as a gladiatorial arena that hosts battles twice per tenday, attracting tourism. Apart from entertainment, Reth produces fish, quarried stone, and lumber. During the Spellplague, half of Reth was destroyed and swallowed by the Bay of Silvanus's waters. Some adventurers take the plunge, battling kuo-toa in those underwater ruins in search for lost treasures.

markers.name: Riatavin
markers.textHtml: Formerly a trade city of Amn, Riatavin seceded to become part of Tethyr in 1370 DR. Riatan's Needle, a 90 foot tall butte, marks the exact center of the city. It is crowned with a magically lit brazier whose light is visible to people travelling along Tethir Road during the night.

markers.name: Rogarsheim
markers.textHtml: Capital city of the Northlander Kingdom of Norland. Most of its industry is centered around fishing, hunting, and shipbuilding. The city is a major Northlander port for trade with far off Waterdeep and Ruathym. The lack of a deep-water harbor means that large ships have to drop anchor several hundred meters from the coast and transport goods ashore via boat. Rogarsheim boasts the largest war fleet among the dozen small kingdoms of Norland, consisting of six longships each crewed by forty fighters.

markers.name: Rulovar
markers.textHtml: Rulovar was an outpost of the undersea realms in the Sea of Fallen Stars, controlled by aquatic elves. It was destroyed in 1369 DR following an assault by the megalodon known as Iakhovas the Ravager.

markers.name: Saerloon
markers.textHtml: Saerloon, a busy port city once called Chondathan, is considered to bethe least politically stable of the Sembian cities. It is home to many thieves'guilds and demonic cults. Saerloon is ruled by a merchant prince who served themerchant council. The city is notorious among merchants for its outrageousimport taxes. The churches of Mystra and Azuth prosper.

markers.name: Saelmur
markers.textHtml: This city-state sits in a natural bay in the Lake of Steam, rising upwards to the crests of four hills, which are capped with various columns, towers, and spires. Saelmur produces a variety of fruits and vegetables from the fields and orchards around the city, though as a port city it also produces a great deal of seafood. It produces a variety of different minerals, some of which are hard-to-find and can be made into spell components. The city is ruled by the Councilium of Prelates, a governing council made up of the high priests of Saelmur's seven largest faiths.

markers.name: Sambar
markers.textHtml: The capital city of Lantan.

markers.name: Sapra
markers.textHtml: Sapra stands on a small bay on the southeast of Ilighôn, directly in front of the cape passage gap in the reefs of Eldath's Ring that encircle the island. The Great Forest and Mielikki's Garden surround the city on land. Sapra is the only port on the island. The city is calm, quiet, and possibly, the cleanest settlement in all of Faerûn. It is considered to be one of the most expensive places in the Vilhon Reach. It is ruled and guarded by the druids of the Emerald Enclave, and welcomes those who want to flee oppression by wizards. Since the late 14th century DR, Sapra is also home to merchant princes from Turmish, who make the city a center of trade and mercantile activity.

markers.name: Saradush
markers.textHtml: Once a great bureaucratic center for the city's royal dynasty, Saradush has become a producer of agricultural products for trade. It is also an important waystation for trade in the area surrounding the Lake of Steam, and a haven for travelers and adventurers alike. Saradush is unusual as there are no major temples within the city. Those people who regularly worship do so in small shrines within their own homes.

markers.name: New Sarshel
markers.textHtml: New Sarshel was founded in response to the decline of trade through the city of Sarshel following the rise of Zhengyi the Witch-King in Damara in 1357 DR. However, these efforts were largely unsuccessful, and in the decades that followed, it became increasingly clear that New Sarshel seemed doomed to be overrun by banditry and piracy. But after the Spellplague of 1385 DR, while many port cities were left high-and-dry by the recession of the waters of the Sea of Fallen Stars, New Sarshel was better able than any other city to rapidly build structures that kept up with the dropping water level, and it thus became an increasingly important and prosperous trading hub. By 1479 DR, New Sarshel is continuing to grow, and Sarshel has come to be known as part of New Sarshel.

markers.name: Scornubel
markers.textHtml:
Scornubel, also known as the Caravan City, is a major tradestop for caravans, located where the Trade Way reached the River Chionthar. There is always someone looking for caravan guards or escorts. In Scornubel, thievery is rampant, and cabals and cults flourish. There are temples to Lathander and Waukeen, and a lot of solid and large warehouses. The foremost locations in the city are the Mother Minx's festhall, the Jester's Bells tavern, the Nightshade nightclub (a gambling club), and the Varambrur's Jack (a clean tavern).

As for 1490, the city's winter population is around 22,000 - threefold in summer. Nowadays the Highlord of Scornubel is Lady Rhauvva Qavarshield, a female half-orc.

markers.name: Selgaunt
markers.textHtml: Selgaunt, formerly known as Chancelgaunt and largest cityof Sembia, is a large merchant port governed by merchant lords. It is thecapital of Sembia since the destruction of Ordulin in 1374 DR.

markers.name: Sheirtalar
markers.textHtml: Sheirtalar, also known as the Shining City by the Sea, is the capital city of Lapaliiya. The gateway for trade between the Shaar and western Faerûn, it is one of the most cosmopolitan cities in the Shining South. This coastal city sits on a slope so steep towards the shore that those on approaching ships could view almost the entirety of the city. It is named the Shining City for all the gold and silver that gild the domes of Sheirtalar's most important buildings in a display of excessive opulence. Greatest of these is the Domed Palace of the Overking, which stands proudly on a mass of granite in the city's upper district. The civic deity of Sheirtalar is Waukeen.

markers.name: Sheirlantar
markers.textHtml: For centuries, it was a custom of the Lapaliiyan upper classes to bury the most important among them in the hills above Sheirlantar, the Tomb City. Thus, many tombs and crypts were scattered over the central part of the range, and undead were an ever-present threat there. The civic deity of Sheirlantar was once Myrkul, until he died in 1358 DR. The town elders eventually switched to Kelemvor, who arose as the new Lord of the Dead in 1368 DR.

markers.name: Shevel
markers.textHtml: Tradespeople established the town of Shevel near the Golden Road trade route to craft and peddle their wares. Over time, the town grew into a thriving, walled city of immigrants within the otherwise-isolated realm of Rashemen. Since the fall of Citadel Rashemar, raids from the lands to the east have been a regular occurrence, and there's always mercenary work to be found in Shevel.

markers.name: Silverymoon
markers.textHtml: Silverymoon, sometimes translated as Aerasumé in ancient Elven, is a sturdy, vibrant city, and it thrives as the pinnacle of human culture. Education and the arts, particularly musical ones, are valued highly in the Moon, but it is most famous for its pervading, magical tradition, and its being home to many notable wizards, sorcerers, and other mages. Revered as the Gem of the North, Silverymoon is also the prime signatory state of the League of the Silver Marches and, therefore, viewed by many as the capital. For over a century, it was ruled by High Lady Alustriel, a powerful wizard and one of the Seven Sisters, as well as a Chosen of Mystra.

markers.name: Skuld
markers.textHtml: Skuld is the capital city of Mulhorand. The City of Eternity, or the City of Shadows, as it is also known, was claimed to be the oldest inhabited settlement in Faerûn, and, before its destruction, was certainly the largest. The Spellplague completely destroyed Skuld, burying its streets and buildings in rubble. When the Mulhorandi gods returned to Toril in the Second Sundering, they reinstalled Skuld as their capital. From its central district, called the City of the Gods, incarnations of Mulhorand's god-kings rule the country and live in grand temples that overlook lush gardens producing delicious fruit and courtyards of gilded statues.

markers.name: Skyclave
markers.textHtml: Skyclave is located on an inlet off the Sea of Fallen Stars. The city is contained within a huge, hollow tower approximately 1,600 m in height. Protruding from the tower are hundreds of windows, spires, balconies, verandas, staircases, and doorways. The lowest balconies are over 60 m above the ground. The inside of the hollow tower is magically bigger than the outside, and is filled with galleries which contain shops, inns, theaters, libraries, and other interesting sites. The tower was erected following the Spellplague, when the descendants of the ancient Imaskari emerged to reclaim the shattered land of Mulhorand for their own. It was the capital of High Imaskar until 1487 DR, when the Imaskari were overthrown by the returned Mulhorandi gods.

markers.name: Soorenar
markers.textHtml: Soorenar was a city-state that headed the Northern Alliance, one of the three most powerful alliances in Chessenta as of the mid-to-late 14th century DR. Following the events of the Spellplague, the city was destroyed, and the flooded ruins came to be known as Drowned Soorenar during the 15th century DR. As of 1480 DR, much of the city's old wealth is said to still lie below the waters, and so the site is a popular destination for treasure hunters and adventurers.

markers.name: Spandeliyon
markers.textHtml: Spandeliyon is the capital of Altumbel, a small human kingdom where people make their living from the sea. The city is also known as the City of Pirates.

markers.name: Sultim
markers.textHtml: Sultim is located in Mulhorand within a region of high plains called the Taranoth. This city is major center of worship for the god Anhur.

markers.name: Suldanessellar
markers.textHtml: An elven city in the Forest of Tethir. It is ruled by Queen Ellesime and houses the Tree of Life. The city is a meeting place for elves and their tribes; a location all elves of the forest call home at one time or another.

markers.name: Suldolphor
markers.textHtml: Suldolphor, called the Golden City, is the easternmost city in Calimshan. Though it paid taxes to Calimshan, many inhabitants secretly view their city as an independent city-state, rival of Ankhapur.in size and wealth. This city is famed for its intricate carvings from bone, wood, and stone.

markers.name: Suzail
markers.textHtml: Suzail is the capital of Cormyr, the seat of power, and one of two Cormyrean ports on the shores of lake Dragonmere. It boasts two large temples, one of Tymora and one dedicated to Oghma.

markers.name: Taskaunt
markers.textHtml: An independent city-state.

markers.name: Tammar
markers.textHtml: This large town was razed by the Tuigan Horde and subsequently besieged by bandits who took heavy tolls from passing caravans. The government of Thesk hired a hundred orc soldiers to rid the town of the bandits and these orcs were later hailed as heroes in Tammar. The town was since rebuilt.

markers.name: Tantras
markers.textHtml: Tantras is the capital of the nation of Vesperin, which unites the city with Calaunt and Raven’s Bluff. As such, Tantras is the base for the Golden Lords, an oligarchic council of the richest and most influential and powerful merchants who rule the land. Tantras is a busy port city, humming with industry yet characterized by diligent entrepreneurs among its ruling merchant families. The people are prosperous and wealthy, from nobles to common folk. Tantras is a walled city with a well-fortified harbor. Ingenious mechanical cranes installed by the followers of Gond from the House of Skilled Hands quickly load and unload cargo to and from trade ships.

markers.name: Telflamm
markers.textHtml: Telflamm is the biggest and most important port city in Thesk. It is actually an independent city-state with subject-cities of Nyth and Culmaster under its control, but it wields so much power and influence over the country of Thesk that it might as well be the capital. Telflamm is a walled city with skyline dotted with temples and civic buildings, constructed in a distinct onion-shape. Telflamm has one of the most prominent harbors in the Reach, built atop a submerged cliff that was the seat of power in the region in the days of antiquity. Trade is the most important aspect of life in Telflamm. Almost all the collected wares traveling along the Golden Way come through its gates.
