The cultural influences of Shou Lung are obvious in the city. Visitors to the city's Shou Town can experience a slice of Kara-Turan life. The city is apparently under the rule of a 'merchant prince', though behind the scenes it is the Shadowmasters, a thieves guild, who run the city.

markers.name: Telpir
markers.textHtml: Telpir is an independent coastal city. Its walls are built in a semicircle around the part of the city what is not bordered by water. The drystone wall is a low structure with a single wooden lookout tower that often collapses during storms. Telpir is a port city and is known to trade with other cities on the Inner Sea. It also has a thriving caravan business that travels the coastal roads in both directions. Telpir maintains close relationships with the neighboring nation of Turmish, with which it shares a mutually beneficial trade pact.

markers.name: Telos City
markers.textHtml: Telos City is the largest and newest city in Vaasa in the late 15th century DR, being founded after the Spellplague inside of a blast crater that was a half-mile (750 m) across and over 800 feet (240 m) deep. Telos serves as the realm's capital and the seat of power for the evil Warlock Knights. No gods have temples in the city, and the people are not permitted to worship them. Instead, veneration of the primordial Telos is practiced, with quasi-religious services held in the Citadel of the Iron Sky. A dozen storm giants loyal to the Voice of Telos watch over the city from a cloud palace that circles above.

markers.name: Telvanlu
markers.textHtml: Telvanlu is the moderately sized capital city of the aquatic elf kingdom of Naramyr, ca. 60 m beneath the surface. It consists of clamshell-like buildings, low to the ground and dug into the silt in order to avoid issues with trade ships that frequently pass overhead. It has a reputation for being a city of artisans.

markers.name: Teth
markers.textHtml: An independent city-state.

markers.name: Teziir
markers.textHtml: Teziir is a protectorate of Cormyr, that formerly boasted itself as the largest free city on Dragon Coast. It is an independent trade city that attempts to displace Westgate as the principal trade city for goods traveling from the Sword Coast to the Inner Sea. Teziir is run like a business by the Council of Teziir. This council's membership of many of the same men and women who founded the city and they run the city consists like it is their own personal property. Teziir has a normal population of around 40,000, which swells to over 50,000 in the summer period. There is also a population of prospectors and miners who ploy their trades in the mountains outside the city. As of the late 15th century DR, Teziir boasts a large Shou population, a result of increased emigration westward along the Golden Way. There was no 'official' religion of the city, though most of the ruling council worship Helm.

markers.name: Thamor
markers.textHtml: Thamor is a trading city exchanging goods with farmers from the badlands along the southern stretch of Threskel, as well as the miners that make their living in the Riders to the Sky mountain range. While Thamor is officially independent and ruled by a human lord, leadership is controlled at least in part by Threskel's capital city of Mourktar.

markers.name: Thasselen
markers.textHtml: A peaceful port city defined by its monumental statues and sculpting industry.

markers.name: Thentia
markers.textHtml: The independent city of Thentia is considered to have an unrestrained albeit disorderly spirit, without the ever-present malice harbored by some cities of the Moonsea. Much of the city is considered to be a rough and crude place. Thentia is a free-spirited place that welcomes those that do not fit in with realms around the Sea of Fallen Stars. A relaxed atmosphere fills the city's streets and its citizens often have little cares beyond their own personal goals. Thentia's head of state is its Watchlord, but the fate of Thentia's citizens is truly in the hands of the Thentia's noble families, each of which is offered support by the city's numerous independent mages.

markers.name: Torl
markers.textHtml: Torl is an independent port city. Its main attraction was originally the gold mines in the mountains around the city, but they had been exhausted for the most part by the late 14th century DR.

markers.name: Trail's End
markers.textHtml: Trail's End, also known as Trailsend, is the capital of the Barony of Polten. It is one of the oldest settlements in Damara after Ravensburg and Heliogabalus. Its grand, magnificent and heavily fortified castle is one of the earliest buildings in Damara, having been built in the 11th century. The city is a large center for travel of the surrounding region, and appeals to adventuring parties looking for work or seeking to explore the ruins in Rawlinswood or the Giantspire Mountains. A local population of dwarven craftsfolk contributes to the city's own exports, with their production of fine armor being of particular note. Although part of Damara, Trail’s End has close ties to Impiltur, which makes it an important player in Damaran trade and politics.

markers.name: Tsurlagol
markers.textHtml: Tsurlagol, formerly known as Chessagol, is a prosperous, walled port city and the southernmost city-state of the Vast, lying at the end of Stormcrest Trail from Sevenecho. It is the smallest of the five so-called 'sister cities' of the Vast, together with Calaunt, Procampur, Ravens Bluff, and Tantras. Tsurlagol has a long-term alliance with neighboring Procampur, with a mutual defense pact. To merchants, it is 'The Gateway to the Unapproachable East'; to pirates it is 'Pirate's Haven'. It has the unenviable record of being raided, destroyed, and rebuilt more often than any other city in the surrounding lands.

markers.name: Tulmon
markers.textHtml: [not Tulon] This city-state stands on a long and low-lying rocky plateau known as the Shoulder of Lehreng, dominating the shallow and muddy Bay of Balefires. The buildings of Tulmon are almost entirely constructed of stone, with tile roofs, along cobbled streets. Accommodations tend to be rather spartan, but handsomely designed and outfitted, showcasing the skills of the local craftsmen. The city's economy was first largely based around agriculture, then shifted to exporting sheepskin, dyed wool, and metalwork. One of its first significant rulers, the cruel archmage Calavarr, was eventually overthrown by Anganather Tchallem, who began the tradition of Tulmon's rulers being referred to as Sultans.

markers.name: Tyraturos
markers.textHtml: Tyraturos is a massive metropolis and trade hub located in the tharch of Pyarados. Its importance is primarily due to its geographic location at the crossroads of the Eastern Way and the Long Road, making it the primary gateway for overland trade entering or leaving the Thayan plateau. It is a dense, high-pressure point where the lifeblood of Thayan trade—slaves and gold—is squeezed through a single, chaotic location before being pumped out to the rest of the world. Tyraturos is the nation's third-largest city.

markers.name: Underhome
markers.textHtml: Underhome, also known as the Great City of the Dwarves, was the capital city of the Deep Realm of the gold dwarves located within the Great Rift, from the Age of the Proud People until the year 1385 DR. It was destroyed when the Spellplague struck. It was a heavily defended fortress, home to the great treasures and wealthiest clans of the gold dwarves of Faerûn. This great dwarfhold consists of three massive caverns, whose colossal, 21.3 m tall, solid-gold gates are nestled into the eastern face of the Great Rift, overlooking the Riftlake. The cavernous tunnels are lined with spired buildings similar to those that can be found on Toril's surface lands. The gold dwarves sealed off the ruins and fiercely protect their former noble city. As of the late 15th century, the ruins remain largely unexplored.

markers.name: Unthalass
markers.textHtml: Unthalass was the capital of Unther and considered once the greatest city in all the Realms. It was destroyed during the Spellplague of 1385 DR. In 1487 DR, after the Second Sundering brought the Untherans from Abeir to Toril, the second incarnation of Gilgeam reclaimed the city and began to develop a project to rebuild it as the capital of the reborn Unther. As of the early 16th century DR, everything in the city has been sacrificed in the name of the God-king's hunger for conquest. The mud-brick streets overflow with filth and even blood, for Gilgeam brooks no dissent within his capital. The tyrant rules all of Unther from an imposing granite fortress in Unthalass called the Citadel of Black Ash.

markers.name: Urmlaspyr
markers.textHtml: Urmlaspyr, formerly known as the city of temples, suffered greatly under the Netherese because of the resistance of his people, and was nearly razed, but was supported by Cormyr who loaned the city a company of Purple Dragon knights to aid in its defense and a significant amount of trade flowed through its port from Cormyr. The city recovered its independence in 1479 DR. It is known for having some of the best glassblowers in Faerûn.

markers.name: Valls
markers.textHtml: Valls is a walled city and the capital of the Duchy of Arcata. It is situated in the dusty plains of central Arcata along a tributary to the Beaumaris River at the western end of the King's Road. Given its location, Valls is a bustling center for trade out of western Damara. Mercenary groups for hire are easy to find in the city.

markers.name: Velen
markers.textHtml: Velen was a city of Tethyr. However, in 1424 DR, they declared their independence from this nation and became an autonomous duchy. While this declaration first created tension with Tethyr, over time relations were re-established and now they consider one another as allies.

markers.name: Velprintalar
markers.textHtml: Velprintalar is the only major port in Aglarond, with a large naval yard and one of the most beautiful cities in Faerûn. It's the capital. The Simbarch Council, made up of sorcerers and other spellcasters, rules Aglarond from a magnificent palace of pale-green Mulhorandi stone. Sorcerers from across Faerûn come here for training.

markers.name: Volothamp
markers.textHtml: Volothamp is a city in Calimshan built upon the side of a cliff overlooking the Shining Sea. It is both a port city and major mining center, famed for its gemcutters, jewelers, and goldsmiths, earning it the moniker of 'City of Gems'. Beyond this, Volothamp is known across the wider reaches of Faerûn for its landmark monumental statues the Eight Erehnirs, and its resident fellowships of mages.

markers.name: Waterdeep
markers.textHtml:
Also known as the City of Splendors, the bustling city of Waterdeep is ruled by a lordly council. The current Open Lord, Laeral Silverhand, keeps her identity known to the public. The Masked Lords rule alongside her anonymously, thus preventing any of them from being bribed or threatened. Waterdeep boasts a prodigious magical academy at Blackstaff Tower, which is run by a wizard holding the office of the Blackstaff—currently Vajra Safahr.

MAP OF WATERDEEP

markers.name: Westgate
markers.textHtml: Westgate is a seedy and squalid metropolis with a history in piracy, powerful crime syndicates, and nameless vampiric crime-lords. It thrives on black-market trade and corruption, and is perhaps one of the most sordid cities in all of Faerûn. The legacy of scoundrels and spies is found in every alley, and danger lurks for the unprepared in every corner. Despite this, it is the largest and most powerful city on the Dragon Coast, and probably the oldest, richest and most corrupt port on the Sea of Fallen Stars. It received the nickname 'Gateway to the West' from its role as the Inner Sea's most prolific port city.

markers.name: Wheloon
markers.textHtml: The town of Wheloon, sometimes nicknamed the Jade Towers, was a busy and prosperous town, ever growing through the 1360s DR, with wealth and entrepreneurs, known as 'hothands'. But in 1473 DR, King Foril of Cormyr decreed that Wheloon be transformed into a prison city, sentencing all those inside to be prisoners for life, sealed in by physical and magical barriers. The decision was made following unprecedented activity by worshipers of Shar in the city, as well as evidence of their alliance with Netheril. By 1479 DR, Wheloon was a dark prison city, no longer a trade hub, its population had fallen to 3,00, and the streets were controlled by gangs of thugs. Even so, a small group of nobles, courtiers, and priests of Chauntea and Lliira began petitioning the Crown to open the city. They insisted the Purple Dragons couldn't prove all the imprisoned were faithful of Shar and believed that the children born since the city was sealed ought to be given the chance at a righteous life.

markers.name: Xorhun
markers.textHtml: Xorhun, also known as Corellon's Cradle and Lifeblood Falls, is the only open elven city in the Vilhon Reach. Predominantly elven, the city is home to a large community of gnomes, and a very small number of humans and half-elves. In the 15th century DR, a kobold tribe from the Orsraun Mountains found a new home in Xorhun's ghetto that became known as the Koboldtown. Commercially, Xorhun serves primarily as a stopping point along the Halondar road. It is populated primarily by craftsmen and merchants.

markers.name: Yartar
markers.textHtml: Yartar is a prosperous and increasingly crowded fortified city, alive and full of trade, day and night. It is a member city of the Lords' Alliance, and holds a number of allies throughout the North. As a crossroads trading town, it is always bustling with caravans entering and leaving its walls. It is renown as a place for merchants, smugglers, mercenaries, and spies from all over the North to meet and conduct business. Due to its unique location, on a major road near the crossing of two rivers, Yartar also enjoys a thriving fishing community.

markers.name: Yesehlmaar
markers.textHtml: Yeshelmaar is the center of civilization in the Great Dale, inhabited by druids, rangers and Wood Elves. Yeshelmaar was carved into a rocky tor, with a pristine lake of cold water at the foot of the hill. It is difficult to distinguish between the town's stonework and the surrounding hillside's volcanic stone. Plantings of trees and green ivy are featured throughout, while elevated walkways and stairways connect the town's spires.

markers.name: Yhaunn
markers.textHtml: Yhaunn, the oldest city in Sembia, was nearly annihilated in 1385 DR by the Spellplague when the cliffs towering over the city broke away from the land and were transformed into floating earthmotes. The Netherese rebuilt the city and by 1479 DR it had fully recovered and had even more residents than before the disaster. The city is run by the Fives Council, made up of the first 75 merchants. Yhaunn is a major port exporting manufactured goods such as textiles, wickerwork, bricks, and glassware. The Harpers are relatively powerful in Yhaunn.

markers.name: Yeshpek
markers.textHtml: Yeshpek is a large fortified city known for its exportation of finely wrought chains, of different sizes and strengths, as well as locks. It is also known as a center of trade of fishing and for trading of crabs and clams.

markers.name: Zazesspur
markers.textHtml: Zazesspur, cut in two by the Sulduskoon River, is the former capital of Tethyr. The city was first settled by Calishite humans as a fishing village and, in −1570 DR, it became a fortified city and the seat of the Calishite Emir of Tethyr. Zazesspur is ruled by a council of lords and an odd mixture of Tethyrian and Calishite influences.

markers.name: Zhentil Keep
markers.textHtml: Zhentil Keep, also called Thargate Keep, was once the most powerful city in the Moonsea region as well as the main base of operation for the Zhentarim. Along with the Citadel of the Raven, Zhentil Keep was destroyed by the Shadovar in 1383 DR during the Shadowbane War. Eventually the Zhents returned and rebuilt it. Zhentil Keep is again a flourishing trade city, but Zhentarim leaders have been slow to return there. Zhentarim soldiers are sent to Zhentil Keep for training, and the governing council is conspiring to regain influence in the Moonsea from rivals Hillsfar and Mulmaster. The church of Bane is considered, for all intents and purposes, the state faith of Zhentil Keep.

markers.name: Addas Babar
markers.textHtml: Addas Babar is a sprawling settlement that is home to many of the D'tarig people, a human population inhabiting the Anauroch desert. Located in a hilly range, the town comprises mud and wood structures, mostly built for living accommodations, scattered across a handful of peaks and troughs. Due to designation as the premiere trading post of the D'tarig, Addas Babar holds a spacious marketplace, where outlanders can resupply and hire local experts and guides.

markers.name: Alamontyr
markers.textHtml: A large fishing and agricultural town, and the easternmost notable settlement within the Border Kingdoms. Because of the relative safety and security of life in Alamontyr, it has become home to many different craftspeople, such as finesmiths, woodcarvers, potters, glaziers, jewelers, and tailors, who export their wares widely.

markers.name: Amnwater
markers.textHtml: Amnwater is a large walled town sitting at the meeting of the Lake Way, South Fork, Esmel Road, and Eshpurta Road, making it one of the most important crossroads in Amn and a vital caravan stop on the long trade route that runs between the east and west of the country. The town's economy is strongly devoted to supporting caravans and merchants.

markers.name: Amphail
markers.textHtml: Amphail was named after one of Waterdeep's early warlords, Amphail the Just, who had estates here. Amphail's main trade is growing food for Waterdeep, but it is also known for its outstanding horses—including Amphail grays, glossy black chargers, and Amphail fancys. Due to this, the village suffers from the stench of horse manure during the warmer months. The wealthier inhabitants or landowners of Amphail are generally the ones who breed horses.

markers.name: Amry
markers.textHtml: Amry is a coastal settlement governed by a local council, and regularly imperiled by the bands of pirates that operate in the Sea of Fallen Stars.

markers.name: Anchoril
markers.textHtml: A town on the island of Suj, located on its eastern shores. Anchoril is connected to the Lantanna Embassy in Waterdeep via a keyed portal.

markers.name: Archenbridge
markers.textHtml:
Archendale, Dalelands.

Archenbridge is a heavily fortified town. Many citizens of Archenbridge are merchant-adventurers eager for battle and gold. Due to the wealth its citizens bring in, the town is attracting a diverse array of shops, taverns, and crafters. The town is dedicated to Lathander but also boasts a famous temple to Chauntea. Archenbridge has spilled outside its walls; residents unable to afford property in the central ward built wooden homes and shops outside.

markers.name: Arthyn
markers.textHtml: A large port town and an independent city-state. The town has rich deposits of copper and iron in the surrounding hills, and as a result Arthyn has a thriving smelting industry. Fine daggers from the city, called 'Arthyn fangs', are sought after across the Border Kingdoms. The town also has a reputation for healing. The temples of Lathander and Chauntea in Arthyn have great power over the town, to the point of choosing the city's governor, the Arthlord.

markers.name: Ashabenford
markers.textHtml: The capital of Mistledale, Ashabenford includes a mill, stables, brewery, and workshops. Most of the village lies on the east side of the river, but the White Hart Inn on the west bank caters to adventurers. Other landmarks include a temple to Tyr, and a shrine to Chauntea. A barrack on the north end of town houses the Riders, Mistledale's only professional military force.

markers.name: Assam
markers.textHtml: Assam is a wallless town on the endless wide plains, drowning in circling packs of herd animals and numerous caravans. The city smells of tanneries and slaughterhouses. All businesses in the city cater to caravans, from the largest store to the dingiest tavern. The city-states of Lheshayl and Ormath rule Assam together. Merchants are allowed to run free by the local government, so much that visitors often assume that trading companies are the rulers of Assam. However, the city is far from lawless. Assam has a strong militia that is capable of repealing invaders and keeping the streets peaceful.

markers.name: Azun
markers.textHtml: A small town serving as a waystop for caravans traveling between the Council Hills to Durpar and the Shining Lands. Plenty of merchants from Durpar also pass through the town on their way toward the lands along the coast of the Sea of Fallen Stars. Many of Azun's residents earn a living by fishing the Lake of Salt and mining the salt flats nearby.

markers.name: Bargewright Inn
markers.textHtml: Bargewright Inn is a hilltop settlement on the River Dessarin that grew up around the inn of the same name. It is not a true village, but rather an assemblage of businesses that have based themselves at a vital crossing to service travelers and later farmers and ranchers who had settled along the river's west bank. From afar, it appears to be a rundown hilltop castle with two towers. Two concentric walls circle the hill and contain the buildings scattered around the slopes. Bargewright Inn provides a variety of services to travelers; in the 1490s DR, these include the inn, tavern, a shop selling dry goods, a pharmacist to treat pack animals, a combined temple and festhall available for hire, multiple blacksmiths, coopers, wagonwrights, and wheelwrights, as well as traders in oxen, mules, and horses, stables, and warehouses.

markers.name: Beldargan
markers.textHtml: An independent city-state completely surrounded by the Barony of Blacksaddle, and protected by a thick, tall ring of thorny hedge maze, with three heavily fortified gates. The town is built on the slopes of Beldargan's Mount, a hill that rises 300 ft out of the surrounding grasslands. Geothermal springs are found on Beldargan's Mount, and a network of caverns is found underneath. Beldargan maintains many orchids of green apples, which provide the town with several export items, including a potent cider and an apple sauce.

markers.name: Beregost
markers.textHtml: Beregost is a small town of about forty main buildings. Its grandest structure is the Song of the Morning, a large fortified temple complex to Lathander, laying to the east of the town's center. Further away stands a ruined shell of an old castle atop a hill of what used to be the Ulcaster School of Wizardry, destroyed in the 12th century DR. Beregost is considered a trade settlement, and it receives a healthy dose of caravaners and adventurers who helm caravans to the south – into Amn, Tethyr, and Calimshan. While officially Beregost is ruled by a town council, in reality, the town is run by the high priests of the Song of the Morning.

markers.name: Black Skull Cove
markers.textHtml: Black Skull Cove is the only settlement on the island of Nemessor. The high surrounding hills help to protect the settlement and docks from the strong winds and storms. It is home to the 600–900 individuals making up the Black Skull clan. Sailors, refugees, and escaped slaves from Tethyr and Calimshan founded this city. Due to the dangers in navigating to the island, the sailors of the Black Skull Clan are some of the best in the region. As many as a quarter of the clan's members are pirates by trade.

markers.name: Blackalblade
markers.textHtml: A small city and the capital of Ondeeme, Blackalblade is one of the weathiest cities in the Border Kingdoms and is famed for its gem-cutting and jewellery-making industry. Its location near the Raundawn Hill gives it access to a plentiful supply of raw gems. Blackalblade is also home to a unique type of festhall called a drauda. They range from regular taverns with some gambling and gossip to dark, violent establishments filled with illicit business.

markers.name: Blackbarn
markers.textHtml: A small, prosperous market town, home to many merchants that travel the Border Kingdoms in caravans or river barges towards ports along the Shining Sea for export to Calimshan and beyond. Blackbarn is a peaceful and safe place to live thanks to the presence of the Ghost Lances, also know as the 'Black Knights'. They are the ghosts of knights exiled from Tethyr centuries ago, who reside in a crypt under Blackbarn. The Lances hunt thieves and brigands, leaving the law-abiding locals in peace.

markers.name: Bloutar
markers.textHtml: A small town with a dangerous reputation, Bloutar is a disorganized maze of unpaved streets and log houses. Its economy is primarily logging and hunting. Furs and wild game meat are especially sought after, and are sold as far as Derlusk and Tharsult.

markers.name: Bryn Shander
markers.textHtml: The biggest of the Ten Towns, a trading center in Icewind Dale, the gateway to its fellow towns of the dale, as well as their lifeline to the rest of civilization to the south. Folks from all around the surrounding settlements gather in 'the Tenth Town' to sell their goods, primarily consisting of scrimshaw made from the bones of knucklehead trout. Merchants from the lands south of the Wall bring in goods that cannot be found in the Frozenfar, such as spices, fruits, wines and hardwood lumber, from as far away as the Heartlands. The circular walled town itself is placed upon a hill, with snowy plains stretching in all directions. Because of this defensive location, residents of cities east of Bryn Shander often take refuge within the city walls during wartime.

markers.name: Caer Moray
markers.textHtml: This coastal port town was the largest settlement on the island of Moray and the royal seat of the island's Ffolk kingdom of Moray until it was sacked by lycanthropes of the Black Blood tribe in the late 14th century DR. The castle was torn down and it was abandoned by the Ffolk for decades afterward. As of 1479 DR, after the Black Blood tribe was defeated, the town has come under the occupation of Ffolk citizens and soldiers loyal to House Kendrick, and is little more than a military outpost.

markers.name: Carradoon
markers.textHtml: Carradoon is a shipwrights' town on the southern shore of Impresk Lake. The city is walled with many multi-story structures huddled inside the town walls. A long bridge connects the city of Carradoon to a nearby island section of the town reserved for the more wealthy inhabitants such as merchants and governing officials.

markers.name: Chandlerscross
markers.textHtml:
Scardale, Dalelands.

Chandlerscross is inhabited almost entirely by farmers, ranchers, and artisans. The ruler of the town is the wood elf Abyssa Whitelock. Chandlerscross temporarily became the capital of the dale, but locals are happy the town has since returned to a quiet life of obscurity, unbothered by Sembians, the Zhentarim, and local crime guilds.

markers.name: Channathgate
markers.textHtml: The community of Channathgate reaps the benefits of caravan trade through the Channath Vale. In addition, farmers on the outskirts of the settlement also trade with the ghostwise halflings of the Channathwood. The halflings do not permit them to travel into their forest except to forage for herbs and berries on the fringes.

markers.name: Corm Orp
markers.textHtml: A small town on the Dusk Road, dominated by halflings. The halflings of Corm Orp grow a variety of grapes to produce sour wines and grape-based dyes for export. They keep goat and sheep in the hills overlooking the settlement, whose hair and wool respectively are used to make blankets for export. Beyond farm goods, the people of Corm Orp produce pottery from nearby, rich deposits of clay.

markers.name: Corsuor
markers.textHtml: Corsuor is a small town in the aquatic elf kingdom of Naramyr, ca. 40 m beneath the surface.

markers.name: Coryllvol
markers.textHtml: The people of Coryllvol fish the river and trade their catch elsewhere, but find far more money in bottling and exporting their pure spring water. The Shimmalvol Waters Company maintains exclusive deals with buyers in Esmeltaran and Athkatla, ensuring the highest prices possible.

markers.name: Dagger Falls
markers.textHtml: Dagger Falls, the capital of Daggerdale, is a former trading outpost. Sturdy stone walls and two gatehouses protect the town. The buildings within, also stone and covered with slate roofs, show the influence of dwarven engineering techniques. A temple to Lathander, Lathanders Light, is the largest religious building in the dale; it's managed by Her Radiance Dareen Travaskyr, an high elf priest. Daggerdale's greatest and most imposing stronghold is the Constable's Tower.

markers.name: Daggerford
markers.textHtml: While it is a small town, primarily a farming community, and considered by some to be a mere stopover, Daggerford has great ambitions to grow and be seen as an alternative to their northern neighbor of Waterdeep. Due to its key location, Daggerford is a vital mercantile hub for its region of the Sword Coast. It is the site where goods moving on ships traversing the Delimbiyr River can be transferred to caravans journeying along the Trade Way or vice versa. It is a walled settlement with a population that by and large lives in the outlying hamlets, farms, and estates, rather than within the town proper. Surrounding the town wall is a modest moat, and sitting atop a hill in the center of the town is the grand Ducal Castle, that has served the dukes of Daggerford since the age of their founder, Tyndal.

markers.name: Dalath
markers.textHtml: Dalath is a rough-and-tumble mining town located near mountains rich in veins of gold, iron, copper, and lead. These mines make Dalath one of the most important settlements of Unther, as those metals are Unther's main exports. Most of Dalath's inhabitants are either the freeholder merchants who manage the mines, or their slaves.

markers.name: Dapplegate
markers.textHtml: This large independent town is home to a wide variety of crafters. Its location makes it a center for import/export businesses, importing trade goods from Calimshan and goods from across the Border Kingdoms, and exporting from the town's large port district. The north bank of the River Scelptar, and the marshes between Dapplegate and the Shining Sea, are populated by Lizardfolk and Mudmaws. Therefore, there is a large demand for hired guards for merchant barges and caravans, and more than six mercenary companies bases are located in the town.

markers.name: Delgora
markers.textHtml: A small coastal town housing Mulhorand's prime fishing fleet.

markers.name: Denzar
markers.textHtml: Denzar is a frontier mining town in Gauros tharch of Thay, serving as a supply and processing center for the many Thayan mines scattered in the Sunrise Mountains. It is a grim settlement shrouded by a constant flow of sulfurous smoke from its wood-fueled smelters. Most structures in the town are rubblestone-walled buildings. The town's inhabitants are hard-working, and harder-drinking outcasts, misfits, and half-bloods hired to work in the smelters. These smelters process copper, with some lead, silver, and occasional gold.

markers.name: Dhedluk
markers.textHtml: Dhedluk is a beautiful village of about 100 rustic cottages interspersed with gardens, dappled by sunlight reaching down through the eaves of gigantic mossy trees. It is a popular place for nobles and Purple Dragons to retire to. The expansion of cleared land is severely curtailed and regulated, so little logging takes place. The local business is mostly made up of skilled woodcarvers and herbalists who take advantage of the woodlands and bogs around the village.

markers.name: Dlusk
markers.textHtml: A fishing town of narrow streets, busy markets, and weathered wooden buildings in the typical Aglarondan style. Dlusk is built on a major trade way that crosses the entire Aglarondan peninsula's northern side, from the city of Undumor to Ingdal's Arm and beyond. Its harbor is filled with over a hundred vessels. It holds an annual spring festival that includes feasts, parades, performances, and contests, which brings visitors from across Aglarond and neighboring places like Thesk and Chessenta.

markers.name: Drawn Swords
markers.textHtml: Drawn Swords is an isolated village atop a single rocky hill that overlooks the surrounding landscape. Rows of cottages built in rings along the sides of the hill ascend upward towards a small copse of trees that surround the tower of Swordskeep. The village is named for the vigilance and mind for defense that is demanded of all of its residents, thanks to the assaults it suffers on regular basis, particularly by the local orcs.

markers.name: Dunfee
markers.textHtml: Dunfee is a small but vital mining town found in Impiltur near the border of Damara.

markers.name: Dungar
markers.textHtml: A small town standing among rolling hills on the trade route between the coastal cities of Lapaliiya and the Talath Pass to Halruaa. It is a farming town focused on growing olives and producing wine, with many olive groves and vineyards surrounding the community. The civic deity of Dungar is Chauntea.

markers.name: Easthaven
markers.textHtml: Once a small shanty town, now a prosperous trading community, Easthaven is one of the larger settlements that make up the collective Ten Towns of Icewind Dale. It is situated on the shore of Lac Dinneshere, a short way away from the Redwaters lake. Easthaven's docks support one of the largest fleets of fishing ships in all of Icewind Dale, with fishing for knucklehead trout being easily the most lucrative and popular of the fishing industries. Easthaven is considered among the most comfortable of the Ten Towns, with good amenities that are uncommon among the settlements of Icewind Dale. Its people are seen as fairly friendly in comparison to their neighbors.

markers.name: Easting
markers.textHtml: Thanks to its small size and nearness to the mountains, Easting became a meeting place for dwarf craftsmen and merchants from hidden delves in the Far Hills and Sunset Mountains. They sell their high-quality smith-work here, as well as the giant mushrooms they fry as a delicacy. Visiting dwarf smiths and miners getting a meal or drink in town are a common sight. The dwarves of Easting are allied with the Bruenghor Clan of the Sunset Mountains.

markers.name: Emrys
markers.textHtml: This large, fortified town occupies a large hill near the southern banks of Emrysar Lake. It is commonly called the 'Throat of Talduth Vale' due to its location on the Sheep Road, which connects the Border Kingdoms to the Shaar. Farmers frequently use this road to bring their flock to the edge of the Shaar for grazing, and a large amount of farmers sell sheep and wool at the local Bawling Market. Emrys was founded around the ancient Emrystarl's Keep, one of the oldest structures in the Border Kingdoms. Emrystarl is rumored to be a powerful warrior who was undefeated in battle.

markers.name: Eveningstar
markers.textHtml: Eveningstar is a small, unwalled farming town in the Heartlands of Cormyr, the home of the famous temple to Lathander, the House of the Morning. This crossroads community is home to craftsmen and farmers. The town proper consists of about fifty buildings. It is a beautiful place, full of trees and gardens. Eveningstar has a rather large population of semi-feral flying cats, known as tressym, who spend most of their time hunting in the fields around the town.

markers.name: Felshroun
markers.textHtml: Felshroun is an independent town protected by a diamond-shaped wall. At each corner of the wall is a tower, which is home to one of the Four Mages, the true power in Felshroun. The town is crowded and labyrinthine, with narrow streets and alleys winding around buildings up to five stories high. Felshroun is famous for its horses. Merchants from as far away as Tashalar and the Vilhon Reach travel to the town yearly to purchase Felshrounan horses. The town hosts a thriving local economy as well.

markers.name: Fort Arran
markers.textHtml: A small town turned outpost, Fort Arran stands at the mouth of River Arran, by the bridge across the river that was part of the Golden Road. The fort's garrison counts a thousand loyal Sespechan soldiers who are ready to fall in defense of their free nation.

markers.name: Freedale
markers.textHtml: Freedale was founded two centuries ago on the banks of the River Ashaba. The goal of the town's founders was to create a new, independent dale. Like so many other would-be dales, however, Freedale briefly flourished, struggled, and eventually collapsed. Now, abandoned buildings fill the center of town, and the few families who remain live inlog: cabins on the outskirts. The Evening Star Inn, an establishment that mostly caters to orc fishers, continues to be operated by the Starlight family.

markers.name: Fugue
markers.textHtml: Fugue is a tent city near the Vaasan Gate. When the Warlock Knights seized Bloodstone Pass and sealed the Vaasan Gate in 1469 DR, Vaasan refugees fleeing from the Knights' war of conquest gathered nearby in hopes of being saved by Damara. By 1480 DR, no help had come and the population had swelled to approximately 2,000 desperate souls living in squalor. It was a sprawling ramshackle camp of desperate, poverty-stricken refugees. The conditions in the tent city are crowded and filthy, and death is a common occurrence. Illicit goods are a big business in the camps.

markers.name: Gaj
markers.textHtml: A market town mostly inhabited by hard-working dwarves and gnomes, housing numerous crafters. Being a major trade settlement, Gaj's industry is based around its shallow copper and silver mines with an occasional gold vein. Gaj produces copper wire grips for tolls and weapons, wire jewelry mounts and handles, teapot handles, and other such items, exported to coastal Chondath and Chessenta.

markers.name: Gallard
markers.textHtml: Gallard is a crowded place of winding cobbled streets and gravel alleys. Its timbered houses are narrow, steep-roofed, and crowded. It is home to many skilled craftworkers including locksmiths, bootmakers, leatherworkers, and smallsmiths. One of the busiest and most prosperous places in all the 'Borders', but also one of the most grasping and grubby, Gallard is full of close-guarded wealth.

markers.name: Gambiton
markers.textHtml: This small village was destroyed in 1370 DR in the opening of the Sothillisian War, when an army of hill giants and goblins poured from the Small Teeth, quickly overran the nearby Hillfort Ishla, and then razed Gambiton on the way to Esmeltaran. The surviving population fled to the west and north, and the village remained in ruin.

markers.name: Glister
markers.textHtml: Glister was the only human settlement in Thar. It was an important stopover for prospectors seeking wealth in the Galena Mountains and, due to its remote location in an otherwise desolate land, food and timber were its main imports, which were often traded for raw ores including iron, silver and copper. In the late 1470s DR, the town was destroyed by orcs of the Blood Skull tribe.

markers.name: Goldspires
markers.textHtml: Goldspires is a massive fortified castle-abbey with high gold-plated gates, overlooking the Merchant's Bay from a seacoast promontory northwest of Athkatla. It is the main center of Waukeen worship in Faerûn and, with over 15 buildings within its walls, it is almost a small city in its own right.

markers.name: Greenest
markers.textHtml: Greenest is a thriving town and the only sizable settlement along the Uldoon Trail in the Greenfields area. It features a water mill, a temple to Chauntea, and a fortified keep which is the town's main defense.

markers.name: Hammer and Anvil
markers.textHtml: This surface village is the main point of trade between the Deep Realm, a kingdom of gold dwarves found in the caverns of the Great Rift, and the surface.

markers.name: Hardbuckler
markers.textHtml: Hardbuckler is a fortified village, later a small town, populated almost exclusively by rock gnomes, and a favored travel stop along the Dusk Road. Most buildings are small stone cottages with slated roofs, built in a seemingly chaotic manner. Each cottage has its own humble fenced garden and a sizable cellar, and its streets are roamed by livestock. The major industry is secure, private storage for any type of goods. Additionally, the gnomes export elaborate locks, sturdy wooden crates, a distinctive green seam-sealing wax, and fungi, grown in every household and sold as fried lichen.

markers.name: Harrowdale Town
markers.textHtml:
When Scardale Town collapsed into chaos and lawlessness decades ago, Harrowdale Town briefly supplanted it as the chief port in the Dalelands. However, the restored order to Scardale Town allowed it to reclaim its status as the foremost port in the Dalelands.

Although business in Harrowdale is now diminished, loggers still bring their lumber to Harrowdale Town, selling to merchants who travel throughout Dragon's Reach. The town includes temples to Oghma and Tymora.

markers.name: Helm's Hold
markers.textHtml: Helm's Hold is a fortified city and cathedral dedicated to Helm. A nearby stretch of Neverwinter Wood known as the Watcher's Forest is named for the fact it is tended by some residents of Helm's Hold. In the years following the cataclysm of the Spellplague, Helm's Hold became renowned as a place of safety and healing that catered to all those in need. As of the late 15th century DR, the town is controlled by the Order of the Gilded Eye, an over-zealous military organization dedicated to the eradication of extra-planar evil throughout the Realms.

markers.name: Heptios
markers.textHtml: Heptios is an independent city-state with a history of learning and commerce. By 1489 DR, it contains the largest library in Chessenta.

markers.name: Highmoon
markers.textHtml: The capital of Deepingdale, Highmoon, is a walled town. Thousands of elves and humans call the town home, demonstrating Deepingdale's commitment to harmony. The Leaves of Learning is a temple to Oghma that houses one of the most famous libraries in Faerûn while Rhauntides's Tower, a small hexagonal tower willed to a warrior monk, is now a monastery where monks practice their fighting arts.

markers.name: Hill's Edge
markers.textHtml: A small city on the Dusk Road, overlooked by the Mayor's Tower, a small fortress for the mayor and bodyguards, and comprising temples to Lliira and to Cyric.

markers.name: Hilp
markers.textHtml: The small town of Hilp marks the western terminus of the Way of the Manticore, which runs east to the town of Wheloon and beyond. Hilp is a market town for the local farmers and has thriving trades in the making of barrels and wagons. A shrub known as stelk is grown commercially in Hilp as fish bait. A stelk shrub resembles a cluster of brown, fist-sized cabbage heads. Aquatic creatures, from goldfish to dragon turtles, are unable to resist it.

markers.name: Hlintar
markers.textHtml: Hlintar is a small crossroads town, known for its locally bred and well-trained horses. There are also several big pig farms, and their top-quality bacon makes the town famous. Merchants of Hlintar trade with all the surrounding settlements, from Calaunt to Ravens Bluff to as far as Dragon Falls and Kurth. They do good business trading magic items with the hedge wizards from the nearby village of Dark Hollow. The town is very wealthy, giving rise to a 'silent vault' temple service analogous to a banking system, and to the particular habit of old Hlintar to conceal one's wealth in the town graveyard. Dwarves of the Vast boycott trade with and travel to Hlintar since the mass murder committed on the local dwarven community by Master Merchant Beindold in the mid-13th century DR.

markers.name: Hluthvar
markers.textHtml: A walled town on the Dusk Road, with a temple to Helm. The town is said to lie in the shadow of the Darkhold and is long seen as more of a fortress against the Zhentarim than an actual trading town.

markers.name: Ieirithymbul
markers.textHtml: Ieirithymbul is a tiny gnome mining village laying in a narrow valley called Felrenden, high in the cradle between the three peaks southeast of Leilon. The three mountains are locally named Mount Sternhelm, Mount Ardabad, and Mount Pheldaer. There are rich copper and iron lodes in the area and a significant deposit of coal. The gnomes engage in prospecting and mining, herding sheep, and gathering edible flowers. Ieirithymbul is little-known to the outside world. Even in Leilon, folks know only that gnomes live in the mountains and came to trade sometimes. The settlement is guarded by the Unseen Protector, a being that folks usually think to be a local guardian spirit.

markers.name: Illul
markers.textHtml: Illul is the center of the faith of Gond in the Realms. The techsmiths of the church of Gond are based and trained in Illul at the High Holy Crafthouse, a large walled monastery.

markers.name: Ilmwatch
markers.textHtml: Ilmwatch is a large fortress-town that protects the bay of Buzzard Beak Harbor to its south as well as inland travel in the region. Soldiers from the Warswords of Impiltur are tasked with protecting merchants traveling to and from Damara as well as those who cross the Easting Reach to Uthmere. Patrols regularly scout the surrounding lands and often accompany merchants traveling along the Merchant's Run and Herald's Road, as they pass close to the fortress.

markers.name: Immersea
markers.textHtml: Immersea is a growing and prosperous town in the Immer Stream Valley, and the ancestral home of the Wyvernspur noble family. It stands on the Calantar's Way and is most commonly used as a stop-over for travelers who venture to and from Arabel and Hilp. The city is known for its storms, mists are very common, and much of the land is covered in flowering vines. Immersea farms raise cattle, sheep, and grow barley as well as corn. Apart from farming, Immersea produces fish, with the local group of fishers, known as the 'Mist Fishers', being a popular attraction. The 'Mist Fishers' embark on their fishing journey in the early mist-filled mornings across the Wyvernwater.

markers.name: Imnescar
markers.textHtml: The small town of Imnescar is considered an agricultural center for the nation's south and is famous for its fruits. It is also a popular waystation for those traveling along the Trade Way.

markers.name: Ingdal's Arm
markers.textHtml: A quiet fishing town with a population of around one thousand, made up of roughly half humans and half half-elves. It is the westernmost settlement in Aglarond, and borders the kingdom of Altumbel.

markers.name: Ironmaster
markers.textHtml: An isolated towered city built into the rocky walls of a valley, and inhabited by mountain dwarves. Food is provided from several sources including deep caverns for mushrooms and hunting and fishing along the Shaengarne. Plentiful iron deposits found deep beneath the city are accessed by mine-shafts. The dwarves fashion all kinds of items from the iron after refining it. Trade between Fireshear and Hundelstone is common. The dwarves are reclusive, barring all non-dwarves from entering the city, outlining their valley with large menhir-like stone markers, and attacking any non-dwarves found within the markers.

markers.name: Jathrin's Jump
markers.textHtml: Jathrin's Jump is full of lawless travellers and lawless residents. The town's economy relies heavily on trading stolen goods and goods that are illegal in most other civilized places. At some point in the town's early history an outlaw named Jathrin was executed at the nearby cliffs. This event earned the town its name. Jathrin's capture and execution also lead to the phrase, 'a person's gotta be smarter than your average Jathrin'.

markers.name: Jhalhoran
markers.textHtml: This city is a major center of worship for the god Osiris.

markers.name: Kinbrace
markers.textHtml: Kinbrace is an important farming town found in western Soravia in Damara, with fertile farming land that are vital for the duchy. One feature that make Kinbrace different than most other towns and cities in the Bloodstone Lands was its lack of a protective wall. Instead, Kinbrace uses a series of six strategically placed castles, each controlled by a wealthy landowner, for its defense system.

markers.name: King's Reach
markers.textHtml: King's Reach is a fortified town located near the River Vesper, at a crossing that is the highest navigable point upstream. It is a wealthy community and a center of trade, mining and prospecting in the surrounding mountains. Many prospecting expeditions set out from and return here, often by circuitous and secret routes to protect their claims and finds. Almost every person in King's Reach attempted prospecting for gold at least once in their life, seeking to strike it rich. With all the ore coming in, King's Reach is the biggest centre of smelting in the Vast, and produces many trade-bars of iron and silver, palm-ingots of copper and nickel, and lesser quantities of gold and zinc. These are kept in secure stronghouses and heavily guarded.

markers.name: Kirenzia
markers.textHtml: [not Kirenzie] Kirenzia is one of the two smallest independent city-states of the Blade Kingdoms. It has busy docks with ships and river barges and the town is hidden behind a gated wall. Like other city-states of the Blade Kingdoms, Kirenzia is governed by a free-voting mercenary Blade Council.

markers.name: Kront
markers.textHtml: Kront is a small crossroads settlement and independent trade outpost situated where the Great Road intersects with the Cold Road on the plains of Ashanath. It is considered a part of the Great Dale. Considering the crossroads town is a neutral locale at a site nestled between several different nations and powers, it makes an ideal location for traders to stop along their journeys. Kront's residents trade goods such as fish from Lake Ashane along with smoked meats. Because of the terrible storms that come off Lake Ashane, all of the structures in Kront are built with cellars in which to take shelter.

markers.name: Kurth
markers.textHtml: Kurth is a small town, though it is big enough to be walled. On the western side is the district known as 'Tweenwalls', formed as the new city walls were built closer in and laying between them and the old walls. It was left to go to ruin, becoming an area of shanties, crude stables, and rundown warehouses. Kurth operates as a stop for caravans, a site for gatherings, and as a farmers' market. Kurth thrives and profits off trade along the North Road and Cross Road, between King's Reach, Mulmaster, and Ravens Bluff. Nevertheless, the town is grim and watchful, and known as dark and haunted. Curiously, many pirates come to Kurth desiring to retire somewhere away from the sea, earning it the nickname 'Pirates' Rest'. With all its former pirates, Kurth is rumored to be stuffed with their loot.

markers.name: Lapalgard
markers.textHtml: A large town based around the ancient stronghold known as the Fortress of Lapalgard. Built against yuan-ti attacks, the fortress stood strong for centuries, but no wars came to it after the fall of the Shoon Imperium in 450 DR. Instead, a town grew around it, enriched by caravans passing between Lapaliiya and Halruaa through the Talath Pass. Lapalgard is led by the Knight-Commander of the fortress, and the civic deity of the town is Tempus.

markers.name: Laviguer
markers.textHtml: Laviguer is a hub for the mining industry in the Earthspur Mountains, working in competition with the neighboring town of Vordric Dun. The mining trade here is very profitable for the kingdom. However, they are rough and rowdy places.

markers.name: Lhazantal
markers.textHtml: Lhazantal is a small town known as the City of Smiths, because the majority of its businesses deal with some aspect of metalworking. The citizens manufacture a variety of farming and other tools and hardware, decent mail armor, and sturdy weapons. In addition to the human population, Lhazantal has the second-highest population of gnomes in Lapaliiya, after Zashuma. The civic deity of Lhazantal is Gond.

markers.name: Lheshayl
markers.textHtml: This small city produces high-quality horses - the Plainsteeds. The quality and numbers of Plainsteeds earned Lheshayl the title of the equestrian capital of the Reach. Lheshayl maintains a trade realtionship with a lizardfolk city of Surkh, as well as Riatavin and Saradush to the east. Hunting is a popular pastime in Lheshayl, attracting ambitious hunters from other regions during its many 'official' hunting seasons, enforced by the Emerald Enclave. Lheshayl is protected by a sizable cavalry known as Riders, the biggest and best-trained cavalry of the region.

markers.name: Lomatra
markers.textHtml: A coastal city-state of the Blade Kingdoms.

markers.name: Longsaddle
markers.textHtml: A sleepy little hamlet which, despite its small size, is a member of the Lords' Alliance thanks to the presence of the powerful Harpell family of wizards. This community is famous for the presence of peculiar wizards who are eccentric and often dangerous in their spell-casting manners. The area surrounding Longsaddle is a vast expanse of grassland that caters to the town's ranchers, and the forest around the town is inhabited by werewolf descendants of Bidderdoo Harpell. These werewolves are called 'the Bidderdoos' and although the townsfolk fear them, they provide some measure of protection for the town.

markers.name: Lotoli
markers.textHtml: A coastal city-state of the Blade Kingdoms.

markers.name: Loudwater
markers.textHtml: Loudwater, called the City of Grottos, sits on the confluence of the Delimbiyr and Greyflow rivers in the Delimbiyr Vale. In this stretch, the Delimbiyr is especially wide, giving the settlement enough room for a modest harbor. It is a picturesque garden city home to a pleasant and thriving cosmopolitan community of humans, half-elves, elves, and many other species. It is also the home of Mielikki's chosen, the Green Regents and their Scions, and thanks to their efforts the city serves as the very image of her faith. The city enjoys both fertile farmland and a key location for commerce, allowing it to both flourish and prosper. The local people make a living through farming and fishing, as well as by providing services to caravans. Loudwater is famed for its many grottos. These small caves and hollows between the hills are believed to be sacred to the city.

markers.name: Luthbaern
markers.textHtml: Luthbaern is a small, crowded town, filled with buildings of slate and stone so close together that there is barely any space for balconies, and cobblestone streets. The town lacks any green space. Luthbaernar merchants are notorious in the Border Kingdoms, with a reputation for being arrogant and pompous. The town is also known for its high quality goods. Farmers also craft Luthland cheese, a popular local cheese.

markers.name: Manshaka
markers.textHtml: Manshaka is a city well-known for its blood arenas and slavery. A home for gamblers and smugglers, it's the second-largest gambling center in all Faerûn, next to Calimport. The minarets of Manshaka are made from red bricks.

markers.name: Manywaters
markers.textHtml: Manywaters would just be a small, sleepy village if were not for the Riven Healing House, a small temple dedicated to Ilmater, and the Riven Rock Pool, a special hot spring. The waters of the pool are capable of healing curses, injuries, diseases, insanity, and parasitic infestations. The water even causes lost limbs to regrow. The pool heals by dissolving any magic item placed within it, absorbing an item's magical properties and converting it so it can heal.

markers.name: Maerlar
markers.textHtml: Maerlar sits at the crossroads of the Great South Road, Great East Road, and the Road to the Dawn in north Mulhorand. This location means that most trade going overland to the capital city Skuld pass through its gates. The precept of Maerlar's main duty is to maintain guards at the crossroads and protect incoming trade.

markers.name: Maerstar
markers.textHtml: The town of Maerstar makes its living in quarrying and breeding horses. The town is a center for breeding horses, and has a number of well-used paddocks. In particular, the people of Maerstar breed a large, black, sleek-coated horse that is highly sought after all around the Inner Sea. Despite the popularity of Maerstar's horses, it is not a town of great wealth, but its people earn their prosperity with hard work. In contrast, Maerstar is also home to a number of country houses belonging to merchants of Procampur and Tsurlagol.

markers.name: Mesring
markers.textHtml: The halfling population of the kingdom of Aglarond is limited to a few hundred, concentrated on farmland in the area surrounding the town of Mesring.

markers.name: Milvarune
markers.textHtml: Milvarune is a major port city in Thesk. The sea shores are clustered with tall stone buildings, and its harbors are decorated with multicolored sails and flags of ships from all across the Sea of Fallen Stars. The city's east gate is the location of a caravanserai. Fishing is one of the main industries in Milvarune is fishing, and the city's main source of food. The population of Milvarune is one of the most diverse group in Thesk. The majority of the inhabitants are humans, with the Theskians of Damaran descent being the main ethnic group, followed by Aglarondans, Impilturans, Shou, and a smaller number of Tuigans. The non human population includes a sizeable gnome population from the local mountains.

markers.name: Morovar
markers.textHtml: Morovar is a larger town and was the capital of the Barony of Morov until the mid 14th century.

markers.name: Mosstone
markers.textHtml: Mosstone is a walled caravan-stop town on the Trade Way. Many buildings within the city walls are rustic cottages. The town is cool, damp, and shady. Many streets are covered with green soft moss, and most residents have lush gardens of tangled greenery that is more akin to wildly growing plants. Mosstone is inhabited by many druids, and is famous for its unlikely communal nature and governance. The town is self-governing, and residents as well and druids reach decisions through consensus. Mosstone values balance and peace, living respecting the forest that surrounds the town as well as its elven inhabitants.

markers.name: Mulptan
markers.textHtml: Mulptan is the great trade city of Rashemen. It is one of the nation's more cosmopolitan locales and is considered the 'Gateway' to the lands of the north. The vast, wind-battered city is encircled by a great stone wall, outside of which is its great trading field, with tents and stalls crowded with merchants and businesspeople from many nearby nations, including Damara and Impiltur. Mulptan is home to the Ydrass and Vrul Rashemaar clans, each of whom enjoys an old and sometimes bloody rivalry with the other.

markers.name: Mulsantir
markers.textHtml: Mulsantir is a great and prosperous city in southwest Rashemen. Its position as a coastal town at the end of a prominent trade route, the Golden Way, aids in its status as a major mercantile destination in the region. Its prominence often makes it a target for invasions from the nation of Thay to the south. Unlike many of Rashemen's towns and cities, Mulsantir features buildings of rough stone built entirely atop the earth. It is encircled by fearsome angular walls or shining gray stone. In summer months, ferries transport caravan workers and traders across the River Mulsantir to Ashanath. During the cold months of the year, the waters ice over, and caravans can cross overland unimpeded.

markers.name: Murbant
markers.textHtml: Known as the 'City of a Thousand Purple Domes', it is a walled port infamous as a hub for smugglers and traffickers.

markers.name: Nalufaen
markers.textHtml: Nalufaen is a city in the aquatic elf kingdom of Naramyr, ca. 60 m beneath the surface. It has a reputation for being a city of artisans.

markers.name: Nashkel
markers.textHtml: Nashkel is an Amnian town on the northern slopes of the Cloud Peaks. Although a part of Amn, Nashkel is separated from the rest of the realm by the mountains and is relatively autonomous. Nashkel is the main overland trade route between Amn and the Sword Coast, but is frequently isolated from the south by winter blizzards and storms in the mountain passes. The town exports barley, beers, sheep and their wool, and the meat and pelts of hunted game. It also provides expert guides for those traveling in the nearby mountains, as well as supplies and equipment for caravans as they passed through.

markers.name: Naupau
markers.textHtml: Naupau is the only known trade city of the nation of Sossal. It has a number of small trading outposts along the Yal Tengri, as well as a decent number of trade ships. All outside visitors to the nation are confined to the walls of the city.

markers.name: Nesmé
markers.textHtml: Nesmé was originally a small frontier city with an economy based mostly around trade. Almost all goods bound for the west and from the west passed through Nesmé, and the city was known for its exceptional steel. The city was ruled by a First Speaker and dominated by its merchants. But in the late 14th century DR, Nesmé was one of the first cities to fall under siege by the orcs of the Kingdom of Many-Arrows. By 1485 DR, the city had fallen to ruin, with monsters from the Evermoors thwarting efforts to rebuild it.

markers.name: Nathoud
markers.textHtml: The Myir, a clan of Nars, originally built this fortified town when they came to settle. The Myir of Nathoud are famous for their breeding of the Nathoudi stallion. A yearly horse fair is held in the town. The buildings of Nathoud are typically made of wood, two stories tall and have large overhanging eaves with gently sloping roofs. Elaborate carvings decorate most buildings.

markers.name: Nethentir
markers.textHtml: Nethentir is a small town (1,870 residents - 82% human, 8% gnoll, 6% orc) located on the shores of Lake Umber, serving as the sister city to Nethjet, which sits on the opposite side of the water. Historically, the town was utilized as a strategic launching point for Thayan invasions directed at the nation of Thesk and the Aglarondan city of Emmech. A defining characteristic of the town is that, while it maintains a port, no roads lead into the settlement. Consequently, merchants traveling from Thesk must forge their own overland paths, and local captains primarily spend their time ferrying goods back and forth to the more successful Nethjet. There is a sense of resentment among the citizens regarding the booming wealth of their sister city; however, many are simultaneously relieved that their lack of prosperity keeps them from drawing the unwanted attention of the Red Wizards.

markers.name: Nethjet
markers.textHtml: Nethjet is a Large Town situated on the shores of Lake Umber. Once a quiet settlement, it was transformed into a bustling hub of activity. While the city of Bezantur handles the majority of Thay's magical exports, Nethjet has become the primary port for the country's older commodities, such as cotton, fruit, and grain. Its strategic location allows ships to travel down the River Umber into the Sea of Dlurg, providing a vital trade route that avoids the long and dangerous journey around the Aglarondan peninsula. This surge in trade has brought booming prosperity to the town, a sharp contrast to its struggling sister city, Nethentir, located on the opposite side of the lake. In fact, Nethjet's success has created a notable divide with its sister city, Nethentir.

markers.name: Nyth
markers.textHtml: The small town of Nyth, in Thesk, took its name from a creature called a 'nyth', similar to a will-o'-wisp, that once haunted the western fringe of the Forest of Lethyr. The creature was defeated by a cabal of wizards.

markers.name: Oberiner's Well
markers.textHtml: Oberiner's Well, also known as Obelner's Well, or simply the Well, is a beautiful and peaceful town that in older times had a bad reputation as a rowdy and lawless haven for criminals. It has all settled down, with any misdeeds now going on behind the scenes and only wild stories and legends of buried treasures being told.

markers.name: Oeble
markers.textHtml: Oeble is well-known as a trade center and as a haven for criminal activity in the region. The streets of Oeble are chaotic and maze-like. Over them stretch the so-called Rainspans, elevated ways connecting the higher-rising buildings of Oeble's southern part. And underneath Oeble lays its shady subterranean underbelly – the twisting underground ways and tunnels known as the Underways.

markers.name: Orbech
markers.textHtml: The towns of Timindar and Orbech were established as logging operations in 260 DR by decree of the ruling Illistine family of Chondath. Soon after, the Grand Druid of the Gulthmere Forest issued a stern warning over the desecration of Chondalwood. The first warning, along with several more, were ignored. In 267 DR, Orbech and Timindar were destroyed by the elves of Chondalwood and the Emerald Enclave. Several stone structures still stood in the long-looted ruins, which saw very few visitors besides the most desperate of adventurers. Those who did visit the town told stories of ghosts wandering the ruins. As of the turn of the 16th century DR, Timindar and Orbech are resettled and pay increasingly exorbitant taxes to the Chondathan capital city of Arrabar, to the point of considering secession.

markers.name: Peldan's Helm
markers.textHtml:
Mistledale, Dalelands.

The small village of Peldan's Helm lies along the dangerous western edge of Mistledale. A stout stone wall protects the village, and the gate is never opened between sunset and sunrise, even for known travelers, as Ghosts from the Barrowfield have been known to possess folk and use them to gain entrance. Recently, goblins have laid siege to Peldan's Hold twice, so bell ringers now stand ready at all times to send a call for help to Ashabenford—whose militia is a day's ride away.

markers.name: Peleveran
markers.textHtml: [not Pelevaran] In 1018 DR, Peleveran was destroyed in a battle between two factions of the Cult of the Dragon, one of which had fallen to the worship of Gargauth. The city has been in ruins ever since. There is a hidden temple deep in the ruins that is rumored to house a portal to the Nine Hells.

markers.name: Peltarch
markers.textHtml: Peltarch is the only permanent town of any size in Narfell as of 1479 DR, with most of the inhabitants being Nars of the Ulyoon tribe. It was constructed from the masonry of Old Peltarch to the north of the current settlement. Peltarch trades with many foreigners, as the caravans that take the route over the frozen ice of the Icelace Lake in winter come to this settlement to do their trade. This makes them wealthier than the rest of their kinsmen, who in turn consider them outlanders.

markers.name: Phandalin
markers.textHtml: Originally a farming community, Phandalin was raided by orcs in 951 DR and subsequently abandoned. It was eventually resettled in the late 15th century as a frontier settlement of farmers and prospectors. As of the early 1490s, it is a modest town of hard-working frontiersfolk built atop the ruins of the old Phandalin, with about fifty well-maintained structures built of logs and flagstones. The town is dominated by an apple orchard on the west side and a ruined manor atop a hill to the east. Phandalin is adjacent to land which is good for farming and ranching, and is near enough to Neverwinter Wood to support a woodcutting and logging industry. Most of the wealth, however, is from mining in the nearby Sword Mountains for gold, platinum, ore, timber, skins, and cold iron.

markers.name: Phlan
markers.textHtml: Phlan, also known as the Jewel of the Moonsea, is a large frontier town with a long and storied history. Endless cycles of the town's destruction and determined rebuilding saw it grow more resilient and formidable. In addition to being a monument to the stubbornness of the Moonsea people, the city is famous as the home of one of the fabled pools of radiance, the source of power for a legendary otherworldly spirit. Phlan was founded atop Valjevo Isle, a small isle within the delta formed by the mouth of the Stojanow river and grew around the isle's namesake castle. Circa the late 15th century DR, Phlan is divided into two prominent areas, the walled off Old City, and the newer region of East Phlan. As of the late 15th century Phlan enjoys a century of steady rule and no disastrous events, allowing trade and commerce to flourish. The city receives caravan shipments of valuable ore and gems from its mining communities to the north, protected by the barbarous warriors of the Ride.

markers.name: Portith
markers.textHtml: Portith is a medium-sized town in the Barony of Ostel. A reputation for being the home of many fine inns and skilled craftsmen helped attract travelers. In addition to the income provided from travelers, Portith operates many successful farming and fishing ventures.

markers.name: Priapurl
markers.textHtml: A small human town on the Trader's Road.

markers.name: Rauthil
markers.textHtml: Rauthil is an important location in that it is the only place along the River Rauthenflow where a bridge was constructed allowing for easy crossing.

markers.name: Ravilar's Cloak
markers.textHtml: Ravilar's Cloak, also known as The Cloak, is a wild and rowdy mining town and a hot-spot for treasure seekers. The Cloak is protected by warriors from all the independent mining groups within the community. Over the years, the town became known for its murders, and for the legend of the 'flying helm'. The inhabitants blame the magical floating helmet for anything that goes missing.

markers.name: Red Haven
markers.textHtml: A small fishing village.

markers.name: Red Larch
markers.textHtml: A waystop on the Long Road seven days north of Waterdeep, located at the intersection of three trails. One trail leads to the Bargewright Inn, another to Kheldell, and the third runs into the hills to derelict, monster-infested keeps. The town was named for a stand of red larch trees that were chopped down about the time of the town's founding. Red Larch is known for its nourishing, though otherwise non-noteworthy food, called crumblecake.

markers.name: Relkath's Foot
markers.textHtml: Relkath's Foot is the largest permanent settlement inside the Yuirwood, and the unofficial half-elven capital of the woods. It was built around four tall, majestic trees that rise more than 9 feet (30 m) from the canopy. These trees are said to have sprung from the buried foot of the deity Relkath of the Infinite Branches, who is said to be sleeping under the soil until he is needed again. A number of the city's inhabitants make their homes on wooden platforms hanging on the massive trees and the surrounding forest, strung together by a complex web of ropes and suspension bridges. Relkath's Foot is more of a center of occasional trade and lorekeeping than actual commerce. However, some of the most skilled woodworkers and bowmakers in Aglarond keep homes in the town.

markers.name: Rodanar
markers.textHtml: The war god and red dragon Tchazzar founded Rodanar to serve as a fortress to thwart orc incursions from the Akanapeaks. Rodanar used to be a large city, but a war with Akanax during the 12th century DR left it in ruins. The new Rodanar that sprung up since was much smaller.

markers.name: Rrinnoroth
markers.textHtml: Rrinnoroth was a settlement located within the dwarven realm of Shanatar. It was a ghost town by the late 14th century DR. Its sole occupant is the mighty red dragon Charvekkanathor, who uses the ruins as his lair.

markers.name: Sampranasz
markers.textHtml: The small fishing port of Sampranasz serves as the center for the Cult of Set in Mulhorand. It has a violent history, having been destroyed three times: once during the Orcgate Wars and twice by natural disaster. As a result, ruins abound in the town, used to good effect by the priests of Set for their secret meetings and general worship.

markers.name: Scardale Town
markers.textHtml:
Capital of Scardale, Dalelands.

Scardale Town was once the largest city in the Dalelands, but its fortunes have waxed and waned over the years. After a long period of rebuilding, Scardale Town is growing into a proper city once more and is now a great port at the mouth of the River Ashaba. About five thousand people call this settlement home. Lord Kharu Aumersair is the ruler of Scardale and the tiefling son of the infamous fallen paladin Scyllua Darkhope, Scardale's previous ruler. Three criminal syndicates—the Dancers, the Shaker Gang, and the Stallions—compete in Scardale Town.

markers.name: Scarsdeep
markers.textHtml:
Scardale, Dalelands.

Oceangoing vessels can't navigate all the way upriver to Chandlerscross. The town of Scarsdeep was founded where most such vessels are forced to turn around. Scarsdeep draws in farmers and merchants looking to expand their market. The main street of Scarsdeep is crowded with taverns, inns, gambling houses, and more.

markers.name: Sebben
markers.textHtml: A small town built around a pond filled by a spring. Sebben is the hub for sheep and goat ranches around the area. The location is pleasantly pastoral, yet keeps regular contact with the outside world.

markers.name: Secomber
markers.textHtml: A small, peaceful town sitting atop three stone hills on the northern banks of the southern-running Unicorn Run river and the western-running River Delimbiyr, and that acts as the de facto border town between the relatively peaceful Western Heartlands and the more savage North along the Sword Coast. The town contains a number of colorful gardens. It is populated primarily by humans, but almost as many halflings call the place home, and some say they dominate the town. Farming, fishing, and stone-cutting are the primary occupations, but Secomber also has a number of folk familiar with the High Forest. Some locals hire themselves out as guides or guards for the passing caravans.

markers.name: Sekelmur
markers.textHtml: A vital crossroads city dominated by massive granaries, wagonworks, and ox-stables.

markers.name: Shaarmid
markers.textHtml: A small town founded by Lapal survivors of the gnoll massacre of Lhesper in 640 DR.

markers.name: Shadowdale Town
markers.textHtml:
Shadowdale, Dalelands.

Shadowdale Town is a sleepy community of about 1,500 people. Lady Khara Sulwood is the human ruler of the town and has been in power for a generation. Shadowdale Town has been home to some of the greatest heroes of Faertin, most notably Elminster and Storm Silverhand.

markers.name: Shalane
markers.textHtml: A small fishing town renowned for the best shipbuilders of all Erlkazar.

markers.name: Shamedar
markers.textHtml: Schamedar is a coastal city that had long been a ghost town but later became a center for religious diversity in that country. A full 70% of Schamedar's citizens are employed by its many temples, and folk most often pilgrimage here to receive temple blessings or to seek the services of scribes and bookbinders. It is also a good place to come to borrow money. Schamedar also has a well-established fishing industry, and is often a stopping point for the slave trade. Schamedar itself also sells slaves captured in the Marching Mountains or the Forest of Mir.

markers.name: Shussel
markers.textHtml: Shussel, a fishing town, was destroyed alongside the rest of Unther during the Spellplague of 1385 DR.

markers.name: Skull Crag
markers.textHtml: Skull Crag is a mining community with a population of 1,000 by the late 15th century DR. It is a squalid hamlet that reeks of manure and other fetid odors. The notable aroma is cleared only when mountain winds blow across Skull Crag. The town has passages and buildings cut directly out of the Storm Horns. One of such passages provides an additional connection to the Skull Crag Keep and a direct tunnel from it to the town gates. The keep itself loomed over the town ominously. The settlement is overshadowed by a large whitened marble outcropping polished and chiseled into a human skull, and used as a shrine to Kelemvor.

markers.name: Songhal
markers.textHtml: The enlightened town of Songhal serves as a center of learning for the kingdom of Impiltur. The House of Many Tomes in Songhal is perhaps the largest and most-renowned temple of Oghma found throughout the Realms.

markers.name: Soolabax
markers.textHtml: Soolabax is a walled town in Chessenta near the border with Threskel. The town's nominal ruler is a vassal of Tchazzar.

markers.name: Ssintar
markers.textHtml: Ssintar is a small trading and military outpost. The God-king Gilgeam has soldiers stationed there, whose job is to protect Unther from invasions from either the Alamber Sea or the Methwood. Near Ssintar are mines rich with deposits of copper and iron.

markers.name: Sumbria
markers.textHtml: A coastal city-state of the Blade Kingdoms.

markers.name: Surag
markers.textHtml: Surag sits in gently-rolling hills on the western side of the mouth of the Surag River, where it feeds into the great Lake Thaylambar. The tumbling waters of the Surag River were tamed by Thay, channeling the energy to fuel the town's many milling businesses. As such, Surag produces crushed ore, flour, and fabric from the fulling mills, serving as materials and food supply center for the tharch. Surag is a small but crowded and busy town of mills, steep slopes, and cobbled lanes. The majority of buildings in Surag are homes of several floors, grist mills, mills for ore, and fulling mills. With time and Surag's expansion, the town built up paddocks around the Surag River gorge, providing trade caravans with amenities, becoming a stop along the trade ways.

markers.name: Surd
markers.textHtml: Surd is a small Sembian agricultural town and a busy merchant caravan stop connected to Ordulin and Yhaunn. It also supports some of the fishing communities that spring along the rich and flat shores of the Dragon Reach.

markers.name: Surthay
markers.textHtml: Surthay is a newer city of Thay, compared to those that date back to the age of ancient Raumathar, built as a bulwark to the forces of Rashemen to the north. Despite its lakeside location, Surthay is not constructed as a port city. It is a crude stronghold with rutted streets lined by simple wooden houses, while the eastern heights are home to much more pleasant mansions and towers. Its massive stonewalls are dotted with fortified towers, armed with massive siege weapons and ballistae, reinforced gates and protected passageways. The legions of the greater tharch of Surthay keep garrison within the city's keeps and towers. Most of Surthay reeks of the fetid smell of the nearby Surmarsh, particularly during summer. Disease-carrying insects often plague the city. Surthay is a city of poverty and rough living for those outside the city's aristocracy. It is rife with criminal organizations.

markers.name: Surkh
markers.textHtml: Surkh, also referred to as the City of Lizards, is the largest, most advanced and civilized community of lizardfolk in the entirety of Faerûn. Surkh is ruled by a King, advised by a council of tribal chieftains. Most Surkhites live in homes constructed out of baked mud bricks. Several habitats are built in shades and around communal pools.
