The lizardfolk of Surkh live off fishing and produce enough seafood to be traded and exported in limited quantity. Many of the locals work as fishers, and the rest take on the mantles of soldiers and gladiators. One of the major religions in Surkh is the worship of Tiamat, the Chromatic Dragon. Despite the fact that the cult considers Surkh to be their territory, the Church of Tiamat is an underground cult.

markers.name: Survale Ford
markers.textHtml: A fortified base of the Champions Vigilant, which is a Tethyrian knightly order of Helm. These fighters, clerics and paladins are prestigious and popular throughout the realm.

markers.name: Szul
markers.textHtml: A luxury way-stop at the base of the Thaymount where the elite enjoy opulent accommodations and fine dining during vacations.

markers.name: Targos
markers.textHtml: A fishing town in Icewind Dale and a leading member of the Ten Towns. The harbor's deep water allows the construction of vessels that are large for the area. As a result, Targos thrived off and now dominates the local fishing industry more than any of the Ten Towns. Like most of the Ten Towns, Targos specializes in trading knucklehead trout. The town and much of the harbor are surrounded by a wooden defensive wall, called the palisade. Targos was originally a sprawling town with uncluttered streets: the buildings had breathing space, being separated by wide avenues. This, however, changed by the mid-1480s DR, when Targos became abuzz with commerce and industry that brought the city's expansion. The newer inhabitants started to construct shacks beyond the city's wooden walls and the town threatened to grow beyond them before long.

markers.name: Tellerth
markers.textHtml: Tellerth is a medium-sized farming town in the Barony of Polten and the easternmost settlement found in Damara. Aspirations that Tellerth's people have of logging in the Dunwood cause tensions with the druids and centaurs of the forest.

markers.name: Teshburl
markers.textHtml: Sitting on a rocky coast with many steep cliffs, Teshburl is the most isolated town of Calimshan. It is the secondary shipbuilding site for the nation's navy; everything in the town revolves around the navy, and Teshburl serves as the nation's headquarters and primary port for it. In addition to the warships, the town produces all manner of rope, cord, and nets and other equipment for sailing. Fishing is a major industry as well. The Coast Trail does pass through Teshburl, but it is little used because of the rocky terrain, and travel across the desert is dangerous. Because of this, almost everyone, including traders, enters and exits the port town by ship.

markers.name: Teshwave
markers.textHtml: Teshwave is the largest trading community in Teshendale. It was taken over by the Zhents in the early 14th century DR and has been controlled by the forces of Zhentil Keep since. It is an exporter of wood and stone that is moved directly to Zhentil Keep. Zhents upturned and quarried out previously lush farmlands and pastures that surrounded the city. The town itself is a ramshackle concoction of ruins, tents, newly-built barracks and some surviving structures from the pre-Zhent town. The town's Zhent garrison commander occupies the Manor House. Teshwave's docks remained operational through the ruinous time, sending river ferries that travel between Zhentil Keep and Dagger Falls.

markers.name: Thasunta
markers.textHtml: Thasunta, also known as the City of Warriors or the City of Berserkers, is a bustling settlement that serves as the home to many of the famous Rashemaar berserkers. Throughout Rashemen's history, these berserkers defended their nation against invasion forces dispatched by the malevolent southern nation of Thay. Thasunta is not directly governed by the Iron Lord, but rather by the most venerated of Rashemen's berserker lodges, the Old Lodge.

markers.name: Thindilar
markers.textHtml: Thindilar is a market town populated by artisans and craftspeople. Life there is busy and energetic, and people there have little time for wild tales of treasure and adventure in their vicinity. Despite the attitude of the locals, there are stories that, early in the Vast’s history, a flying cloud castle home to cloud giants was defeated in some magical battle and crashed in the area, with some of the cloud giant treasure still laying there—whether buried deep or hovering high in the air, suspended by a lingering trace of the cloud castle's flight magic.

markers.name: Thunderstone
markers.textHtml: Thunderstone is a small town in northeastern Cormyr. It has a relatively small economy, relying mostly on the caravans, adventurers, and travelers passing through (the Thunderway begins at Thunderstone, before running through High Dale and ending at Saerb in Sembia, and the Hullack Trail also begins here, and runs west through southeast Cormyr). The town does however have a local industry for fishing and logging and a thriving pelt trade. The town has no fortifications, nor a militia, but boasts a 100-strong garrison of the Purple Dragons. Their duties include enforcing the laws of Cormyr; guarding the pass to Sembia; and defending the town from threats.

markers.name: Tibold
markers.textHtml: A mining town built on top of an ancient dwarven city. It is said a third of the city is underground in caverns.

markers.name: Timindar
markers.textHtml: The towns of Timindar and Orbech were established as logging operations in 260 DR by decree of the ruling Illistine family of Chondath. Soon after, the Grand Druid of the Gulthmere Forest issued a stern warning over the desecration of Chondalwood. The first warning, along with several more, were ignored. In 267 DR, Orbech and Timindar were destroyed by the elves of Chondalwood and the Emerald Enclave. Several stone structures still stood in the long-looted ruins, which saw very few visitors besides the most desperate of adventurers. Those who did visit the town told stories of ghosts wandering the ruins. As of the turn of the 16th century DR, Timindar and Orbech are resettled and pay increasingly exorbitant taxes to the Chondathan capital city of Arrabar, to the point of considering secession.

markers.name: Toreus
markers.textHtml: Toreus started out as a lone garrison for Mordulkin soldiers, but grew into the third biggest city of the confederation of Chessenta in the 15th century DR. This city is known for its assertive business strategies, and goes as far as trading with marauding pirates and criminals. Even a Red Wizard enclave is allowed to operate within. All types of coins are accepted in this settlement and can be used to purchase practically anything.

markers.name: Torsch
markers.textHtml: A settlement standing on a trade way built in the days of young Vilhon Reach—the Old Road. Deep underneath Torsch lays the exact center of Vaerndoun, a sprawling drow cave complex. Its winding tunnels, lakes, and caves stretch for over a hundred miles. Once per year, merchants from Torsch participate in the trade-moot known as the Eleave in the caverns of Vaerndoun.

markers.name: Trademeet
markers.textHtml: Trademeet, also known as the City of Merchant's Peace, is the smallest of the region's caravan stops. Due to the Merchant's Peace, all commerce in Trademeet is bound to be carried out honestly and fairly. The town is centered around Waukeen's Circle, a prehistoric circle of seven upright stone slabs. In 1370 DR, Trademeet was ransacked by the ogre forces of the Sythillisian Empire. Much of the town was burned to the ground. More than 60% of the population was killed.

markers.name: Three Swords
markers.textHtml: Three Swords does brisk business with Khôltar which is hungry for food and drink, especially the potent spirit amberfire that originates in Three Swords. The town is surrounded by walls and benefits from regular patrols of the Trader's Way and the Landrise by the Steel Shields from Eartheart.

markers.name: Triboar
markers.textHtml: Triboar is a lively crossroads frontier town full of the hustle and bustle of roaming merchants, caravanners, and other travelers. At the heart of the town, where the roads cross, is a vast marketplace where local farmers, ranchers, and roadside salespeople offer their wares. The Triboar economy revolves around trade, by selling wagons and caravan services, as well as by farming and dealing in horses, cattle, and forged ironware.

markers.name: Tulach
markers.textHtml: Despite its small population, Tulach is ruled by a self-proclaimed King. He is known for sending mercenaries to eliminate rust monsters in the mines of the Maerwatch, which are rich in metals like copper and silver.

markers.name: Two Stars
markers.textHtml: Laid at the intersection of the Cold Road and the Golden Way, more trade passed through Two Stars than anywhere else in Thesk. The entire city is surrounded with a 30 feet-tall (9 m) stone wall. The Golden Way passes into the city, then along much of its inner wall; much of the road inside the city is used as a trade bazaar.

markers.name: Ulver's Lance
markers.textHtml: Ulver's Lance is a quaint, quiet little town. Its main industry is cart-building and woodcarving. The namesake of Ulver's Lance was a hero named Ulver who kept the Halondar road safe from monsters who raided merchant caravans in the nation's early years.

markers.name: Umratharos
markers.textHtml: A rising industrial city within the tharch of Delhumide, specialized in pottery, metal casting, and wood-carving.

markers.name: Velarsburg
markers.textHtml:
Harrowdale, Dalelands.

Velarsburg is the busy heart of Harrowdale's logging and carpentry trades. Logging mills and lumberyards surround the town's cottages and workshops. A large, muddy market space fills the center of the village. The people of Velarsburg follow centuries of tradition that include strict lumber quotas and replanting. Proud of their relationship with the forest, they consider their way of life a model that other Dalefolk would be wise to emulate. Velarsburg is home to an impressive temple to Chauntea, overseen by the wood elf Thevil Oakenstaff, and a shrine to Malar.

markers.name: Voonlar
markers.textHtml: Voonlar is more urbanized than most farming towns because it is located at an intersection of trade routes. It is both a way station and terminus for many caravans plying the Moonsea and Dalelands regions. The lion's share of these do business with or are owned by the Shield Trading Company headquartered in Voonlar. In the 14th century, Voonlar was ostensibly an independent town but was actually ruled by puppets of the Zhentarim. As the years passed, and with the return of the Netherese to Anauroch, the Zhentarim were forced out of the Moonsea area and Voonlar became a bone of contention between the growing Netherese empire and the renewed influence of a revitalized Myth Drannor.

markers.name: Vordric Dun
markers.textHtml: Vordric Dun is populated by rough-and-tumble miners that live hard lives. As a group, they compete with the miners of Laviguer for the resources located deep beneath the Earthspurs. They bring significant wealth to Impiltur in the form of the valuable gems, ore and minerals, among them the rare specimen known as bloodstone, they excavated from their mines.

markers.name: Waymoot
markers.textHtml: Waymoot is the biggest settlement in the King's Forest in Cormyr. It was built on a five-mile man-made clearing. The town is a mish-mash collection of buildings that spring up around a wide stone-walled keep at the center of the clearing. Waymoot is a trade town that breeds and trains horses for private and army use, as well as builds and repairs wagons.

markers.name: Whitehorn
markers.textHtml: Whitehorn, named for the nearby sharp, snow-covered crags, is a frontier trading and resupply town for prospectors, miners, and woodcutters. It is an unwalled town that began as a temple-farm dedicated Chauntea called the Favored Fields of Chauntea, before it grew and was settled by smithies and toolmakers and repairers. The muddy streets are lined with stone cottage buildings and there are dozens of wells. The town is under constant threat of orc attack. It is defended by steep earthwork fortifications and has a standing militia.

markers.name: Yhep
markers.textHtml: This fortified town is built around its harbor. A part of the town is built right on the water, and the majority of the residents who live here are fishermen and oyster farmers, who dock their vessels under their homes. The economy of Yhep is based on seafood and jewelry exports. However, the most valuable part of the Yhevvan economy are the rare deep red pearls cultivated in the oyster beds in Yhep's territory.

markers.name: Ylraphon
markers.textHtml: The small town of Ylraphon had long been an orcish stronghold before being conquered by humans some time before the 11th century DR. Under human rule, Ylraphon was governed by a loose council of independent human merchants, and trade was important. However, the neighboring city of Calaunt was an aggressive competitor, and Ylraphon's prosperity declined through the mid–14th century DR. Finally, amidst the Dracorage of 1373 DR, a flight of dragons destroyed the city.

markers.name: Yûlash
markers.textHtml: Yûlash is a fortress town sitting atop a large plateau giving it a considerable tactical advantage over all the nearby settlements. It is positioned between the two cities of Zhentil Keep and Hillsfar, and the ongoing conflict between these two cities means that Yûlash spent much of its history in ruin and occupied by soldiers. Having been controlled by Zhentil Keep for much of its history, the city's culture and politics are easily influenced by the will of the Zhentarim.

markers.name: Zashuma
markers.textHtml: A small farming town focused on growing olives and producing wine. Unusually, Zashuma is mostly populated by rock gnomes, unlike the predominantly human communities of Lapaliiya. The civic deity of Zashuma is Garl Glittergold.

markers.name: Zutria
markers.textHtml: A coastal city-state of the Blade Kingdoms.

markers.name: Abbey of the Golden Sheaf
markers.textHtml: The Abbey of the Golden Sheaf is devoted to Chauntea. The structure consists of a large walled compound with a dozen separate triangular-shaped crop fields that spread outward from the center, and an earthen exterior wall covered with vines. A spired abbey stands in the center of the fields. An extensive network of underground granaries stores enough food to feed the dale for years. The clergy spends their time tending the countless crops and gardens, while also performing public and private ceremonies in worship to Chantea. The abbey earns a considerable portion of its income from selling rare seeds from the Mhair Jungles and Chult.

markers.name: Aelor
markers.textHtml: Aelor was settled in 277 DR, practically overnight, following a discovery of seemingly rich gold vein. However, this proved to be untrue and gold deposits were exhausted only five year later. By 282 DR, most industrious prospects of Aelor suffered financials losses and by 284 DR, Aelor became a ghost town of abandoned buildings. Most inhabitants of Aelor moved on to the city Amah. By 1370 DR, Amah returned to life as a small independent city-state.

markers.name: Ahruukar
markers.textHtml: A town in the Thesk Mountains. A city of Gold Dwarves.

markers.name: Antlerbones
markers.textHtml: Antlerbones is a waymeet, which is the name given to temporary villages of tents that crop up at large campgrounds found in Narfell. The Nar tribes share these sites freely between themselves and even with outsiders. Given their transitory nature, the population of a waymeet is highly variable.

markers.name: Arnglar
markers.textHtml: Arnglar is a sleepy hill village situated not far from where the River Rith and Tumblestone River originate. Its high elevation exposes it to the winds and the elements, making it difficult to grow crops that are not protected. The village is generally unfriendly to visitors but will trade wool, tallow candles or mutton sausages with passing merchants. Arnglar is home to shepherds who keep flocks on the hillsides. This location was once the elven realm of Glorfindral, and the burial mounds of the elven kings form a ring around the village.

markers.name: Arrowmark
markers.textHtml: Arrowmark is a small and quiet tassel, composed of cottages built along a very straight main road, built in the shade of large trees. Arrowmark is famous for the quality of the arrows and bows produced by the locals. The bowyers and fletchers of Arrowmark tend to test their new arrows and bows on the main street, taking advantage of its straightness. A sounding of three chimes warns of an upcoming test. The modest stone fortress of Arrowpoint is home to the Pegasus Archery Company, a mercenary unit that vowed to defend the High Dale. Would-be archers from throughout the Dalelands come to Arrowpoint for instruction. Arrowmark is also home to who is likely the most famous ghosts in the Dalelands: the Gray Lady.

markers.name: Asbravn
markers.textHtml: Asbravn is a small town lying in a shallow valley in between the Dusk Road and the Uldoon Trail. It has lush farms, wood lots, well-kept barns, drainage ditches, ponds, and an air of tranquility. The town serves as the market center of the southern Vale farmers. Buyers from Berdusk and Iriaebor come to the market each day. The only relic of Asbravn's long past is an old, stout, cracked stone pillar, surmounted by crumbling horse heads. This pillar comes from when the town was a city, Urdrath of the Horsemen. The Horsemen moved from Tunland long ago when they were nomads. Urdrath buried their dead in catacombs beneath the streets where unwary people now pass.

markers.name: Asple
markers.textHtml: Asple is a small, independent fishing community. Even though its main production is fish, the town also houses a rich and prosperous stone quarry that supplies the Reach. The town of Asple also attracts industrious adventurers in search of old sunken ships flush with treasures as the local fishers are quite knowable on the matter.

markers.name: Auckney
markers.textHtml: Auckney is a small fiefdom ruled by the noble Auck family. The village hugs a small, round harbor that is dominated by an island topped by Castle Auck. Sea cliffs line the southern edge of the harbor and beyond the village's farms is the steep Maerlon Mountain. The village is constantly beset by fog and strong, cold wind. An abandoned lighthouse sits on a cliff to the north of the harbor. The Lord of Auckney runs a number of towers surrounding the fiefdom. Auckney is mostly inhabited by humans, but a population of gnomes settled there, having to work extra hard to be accepted by its human inhabitants.

markers.name: Aurilssbarg
markers.textHtml: Aurilssbarg is the largest settlement on Ice Peak island. This small town of about 3,000 citizens acts as a general trading post for incoming traders (mainly from Luskan who has established a monopoly), as well as those of Bjorn's Hold and Icewolf. Wares such as scrimshaw, oils, animal skins, and other provisions are traded. The ground and paths of Aurilssbarg are made up of felled tree trunks. The buildings are also made of wood, and their roofs are covered in turf.

markers.name: Bambryn
markers.textHtml: Bambryn is a way-village marked by its rural beauty and common hedgerows. Thanks to its proximity to local trade routes, Bambryn is popular with merchants and adventurers, particularly those who have been successful but not fully retired; there are manor houses belonging to those who settled there. It is a wealthy village, largely invested in agriculture, with many horse-ranches and farms. Bambryn produces quality horses and luxury crops, like flowers, truffles, and other exotic fungi. These plants are kept fresh with cantrips prior to export.

markers.name: Banathar
markers.textHtml: The humble village of Banathar began its life as a brigands' stronghold. But by the mid–14th century DR, the stronghold was abandoned and the village took on a new identity as a sleepy mining and sheep-herding village, also serving as a waystop on the Lilit Pass trail. There were many stories that claimed that the bandits who settled Banathar left behind countless buried treasures, there for adventurers' picking. Over the village's history, many coin-filled leather satchels had been discovered around the village.

markers.name: Battlerise
markers.textHtml: Besides a small population of turnip farmers, the hamlet of Battlerise comprises a fortified waystable. The settlement is named for the nearby hill topped with the ruins of Battlegate Castle, once the seat of the extinct Auantiver noble family. Various nobles attempted to rebuild it in order to use it as a base for trade with neighboring Sembia. However, their efforts were defeated by the Cult of the Dragon, who had already been using the castle ruins as a headquarters. The hamlet itself was once a larger village, but its population was greatly reduced after it was attacked by monsters raiding out of the Vast Swamp such as lizardfolk.

markers.name: Bay Town
markers.textHtml: Although centrally located on Traders Bay, a major waterway, the small settlement of Bay Town never gained any economic prominence, and was largely overshadowed by ports on the coast of the Easting Reach. Instead, Bay Town caters only to the dregs of society. The town is a haven for thieves, pirates and a slew of other unfavorable types. Visitors have to be wary lest they end up shackled into slavery.

markers.name: Beliard
markers.textHtml: Beliard is a small trading village that is home to, as well as a meeting place for, the many cattle ranchers that drive their herds across the nearby hills. It is located at the crossroads where the Stone Trail intersects the Dessarin Road.

markers.name: Berranzo
markers.textHtml: Berranzo was originally built by Calishite humans circa 1330 DR for the purposes of mining and refining, and was home to some 2000 people. Wizards here employed fire elementals to refine the ore. But, circa 1333 DR, after only three years of operation, every inhabitant of Berranzo was driven mad and most killed, the reason unknown. For some months thereafter, Northmen captains came across ore ships adrift in the sea, crewed only by gibbering lunatics and the dead, their precious cargos still present. The Berranzo colony was abandoned and left to fall into ruins by 1370 DR, with even the bold local Northmen afraid to venture there.

markers.name: Bezentil
markers.textHtml: Bezentil is a walled trading settlement that is easily recognizable by the ten, 5 ft (1,50 m) tall stone markers that are situated 10 ft (3 m) apart along the road passing through tows. Each of these is inscribed with the symbol of a tree set within a circle. It is a town of farmers and outdoorsmen, and houses an important site of worship for the followers of Malar. Although located in the Great Dale, Bezentil is a former waymeet (a temporary tent village of the nomadic Nar people) and was previously considered by the Nar people to be a part of their territory, before it grew into a permanent settlement in the mid-14th century.

markers.name: Blackfeather Bridge
markers.textHtml: Blackfeather Bridge is the name of a village surrounding the bridge of the same name across the River Ashaba. The village is one of the largest settlements in Featherdale and marks its western end. The bridge itself was originally made of wood, but was replaced by a stone one in the mid-14th century. It is considered a sacred site and a pilgrimage site for the church of Cyric, as it is the location Cyric killed Leira, and where mortal Cyric passed in life.

markers.name: Blackstone
markers.textHtml: Cantrev Blackstone is a small iron mining town located in Fairheight county of the kingdom of Callidyrr, under constant threat of raids by the goblins and orcs of the Fairheight Range. It is nestled in a valley overlooking the border with Gnarhelm, near the source of the Swanmay River, and serves as the start for the western branch of the High King's Road. Blackstone appears to be less of a town and more of a collection of provision shops with housing added as an afterthought. While the town is generally shabby, there are hints of the great wealth that has been extracted from the mines. Black smoke permeates the town and valley, stinging the eyes. Foul smells and the detritus of industry are everywhere.

markers.name: Blanaer
markers.textHtml: Blanaer is a stopover village home to hardworking farmers. It is a local agricultural center, with a market for selling sheep and cattle from nearby farms, paddocks to hold stock, and piles of manure for sale. 'Stinks like Blanaer' is a common expression in the Vast. However, in the early and lawless years of human settlement of the Vast, bandits battled openly along the Cross Way near where Blanaer later arose, giving rise to local treasure tales of chests stuffed with coins buried by thieves on the run.

markers.name: Bowshot
markers.textHtml: Bowshot is a prosperous farming community sitting aside the Trade Way. It was named for its location, being a 'bowshot' from the Misty Forest when the village was founded. The woods became considerably farther away as the years of logging have taken their toll. Recently caverns have been discovered at the western edge of the hamlet. The caves may be connected to the upper Underdark and a large vein of silver.

markers.name: Bristar
markers.textHtml: Bristar is a village of moon elves called Velethuil by its inhabitants. The name 'Bristar' is a contraction of 'Brightstar', the name of the clan of elves living there, and the elven name for the settlement comes from their former leader, Velethuil. The elves of Bristar have a strong alliance with the Dalesmen, lending their archers to the dale's militia in their shared fight against the drow. They are particularly protective of Lake Eredruie, a sacred pool of water whose waters have healing properties to elves and half-elves.

markers.name: Brotha
markers.textHtml: Brotha is a small agricultural village found within the Barony of Ostel. The farmland surrounding the village is quite productive, helping Ostel maintain their independence from the larger baronies of Damara.

markers.name: Buldamar
markers.textHtml: The village of Buldamar began as a collection of camps setup by adventurers exploring the nearby Okothian ruins called the Vaults of Record. The prosperity of the inhabitants who stay in Buldamar rise and fall over the centuries depending on the number of explorers drawn to the region. During slow times the villagers scrape together a living mining salt and fishing on Azulduth.

markers.name: Cairnpur
markers.textHtml: The village of Cairnpur is one of several overnight stops for caravans that travel along the Merchant's Run, part of the Herald's Road that stretches between Impiltur and the Cold Lands. It receives protection from Ilmwatch's garrison contingents.

markers.name: Calling Horns
markers.textHtml: The Calling Horns is an inn named after the legendary battle where an army of humans and dwarves united to defeat the last army of trolls. After 1485 DR, the Calling Horns Inn was owned and run by Tamalin Zoar, and the location expanded into a small settlement.

markers.name: Chalarstaukh
markers.textHtml: Chalarstaukh is the largest goblin realm in the Rauvin Mountains. It is a network of caverns inhabited by goblins, who subsist on yellow cave-worms. This habit means that the Chalarstaukh goblins are called 'Worm-Eaters' by the orcs above, who often raid the goblin warrens.

markers.name: Claw Hollow
markers.textHtml: Claw Hollow is one of the hideouts of the People of the Black Blood, a group of Malar-worshiping lycanthropes. The stronghold of the tribe is built within two rings of stockades, erected around a rocky promontory and a network of underground caverns. The refuge acts as a rally point, and a safe-zone when the Knights in Silver are out hunting them in the forest. Often, nearly half of the tribe is away from the settlement, busy hunting in the nearby woods, where there are a dozen or so shelters belonging to the tribe.

markers.name: Corth
markers.textHtml: [not Corst] Corth is a fishing village situated on a small cove with nearby beaches of white sand. At the center of Corth Cove is a craggy rock that juts above the waves. Corth boasts a small fishing fleet that brings it considerable wealth during the spring and summer seasons. The village is also a popular destination spot among Aglarondans, due to its scenic beaches and rustic atmosphere. Like other human communities in western Aglarond, the people of Corth trace their heritage back to the pirates and other outlaws that once called the region home.

markers.name: Cromm's Hold
markers.textHtml: Cromm’s Hold is a rather short and squat stone keep, encircled by a wall. It watches over the Lizard Marsh in order to detect all signs of lizardfolk activities. The Cromm Baron family has been ruling the keep at least since the mid-14th century DR.

markers.name: Culmaster
markers.textHtml: The town of Culmaster stands east of Cape Dragonsfang, on the road that connects Telflamm and Thassalra. It has been a satellite city of Telflamm since the late 10th century, and was a pirate safe harbor of sorts by the mid-14th century.

markers.name: Dhast
markers.textHtml: [not Dahst] Dhast is a settlement in the Fang, a large promontory that was settled in the 11th century DR by humans refugees of Velprin defeated by the half-elves of the Yuirwood. The inhabitants of the Fang rule themselves, but are still considered Aglarondan citizens. As of the mid-14th century DR, the population of Dhast was split between a clifftop community and a smaller number of fishermen living on the beach. The two portions of the hamlet were linked via a trail, and a rivalry existed between them, but both communities were distrustful of outsiders.

markers.name: Dark Hollow
markers.textHtml: Dark Hollow has a reputation as a strange and hazardous place, where faerie dragons play, intruders are the victims of practical jokes by brownies and other forest folk, and common animals are the familiars of crazed wizards. These wizards are said to make their homes in rundown cabins in the forest, and to be conducting dangerous experiments with new spells. There are many hedge wizards in Dark Hollow, of all kinds and practices. With magic being an expensive practice, these hedge-wizards are often broke, and live day-to-day on potion sales. 'Magic merchants' of Ravens Bluff and Hlintar do good trade buying their potions and selling them spell components in exchange.

markers.name: Daroush
markers.textHtml: Daroush was established in 50 DR as a dwarven mining colony. Gold was one of the main metals mined here. At some point, after the mines ran dry, the dwarves abandoned Daroush and returned to Alaoreum. By 1370 DR the mining village was almost completely empty. Some humans still mined for gold, but the best ore had been extracted by the dwarven founders of the mine, and there were reports of dark dwarves (likely duergar) roaming the mine shafts.

markers.name: Dauntshield
markers.textHtml: Dauntshield is a small, stark village that stands on the top of a barren rock ridge. Most structures in the town are plain and constructed out of stone. In the past, Dauntshield was a busy center of blacksmithing, forges, and smelting. The village produced iron and silver ingots and tradebars that were quite widespread. Eventually, the rich veins were exhausted. By the mid-14th century DR, the town was reduced to a humble shepherd and farmer community with a few ambitious and optimistic prospectors. The town has one old keep, located on the south end of the ridge.

markers.name: Dawngleam
markers.textHtml: Dawngleam is a fishing village on the Bluemist Trail. Before the expansion of its docks began in the 1360s DR, the village nearshore consisted of a group of tiny islands joined to the shore and each other by bridges. They later became anchoring points for the new docks. The islands are actually surviving fragments of an ancient citadel of mages founded by an elf mage. Not all the magic is lost, however: command words can cause ghostly bridges to appear on certain spots of some islands, and pirates and smugglers used this power to evade their enemies. The rapid growth of Dawngleam started in the 1360s when the Cormyrean navy established a base there to guard against pirates. At that time, Dawngleam adopted a new fish-packing process developed in Chessenta, launched a joint venture with merfolk to set up protected clam beds, and started the construction of new shipbuilding facilities.

markers.name: Dead Tree Hollow
markers.textHtml: Dead Tree Hollow is a small valley that contains a number of ponds and wooded areas, and a small and sleepy village. For many years, it was the site of an ancient and gnarled oak tree, so huge that its hollow trunk was as wide as a regular cottage. A druid once lived within, until a dragon burned the tree and forced him out. The location subsequently developed into the village known as Dead Tree Hollow by the late 14th century. A long time ago, the flying carriage of Lady Alauthshaee was attacked by Ilthuryn, her scorned suitor, with his spells. It supposedly crashed to earth within Dead Tree Hollow and Alauthshaee and her guards were buried alive. A side-effect of Ilthuryn's magic was to render the entire valley a dead-magic zone.

markers.name: Deadsnows
markers.textHtml: Deadsnows is a large village made of wooden buildings encircled by a stone wall, nestled in the eponymous Deadsnows Vale. Just west of the town is the Icespear Stream and beyond them, to the northwest, are the upland pastures, an area of the valley used to herd livestock. Starting in 1372, thanks to a gold rush, the village experienced a great boom, with throngs of visitors and travelers, until the 1385 DR when it was destroyed in the Spellplague. By the end of the 15th century DR, the town is active again, but lacks leadership after Lord Delvon Icespear was killed without an heir during the War of the Silver Marches.

markers.name: Doncastle
markers.textHtml: Doncastle is a small city founded by bandits and outlaws in Dernall Forest in the kingdom of Callidyrr. The village's structures are concealed within the trees of a swampy, forested valley. Some buildings are underground and disguised in mounds, others sit within the treetops or around tree trunks, and some sit atop stilts above the mud. Inhabitants move from tree to tree either by canoe or by scampering along vines and walkways above the ground, and the town is bristling with defenses such as ditches, barriers, and lines of fire for archers. Doncastle was founded in the early 14th century DR by Hugh O'Roarke, who became an outlaw after rejecting High King Reginald Carrathal's claim as the rightful ruler of the Ffolk. It was destroyed in 1346 by the forces of the evil wizard Cyndre. The town was rebuilt during Tristain's reign as High King. It had grown to the size of a small city as of the late 15th century, when the Dernall Forest had become treacherous owing to the presence of unseelie fey from Nachtur.

markers.name: Doomspire
markers.textHtml: The fortified village of Doomspire is home to the hobgoblins of the Red Flayers tribe, who for generations have been servants of the blue dragon clan called the Blood of Morueme. The purpose of the settlement is to guard Morueme's Cave, the dragons’ lair under Dragondoom Mountain.

markers.name: Dragon Falls
markers.textHtml: The small village of Dragon Falls sits upon a crag over a thundering waterfall, the only major cataract on the Fire River. However, the Dragon Falls themselves are actually a spring that cascades over a bluff alongside the road and join the Fire River. A ferry, pulled across the river on ropes, is operated as a service to merchant caravans. Local legend says the falls were once home to a red dragon, Halarglautha Firewings. His reign of terror ended when she was slain by a band of human adventurers. They took possession of the dragon's hoard of coins, but instead of carrying the loot away, they stayed, constructed a stronghold, and settled there while a village grew around them. By 1370 DR, their great-grandchildren make up most of the population of the village of Dragon Falls and the treasure has all been spent.

markers.name: Dunbridges
markers.textHtml: [not Dunbridge] The village of Dunbridges is located in the middle of small hills covered in verdant pastures. The village is build around several water mills and this results in the village being saturated with millponds and springs. The village is known for its many bridges. Dunbridges offers several trade goods for export: Dunbradar cheese made from goat's milk and nuts; fish from the millponds and streams within the village; and high-quality woolen goods, especially the unique blankets weaved in the village.

markers.name: Dungrunglung
markers.textHtml: Dungrunglung is a settlement of grungs surrounded by a tall maze of magical, twisted vines. The interior consists of a large pond with narrow edges dotted with tents. In the center of the lake there is a giant shrine dedicated to the goddess Nangnang.

markers.name: Dynnegall
markers.textHtml: The village's location on the swampy Lac Dynnegall and near the Breasal Marsh provides it with an excellent source of peat, valued for heating, and of kryne, a nutritious plant. As of the late 15th century DR, soldiers stationed in the port of Caer Moray by High King Derid Kendrick of Callidyrr had the responsibility of making sure trade flowed to and from Dynnegall. In the years that followed, the Black Blood tribe overran the village, and intended to sacrifice its inhabitants at the nearby moonwell to summon Kazgoroth.

markers.name: Eagle Peak
markers.textHtml: Eagle Peak is a human hamlet built on a large outcrop, with a population of ca. 150 in the mid-14th century. It is a popular caravan stop.

markers.name: Edive
markers.textHtml: The folk from the small village of Edive perform lake fishing and farm root vegetables, and sell the surplus to travelers and nearby military sites such as Citadel Amnur and Citadel Rashturl. Its only notable feature is its mysterious glowing bell tower.

markers.name: Elmwood
markers.textHtml: Elmwood is a small farming community founded in 163 DR as a stopover for ships to and from the River Lis. The village is home to woodcutters and fishermen. South of the Still Waters, near the southeastern edge of the village, lays an ancient stone well, dried long time ago, that leads to an underground, monster infested area. Elmwood was accidentally destroyed in 1491 DR when a devastation orb intended to be set off in Mulmaster prematurely detonated there, leaving only a single survivor to report the tragedy.

markers.name: Elupar
markers.textHtml: Elupar is a small, sleepy fishing village. The people of Elupar are afraid of the lizardfolk from the nearby city of Surkh, even though this wariness is irrational and fueled by nothing but stories. Like other city-states of the Vilhon Reach, Elupar celebrates the independence from Chondath every year with a wild, joyful celebration during the festival of the Rotting Dance.

markers.name: Elventree
markers.textHtml: Elventree is a modest sylvan community largely comprising nonhuman refugees from Hillsfar's Great Law of Humanity (passed in the 14th century and banning non-humans from the city). It became a haven for elves, half-elves, rangers, artists, druids, and later an outpost for the Harpers. Elventree has been built up with wood on ruins of stone buildings. There are also a number of structures in the branches of trees, which are linked by rope ladders, walkways, and bridges. Elventree is home to a number of fine bowyers and fletchers, as well as wood carvers. Elventree also produces herbs, medicinal plants, exotic fruit, and tapestries.

markers.name: Fallentree
markers.textHtml: Fallentree is a village lying by the side of the road where Feldar's Trail meets Blaern's Trail. The lands around are rolling hills with small woods of dark pines. The village is known for its horse farms. The area was once plagued by bandits, who lurked in the pine woods and ambushed travelers, sometimes by felling trees on the road, hence the name of the place. A few of these old bandit gangs left their buried treasures in the area, and small stashes of loot are regularly found at the foots of old pine trees. Legend has it that, beneath one of the cottages, is a cavern home to an ancient temple of Garagos, Master of All Weapons.

markers.name: Farview
markers.textHtml: This small village was destroyed by the lycanthropes of the Red Shadow in the early 15th century DR, then liberated by Northlander followers of the Storm Maiden in the late 15th century. By the turn of the 16th century DR however, the inhabitants of Farview have succumbed to the magical disease called the Rusting. They work tirelessly to extract resources from the nearby land, from felling the Shannyth Forest to dredging the Breasal Marsh to mining the Trollclaw Range. They use the fruits of these labors merely to build more tools for more resource extraction. As of 1501 DR, efforts by High King Derid Kendrick to dislodged these rusted workers have gone nowhere.

markers.name: Feather Falls
markers.textHtml: Feather Falls is one of the largest settlements in Featherdale, located at the base of the waterfall after which it was named. The village serves as the primary dock for boats that navigate down the River Ashaba. A portage path winds alongside the falls, and individuals earn a living there transporting cargo. The most prominent landmark found in the village is Cholandrothipe's Tower, a fire-blackened ruin that was home of the wizard Cholandrothipe. The tower is guarded by deadly magical traps and was ruined by its own defenses when a group of mages from Thay killed the wizard and attempted to break into the place. Behind the waterfall lies a network of caves called the Temple behind the Falls, that has an evil reputation although no one is sure what entity was once worshiped here.

markers.name: Fendarl's Gate
markers.textHtml: Fendarl's Gate is a village of shepherds in the Sunset Vale, on the northern bank of the Chionthar, in a bight where it makes a sharp curve. The trade of Fendarl's Gate is in wool and mutton, so it is mostly visited by merchants and dedicated diners. Fendarl's Gate was founded by and named for a retired warrior called Fendarl. His descendants would continue to lead the village, including Eldebuck Thorm Fendarl who reigned by the 1360s DR. He boasted himself as the 'High Knight-Emperor of the Vale' and occupied the small castle, which he called 'the Imperial Palace'.

markers.name: Findar
markers.textHtml: Findar is a settlement in the Fang, a large promontory that was settled in the 11th century DR by humans refugees of Velprin defeated by the half-elves of the Yuirwood. The inhabitants of the Fang rule themselves, but are still considered Aglarondan citizens. Findar is situated on the coast on the west side of the Fang, beneath the cliffs, and the sandy ground makes it difficult to grow crops. Findar's occupants make their living from the sea instead. They are hostile towards any non-humans.

markers.name: Fireshear
markers.textHtml: A remote mining city populated by humans and dwarves, comprised of buildings dug out of the rock and made of stone. They resemble igloos, which allows them to tolerate the bitter winds and heavy snows on the coast without collapse. Many of the homes have deep cellars for storage of food for the harsh and isolating winters. The city sits above cliffs carved with ramps that allow access to a shallow bay. However, from the late fall through early summer, the city's stone docks are unusable as the bay freezes over. The city is dependent on imports of food and most necessities. Its location was chosen because of the rich copper and silver veins in the area that were exposed by a violent event, sometimes thought to be a volcanic eruption but in fact was a cataclysmic meteor impact in the distant past. The mines are located in a rift that ends in the remnants of the impact crater. The walls of the bowl crater are blackened by fire, giving the city its name.

markers.name: Friendly Arm Inn
markers.textHtml: The most prominent building around this walled hamlet is the stone keep which houses the titular inn. This keep was once the stronghold of an evil cleric of Bhaal, but adventurers cleaned out the keep and renovated it in the 14th century DR. The inn is a popular stop for people traveling on the Coast Road, and a true safe haven for all those that stay within its walls.

markers.name: Gillian’s Hill
markers.textHtml: Gillian's Hill is the smallest of the four hamlets that lay on the Trade Way that stretches to the south of Daggerford. It is located on a mount of grass and dirt with a small tower fire cairn on the hill's summit. It is used as a signal tower to warn Daggerford of approaching armies from the Dragonspear Castle or Serpent Hills. The main threat to Gillian's Hill, apart from Trade Way's bandits, are regular raids of lizardfolk of Lizard Marsh. Gillian's Hill is a center for the many farmers from adjacent communities to get together, exchange news and gossips, and trade goods. All local produce travel through the hamlet, the usual trade including Churlgo Cheese, corn, grains, flour, fruits, vegetables, and cider. The village has its own old boogeyman legend. The creature known as Gothma is said to be a giant vampire bat who lives in the crypt underneath the town's graveyard.

markers.name: Glaun
markers.textHtml: The village of Glaun lies close to the fetid Glaun Bog. The locals know how to avoid the worst of its dangers, and they venture in to gather iron for smelting and peat for burning.

markers.name: Gnarhelm
markers.textHtml: Gnarhelm is the capital city of the kingdom bearing the same name. The city is situated on the rolling plains of grass and clover that dominate much of the land just east of Salmon Bay. This region is the only major agricultural center in Gnarhelm. The port is one of the main stopovers for maritime trade among the Northlander kingdoms of the Moonshaes, as well as for some trade north to Ruathym and Waterdeep.

markers.name: Golden Cove
markers.textHtml: Golden Cove is a series of caverns and fjords that serves as the base of pirate crews and their ships. It is shared with Clan Thump, a matriarchal ogre clan.

markers.name: Goldenfields
markers.textHtml: Goldenfields, nicknamed the Granary of the North, is a walled abbey to Chauntea of spectacular scope and size. It is the most prolific provider of crops, grains and fruits to the people of the North. The walled temple-complex features a small town of several dozen longhouses, an abbey, an inn, a brewery and the vast farms, orchards and gardens for which the place is famous. Goldenfields provides much of the food consumed in Waterdeep and the neighboring settlements, but does not seek payment in return. They donate this food to others in honor of Chauntea.

markers.name: Graevelwood
markers.textHtml: Graevelwood is an isolated gnomish clanhold located underneath a rocky crag in the eastern reaches of the Arn Forest. The settlement does little trading with Deadsnows and Citadel Adbar, but always with as much secrecy as possible. The traders are always careful to cover up their tracks so their path could not be followed back to the clan. Many of the folk living in Graevelwood are known for their skill in trapping.

markers.name: Greystaad
markers.textHtml: [not Graystaad]

markers.name: Greatgaunt
markers.textHtml: A fortified town founded by the Greatgaunt noble house of Cormyr.

markers.name: Griffon's Nest
markers.textHtml: Griffon's Nest is a permanent settlement of the Griffon tribe of Uthgardt barbarians. It is made of wood-and-earth huts with thatched roofs that encircle the tribe's central longhouse and two warehouses. These structures are surrounded by a palisade of sturdy logs, often decorated with the heads of the tribes enemies. Griffon's Nest was once as big as a small city, but after the death of its visionary leader, Chief Kralgar Bonesnapper, the settlement steadily declined, turned away from contact with outsiders, and was reduced to a hamlet of about three hundred people by 1485 DR.

markers.name: Grunwald
markers.textHtml: Grunwald is a small, yet civilized Uthgardt village, and the headquarters of the Thunderbeast tribe of Uthgardt people. Compared to other Uthgardt tribes, the people of Grunwald were fairly welcoming to outsiders, though they did not appreciate loiterers. Grunwald was built upon the ruins of a dwarfhold, which was referred to by the dwarven name Thornhammer. The homes in Grunwald are family halls, long and ovaloid mounds, said to resemble burial mounds overgrown with bushes and scrub. They are constructed of heaped stone, topped with timber roofs spread by mud, which often have grass and moss growing atop them. The stones of these houses are sourced from the above-ground ruins of the ancient dwarfhold, while their timber is harvested from the surrounding forest. The primary product of Grunwald is timber from the Lurkwood, which is primarily traded in the wintertime with Mirabar and Longsaddle.

markers.name: Guidodale
markers.textHtml: The village of Guidodale is one of several overnight stops for caravans that travel along the Merchant's Run, part of the Herald's Road that stretches between Impiltur and the Cold Lands. It is largely populated by Vaasans and Damaran folk, and receives protection from Ilmwatch's garrison contingents.

markers.name: Gullykin
markers.textHtml: Gullykin is a small Halfling settlement laying hard up against the overgrown, fallow, rubble field that was once the elven town of Firewine Bridge. The citizens of Gullykin do not explore the ruins of Firewine Bridge as they believe that doing so invites the misfortune of 'those who died and do not sleep'.

markers.name: Gundbarg
markers.textHtml: Gundbarg, called the 'Gateway Port', is the biggest settlement on all the islands of the northern Trackless Sea, with a population of 12,000. Its economy is based entirely on fishing, farming, mining, and trading. Almost every ship to cross the Trackless Sea stops at Gundbarg to take on fresh water and food, make repairs, replace crew, or exchange goods. The city provides all the services required by these seafarers.

markers.name: Halendos
markers.textHtml: Halendos is a small rustic village along a meadow at the edge of the northern Yuirwood. It was built on the site of an old human city that was burned to the ground during the war between humans and the half-elven descendants of the Yuir. The buildings in Halendos are made of wood, all painted in bright forest colors. Each of the houses has its own garden and many have sod roofs from which flowers and vines grow. Most of the inhabitants are farmers and herders. They are largely human, with some half-elves. All are strongly influenced by the Yuir half-elves and the mystical, fey nature of the area in which they live. Many have inherent magical abilities or are subjected to prophetic dreams, and a higher proportion of children grow up to be wizards. Overall there is a laid back atmosphere with care for the local environment.

markers.name: Hammerstaad
markers.textHtml: Hammerstaad is the Northlander capital of Norheim, and a major port in the maritime trade of the Northlanders. The city is located on a natural bay. A ridge of mountains comes right up to the sea at the site of the city, save for a small strip of land where Northlanders beach their longships to come ashore. Hammerstaad is the seat of power for the king of Norheim. The city is protected by a nearby fortress where the king and his family live, which is a wooden lodge built around an old fortified tower of Ffolk construction.

markers.name: Hap
markers.textHtml: Hap is a small village at the base of the former volcano, Haptooth Hill. It is a minor, peaceful settlement inhabited in large part by woodcutters and herb-growers. Despite its serene nature, it does attract many adventurers looking to explore the ruins and tunnel system under Haptooth Hill. On the top of the hill is the Tower of Dracandros, a keep formerly inhabited by the red wizard Dracandros and his drow minions until he was overthrown by adventurers in the mid-14th century DR.

markers.name: Helmsdale
markers.textHtml: Helmsdale is a small farming town found in the Duchy of Soravia, producing food in support of the dwarves of Ironspur. Zhengyi the Witch-King used Helmsdale as his base of operations in the mid-14th century during the Vaasan War and the Bloodstone Wars, with his armies being fully expelled before the end of 1359 DR, with the help of forces from Ironspur.

markers.name: Hidden Vale
markers.textHtml: The Hidden Vale is a secluded valley, remarkable for the vast varieties of species of wildflowers that bloom in the summer and fall, and the swirling fogs that conceal some of the valley's features. The valley serves as home to a population of gnomes, whose homes are hidden and tucked away among various features of the vale's sheer cliff walls. Although they share their lives with the rest of society within High Dale, the gnomes typically ignored life as it occurred outside the region. However, after the deity Gond manifested as a gnome on Toril in 1358 DR, many of the gnomish youth were inspired to cast aside a life of comfort and seek out lives of adventure.

markers.name: High Haspur
markers.textHtml: The village of High Haspur sits upon a series of low bluffs looking over the High Trail. This commanding view gives the villagers a good opportunity to defend against the regular orc and goblinoid raids. High Haspur was ruled for a number of generations by the Morninglight family, a gnome clan who also runs The Elf in Armor inn there. High Haspur is reportedly a wealthy place, as the Morninglights work at mining and smelting gold. They also create numerous mechanical inventions, designed to defend caves and tunnels against attackers. The famed violinist and bard Nikolai Tallow hailed from High Haspur, and local legends claim that on nights when mists envelop the land, his ghost plays his phantom violin with the shadows of the dead dancing to the melody in the light of the moon.

markers.name: High Hold
markers.textHtml: High Hold is one of the many small towns and villages found in the Rauvin Vale, within the Silver Marches. It is named for the foreland it is set upon, that looks over the Evermoors from the opposite side of the River Rauvin. The town falls under the authority and governance of Silverymoon.

markers.name: Highbank Forest
markers.textHtml: A sleepy village of cottages amongst the woodlands, Highbank Forest is most known for its talented wood-carvers. They fashion furniture and toys of extraordinary quality and intricate design that are famous across the Realms. Clever merchants snap these up and sell them to the rich and noble across Faerûn. A ruined keep stands atop a steeply sloped and wooded hill that lays just to the north of the town. The villagers do not know that it was once home to a school of wizardry; instead, they remember it as the base of Lord Wolf, a local bandit lord. Amidst the ruins is a shaft descending into the underground dungeon levels. The villagers toss their rubbish into the pit. However, every one of them, even the children, knows well not to go down the hole. Those who have never returned, bar their screams.

markers.name: Highcastle
markers.textHtml: The largest village in High Dale, Highcastle, has less than a thousand inhabitants. Homes and workshops cluster around a castle that gives the village its name. The High Fire Tavern is usually the only refuge in town for winter travelers. High Constable Coppercroft lives in the castle with his family and a small retinue of servants and aides.

markers.name: Hoarbridge
markers.textHtml: Hoarbridge is a waymeet, which is the name given to temporary villages of tents that crop up at large campgrounds found in Narfell. The Nar tribes share these sites freely between themselves and even with outsiders. Given their transitory nature, the population of a waymeet is highly variable. The Far Quey tribe lives north of Hoarbridge on the other side of the Icelace Lake, and Hoarbridge is the nearest trading place that the tribe can reach. Hoarbridge is also the end destination of the Cold Road. It owed its location and early importance to the ancient dwarven kingdom of Dareth, as the metal wealth of the dwarves attracted many men, until Dareth was overtaken by white dragons in –343 DR.

markers.name: Holgerstead
markers.textHtml: Known for its berserker warriors. Until Aumark Lithyl united the tribes, Holgerstead was ruled by First Axe Wedigar Ruthmaald.

markers.name: Horst
markers.textHtml: The village was located along the Shannyth River on the edge of the Breasal Marsh. A simple, single-span bridge leads to a road heading deeper into the marsh. The town is surrounded by irrigated farmland. By the late 15th century DR, the village was abandoned and lay in ruins, the surrounding farmland was flooded and overgrown and the buildings and gateposts of the town were collapsed. However, the bridge, the cobblestone streets, and the causeway leading into the marsh were still intact and usable.

markers.name: Huddagh
markers.textHtml: Huddagh is a settlement that lays along the Way of the Manticore. It is well known for its unique purple mineral water wells that seemingly never fail. The water is used by several local churches in creation of Holy Water. Among other trades, Huddagh is home of many renowned jewelers and potters.

markers.name: Hundelstone
markers.textHtml: A trade town of gnomes, dwarves, and humans located along the Ten Trail. Most of the dwarves and gnomes are miners, while most of the humans serve as monster slayers, guides and guards to the caravans, or hunters (namely of wolves, raptors, crag cats, and the ever-plentiful rock hares). The town's buildings are built low to the ground, with the majority of living space consisting of cellar rooms cut into the rock.

markers.name: Ideepton
markers.textHtml: The small village of Ideepton produces fish and root vegetables. The locals keep the crops and sell the excess to marching military or inhabitants of surrounding citadels. The settlement is rarely visited by travelers, and its most notable feature are its blue glowing waters, sacred to the followers and clergy of the church of Eldath.

markers.name: Ilinvur
markers.textHtml: Ilinvur, also spelled Ilinver, is a mining settlement that spent much of its existence as a client to powers on the Moonsea, including Melvaunt, Thentia, and Phlan. Ilinvur's main export is metal mined from the West Galena Mountains, usually traded for food. The settlement is also a crossroads and stopover for adventurers and merchants. As of 1480 DR, the town is a small collection of two-story bungalows spread out around the town square where most shops and merchants are. The town comprises only 250 residents—mostly human, with several dozen dwarves. The village is under the protection of Phlan and is governed by a diarchy. In the spring of 1480 DR, the town and the surrounding country underwent waves of attacks from barbarians and elementals originating out of the Tortured Lands. Much of the village was burned down and effectively abandoned.

markers.name: Ilyaport
markers.textHtml: Ilyaport was originally a port town built around a temple to Umberlee known as the Shell House, home to the Green Priests Umberlant order. The settlement flourished, becoming quite a grand town. However, during the Rage of Wizards in 1142 DR, when rival wizards and yuan-ti engaged in wildly destructive spell-battles up and down the Tashtan Coast, the Shell House was destroyed. Without the temple, Ilyaport's prosperity ebbed in the shadow of the city of Ithmong. It was just a simple fishing village by the mid–14th century DR.

markers.name: Irl
markers.textHtml: The village of Irl lays just west of the River Rith, due north of The Realm of the Mount. The village's main claim to fame is in the form of jewlers and their gems. Governmental rulings are made by the Eight-sided Stone, a covert council whose members are the elders of the most powerful Irlian families. The jewelers of Irl are secretive and reclusive, and reputed to be among the richest and most powerful humans in all Faerûn.

markers.name: Jahaka Anchorage
markers.textHtml: Jahaka Anchorage is a port settlement located in a secluded sea cave in the Jahaka Bay. It serves as the secret anchorage for a group of pirates that raid ships in the waters surrounding Chult. Any ship nearing the anchorage has to navigate through heavy mist and a perilous circle of underwater rocks.

markers.name: Jalanthar
markers.textHtml: Most of the inhabitants of this hamlet are trappers and hunters, and also hire themselves out as guides. The buildings are half-buried in the ground and look like green hills. Many of the new buildings are built directly into the hillside and are covered with turf to resist burning. Additionally, they have strong doorways to resist battering. Most of the inhabitants of Jalanthar know the surrounding area and many of them have had experienced clashes with orcs and other raiders.

markers.name: Jiyyd
markers.textHtml: Jiyyd was originally a waymeet, which is the name given to temporary villages of tents that crop up at large campgrounds found in Narfell. However, by the late 15th century DR, it has grown into a permanent village of hide tends and stone huts. It is known as the first civilized stop along the Long Road road in Narfell for travelers from Damara, and its position as a crossroads between the Long Road and Peltarch makes it a major site of trade and barter between the Nars and foreigners. The occasional goblin tribe will also come to Jiyyd for trade.

markers.name: Julkoun
markers.textHtml: This pastoral hamlet is home to about forty households of stout farmers. The hamlet is filled with small gardens surrounded by low stone walls and hedgerows. The streets are grassy and often filled with grazing goats and sheep.

markers.name: Kalathtyr
markers.textHtml: Kalathtyr is a fishing village at the mouth of the River Specie, consisting of a small collection of huts raised on stilts. Kalathtyr is permeated with the smell of dampness and mold and surrounded by mud, sea-chills, and ever-present swarms of nasty stingflies and bite-gnats. The village's main industry is fishing, and the locals catch abundant silverfins and burdolins. When hauled to shore, silverfins are turned into saltfish, pickled for export to be cooked into fishpaste by purchasers or smoked and made into smoked fish tarts. The village's abundant seabirds are shot down by sling-wielding children and used in some of the most common dishes. The lands around Kalathtyr—the Zehoarast Floodplains—are treacherous, hiding areas of deadly mud. Kalath people know their way around the most dangerous mud pits of the floodplains and navigate the mud and River Specie on skiffs.

markers.name: Kheldell
markers.textHtml: Kheldell is a small village of log homes. The local economy is centered around logging and the horse-powered sawmill. Loggers only cut the trees that local druids, the Dusk Circle, allow them to fell, and in turn, plant more trees than they take.

markers.name: Kholmeth
markers.textHtml: This shepherding village is located within Impresk, a barony of the monarchy of Erlkazar, which is responsible for ensuring that the monster population of the Snowflake Mountains remains low and defending the trade road to Riatavin. Kholmeth is the closest settlement to the baron's hunting lodge in the central Snowflakes.

markers.name: Kagarra
markers.textHtml: [not Kagarr] Nagarr, sometimes marked as Kagarra on maps, also named Yacla around the 9th century DR, was originally a simple farming-fishing village. But by 1370 DR with the rise of Janol I of Ankhapur, major reconstruction and increase in population as a trade transit point gradually expanded it to accommodate trade. In the late 14th century DR, it was a fortified village and a protectorate of the city of Ankhapur. The most common stories told in the inns and taverns of Nagarr all involved the monstrous and mysterious naga. The locals had no idea that separate species of good, evil and neutral naga all lived in the area, and the consensus was the only good naga was a dead naga.

markers.name: Kond
markers.textHtml: The community of Kond is carved into the steep cliffs along the southern side of the Clefts of Razack above the Beaumaris River. It was originally a delve for minotaurs, but they were routed in 1453 DR by the Warlock Knights of Vaasa. As of the late 15th century DR, it is a waypoint along the Dead Man's Walk, which scales up from the canyon floor to the west to the top of the cliffs to the east. The folk use an ingenious system of winches and pulleys, along with magic from some warlocks, to get the goods up the 500-foot (150 m) cliff face to the wagons waiting at the rim.

markers.name: Kork
markers.textHtml: The inhabitants of Kork were wiped out in the late 15th century during a raid by the savage Black Blood Tribe. Following the attack, Kork was eventually repopulated by mercenaries and settlers from Amn. The Amnian settlers constructed a road leading to Moray's interior to gain better access to the valuable peat and kryne found there. By the early 16th century DR however, Moonshavians had banded together to drive out the Amnians.

markers.name: Kormul
markers.textHtml: Sitting at the crossroads of three major roads, Kormul is a bustling trading center. Once a village, it has grown by the late 15th century DR and is known as the City of Cults. Most of the trade done in the city is in salt and spices, but drugs and poisons are also major trade goods. Members of all of the humanoid species can be found in Kormul, with the majority of them being human, but with significant minorities of other peoples. Loxo and wemics make up the majority of the daytime traders, while humans make up the majority of the gangs, who fight each other in the streets. Yuan-ti spend their days basking in the sun before setting out to trade in heated buildings by night. Drow venture up to the city each night from their Underdark bases as well. These two groups hold the most power in the city.

markers.name: Kythyss
markers.textHtml: Kythyss is a small coastal city in the kingdom of Callidyrr. Historically the city was ruled by the High King or Queen from Caer Callidyrr, but as of the late 15th century DR, it had come under the thumb of ruthless mercenaries led by Ghelvan 'the Buzzard', who were in turn loyal to Amn. The mercenaries were infiltrated from the nearby island of Snowdown, then under Amnian occupation. Ghelvan's lieutenants dictated the city's policies while Ghelvan himself kept the people fearful and oppressed with violence and bloodshed. By the early 16th century DR however, Moonshavians had banded together to drive out the Amnians.

markers.name: Lathtarl's Lantern
markers.textHtml: Lathtarl's Lantern is a small fishing village on a rocky stretch of the Sword Coast. It was named after a pirate-turned-wrecker who was active here in the 11th century DR. It is known as a lawless place and was famous in the 14th century for its busy and wild inn, the Wailing Wave.

markers.name: Liam's Hold
markers.textHtml: Liam's Hold is a friendly little settlement standing on the eastern side of the Trade Way. Its most notable feature is the two-acre-wide flat-topped tor of rock that overlooks the area. The village is crammed on the tor's top and surrounded by walls. Liam's Hold's economy is supported by countless caravans that move along the Trade Way and is home to crafters and artisans who sell their goods to traveling merchants and travel to the city of Baldur's Gate to fetch better prices. The hamlet was named for the mighty warrior and wizard Sunder Halyndliam, whose crypt is located under the hamlet's tower. The inhabitants claim that plundering the old dead wizard's crypt would result in the tower's collapse and hamlet's destruction as the magic of the crypt was the only thing that held the structure together. However, that is likely just an excuse to keep the crypt under the Holdfast Inn undisturbed.

markers.name: Llandrain
markers.textHtml: [not Llandrian] As of the late 15th century DR, the town of Llandrain was tightly controlled by Amn and was home to many miner operations which extracted valuable metals from the nearby Andover Heights. Dwarven mercenaries hired by Amn resided in the town to ensure that mineral resources were mined and protected efficiently. At the same time, Llandrain was home to the largest population of Ffolk patriots loyal to House Kendrick who despised Snowdown's Amnian occupiers. By the early 16th century DR however, Moonshavians had banded together to drive out the Amnians.

markers.name: Llewellyn
markers.textHtml: Cantrev Llewellyn is a small coastal city located in the kingdom of Callidyrr, relying on the rich fishing in Llewellyn Harbor, and clashing over rights to the waters with Kythyss to the south. It is a plain and prototypical Ffolk settlement, with several hundred cottages that have walls plastered and painted white. The area around the docks is a large grassy park surrounded by shops, alehouses, and merchants selling fruits and meats. A stone tower located about 1 mile (1,5 km) inland from the shore provides a degree of protection against raiding Northlanders. A large community of halflings resides within the town and has additionally excavated burrows in the hills near this defensive tower.

markers.name: Llorkh
markers.textHtml: Once a large and important mining town, Llorkh dwindled and was finally annexed by the Zhentarim in the mid–14th century DR, becoming the westernmost outpost for their caravans and efforts to dominate trade in the North. After a few decades of Zhent occupation, the veins of gold and silver in Llorkh's mines played out. The occupiers left, leaving Llorkh's buildings in a state of ruin. The town suffered a dramatic decline in the 1470s DR. By 1479 DR, it lay in ruins and served as a stronghold for a self-styled bandit king and his henchmen. The bandit king was eventually killed and people started to return to Llorkh in an attempt to restore it. It was then that a clan of stone giants, in alliance with the Uthgardt Blue Bear Tribe, smashed the town into rubble.

markers.name: Macsheehan
markers.textHtml: Canterv MacSheehan is a small Ffolk town located in the kingdom of Corwell just north of the Corwell Road. The settlement sits in the foothills of the hidden elven kingdom of Synnoria along the southern end of the Cambro Mountains. The town was thoroughly destroyed and its inhabitants displaced during the Darkwalker War of 1345 DR, and rebuilt after the war. Following the rise of Sarifal, MacSheehan was abandoned and remained so until the late 15th century DR. It has been rebuilt and reinhabited as of 1501 DR, after the lands of Corwell were resettled under the leadership of Tarilyn Kendrick following Sarifal's retreat into the Winterglen.

markers.name: Maerduuth
markers.textHtml: Once a large city, Maerduuth was destroyed during a war with Akanax in the early 14th century DR and reduced to a small trading village thereafter.

markers.name: Malauth
markers.textHtml: Malauth is known as the 'most sinister' goblin realm in the Rauvin Mountains. It is located in the central southern foothills of the mountains.

markers.name: Maracrath
markers.textHtml: The village of Maracrath is one of several overnight stops for caravans that travel along the Merchant's Run, part of the Herald's Road that stretches between Impiltur and the Cold Lands. It receives protection from Ilmwatch's garrison contingents.

markers.name: Maskyr's Eye
markers.textHtml: Maskyr's Eye, or simply the Eye, is the name given to a misty wooden vale and the settlement within. The place was named for the archmage Maskyr One-Eye, who, in the 7th century DR, gave his right eye to the dwarven king Tuir Stonebeard in payment for the vale. Over the centuries, a village grew around the mage's abandoned tower. An overgrown and idyllic little place, it is a small community, consisting of twenty main buildings, mainly cottages and barns built of timber and stone with thatch roofs. Much of the stone used in the walls was once part of Maskyr's old tower. The primary trades are farming and the breeding and training of horses, particularly a rugged breed of horse, popular all over the Vast, called the 'blackhair'. Two or three times a year, dwarves journey out of the Earthspur mountains and stay in Maskyr's Eye to trade for four days, purposefully giving no warning of their coming. Hearing the news, merchants from Mulmaster rush to the village to trade their goods for fine dwarven metalworks.

markers.name: Minsor Vale
markers.textHtml: Minsor Vale was originally a Selûnite temple-town and the capital of the theocratic realm of Minsorran, founded in 300 DR in the shadow of the Shoon Imperium. The cities of Minsorran were depopulated in 600 DR during a harsh midwinter blizzard when their people disappeared without any apparent explanation. All four cities fell to ruin, with Minsor Vale eventually gaining a new population after centuries, reaching a quarter of its former high. The new Minsor Vale is a fishing village surrounded by the ruins of the former capital, notably an abandoned seven-story temple to Selûne that looms over the center of the village. Minsor Vale supports itself with fishing and the farming of root vegetables. The Alandor River is an ideal barge route that doesn't freeze in winter, with light water traffic from Minsor Vale around Lake Weng and down to Crimmor and Athkatla.

markers.name: Mishtan
markers.textHtml: Mishtan is one of many villages within the Great Vale of Mulhorand, and is one of the nation's sixteen preceptual governments. It stands out from others due to being one of the centers of Osiris worship in Mulhorand and because it is the gateway to the Land of the Dead—the resting place of Mulhorand's pharaohs and their families. Tombs for the pharaohs and other construction projects are a never-ending occurrence in Mishtan, as the Osiran clergy are in charge of the Land of the Dead. During the flood season, the village is typically teeming with artisans, masons, and slaves.

markers.name: Moortown
markers.textHtml: Moortown is a small settlement encircled by an eroded earthen and stone wall built on the remains of an earlier palisade. It was meant as a stopover for merchants along Dead Man's Walk, the main road passing through Vaasa from east to west. Moortown was founded in the 13th century DR and repeatedly ruined and resettled in the following centuries. In the late 15th century, the Warlock Knights of Vaasa are in control of the town, but the Cult of the Dragon (who had been operating in and around the town for over a century) is rumored to still be active in the area.

markers.name: Mornbryn's Shield
markers.textHtml: Mornbryn's Shield, named for the ranger Mornbryn, is a village at the confluence of the River Surbrin and the Laughingflow. It sits on a natural rampart that encloses the west and south sides of the settlement, protecting it from the spring floods of the rivers. Mornbryn's Shield is home to hardy shepherds and fishers who endure the dangers and weather of the Evermoors – bone-chilling humid winds, damp fogs, and hungry trolls lurking hidden from view. The main export for the village is Shield moss, cultivated and grown by the folk of the hamlet. It possesses medicinal properties, is used as a secret ingredient by perfume makers of Amn and Waterdeep, and is consumed as a fashionable dish among the nobility of Calimshan, Waterdeep, and Tethyr.

markers.name: Mouth o' Gargoyles
markers.textHtml: The village's name originates with a long-dead wizard who lived in a cave on the site, and used gargoyles to launch raids against river cargoes and travelers. Eventually, other wizards got rid of the winged terrors and their master. The magical battle destroyed the cave and caused an area of wild magic to manifest where the village later sat. This eldritch curse made the entire place extremely unpredictable for the use of magic, leading to a permanent ban on the use of magic within the village by order of the Crown. One particular professional trade engaged in by the villagers is the production of oils: oilmakers known as 'lighters' pump natural oil from deep fissures near the site of the destroyed cave. Once in a while a fireball or a jet of flame will burst from the earth and hurl the lighter into the air. One type of oil produced by the lighters is amberglow, used as a lubricant and to prevent blades from rusting.

markers.name: Mulltown
markers.textHtml: The small village of Mulltown is the first Impilturian stop along the Merchant's Run headed south from Damara, and is ideally located to provide commercial support for the larger city-states of Impiltur. Fine inns, skilled smithies and protection by soldiers from Ilmwatch help to cater to the many travelers who pass through.

markers.name: Myrrdale
markers.textHtml: The town sits among the eastern foothills of the Cambro Mountains near the hidden elven kingdom of Synnoria. It consists of a small village surrounded by farms and fields. As an inland cantrev, it is generally seen as being safe from raids by the seafaring Northmen. The town was utterly destroyed by the Bloodriders cavalry during the Darkwalker War of 1345 DR. The town would ultimately be abandoned following the rise of Sarifal, and was little more than a ruin as of the late 15th century DR. However, it has been rebuilt and reinhabited as of 1501 DR, after the lands of Corwell were resettled under the leadership of Tarilyn Kendrick following Sarifal's retreat into the Winterglen.

markers.name: N'Jast
markers.textHtml: As of the mid-to-late 14th century DR, N'Jast was little more than a waymeet, which is the name given to temporary villages of tents that crop up at large campgrounds found in Narfell, but it has become a permanent settlement by the late 15th century DR. The village sits at the crossroads of the Long Road and the Cold Road and is predominantly a trade outpost. Merchants on the Long Road come from Damara in the west and Rashemen in the east, while merchants on the Cold Road come from the settlements and dwarfholds of the Icerim Mountains to the north and Thesk to the south. It is also an important stopover for both merchants and Nars heading to the Bildoobaris trademeet.

markers.name: Nar-Sek Qu'istrade
markers.textHtml: p>Nar-sek Qu'istrade, also called Horseshoe Canyon, is a neutral meeting ground for the tribes of Narfell and a defensive location in times of foreign invasions. It is a circular canyon of five square miles (12 km2) of rolling grass, encapsulated by high cliff walls. The single entrance to the canyon is a crack wide enough for only two riders abreast. Protected from even the harshest weather, Nar-sek Qu'istrade is often used by various Nar tribes as they migrate by. On the worst winter days, it is normal to see several tribes sheltering together, even those who, on the open grassland, would be fierce rivals. Should a tribe be attacked by foreign invaders, they will attempt to make their way to Nar-sek Qu'istrade where they will light pre-prepared beacons, signalling to all Narfell that enemies were near.

markers.name: New Moranay
