markers.textHtml: New Moranay is a small town located in Agmoth county. It serves as an important way-stop for the local merchants as they transport ore down the hinterlands of the Earthfast Mountains south to the great coastal cities of Impiltur.

markers.name: Newfort
markers.textHtml: This small hamlet comprises about two score wood cottages built around a small stream, atop a small hill encircled by a wooden palisade. Newfort was founded in 1371 DR by two ex-members of the Zhentarim who'd retired in disgust and wished a new life. Because of this, the village attracted many people that fled the Moonsea following the rise to power of the Zhentarim. By the time of the War of the Silver Marches in the late 15th century DR, the village has become a way station welcoming any travelers to stay the night in safety.

markers.name: Noanar's Hold
markers.textHtml: Noanar's Hold is a village that grew up around one of the most famous hunting lodges in the North. Cottages and well-kept stables ramble between the trees. In the 14th century DR, the Hunt Lords ruled the settlement and arranged hunting parties for rich sportsmen of the Sword Coast. The village's hunts could involve anything from pursuing normal creatures to sentient beasts and humanoids that were less than willing participants. Eventually, less and less people came, as rumors got out that the Hunt Lords encouraged their guests to kill one another.

markers.name: Nobis
markers.textHtml: Nobis is a waymeet, which is the name given to temporary villages of tents that crop up at large campgrounds found in Narfell. The Nar tribes share these sites freely between themselves and even with outsiders. Given their transitory nature, the population of a waymeet is highly variable.

markers.name: Nordahaeril
markers.textHtml: An elven settlement home to about 100 sylvan elves by the 14th century DR.

markers.name: Nun
markers.textHtml: This village rests on the site of the Battle of Nun (part of the Rotting War conflict of 902 DR). Nun is often refereed to as a shrine rather than a village because the majority of its inhabitants are priests. Although Nun is filled with followers of numerous faiths, Tempus is the most prominent. There are also notable shrines to Silvanus and Tyr. Nun is a peaceful village and avoids getting involved in the politics of its region.

markers.name: Olostin's Hold
markers.textHtml: Olostin's Hold is a fortified keep, home to about 200 people and providing security for about 600 farmers and ranchers in the surrounding area. It is the only permanent human settlement in the High Forest. The locals are very careful to not irritate the forces of the forest. The woodcutters cut down dead trees only.

markers.name: Orlbar
markers.textHtml: Orlbar was once a simple, sleepy shepherding village. But in 1369 DR, it was taken over by the Zhentarim. The construction of a temple to Xvim—said to have been done with the help of devils, and reconsecrated to Bane when he was resurrected in 1372 DR—dashed the last hope of the people of Orlbar for freedom. But after 1485 DR, the village was abandoned, with its last inhabitants driven away by the stone giants of Deadstone Cleft. Utterly erasing the village, the giants leveled every building.

markers.name: Osker
markers.textHtml: Osker is a sleepy fishing settlement of 700 inhabitants. It is a charming village of steep wooden roofs and aging homes built around a dark-wood clocktower. This clocktower is a creation of the eccentric dwarf named Snorri Rockhammer in the early 14th century DR. The clock itself is a wondrous device of clockwork that rings every 15 minutes and daily, at midday, releases a parade of mechanical monsters.

markers.name: Oslen
markers.textHtml: [not Oslen] Oslin is a small trading village that stood as early as the 4th century DR. It was famous for the Magical Chessmen of Ultham-Urre, a set of chess pieces and magical artifacts created by the archmage Ultham of Chessenta in the 10th or early 11th century DR, and that the village used as a defense by creating a magical militia of its people. The Chessmen mysteriously disappeared in the late 10th or 11th century DR, along with half the population of the village, never to be seen again.

markers.name: Ostrav
markers.textHtml: Ostrav is a small farming village found in the grasslands of the Duchy of Arcata, known for its political neutrality, friendly inhabitants and fabulous inns.

markers.name: Parnast
markers.textHtml: Parnast is a small village comprising a central town square around which are several dozen simple structures, built within a wooden palisade. The wary and hardworking Parnasters earn their living as farmers, laborers, crafters, transporters, and loggers. The village was controlled by the Zhentarim in the second half of the 14th century, then suffered from a long period of decline, before coming under control of the Cult of the Dragon in 1480. The cultists were eventually driven out later that year, but the village was then besieged by the hill giant Bad Fruul and his horde of orcs and other monstrous creatures.

markers.name: Pontswain
markers.textHtml: Cantrev Pontswain is a small coastal town located at the end of Corwell Road in the kingdom of Corwell. The town sits along the southern coast of Corwell Firth near the edge of Kimball Moor. It was abandoned following the rise of Sarifal, and lay in ruins as of the late 15th century DR. However, it has been rebuilt and reinhabited as of 1501 DR, after the lands of Corwell were resettled under the leadership of Tarilyn Kendrick following Sarifal's retreat into the Winterglen.

markers.name: Port Llast
markers.textHtml: Port Llast, formerly Grath's Hold and Last Port, is a small town of low stone houses and narrow streets, mainly known for its skilled stonecutters and excellent harbor—a small bay sheltered by a high, rocky spit, with a fine beach and inlet. The town is closely allied to Neverwinter, but Luskan covets Port Llast, wishing a southern base for its warships. Most of the humans of Port Llast are fishers, but Port Llast's primary land-bound industry is cutting stone, with quarries situated on the coastal headlands just to the south. Port Llast has a violent history, having been assaulted and/or occupied by sahuagin, pirates, a drow raiding party, and frost giants, all in the second half of the 15th century DR. It was always retaken and rebuilt.

markers.name: Quaervarr
markers.textHtml: Quaervarr is a small woodland settlement that started out as a logging camp, but grew to be a small town that both tends and logs the trees of the Moonwood with great care.

markers.name: Quarrelshigh
markers.textHtml: This fortified gnomish village has been in existence since at least 870 DR, and probably long before. Its primary industry is mining rich veins of mithral and diamond deposits. Quarrelshigh sits between three high peaks and is protected all around by a deep ravine. It has extremely strong defenses that have never been breached. Every tunnel is set with deadly traps, and some tunnels are decoys filled with nothing but traps. These defenses are tested every summer by trolls and ogres from deeper in the mountains. Only a resident of Quarrelshigh can guide a visitor in or out of the village safely.

markers.name: Rassalantar
markers.textHtml: Rassalantar is a hamlet and caravan stop on the Long Road, under Waterdeep's protection. The small community consists of six walled farms centered around a spring-fed pond which drains into a stream and empties to the east into the Stump Bog, a sprawling, desolate marsh haunted by monsters. To the west of the farmhouses, a narrow patch of trees known as Keep Woods masks the ruins of Rassalantar's Keep. The keep was built long ago by the warrior Rassalantar and its ruins are used by traveling vagabonds, doppelgangers, and hungry monsters as a place of shelter.

markers.name: Rauvincross
markers.textHtml: The village of Rauvincross is home to many of the drivers and mule teams that work the paths around the Moon Pass Gorge.

markers.name: Reddansyr
markers.textHtml: The small settlement of Reddansyr is located at the junction between the Grand Ride and the Trader's Road, along the route that connects Westgate with the cities of Teziir and Starmantle, and serves as a launching point for caravans heading south from the region. It has a well-earned reputation as an adventurers' town because it is a good source of information for such people.

markers.name: Redwater
markers.textHtml: The settlement of Redwater became a destination for Helmite pilgrims following the mid–14th century DR, after the Guardtower of the God was sacked by followers of the Gods of Fury. Sometime later, the divine relic known as 'the Helm of Helm' rose from the rubble of the destroyed temple and presented itself to a Helmite priest known as Rolor, who used it to fight Zhentarim forces near Darkhold before disappearing in the Tunlands.

markers.name: Reitheillaethor
markers.textHtml: Reitheillaethor is a wood elf village comprising numerous naturalistic buildings and shelters that appear to grow out of the roots and trees of the forest floor. Seven outer structures encircle a central, white marble lodge built within the space formed by four great oak trees, but the village's residents actually live amongst the branches of the weirwood and shadowtop trees, high atop the forest floor. For some of its history Reitheillaethor existed as a winter retreat for the forest's elves. By the late 15th century DR however, it has become one of the first communities of the High Forest that joined with others with the hopes of uniting all the tribes of the High Forest and restoring the kingdom of Eaerlann.

markers.name: Rethgaard
markers.textHtml: Also known as Sonnmorndin, the ruined fortress of Rethgaard still stands nearly 6,000 years after it was originally built by ancient seafaring dwarves.

markers.name: Rivenshield
markers.textHtml: In the mid-14th century, this village was known as the base of operations for the Shieldbreakers mercenary company. This company was made up of ogres known for being unbiased and honorable, and for always refusing contacts that involved the murder of unarmed foes. They fought alongside Elestam's Crusaders in the Goblin Wars of 1358 DR.

markers.name: River's Run
markers.textHtml: Having only a mere five buildings, the hamlet of River’s Run serves as a meeting place for the local farmers and trappers. The village is settled almost directly above a mass grave of soldiers from the Imperial Chessentan army. In 1358 DR, a cleric of Tempus named Theodoric was driven mad by a servant of Anthraxus named Yrkhetep and convinced to raise the dead bodies buried beneath River's Run as an undead army.

markers.name: Rivermoot
markers.textHtml: Rivermoot is a very small town in the Lower Rauvin Vale. It sits between the northern bank of the River Rauvin and the River Surbrin, at the foot of the Frost Hills. It is protected by a sturdy palisade on its east side, and by the two rivers on its south and north-west sides, making it defensible. However, during spring, it suffers destructive floods from the two rivers. Therefore, the majority of buildings are built standing on pilings at least 10 feet (3 meters) off the ground. The town was officially a part of Silverymoon's lands and a protectorate of the League of the Silver Marches.

markers.name: Roaringshore
markers.textHtml: This small, isolated village is nestled in a small deepwater cove on the coast. It is known as known as a lawless place and a pirate hold, and is frequently raided by mercenaries hired by Amn and Baldur's Gate.

markers.name: Rock Orchard
markers.textHtml: This small village is one of many hamlets that are sprinkled along the minor trade route of the Firedrake Road, that bends along the Firedrake Shores, through the Dragon's Neck Peninsula, and reaches the Dragon's Head Peninsula. Rock Orchard is best known for its apple harvests.

markers.name: Ruathym (village)
markers.textHtml: With a population of 6,000, Ruathym is the largest settlement on the island of the same name. It lies close to the magical ruins of Inthar. As of the turn of the 16th century DR, Queen Forfallen and her pirates affected by the magical disease known as the Rusting have begun trading with the city of Ruathym. The city provides safe harbor for her, allowing her to offload ill-gotten goods in exchange for supplies and fresh crewmembers.

markers.name: Saden
markers.textHtml: Saden is a small independent village. The place is well known for its ancient temple of Marduk, which is located in its central plaza. As of 1480 DR, the temple has been repurposed for the use of the faithful of Bahamut.

markers.name: Saerb
markers.textHtml: Saerb is a Sembian settlement located where the Dawnpost, the Thunder Way, and the Belduuk Road meet, near the border with Archendale. It is a small village where lived merchants that preferred a quiet life in the countryside instead of the chaos of the cities.

markers.name: Samra
markers.textHtml: Samra is a relatively small fishing town. It has few defenses and no walls. The city has docks, but they are unaccommodating for bigger vessels, leaving Samra deprived of a huge part of the trade in the Vilhon Reach. As of the turn of the 16th century DR, Samra is paying increasingly exorbitant taxes to the Chondathan capital city of Arrabar, to the point of considering secession.

markers.name: Sarbreenar
markers.textHtml: The land around Sarbreenar is full of sharply rising cliffs, rocky ridges, and pinnacles, where avalanches are a common hazard. Between them lay about a dozen wet, narrow valleys fashioned by mountain streams and filled with moss and mists. These valleys are home to local farms. Sarbreenar provides a market to them. At the center of the hamlet is Beluar's Tomb, the burial place of Beluar and his riders, and the only memory of the hamlet's violent past. Sarbreenar was originally built as a keep and defensive outpost by the forces of Roldilar, a dwarven kingdom that dominated the lands in the early 7th century DR, and Beluar was an elven hero who died while helping the dwarves retaking the place from the orcs in the mid-7th century.

markers.name: Sendrin
markers.textHtml: A temple to Savras was founded in the village of Sendrin at the time when humans first dominated the Vast. With Savras then seen as a wise user of magic rather than just a diviner, Sendrin became a mecca for wizards. However, when Azuth defeated Savras and supplanted him as god of magic, Azuth's followers destroyed the temple. The wizards stopped coming, and Sendrin shrank into a simple farming village. Its main industries became basket-weaving, ranching cattle, and making furniture. As a former town of magic, Sendrin is still home to many big and impressive stone buildings, shops, and houses. Of the old temple of Savras, nothing survives bar the Mouth of Savras: a stone idol shaped like a human male head, as tall as a man, sitting at the bottom of a crater of black and melted stone. The relic has healing powers and is said to answer questions on magic, although only with cryptic statements.

markers.name: Serpent's Cowl
markers.textHtml: Serpent's Cowl is a small village on the east bank of the Winding Water. It features a ferry crossing. The village was named for the temple that was once found on this location and that was linked to the yuan-ti city of Ss'thar'tiss'ssun. The village itself is warded against dragons to prevent them from digging up the destroyed temple. Netherese wards designed to keep out serpentfolk are also active here.

markers.name: Sevenecho
markers.textHtml: The small hamlet of Sevenecho was named for the most prominent of its local families, the Sevenechoes. Lying at the crossroads of the North Road and Stormcrest Trail, it is a useful stopover for merchant caravans and travellers. Sevenecho also serves as a market for the farmers scattered about the surrounding lands. But the settlement persists here largely due to the draw of the Worried Wyvern inn, which lays at the heart of community affairs. The hamlet comprises a number of widely spaced buildings, connected by unpaved streets. Sprinkled amongst them are five big, old manor houses belonging to the local nobility. Sevenecho has a reputation as a damp and rainy place, with mists often veiling the hamlet in the mornings. Many ponds and deep wells can be found throughout the town.

markers.name: Shallain Freehold
markers.textHtml: Shallain Freehold is a small family landholding situated atop a low hill and comprising a dozen wooden buildings surrounded by a log wall. It was built by a halfing family of goat herders. The halfings enlist passing adventurers to protect the freehold against goblins, gnolls, and other dangerous creatures that threaten the homestead.

markers.name: Shatar
markers.textHtml: The folk from the small village of Shatar perform lake fishing and farm root vegetables, and sell the surplus to travelers and nearby military sites such as Citadel Amnur and Citadel Rashturl. Its only notable feature is its remarkably well-made carved stone docks.

markers.name: Smuggler's Stone
markers.textHtml: Smuggler's Stone is a village on the Bluemist Trail, that runs along the coast from Marsember in the west to Dawngleam in the east. The fishermen's huts are clustered around the steep sides of the stormy cove and supported on stilts. The settlement is named for a large rock that shelters the small harbor. By 1367 DR, it was against Crown law to set up lights on the Stone because they were once used to help pirates and smugglers guide themselves into the harbor. War Wizards observed the area with the farscry spell and alerted the Purple Dragons if illegal activity was detected. There are many tales of unclaimed treasure stashed by pirates around the area, and beneath the hills north of the village there is said to be an abandoned mine belonging to the gnomes.

markers.name: Snowcap
markers.textHtml: Snowcap is a waymeet, which is the name given to temporary villages of tents that crop up at large campgrounds found in Narfell. The Nar tribes share these sites freely between themselves and even with outsiders. Given their transitory nature, the population of a waymeet is highly variable.

markers.name: Snowmantle
markers.textHtml: Snowmantle was a quiet and nondescript village, known to produce a unique wine made from a type of mushroom that could only be found in the Border Forest. It all changed when the Zhentarim invaded the region in 1355 DR, occupied the peaceful hamlet and transformed it into a slave labor camp and a significant source of lumber for Zhent operations in the Dalelands. The occupation ended in 1369 when an enormous nature elemental, summoned to aid adventurers liberating the village, leveled the settlement. Some of Snowmantle's more established residents returned to the town's ruins a month later to begin rebuilding their home.

markers.name: Soubar
markers.textHtml: Soubar is a small village often used as a way-station for traders traveling the Trade Way. Soubar has no formal government, which leads to a lawless culture full of brigands and ruffians. As of 1489 DR, priests of Bane were rebuilding the Black Abbey, a former Baneite monastery. Although the project was unpopular with some locals, it had brought in much needed skilled workers, trade, and wealth.

markers.name: Starfall Stream Pool
markers.textHtml: Starfall Stream Pool is a settlement at the head of the Perloush valley. Starfall Stream Pool is one of the most popular rendezvous spot for lovers who seek intimacy during the Feast of the Moon, a grand feast and celebration for the inhabitants of Turmish. The town became regularly patrolled after the Time of Troubles and the increased activities of the dreaded Cult of Malar whose members considered frolicking loves to be their prey.

markers.name: Steppenhall
markers.textHtml: Steppenhall is a small village and farming community located in the Duchy of Soravia. It sits along the winding banks of the Icelace River near its junction with the Galena Snake, making it an important staging point for re-routing goods to the rest of the duchy. It also serves as an informal meeting ground and watering hole for farmers from all across northeastern Damara. Given the close proximity with Narfell, many inhabitants of Steppenhall maintain close relationships with Nar tribesmen. Some of the tribesmen, especially of the Var, even visit the village on a regular basis to hear the latest news of the region.

markers.name: Storaad
markers.textHtml: This small, isolated coastal town was abandoned following the rise of Sarifal, and lay in ruins as of the late 15th century DR. However, it has been rebuilt and reinhabited as of 1501 DR, after the lands of Corwell were resettled under the leadership of Tarilyn Kendrick following Sarifal's retreat into the Winterglen.

markers.name: Sudrav
markers.textHtml: Sudrav is a small mining town. In the 14th century DR, its inhabitants were known to be disgusted by the politics of Damara, and considering themselves more politically aligned with Impiltur.

markers.name: Surcross
markers.textHtml: As of the late 15th century DR, Surcross is the largest Thayan settlement west of the Thayan Plateau, and a heavily fortified military base. This is because Szass Tam sees the town as the first line of defense in the case of an assault from the west. The citizens are outnumbered by the Thayan soldiers stationed there, both living and undead, and the city is controlled by a Thayan military governor who rules from the town's central citadel, the Tombstone. Surcross is ringed by a heavy stone wall known as the Wall of Crypts, because of the hundreds of corpses stored within it as a reserve army of zombies. The main gate, known as the Gates of Bone, is made from the bones of humanoids and monsters, and can animate as an undead construct. Five thin, windowless towers standing along the perimeter are known as the Fingers of Szass Tam, and employ a number of powerful defensive magics. In 1479 DR, during the Shadovar–Thay War, Surcross came under attack from the floating Netherese citadel of Kolthunral. The lands around the town were reduced to a necromantic wasteland known as the Charnel Fields, and only the protective shields of the Fingers of Szass Tam prevented the town from being obliterated by areal bombardment.

markers.name: Swords Pool
markers.textHtml: The village of Swords Pool lays in a wooded dell on the North Road. It serves as a stopover for travelers, and in the 14th century, it was notable for its high-quality inn, the Inn of the Nine Swords. Behind the inn lays Swords Pool itself, a deep and dark body of water stocked with fish. The pool is the source of a local treasure tale that gave it, the village, and the inn their names. In the early history of the Vast, when the Nine Swords Company disbanded, the famous wizard Thallastam of Procampur concealed his companions' nine magical swords in the pool. The swords could never be found in the pool by dragging it or dispelling magic.

markers.name: Swordslake Creek
markers.textHtml: The mining hamlet of Swordslake Creek was named after an ancient massacre – a battle between a tribe of orcs and a human troupe that tried to erect a stronghold and emerged victorious. A small village was built around a keep; however, by the 14th century DR, the keep was nothing but a shattered ruin, surrounded by a handful of mining residences and general stores that catered to the locals and their mining trade.

markers.name: Tavilar
markers.textHtml:
[not Tayilar]

The village of Tavilar is located on the Hunt Trail between Ylraphon and Kurth, at the southern edge of the lands reportedly claimed by the Mage Who Never Dies, the mysterious self-proclaimed 'master of the Flooded Forest' in the 14th century DR. A track leads north from Tavilar to the haunted Mage's Tower, and the Pass Trail leads east to Blanaer. Tavilar is known for having been the home of the Turning Key, an adventuring company based in the House of Key in the 14th century. Locals claim that the Turning Key had been quite wealthy, and that their treasure must still be hidden somewhere in the area.

markers.name: Tel Badir
markers.textHtml: Tel Badir is a village situated atop a hill, overlooked by the nearby Tethyamar Mountains, and south of Teshenfall Pass. Its streets wind along a bed of water known as 'the arm', or Sixstreams River. It is inhabited by humans of the D'tarig population. The inhabitants of Tel Badir frequently conduct trade with members of the Zhentarim, though it is also a location that the nomadic Bedine often come to for trade, and that many outlanders flock to do business under a low profile. The D'tarig trade goods that are common to the region, including camels and frankincense resin tree. Starting in the late 14th century DR, the Zhentarim began working to take over the village, hoping to transform it into one of their trading bases, and triggering latent rebellion from many inhabitants.

markers.name: Tempus's Tears
markers.textHtml: Tempus's Tears is a dwarven and gnome settlement at the intersection of the Skuldask Road and Thundar's Ride. There is a large standing stone set up at the intersection with a votive inscription to Tempus. The dwarf and gnome villagers live in burrow homes as the area of the village is covered in stone ruins. The villagers say the stones are under the protection of Amaeraszantha, an amethyst dragon.

markers.name: Theymarsh
markers.textHtml: Theymarsh is a port town known in the north for its delicate blown and worked glass – notably bird figurines. Another export of note is a quality ale – a fine dark lager. Other major industries in Theymarsh are logging, mining, and fishing.

markers.name: Tinnir
markers.textHtml: Tinnir is a secluded village hidden away in a small forested valley. It receives some visitors from among the fisherman that make their catches in Lake Tirulag. Most of the village's structures are whitewashed and typical of those found in the region.

markers.name: Toaketh
markers.textHtml: This small village is one of many hamlets that are sprinkled along the minor trade route of the Firedrake Road, that bends along the Firedrake Shores, through the Dragon's Neck Peninsula, and reaches the Dragon's Head Peninsula. The village was named after the wizard named Ildabar Toaketh, a recluse who was long dead by the late 14th century DR, with his humble often-plundered tomb being part of the village. Toaketh's main claim to fame were its Toaketh knives, special never-dulling small blades. The secret of their creation was kept by the craftsmen of the village, and the knives were considered Mystras blessing to all chefs and cooks of the Sword Coast and wider Faerûn.

markers.name: Tomrav
markers.textHtml: Humans, dwarves, and half-orcs make up most of the population of this small mining settlement. The resident half-orcs help maintain positive relations with the local goblinoid tribes.

markers.name: Triel
markers.textHtml: A small village at the intersection of the Trade Way and the Dusk Road. Triel has few farms in the area and has to import everything it needs, especially food. The village has a log-and-boulder stockade and locks its gates at night. Much of the room behind the stockade is used to store grain and dry vegetables for the winter months when merchant visits are few.

markers.name: Trollclaw Ford
markers.textHtml: The Trollclaw Ford is a hamlet located on the Winding Water and crossed by the Coast Way, connecting Baldur's Gate to Waterdeep.

markers.name: Trollford
markers.textHtml: Trollford is the only safe, shallow ford on the Amstel River for many miles, as the river is too swift and deep for much of its length. The site was once the center of a troll civilization that was wiped out by humans from Calimshan during its Third Age (−1900 DR to −900 DR), a period of northern expansion. The fallen kingdom's vast wealth was never found, and treasure hunters repeatedly search the area to no result. A small population of trolls continues to persist in the region, and opportunistically raids caravans on the road. Council Army troops in Eshpurta receive special training on dealing with trolls, and provide paid escorts from the city to past the ford.

markers.name: Trondhelm
markers.textHtml: The small village of Trondhelm was initially a stronghold of the Northmen, but like the rest of the settlements on Oman's Isle, Trondhelm was overrun and ruled by giants by the later part of the 15th century DR.

markers.name: Tulbegh
markers.textHtml: Tulbegh is a small coastal village standing at the base of a peninsula that protrudes from the mainland immediately south of the town. It is often surrounded by dark rain clouds, receiving its heavy share of precipitation. As of the late 14th century DR, glass bottleworks in Tulbegh remained a profitable business. However, the town's main industry and the source of food is fishing in the Sea of Fallen Stars. Lastly, Tulbegh farmers wareere renowned across the Realms for their osquip milk that is mixed with a certain type of seaweed mash to make baby food. Tulbegh is occasionally menaced by roving sea monsters, including sea trolls and sahuagin.

markers.name: Tulmene
markers.textHtml: Tulmene is a small settlement known for being the largest fishing village on the south coast of the Tethyr Peninsula, with shipyards and large warehouses.

markers.name: The Way Inn
markers.textHtml: The Way Inn is a large walled compound and a famous landmark on the western side of the Trade Way atop a grassy plateau. Each of the three gatehouses is the size of a small keep and armed with a catapult on the roof. The inn itself is made of stone with a tile roof and topped with a lookout tower. In 1363 DR an ancient black dragon, alongside the hordes of Dragonspear Castle, leveled the structure and the surrounding village. A year later the inn was rebuilt, thanks to sponsoring by several merchant families based in Waterdeep.

markers.name: Uldfest
markers.textHtml: The small settlement of Uldfest is the northern end of the Reach, a perilous trail that runs for more than 1,000 km from Vaasa to Sossal. The Reach traverses some of the most unforgiving and harsh landscapes in all of Faerûn, through the Cinnabar Wastes, the Lugsaas Chain, the giant kingdom of Kultaakar, the Surkyk Range, the Great Glacier and Novularond.

markers.name: Ulf of Thuger
markers.textHtml: The capital city of the nation of Trisk. Its inhabitants fish and farm barely enough to put food in their mouths, and additional needs are satisfied by piracy on other Northmen and pirate ships. Their main activity is recording and cataloging the flux of information that filters into the island through the Kraken Society spy network.

markers.name: Ulgoth's Beard
markers.textHtml: Ulgoth's Beard is a hamlet home mostly to poor shepherds and fishermen. The purpose of the settlement is to warn Baldur's Gate of pending attacks or pirate raids. The beacon is located on the cliffs at the mouth of the river. Ulgoth's Beard was named because the beacon is said to metaphorically singe the beard of a pirate named Ulgoth, whose attack was robbed of surprise by the beacon. To travel to Ulgoth's Beard, one is required to navigate rolling grassy hills or arrive by boat as there is no road.

markers.name: Uluvin
markers.textHtml: Uluvin is a sleepy crossroads town connecting several trails, including the Iron Road and the Secomber Trail, along with trails that lead along the Unicorn Run into the High Forest and the Stronghold of the Nine. It is a sparsely-populated settlement with a few scattered trees and dusty roads. The village is avoided by most travelers and merchants as it offers little in the way of trade. Uluvin was destroyed in 1373 DR by the drow-dragon Nurvureem, repopulated by the late 15th century, and ransacked in 1491 by a group of hill giants.

markers.name: Uthmere
markers.textHtml: Formerly a port town, the walled village of Uthmere was left high and dry when the Sea of Fallen Stars receded following the Spellplague. As of the late 15th century DR the village functions primarily as a trading post.

markers.name: Urling
markers.textHtml: Urling is a small, hidden-away settlement. At first glance, the village appears to be nothing more than a primordial grove of evergreen and alder trees. In between the trees are rolling grassy mounds, within which are the traditional stone lodges that are associated with Rashemen. Urling is home to many witches of Rashemen's Wychlaran, but it is not exclusive to their order as is the Urlingwood. Rashemaar commoners native to the country are allowed residence within, including the witches' spouses, family members, and servants.

markers.name: Urml
markers.textHtml: Urml is a small town with a population of about 800, comprising mostly craftsmen, with the rest being students of the local college or farmers. It comprises the Keep of Military Knowledge, a school and library were students come to train in the use of weaponry and hone their fighting skills. A large militia protects the town from goblins raids from the nearby Orsraun Mountains. Urml has a thriving industry made of craftsmen specializing in the production of high quality weapons, armor, and other military related items. A number of small wheat farms surround the place.

markers.name: Uttersea
markers.textHtml: The capital of Tuern is built upon a collapsed volcanic crater. The crater is huge on all sides, and acts as a weather shield to those of Uttersea, so the inhabitants are able to resist heavy winds and rain. The geothermal activity beneath the volcanic crater warms the surface above it. Uttersea is ruled by a high king, who acts as the king of kings on Tuern. Those of Uttersea make profit by fishing, farming and whaling. They also spend time mining for adamantine and diamonds, but they are unable to refine these rare materials.

markers.name: Vilkstead
markers.textHtml: With richer fishing water than its neighbor, the throne city of the island of Utheraal has enough catches to satisfy its own needs and make a living by exporting large amounts of dried, smoked, salted, and pickled fish to Gundarlun, which in turn ships it to cities all across Faerûn. It also produces a pungent, salty, herbal goat cheese called Vilksmaarg, popular in Sword Coast taverns.

markers.name: Viperstone
markers.textHtml: This small village is one of many hamlets that are sprinkled along the minor trade route of the Firedrake Road, that bends along the Firedrake Shores, through the Dragon's Neck Peninsula, and reaches the Dragon's Head Peninsula. The hamlet's name comes from the abundant population of venomous snakes found sunning on the nearby rocks. This regularly brings a number of alchemists from the land of Calimshan, who gather baskets full of the dangerous creatures at a time.

markers.name: Westbridge
markers.textHtml: Westbridge earned its name for its location on the west side of the Stone Bridge. It is located at the intersection where the Long Road intersected with the Stone Trail. It is a farming village, but the residents often hunt game in the nearby Kryptgarden Forest.

markers.name: White Ford
markers.textHtml: The fortified village of White Ford grew up around an artificially-created ford in the River Arhen. It is protected from goblin and worg raids by a stone wall called the Wolf Wall, by an inner keep, and by a company of archers. The proximity of White Ford to nearby metropolises such as Highmoon and Archenbridge contributes to mercantile traffic and to the town’s wealth.

markers.name: White Rock
markers.textHtml: White Rock is a small town in the kingdom of Callidyrr. As with other settlements in and around the Fairheight Range, it was founded for the purpose of mining for metals. The town maintains a militia of hundreds of fighters to protect itself from the constant threat of raids by the goblins and orcs of the dangerous mountains.

markers.name: Winter Edge
markers.textHtml: Winter Edge is a hamlet in the Silver Marches. It was briefly turned into a military encampment for the army of Silverymoon in 1370 DR, when king Obould Many-Arrows and his army of orcs attacked Mithral Hall. During the War of the Silver Marches in 1485 DR, the town again came under attack by orcs from the Spine of the World.

markers.name: Withermeet
markers.textHtml: Withermeet is a small village found in the Barony of Polten. The village proper consists of several dozen structures catering mostly to travelers passing through on their way north. The folk of Withermeet are known as highly conservative individuals who resisted change over the few centuries since the town's founding.

markers.name: Womford
markers.textHtml: Womford is a tiny village part on the Iron Road. The ford was once the site of an old wooden bridge on the River Dessarin, which was later destroyed. A new bridge was constructed at the ford by 1370 DR. The later settlement of Womford, according to local legend, was to have originally been called 'Ironford'. The name supposedly changed to 'Wyrmford' after a dragon was defeated in the area. Due to the accent of the locals, the pronunciation of Wyrmford was altered, hence the name 'Womford'. Womford's economy is based on the grain from surrounding farms. The village has several granaries and a gristmill. The dock allows for the grain and flour to be shipped to other communities along the Dessarin River. There is an under-culture of bandits, smugglers, and river pirates that use the dock for nefarious purposes.

markers.name: Wright's Ferry
markers.textHtml: The small hamlet of Wright's Ferry includes several orchards and pig farms, the horse ferry for which the village was named, a small pond, and a single tavern. A bizarre ring of standing stones called the Chessmen of Valsprendar is believed to be linked to some magic ritual. The stones actually mark the burial site of Valsprendar, a powerful wizard who enchanted the stones The Chessmen has become a holy place for followers of the Red Knight, and a war priest calling herself Elaine of Valsprendar has moved into Wright's Ferry to watch over the site.

markers.name: Wyvernhunt
markers.textHtml: Wyvernhunt is a small shepherding community trading in wool and goat's milk cheese. Scattered across a high meadow slope are various boulders and tiny stone cottages, some of which no more than walled-up caves. Nearby on the slopes lays Wyvernhunt Keep, an abandoned brigands' stronghold, and several fields up the slope above the hamlet stands Spear Rock, a highly visible shrine to Tempus. Several copper mines lay further up in the hills; once the area was plagued by wyvern aeries, hence the name, but they were rare by the 1360s DR, making the mines safer.

markers.name: Yarthrain
markers.textHtml: Yarthrain is a small village comprising a handful of great stone houses surrounding a ruined stone castle. The village was founded in the late 13th century DR by an Amnian mercenary who declared himself 'baron of the Backlands' and immediately called for construction of a castle. While the baron proved ill-prepared for rulership, the settlement survived his brief reign. The folks of Yarthrain now provide for themselves by growing crops, herding sheep and cows, and fishing from the Winding Water. Rumors of Zhentarim control over the village circulated across the region circa 1369 DR.

markers.name: Yellyark
markers.textHtml: The village of Yellyark is home to the Biting Ant tribe of Batiri goblins. The trees upon which it is built wareere planted in the center of three enormous ant colony mounds. The settlement is constructed in quite a unique manner; it is inside a grouping of bound trees, attached to a larger, stronger tree. This creates a sort of 'catapult' effect. If Yellyark is truly threatened, the bindings that hold down the bundle of trees can be severed and the whole village can be launched a great distance away over the jungle canopy. Yellyark employs more traditional measures of defense, such as tripwire alarms, camouflaged batiri sentries, and ant swarm defenders. Around 1485 DR, around forty goblins live within the village.

markers.name: Zarach
markers.textHtml: Zarach is a small farming community found in the southern region of Ostel. Its location at the convergence of the Great Imphras River and the Sidewinder River makes it a prime trading hub for the barony, and a constant stream of wagons and caravans from outlying communities makes it a bustling place to live.

markers.name: Araugul
markers.textHtml: Araugul was the great fortress-city capital of the Hlundadim realm in the Goblin Marches. Dust storms and the desertification of the Windy Plains by 268 DR drove the inhabitants of Araugul into a panic, sending them fleeing south as the land seemed to turn against them. The nation of Hlundadim ceased to exist. Over the following centuries Araugul and the lands around it were slowly resettled as the goblinoids and their kin attempted to rebuild what was lost.

markers.name: Arnise Hold
markers.textHtml: The Arnise Hold is the familial keep and homestead of the noble Arnise family of Amn. It is a small, rather plain, two-story stronghold with a small courtyard and spacious cellar. It offers a few amenities to visitors and farmers in the region. The Arnise family possesses a great deal of hospitality, a trait well-appreciated by their vassals.

markers.name: Candlekeep
markers.textHtml:
The Great Library

Largest repository of lore and writings in all the Realms. To gain entry to the halls of wisdom a visitor must gift the library collection with a work of writing not already in the possession of the monks.

markers.name: Castle Crag
markers.textHtml: Fearing that bandits, orcs, and other marauders in the Stonelands and Tilver's Gap might one day threaten the northern border of Cormyr, the kingdom began building the fortress of Castle Crag around 1347 DR as a local strongpoint to protect the frontier. It marked the greatest expansion of the kingdom in quite some time. However, with Cormyr's annexation of Tilverton in 1357 DR, Castle Crag was no longer its easternmost outpost. By 1367 DR, Castle Crag had stopped all goblin raids further south, as had the waning of goblin power in the Goblin Marches. Nevertheless, Castle Crag remained a key defense of the realm.

markers.name: Castle of Illusion
markers.textHtml: Centuries before the 14th century DR, this remote keep was the home of the mighty gnome illusionist Fitzmilliyun Sparkledrim. He erected the keep and defended it with illusions, traps, and puzzles. After his death, the castle stood abandoned for centuries, still protected by its illusions. But by 1358 DR, the evil wizard Maclyon had claimed the castle. Maclyon meticulously unveiled the secrets of the Castle of Illusion and became its master. He was still in control by 1372 DR, but had begun summoning fiends to serve him and defend the keep.

markers.name: Castle Perilous
markers.textHtml: On a single night in 1347 DR, the fortress called Castle Perilous was raised by Zhengyi with the aid of demons. Zhengyi then declared himself 'the Witch-King,' and gathered an army of monsters and assassins to use in a conquest of Vaasa and Damara. The castle collapsed into ruins when Gareth Dragonsbane and his party of adventurers defeated Zhengyi in 1359 DR. However, in 1477 DR, an earthquake struck the region and the castle shed its crumbling exterior to reveal seemingly new sleek black stone underneath, inscribed with runes that absorbed light. The Warlock Knights of Vaasa worried Zhengyi's demons had rebuilt the castle overnight and sent investigators, but none could pierce the defensive runes on the castle's exterior. As of 1489 DR, the cause of Castle Perilous's rejuvenation continues to remain a mystery.

markers.name: The Citadel
markers.textHtml: The Citadel is located atop the tallest peak of Thaymount, a chain of volcanic mountains in the heart of the tharch that shared their name. It is an ancient fortress-complex that serves as the inner sanctum of Szass Tam and the country's infamous Red Wizards. Even before the wash of undeath overcame Thay, the Citadel was said to be an unholy place, haunted by utmost evil and teeming with secrets that should never see the light of day. After taking over the Citadel proper, the Red Wizards led a number of expeditions into the fortress's depths, with varying results. Some groups managed to extricate large caches of treasure, while most others never returned from within.

markers.name: Citadel Adbar
markers.textHtml: This dwarven citadel consists of a massive fortified fortress that provides surface access to miles and miles of defensive granite corridors, tunnels, and hallways that wind their way under the Ice Mountains. The dwarves dwelling here make their living by mining, smelting and forging their native ores. Their constant work causes a permanent cloud of smoke to hang over the city and creates such a din and clangor that it is just as well that the city does not entertain many guests. Like other dwarven communities in Faerûn, the number of dwarves in Citadel Adbar had been steadily dwindling over the last several generations.

markers.name: Citadel Amnur
markers.textHtml: Citadel Amnur is a famed fortress that is paired with the nearby Citadel Rashturl. It was first built in 500 DR, and was immediately put into use. Citadels Amnur and Rashturl were rebuilt in identical designs shortly before 1350 DR to head off the threat of devils and orcs from Dragonspear Castle. Citadel Amnur lies near the source of the Amnurflow river and strategically close to the Neck pass. The fortress is accessed by a narrow pass blocked by a barbican gatehouse. The walls are 400 feet (120 m) high, with the first 350 feet (105 m) made up of the carved mountainside it sits on, and the walls proper an additional 50 feet (15 m) of masonry.

markers.name: Citadel of Assassins
markers.textHtml: The Assassins Guild of the Galenas was chased from Heliogabalus in the mid-to-late 14th century DR after the fall of Zhengyi, the Witch-King. They took refuge in a complex of caverns in the mountainside of the Galena Mountains. Suites of rooms reach far back into the mountain, some carved by hired stone masons and miners, many others created by magic. The caves are full of comforts and treasures, inviting aromas and magically lit walkways.

markers.name: Citadel of Conjurers
markers.textHtml: The Citadel of Conjurers, originally known as Dun-Orthass, is a dark tower that was constructed from black stone at the mouth of the Moaning Gorge in –153 DR, in the final years of the Narfell empire. Beneath the tower proper is a labyrinthine series of secluded chambers and catacombs that are believed to hold a number of artifacts and treasures from Narfell, and are rumored to lead to the Hall of the Hidden Throne, a massive demoncyst. The sages of Faerûn identified the citadel as a powerful evil node. Faerûnian demonologists and experts of fiendish lore believe the halls of the citadel to be infested with demonic activity.

markers.name: Citadel Felbarr
markers.textHtml: A dwarven citadel that at different times in history had belonged to dwarves, humans, orcs, and, since 1367 DR, once again the dwarves. The Citadel sits about as far up the River Redrun as is navigable, meaning that the citadel is connected by river all the way down through the River Dessarin.

markers.name: Citadel of the Mists
markers.textHtml: The Citadel of the Mists is a castle built on top of an ancient elven armory. It comprises two buildings that enclose a narrow triangular courtyard. Its most striking feature is a set of three towers of different heights. The Citadel is cloaked by an ancient wardmist spell that creates a permanent magical mist and makes it almost inaccessible. There are many rumors about treasure in the vaults beneath the citadel.

markers.name: Citadel Rashturl
markers.textHtml: Citadel Rashturl is a famed fortress that is paired with the nearby Citadel Amnur. It was first built as a log fort in 897 DR in response to a decade of monster incursions, and was upgraded to a stone fortress in 901 DR. Citadels Amnur and Rashturl were rebuilt in identical designs shortly before 1350 DR to head off the threat of devils and orcs from Dragonspear Castle. Citadel Rashturl lies near the source of the Rashturiver. The Citadel is accessed by a narrow mountain pass that includes a 50 feet (15 m) long drawbridge that crosses a chasm. The walls are 400 feet (120 m) high, with the first 350 feet (105 m) made up of the carved mountainside it sits on, and the walls proper an additional 50 feet (15 m) of masonry.

markers.name: Citadel of the Raven
markers.textHtml: The Citadel of the Raven was a well-fortified and well-protected fortress of the Zhentarim, resting in the center of 10 miles (15 km) of interconnected fortresses on the western end of the Dragonspine Mountains. In 1383 DR, during the Shadowbane War, the Shadovar moved against Zhentil Keep, and razed the Citadel of the Raven along with it. As of 1479 DR most of the outlying towers and lesser fortifications are heaps of overgrown rubble, with miles of tunnels buried beneath the old walls. The Zhentarim begin to return in small numbers to secure and rebuild the central citadel, which has remained mostly intact.

markers.name: Dantalien
markers.textHtml: This Aglarondan citadel is constantly in a state of warfare with the nearby fortress Undumor, which was lost in the 1400s DR to the new Thayan regime under Szass Tam, serves as an outpost of Thay in Aglarond and is garrisoned by numerous undead. Citadel Dantalien conducts regular assaults on Undumor in attempts to clear the undead, but they are rumored to be digging beneath the river in order to circumvent Citadel Dantalian altogether.

markers.name: Dark Arrow Keep
markers.textHtml: Dark Arrow Keep was the central stronghold for the orcish Kingdom of Many-Arrows and the seat of power for its kings. The town was built around a massive central Citadel and enclosed by a palisade resting atop a high wall of earthworks and rubble. Inside the walls was a sprawling and chaotic settlement where tents vastly outnumbered the crudely-made stone and wood buildings. Known for its metal workers, Dark Arrow Keep was the industrial center of the kingdom. It boasted more than two dozen forges, which made use of the easy access to iron ore mined from the Spine of the World. After the orcs' defeat in the War of the Silver Marches in 1485 DR, the dwarves of Mithral Hall, Citadel Adbar, and Citadel Felbarr proceeded to dismantle the entire site. It was not until the turn of the 16th century DR that efforts began to rebuild the keep. A self-proclaimed orc king, Orrusk Homebringer, began reconstruction and was attracting an ever growing following as of 1501 DR.

markers.name: Darkhold
markers.textHtml: The Darkhold is a formidable and powerful stronghold belonging to the Zhentarim, a shadowy mercantile and paramilitary organization. It serves as a major base of operations for their influence in the region.

markers.name: Fort Agis
markers.textHtml: Fort Agis, also known as 'the Memnonnar Citadel', is one of the four border keeps of Tethyr, collectively known as the Calimmon Garrisons. It shares the name of the port of Agis in Shoonach and the River Agis that they both overlook. Like most of the Calimmon Garrisons, this non-elaborate fortress was built from huge stones in the course of the 8th century DR. Its walls are 10-to-15 feet (3 à 4,50 m) thick and 20 feet (6 m) high, with a deep, empty moat around them. As the closest fort to Calimshan and the most vulnerable to dangers (including from Memnonnar), it is the most militant and watchful of the forts, with the largest garrison.

markers.name: Fort Beluarian
markers.textHtml: Fort Beluarian is a trade fort run by the Flaming Fist mercenary company on behalf of the city-state of Baldur's Gate. The fort is well supplied and well defended against the monsters of the jungle. Beluarian Flaming Fist members offer hired adventurers to perform certain tasks to aid their efforts, and grant charters to any adventurers or explorers who seek to venture into the jungles of Chult.

markers.name: Fort Calim
markers.textHtml: Fort Calim, also known as 'Shoonach's Sunset', is one of the four border keeps of Tethyr, collectively known as the Calimmon Garrisons. Like the other Calimmon Garrisons, this non-elaborate fortress was built from huge stones in the course of the 8th century DR. Its walls are 10-to-15 feet (3 à 4,50 m) thick and 20 feet (6 m) high, with a deep, empty moat around them. The Fort was built on the ancient foundations of a long-lost dwarven fortress. Due to its position close to Shoonach, it serves equally as a bulwark against Calishite aggression and monsters emerging from the Shoonach ruins.

markers.name: Fort Morninglord
markers.textHtml: Fort Morninglord is a keep looking over the River Chionthar. In 1476 DR, the entire garrison of paladins disappeared overnight and every stone in the fort was blackened and every door and window fused shut. The High Observer of Elturgard feared the fort was contaminated by some unknown evil and ordered it sealed. All entrances were bricked up and thus it remained by 1489 DR. The paladins of the Order of the Companion maintain a fortified encampment close by, and use it as a base from which to patrol the surrounding land, guarding Fort Morninglord against curious trespassers and adventurers.

markers.name: Fort Tufenk
markers.textHtml: Fort Tufenk, also known as 'the Fortress of Fire', is one of the four border keeps of Tethyr, collectively known as the Calimmon Garrisons. Like the other Calimmon Garrisons, this non-elaborate fortress was built from huge stones in the course of the 8th century DR. Its walls are 10-to-15 feet (3 à 4,50 m) thick and 20 feet (6 m) high, with a deep, empty moat around them. The legend is that the ridge upon which the fort was built was constructed by jann during the First Era of Skyfire to serve as a defense against Memnon's Army of Fire.

markers.name: Fortress of the Half-Demon
markers.textHtml: The Fortress of the Half-Demon is a ruined stone fort located north of Rashemen. It was built in antiquity by the Nar people and has a huge iron gate fashioned into the face of a leering demon. One of the gates is half-open, thereby the name 'Half-Demon'. The fortress is square in shape and has a huge stone keep in the center of the courtyard. Underneath the fortress are a huge number of catacombs and passages, at least some of which lead to pocket planes which were used as extra burial space long ago.

markers.name: Frost Keep
markers.textHtml: Frost Keep is a frost giant fortress constructed from glacial frost and ice. The giants are known to disrupt trade caravans along the Northern Means to the southwest. As well as selling the stolen wares and using the magic to empower themselves, they will cook and eat the tradesmen as well as their animals.

markers.name: Glarondar
markers.textHtml: Glarondar is a fortress of black stone in Aglarond. It is vital to the realm's security, especially from Thay and constant fears of invasion. The material that the fortress is constructed out of is reminiscent of obsidian and appears to be virtually indestructible; the black rocks were raised from under the Umber Marshes by King Brindor's magic, his wizards, and galeb duhrs sometime before his death in 1128 DR. By the mid–14th century DR, Glarondar was surrounded by a small town. A large chunk of the town’s population was half-elves who lived among humans with few to no conflicts, and often traded with less-civilized half-elves from Yuirwood.

markers.name: Hammer Hall
markers.textHtml: Hammer Hall is a log house and adjoining stables, surrounded by a wooden palisade, built by an adventuring group to explore the nearby abandoned dawrven stronghold known as Halls of the Hammer.

markers.name: High Horn
markers.textHtml: High Horn is a huge Cormyte military stronghold at the top of High Horn mountain. The stronghold guards the road to the west through High Horn Pass in order to prevent potential invaders from passing the Storm Horns. There are royal gem mines in this area also. In the winter, it is home to half of the entire army of Cormyr. The stronghold is often home to War Wizards as well. As of the mid 14th century DR, it had never come under serious siege or attack.

markers.name: Hornshield
markers.textHtml: This strong castle atop Mount Shalandragar contains a small but proficient brasscasting, glassblowing, and ironworking industry.

markers.name: Iltakar Garrison
markers.textHtml: Iltakar Garrison, also known as 'the Katar Garrison', is one of the four border keeps of Tethyr, collectively known as the Calimmon Garrisons. Like the other Calimmon Garrisons, this non-elaborate fortress was built from huge stones in the course of the 8th century DR. Its walls are 10-to-15 feet (3 à 4,50 m) thick and 20 feet (6 m) high, with a deep, empty moat around them. Out of the four Garrisons, Iltakar experiences the most attacks by raiders from the Calim Desert.

markers.name: Ironfang Keep
markers.textHtml: Ironfang Keep was originally one of twelve fortresses constructed by the fire giants of the kingdom of Helligheim on its southern border, between –30,000 and –24,000 DR. The giants abandoned the keep after –24,000 DR and it supposedly stood empty ever since. However, there are stories of entire orc tribes in the area being abducted by shadowy creatures, and myriad elves, giants, and even some dragons also disappeared in the region. Beastly hybrid creatures are encountered in its vicinity, which leads credence to rumors of someone experimenting on living beings within the Keep. Ironfang Keep became a place of mystery to the peoples of the Moonsea, and all those who tried to enter were later found mad, delirious or killed in horrible ways. It is suspected that mysterious and very powerful mages live within Ironfang Keep, but it is unknown who they are and what objectives they have.

markers.name: Ironspur
markers.textHtml: Ironspur is one of the three major dwarven holds in the northern Galena Mountains. The city is a rugged settlement situated amidst harsh and dangerous mountainous terrain. The nearby mountains are mined for silver, iron, and bloodstone, while the city itself is renowned for its forges. Most food and agricultural products have to be imported from nearby Helmsdale. The dwarves developed a surprisingly good relationship with the half-orcs of Palischuk following their refounding of that city in the mi-to-late 14th century DR.

markers.name: Keep of Sorrows
markers.textHtml: The Keep of Sorrows is a granite fortress, surrounded by a double ring of walls, that is said to be impregnable.

markers.name: Kepetur
markers.textHtml: Kepetur is an abandoned Mulhorandi prayer tower, built in the form of a massive obelisk, that held historical significance in the conflict between Mulhorand and the Red Wizards circa the 10th century DR. Priests of Anhur managed to hold off the Red Wizards at Kepetur for numerous tendays, but eventually fell when the demon lord Eltab appeared in the Realms to join the conflict. It is said that Kepetur houses many abandoned Mulhorandi relics.

markers.name: Mavalgard
markers.textHtml: Mavalgard is a minor keep and surrounding village founded by the Impilturan family of Clan Maval.

markers.name: Mithral Hall
markers.textHtml: One of the best known dwarven strongholds in Faerûn, originally a mithral mine belonging to Clan Battlehammer. Sunken into the valley known as Keeper's Dale, the main entrance to Mithral Hall is kept by two huge, well guarded granite portals. Once inside the city, one is faced with a maze of tunnels and numerous caves and rooms. The tunnels and chambers of Mithral Hall are a self-contained city complete with taverns, forges, workshops, homes, and shrines to Moradin, Clangeddin, and Dumathoin. After being built by Gandalug Battlehammer and his kin, Mithral Hall accumulated a long and fascinating history filled with all manner of heroes, villains, victory and defeat. As of 1479 DR, the peace treaty signed in 1372 by Bruenor Battlehammer and the orc king Obould still holds true.

markers.name: Monguldarath
markers.textHtml: Monguldarath is a small, fortified keep that belongs to Tethyrean Counts of Fyraven. It is built atop a ridge overlooking the waters of the Firedrake Bay. Surrounding the keep and its farms are groves of duskwood trees. Like many places in Tethyr, Monguldarath is rumored to be haunted by phantoms from the events of the Ten Black Days of Eleint in 1347.

markers.name: Mount Noblesse
markers.textHtml: Mount Noblesse is a fortified manor which, as of the late 14th century CR, was one of the bases of operations of the Order of the Silver Chalice — a knightly order dedicated to the goddess Siamorphe the Noble. The manor served as their chapter house, where their primary work and training was conducted.

markers.name: Morninggold Keep
markers.textHtml: Morninggold Keep, also called Gold Keep and Castle Morninggold, is the county seat of County Morninggold in the Golden Marches of Tethyr. The small keep is located in a broad valley around a corner of foothills. It is a fortified house, constructed with granite. Attached to the back of the house is a circular tower, which is older than the rest of the structure. Its smooth rounded stones are a yellowed fieldstone, which looks like gold when the setting sun shines on it. The keep and its county belong to the Gulderhorn family of Tethyr, who founded the Order of the Silver Chalice — a knightly order dedicated to the goddess Siamorphe the Noble — in the mid-14th century DR. For some time in the 14th century, it was the home of then-countess and later queen Zaranda Star.

markers.name: Nethwatch Keep
markers.textHtml: The fortress of Nethwatch Keep is under the command of Tharchion Eseldra Yeth, a female human vampire spellcaster. She was appointed as tharchion of Lapendrar circa 1387 DR and still holds this position as of the late 15th century.

markers.name: Omvudurth
markers.textHtml: Omvudurth is a sprawling fortress made of crude dark stone. It is the stronghold of a small army of goblins.

markers.name: Rauvinwatch Keep
markers.textHtml: As of the late 14th century DR, Rauvinwatch Keep was the headquarters for the Argent Legion, the grand army that comprised soldiers from the member-cities of the Confederacy of the Silver Marches.

markers.name: Srindin Castle
markers.textHtml: Srindin was once an elven explorer who, after a life of exploration and adventure across all the Realms, retired in the Anauroch. There, he discovered a hidden fertile valley. He built a small castle where he lived as a hermit. After his death, the valley was named after him and some adventurers arrived at Srindin's castle in order to find his treasures, but some magic protected them.

markers.name: Swordcrag
markers.textHtml: The tall grey rocky crag, which has given the name to the village, stands as a marker, visible for miles. This small mining village was conquered during an invasion of the orcs of the Dragonspine Mountains in −87 DR. Since then the orcs control the mines below the imposing crag.

markers.name: Thornhold
markers.textHtml: Thornhold, also known as Stoneshaft Hold among its dwarven residents, is a fortress owned by Lord Stoneshaft of the Stoneshaft clan of mountain dwarves.

markers.name: Tordraken
markers.textHtml: The Castle of Tordraken was the ducal keep of the Duchy of Draknor and later the Cape Velen duchy. It is built atop a tall sea cliff and has ballistae and catapult batteries installed in case of naval assault.

markers.name: Undumor
markers.textHtml: Undumor, the Hollow City, previously known as Emmech, is a fortress located at the mouth of the River Umber.

markers.name: Val-Murthag
markers.textHtml: Val-Murthag was a fortress-city that existed during ancient empire of Narfell. It served as a vital bulwark against the Raumathari empire to the east, and as the empire's profane center of worship, where they could freely call upon the demons of the Abyss. The fortress was destroyed by Raumathari battle-magic in −160 DR. All that remains of Val-Murthag above ground are a series of collapsed stone walls. Deep below the surface however, remain ruined dungeons that still hold imprisoned demons, ghostly remnants of Nar priests, as well as magic artifacts and specifications of unholy rituals.

markers.name: Xantharl's Keep
markers.textHtml: Xantharl's Keep features a central keep, its surrounding village and a fortified wall. During the late 15th century DR, the keep was protected by 150 defenders, over twice the population of the accompanying village, and the keep's shield dwarf commander reported to the marchion of Mirabar.

markers.name: Abarat's Folly
markers.textHtml: Abarat's Folly is an ancient elven tower made of magically hardened ivory. The fourth floor and roof are unfinished. This magical location is a nexus of portals created by Abarat the Alabaster, an elf mage from the 7th century DR. Monsters attacked the tower before he could finish it, but when his fellow mages arrived to help, they found no sign of him or the monsters, and he was never heard from again. Over the centuries, the interacting magical fields of his portals have distorted and weakened each other, making this place a hotspot for monster activity and a treasure trove of world- and plane-hopping opportunities.

markers.name: Agatha's Lair
markers.textHtml: Agatha's lair, also known as Agatha's grove, is the lair of the banshee Agatha, the 'ghost of Neverwinter wood'. It is at the end of a path whose entrance is marked by a group of birch trees, and protected by magic. In the late 14th century DR, the people from the nearby village of Conyberry treated Agatha with respect and called her a guardian and a friend.

markers.name: Anhaurz
markers.textHtml: In 1385 DR, during the Spellplague, the city was hit by azure fire, melting the city and killing its every inhabitant. By 1478 DR, So-Kehur was assigned the restoration and stewardship of the fortress by Szass Tam. Under his rulership, the city was prided for its impressive garrison capable of withstanding any attack.

markers.name: Ascore
markers.textHtml: Ascore was a port city of the dwarven nation of Delzoun on the northern shores of the Narrow Sea during the time of the Netherese Empire. However, after the collapse of Netheril, as the Narrow Sea dwindled, the dwarves commenced a gradual withdrawal from Ascore around −333 DR, steadily abandoning the former port city to the sands. By the 14th century DR, it was a lonely, windswept ruin, reputed to be haunted by ancient evil. The dwarves built their docks and even their ships wholly of stone, on a characteristically massive scale. In the ruined city, these were still visible, half-buried in the sand. Thirteen tall, pentagonal pyramids stood in a circle in the very center of Ascore, carved all of a strange red stone. From afar, these were a bizarre landmark for adventurers on the The Long Run journey along the edge of Anauroch.

markers.name: Bell in the Depths
markers.textHtml: The Bell in the Depths is the name given to the underwater ruins of Northkeep, a legendary island kingdom that was the first human citadel in the Moonsea region. In 400 DR, 40,000 mages, shamans, witch doctors, and priests of all inhuman species gathered on the shore of the Moonsea, and brought the vengeance of their gods down upon the human interlopers. Their gods destroyed their priests for disturbing them, but they also sank Northkeep beneath the waves. The upper reaches of Northkeep—its slender, now-broken spires—can be seen beneath the water by boats that sail near by. This is not attempted often, as the region is said to be haunted by the original defenders of Northkeep. On foggy nights, the bells can be heard as far away as Hillsfar.

markers.name: Blaskaltar
markers.textHtml: The settlement of Blaskaltar was home to the First Shrine of Akadi, the Queen of Air, her first shrine known in Faerûn and the holiest site of her church. However, the structure has long since been erased by time, the elements, and neglect, leaving only a mound. Nevertheless, every Midsummer, the faithful of Akadi from all around Faerûn, after traveling for tendays, gather here for their only calendar festival. In this way, they remember the history of their church so they do not forget their past nor are doomed to repeat it.

markers.name: Bloodstone
markers.textHtml: Bloodstone was a settlement in Bloodstone Pass, the only real accessible link between the lands of Vaasa and Damara and an important commanding point for the trade of the semi-precious gem known as bloodstone. It was within the Barony of Bloodstone and later became the capital of the Kingdom of Bloodstone encompassing Damara and Vaasa. In 1460 DR, the Warlock Knights of Vaasa rebelled against the Kingdom of Bloodstone, and in 1469 DR, they burned Bloodstone to the ground. As of 1480 DR, the site was garrisoned by the Warlock Knights, and the settlement remained in ruins through the turn of the 16th century DR.

markers.name: Brassune
markers.textHtml: Brassune was the first city the genasi of Akanûl built after the Spellplague, atop the ruins of a Shyran city. However, in 1429 DR, Brassune was attacked by the Abolethic Sovereignty and destroyed, with the survivors fleeing to Airspur. As of 1479 DR, the ruins of Brassune are still attacked by the minions of the Abolethic Sovereignty, and for this reason the genasi military organization known as the Firestorm Cabal still watches over its ruins.

markers.name: Castle Al'hanar
markers.textHtml: Castle Al'hanar is a ruined fortress built during the time of the Kingdom of Eltabranar. It is rumored to contain more magic in one location than any other place since the reign of the Imaskari. This castle was once the base of the Knights of the Eternal Dragon, an order of Myrkul who swore themselves to the destruction of the Everlasting Wyrm, a green dragon dracolich dwelling in the Sharawood.

markers.name: Castle D'aerthe
markers.textHtml: Circa 1368 DR, a half-orc wizard named Arrayan Faylin inadvertently activated a Book of Creation and caused a replica of Castle Perilous to begin growing atop an orc burial ground in the region. The sellswords Artemis Entreri and Jarlaxle claimed the castle for themselves shortly afterward. They dubbed the fortress 'Castle D'aerthe' and claimed the region as the 'Kingdom of D'aerthe', provoking Gareth Dragonsbane and his army to lay siege to them. The battle was brief, for the occupants had abandoned the castle immediately once the siege began. They later claimed that whole fiasco had been a ploy to accelerate Gareth's annexation of Vaasa.

markers.name: Castle Daggerdale
markers.textHtml: Lord Kirkland Morn's sturdy stone keep is the official seat of Kirkland's rule, he brings his court with him when he travels to Dagger Falls. Castle Daggerdale houses over a hundred mounted soldiers known as the Morn Riders ; they patrol the roads and borders of the dale.

markers.name: Castle Tethyr
markers.textHtml: Castle Tethyr was once the palace of the royalty of Tethyr before its fall in 1347 DR. In its day, it was the largest castle in the Lands of Intrigue. After its fall, hordes of undead infested the ruins, including ghasts, ghosts, haunts, and shadows—all the spirits of those who died in the betrayal that happened when the castle was burned to the ground.

markers.name: Citadel Rashemar
markers.textHtml: As of 1359 DR, the citadel was overrun by the Grand Army of the Tuigan when they invaded the nation of Rashemen. The defending troops held out for three months into the following year, but finally succumbed to the Tuigan besiegers. The horde slew everyone remaining within the fortress, and leveled every last structure.

markers.name: Conyberry
markers.textHtml: Conyberry was a small village consisting of around a dozen houses standing close to the eastern side of Neverwinter Wood, sheltering within the forest edge. In the late 14th century DR, an unusual defender of Conyberry was the elven banshee named Agatha, whom the villagers looked on almost in affection as a protector and friend. However, in the early 15th century DR, members of the Uthgardt Gray Wolf Tribe attacked and destroyed the town. While the Gray Wolfs maintained their alliance with the Netherese, they used Conyberry as a place to store supplies and as training grounds, but after the Netherese were destroyed, the barbarians abandoned the town and left it laying in ruins.

markers.name: Dajaan
markers.textHtml: The ruins of a large port city thought to have once been the capital of the kingdom of Tathtar.

markers.name: Dalelost
markers.textHtml: Dalelost is a ruined city lying in the Land of the Lions. Once upon a time, this settlement was a competitor of Yhep for much of the local pearl trade. But despite its prosperity, over the course of a decade several disasters and misfortunes—the nature of which was forgotten to history—left Dalelost in ruins and abandoned by its people. It is thought that greater undead exist in lairs below the ruins.

markers.name: Delhumide
markers.textHtml: Delhumide was one of Thay greatest cities and bastions of power. When the Red Wizards rebelled, they destroyed the town, using the darkest sort of sorcery to accomplish their task. In 1375 DR, the ground where the city's ruins still stood was still unclean. Demons walked there and a man could suffer from madness or leprosy just by venturing there. The surrounding tharch is now an agricultural sector producing wheat and corn.

markers.name: Dragonspear Castle
markers.textHtml: Dragonspear Castle was a dwarven fortress built on the edge of the High Moor in the mid-13th century DR and destroyed at the end of the same century. The castle is infamous for the inter-planar portal located in the dungeon beneath its keep. This portal links the castle to Avernus, the first level of the Nine Hells. Aside from the portal, perhaps the most notable feature of the castle's keep is the skeleton of a colossal red dragon that drapes over the battered rooftop. After the castle's destruction, its ruins served as home to monstrous humanoids, and became the battleground for two wars between Faerûnians and devils from the Nine Hells.

markers.name: Dun-Tharos
markers.textHtml: As of 722 DR, the Great Druid of Leth erected a tree-fortress around the ruins of an old citadel named Dun-Tharos. The Circle of Leth controlled Dun-Tharos until 1371 DR, when the Chosen of Talona, known as the Rotting Man led an army of corrupted druids and volodnis to drive out the Circle. The Rotting Man killed and animated the fortress's trees and made Dun-Tharos his home, until the Rotting Man too was driven out. Unfortunately, the conflict that led to this occurrence, also roused the demonic energies that dwelt beneath the citadel. Still dead and rotting from the influence of Talona, infested with demons, Dun-Tharos became a place of immense danger, ruled by several petty demon lords and their fiend-worshiping mortal cultists, the Fraternity of Tharos.

markers.name: Essembra
markers.textHtml:
Battledale, Dalelands.

The largest settlement in Battledale. Cottages and farms line the road leading to Battle Court, a walled compound encompassing about fifty buildings that includes a temple to Gond and a moated keep. Essembra was abandoned for years after Sembians and their Shadovar allies sacked it and burned it to the ground. Now the dragonborn Volsembra, Protector of Battledale, leads the reconstruction efforts, with help from adventurers willing to clear out ruined buildings inhabited by outlaws, wild beasts, or restless undead.

markers.name: Firetrees
markers.textHtml: In the 14th century DR, the small town of Firetrees was a farming community and a popular stop for trading caravans, either land-bound or for those ships navigating the River Alamber. It was named after its red-colored trees, that shine in the night with unnatural phosphorescence, and was known for its feasts and festivals, and for being one of the most important centers where the cult of Tiamat had more influence than the cult of Gilgeam in Unther.

markers.name: First Necropolis of Nykkar
markers.textHtml: The First Necropolis of Nykkar is a sacred site of temples and burial plots in ancient Calimshan where all classes beneath the rulers were interred from the time of Coramshan through the First Age of the Calimshan Empire. It is situated atop a wind-blasted plateau. The most distinguishing feature of the ruins is that of a large, wind-eroded vertical stone slab that stands at the far eastern edge of the plateau. While most of the inscription on it is illegible by centuries of scouring winds, the letters or word 'KAR' can be made out. Millennia after the necropolis fell into disuse, strange necromantic magic residues linger at the site.

markers.name: Flaming Tower
markers.textHtml: The Flaming Tower, also known as the Tower of Flame, is a massive ruined tower made of solid black granite. An elevated bonfire rests upon its roof, from which the tower's name is derived. The tower was originally built by fire giants. It later became populated by Zhentilar, before the Knights of Myth Drannor destroyed it and left it in ruins in 1350 DR. Zhentilar forces reoccupied the ruins shortly after and began to rebuild the tower. But after Zhentil Keep was destroyed by the shadovar in 1383 DR during the Shadowbane War, the Zhents left, and the Flaming Tower came to be occupied by elven members of the Eldreth Veluuthra, who hoped to drive out Daggerdale's humans.

markers.name: Fort Ilbratha
markers.textHtml: This fort was built to be the northernmost defense of Valashar, a short-lived kingdom within the Shoon Imperium in the 4th century DR. In 376 DR, with the magical sword Ilbratha in hand, Prince Azoun Obarskyr I of Cormyr overran the few defenders of the fort and razed it to the ground, with the campaign continuing deeper into the Shoon Imperium until his victorious sacking of Ithmong. The garrison's original name was lost; history recorded it as 'Fort Ilbratha' after the famed sword that destroyed it. The log palisade that once ringed the fort was entirely erased, but the stone foundations survived at least into the mid–14th century DR.

markers.name: Fortress Narder
markers.textHtml: Fortress Narder was built in antiquity by the Nar people as a gathering point for the Nar armies debarking for Raumathar. It was destroyed in –150 DR, during the last great conflict with Raumathar, by a spell that reduced it to rubble. Demonic presence was still lingering in the depths of the ancient fortress in the late 14th century.

markers.name: Galath's Roost
markers.textHtml: Galath's Roost is a fortress in Mistledale, occupied by its namesake, a bandit captain named Galath, during the 10th century DR. After Galath's death at the hands of Sembian soldiers, his ghost was rumored to haunt his fortress. This hasn't prevented the keep from being used as a base by drow infiltrators, Zhentarim smugglers, and—in the early 16th century—a bugbear leader and his a war party.

markers.name: Gazzeth
markers.textHtml: The name 'Gazzeth' was lost to history and, by the mid-1300s DR, the ruins were known simply as 'ruined orc city'. A legend formed around the city's forgotten name, claiming that if this name was ever discovered, then its long-dead inhabitants would rise, gather into an army, and march on Thentia. Given this legend surrounding the lost name, few in the Moonsea are eager to find it. Giant forms of many kinds of vermin infest the ruins, which keeps people away.
