markers.name: Gildenglade
markers.textHtml:
Gildenglade was once the second-largest settlement within Turmish, and a haven for non-human populations, home to many of the dwarves, half-elves, and elves of the nation. Its economy was based primarily upon woodcutting and mining. In 1423 DR, Mount Kolimnis of the Aphrunn Mountains erupted, burying the city under several hundred feet of ash and hot mud and killing almost all the inhabitants. Several years after the eruption, cold lava cracked open, revealing a series of caverns that led into the dead city.

<%>Some brave adventurers attempted to helm an excavation but were faced with a strangely-well preserved ghost town infested with deadly denizens of the Underdark.

markers.name: Glaurachyndaar
markers.textHtml: [not Myth Glauroch] Glaurachyndaar (formerly Myth Glaurach, the City of Scrolls) was an Eaerlanni garden city with ornate buildings and white domes, situated on the river Starsilver, a tributary of the river Delimbiyr, and built on the ruins of an Aryvandaaran city. The city was raided by orcs in 864 DR and destroyed. In its original state, the city’s mythal barred all fiends and anyone who had ever eaten the flesh of elves, dwarves or humans, but this was destroyed in the late 14th century and remains so. Other aspects of the mythal continue to function, such as the deactivation of certain spells.

markers.name: Hellgate Dell
markers.textHtml: Hellgate Keep was originally founded as Ascalhorn, an ancient elven city laying atop the peak of Ascal's Horn along the river Skull Creek. It was overtaken by demonic forces in the 9th century DR, and eventually destroyed in 1369 DR by an explosion wrought by an artifact known as the Gatekeeper's Crystal. The central ravine created by the destructive power of the Crystal became known as Grintharke's Gulch, an inverted-pyramid shaped gorge 180 m long on each side and 120 m deep. The site of this Keep's ruins came to be known as Hellgate Dell. It is a 21 m tall crag of debris that has been flung from the Keep during its destruction. Over time it became overgrown with moss and small shrubs. Only three sites remain prominently above the ruins: the Tower Arcanatorus, the Three Torn Towers, and an armory. The hill is surrounded by a ring of twenty-five large oak trees, five of which are actually treants tasked with guarding the area. An array of wild magic regions were left lingering throughout the surrounding area, as well as several layers of dead-magic zones nestled between them in the central ravine.

markers.name: Hilltop
markers.textHtml: The area around the tiny village of Hilltop is described as a very inhospitable land due to its cold climate and potential hazards, such as roaming monsters. The settlement was founded by ex-soldiers of Zhentil Keep who'd retired in disgust and wished a new life. In the 14th century DR, it was famous for hosting the “adventuring school” founded by Drogan Droganson, a renowned dwarf wizard and cleric of Mystra.

markers.name: Hisari
markers.textHtml: Hisari is an ancient, ruined sarrukh city that was later taken over and resettled by their descendants, the yuan-ti. It is located at the base of The Crown, the great volcano of the Sky Lizard Mountains, in a great basin in the jungle. It was originally a grand city, comprising numerous golden-domed structures made of white-washed bricks. Around the time of the Spellplague, it was swallowed into the earth beneath Chult and overgrown with vines and other vegetation. While it remained whole and intact, only the dome of its grand palace remained visible from the canopy of the Chultan jungle.

markers.name: Hlath
markers.textHtml: Hlath was one of the largest and most powerful city-states in the Vilhon Reach. It was an old walled city with streets of cobblestone that veered between mostly four and five-story buildings. The city walls rose 50 feet (15 m) up and its harbor was well-protected. Outside Hlath's walls were numerous long warehouses that stored the city's main export - lumber, specifically timber and exotic woods, the latter of which were shipped out in both carved and raw states. These were cut from the nearby Nunwood, under the watchful eye of the Emerald Enclave. Hlath was destroyed during the Spellplague and still lays in ruins as of the early 16th century DR.

markers.name: Hlaungadath
markers.textHtml: Hlaungadath is a ruined former flying city of Netheril. The city landed in the desert near the High Ice and was eventually abandoned, but the ruins remained largely intact. Hlaungadath's architecture is clearly Netherese, with square column bases and pointed arched windows. The city is filled with sand-covered ruined domes, colonnades, ramparts, and towers. The Bedine believe some kind of evil lives in the place, and over the years multiple tribes occupied then abandoned it due to the evil they found in their dreams but could not describe.

markers.name: Ilipur
markers.textHtml: Ilipur was a large port town functioning as an unloading point for goods that were moved overland to Elversult. When the Spellplague struck Faerûn in 1384 DR, the waters of the Sea of Fallen Stars receded, and many coastal settlements were left destitute and outright abandoned. Ilipur was one of those towns; by the late 15th century it is known as Haunted Ilipur. The empty seaport is littered with overturned skeletons of decaying ships. The town was not fully abandoned, however: a band of pirates called the Blue Dawn is in control of the remnants of the city.

markers.name: Iljak
markers.textHtml: Iljak was a port city and one of the largest and most powerful city-states in the Vilhon Reach. Its main industries were fishing and agriculture. It has a history of conflict with its neighbors Shamph and Hlath, and sometimes it fell under the control of Chondath. Iljak was destroyed during the Spellplague and still lays in ruins as of the early 16th century DR.

markers.name: Inthar
markers.textHtml: Inthar is a ruined fortress sitting atop a rocky sea crag. Its origins are unknown and it is avoided by almost all sailors. During the night time, eerie green lights shine within this fortress, silhouetting shadowy shapes, and faint whispers can be heard from afar. Some tales speak of there being a hidden shaft within the fortress that leads straight down to the Nine Hells of Baator.

markers.name: Iron Keep
markers.textHtml: Iron Keep is a small city named for the massive fortress of the same name that stands above it, and it served as the capital of the Northlander kingdom of Oman prior to that realm's destruction by giants and fomorians in the late 14th century DR. It lays abandoned and in ruins as of the late 15th century DR. The settlement lays on the eastern side of Iron Bay where a large valley meets the bleak shore. It sits along a long beach which provided space for the stowage and construction of Oman's famous longships. The castle is massive, a veritable bastion of reddish-black stone, the rusty color of which is said to be the source of its name. Before its fall, it was known as the strongest fortress possessed by any Northlander kingdom.

markers.name: Ironcloak
markers.textHtml: The ruins of Ironcloak are a brutal example of how the Emerald Enclave deals with those who damage the environment. In the 13th century DR, Ironcloak was a town ruled by Lord Ironcloak, a rich and powerful noble merchant who turned the settlement into a busy logging town, but he was ruthless and reckless in his pursuit of wealth, cutting down trees at a shocking speed and dumping rubbish in the Alaoreum River. After their warnings were ignored, the druids of the Emerald Enclave sent earth elementals destroy the settlement, and the Alaoreum River itself flooded its banks and inundated the town, wiping away buildings and people. In the years that followed, adventurers sometimes searched the ruins of Ironcloak, looking for Lord Ironcloak's fortune, but found little of note.

markers.name: Ishau
markers.textHtml: Ishau is a sunken village located at the bottom of Refuge Bay. It is home to reef and hunter sharks, along with plesiosaurs, and periodically, sea hags navigate through the ruins looking for treasure or the remains of the dead.

markers.name: Karse
markers.textHtml: Karse was founded by the Cult of Karsus shortly after Karsus's Folly in -298 DR. It was located at the base of a tall butte of red stone which was thought by the cultists to be the remains of Karsus himself. The city was reduced to rubble by the Cult of Karsus itself just ten years after its founding due to internal strife sown by the lich Wulgreth. Wulgreth then moved into the remains of the city to ensure that it would not be rebuilt. The ruins of Karse are overgrown with dark forest. In the midst of this forest is the Black Glade, a ring of thirteen massive dire oak trees. These strange trees are somehow connected to the thirteen red, pentagonal pyramids found at the center of the ruins of Ascore.

markers.name: Kheldrivver
markers.textHtml: Founded as monastic community worshiping Oghma, the village was destroyed several times until abandoned and taken over by trolls in 1166 DR. Finally, the warrior Kheldrivver organized a force to deal with the trolls and rebuilt the monastery.

markers.name: Leilon
markers.textHtml: Leilon is a small mining settlement that also serves as a stop for travelers on the High Road. Its sits on the edge of a shallow mudflat that extends to the Sea of Swords, with the crags of the Sword Mountains looming over the town. Leilon was abandoned following the Spellplague but was rebuilt and resettled in the late 15th century DR with a wooden palisade and surrounding ditch. The original town contained stone cottages with slate or thatched roofs. As the town was rebuilt, much of the initial buildings were simple wooden constructions. Leilon's most defining landmark, the High Tower of Thalivar, was erected in the center of town by the wizard Thalivar sometime in the late 13th century DR, and was abandoned not long after when the wizard disappeared.

markers.name: Lhesper
markers.textHtml: Lhesper was destroyed by a pack of rampaging gnolls in 7th century DR. As of 1372 DR, Lhesper was taken over by members from the Coiled Cabal - a secretive order of yuan-ti wizards devoted to Sseth, the World Serpen - who were searching for the Emeralds of Merrshaulk.

markers.name: Lhuvenhead
markers.textHtml: Lhuvenhead was a lucrative river town with decrepit docks, crowded with barges and keelboats all year long. A number of inns, taphouses, and festhalls catered to the riverfolk who worked on the Rauvin. The village served as a summer retreat for the nobility of Sundabar and Everlund. However, in 1485 DR, Lhuvenhead was destroyed by the orc forces of Many-Arrows led by Hartusk as they approached Everlund. The village had already been abandoned so there were no casualties, but all the docks and buildings were burned or torn down.

markers.name: Lost Ajhuutal
markers.textHtml: The city's name stems from a common misconception that it is the lost capital of the ancient Maridlands. Actually, Lost Ajhuutal is a partially submerged and ruined city that for millennia served as home to a community of aranea, a magical species of sentient, shape-changing spider-like creatures. The city was constructed of stone, elevated above the swamp and infused with magic, but over the centuries it fell in a state of ruin due to the aranea lacking the resources to rebuild. The part of the city that remained virtually intact across the centuries is its undercity. Most the aranea are a relatively pacifistic people, but a third of the population are priests who revere a minor demon lord Zanassu, the 'Spider That Waits', and who have more aggressive tendencies.

markers.name: Lost Xandar
markers.textHtml: Xandar was a capital city of the old kingdom of Tethyr. In the 3rd century DR, it was destroyed by a sudden eruption from the otherwise quiescent Kossuth's Eyes volcano. The locals are well aware that there is a hidden city beneath the pine-covered foothills of the mountains, but although the promise of royal treasures is tempting, the effort needed to dig through the rock would be enormous and it would ruin their fields and pastures. A few attempts were made using magic, but they only found common entombed homes. Lost Xandar remains a subject of interest to sages and scholars, but the issue of gaining access to the buried site is a near-insurmountable obstacle.

markers.name: Lothen of the Silver Spires
markers.textHtml: The ruins of a majestic city that once belonged to the fallen elven kingdom of Eaerlann. Today, its silver-topped spires are broken and covered in vines. It is a major site of elven heritage and history. While beautiful, it is dangerous, as it is often contested by orc tribes (like the Tanglethorns) and scavengers looking for ancient elven magic.

markers.name: Lundeth
markers.textHtml: Lundeth was a village scaling the steep sides of a rock pinnacle surrounded by the Salt Flats. It was made up of sun-fire clay huts and had a deep water well. This village was founded and inhabited by a Bedine tribe named the Goldor, but sometime prior to 1360 DR, the Zhentarim assaulted the village and seized it as a base for their western operations. After the 1360s, as the construction of the Black Road was finished, the Zhents left. All that remains within the village are those undead remnants of the Goldor.

markers.name: Lyran's Hold
markers.textHtml: In the late 14th century DR, Lyran's Hold was the stronghold of the undead Lyran. It was defended by numerous animated armors.

markers.name: Mbala
markers.textHtml: Mbala is an abandoned town atop a sheer, 1,800 feet (600 m) tall plateau. A narrow path winds around the plateau for three miles, including several switchbacks, effectively prohibiting an army from marching upon the town. Mbala was once the seat of a small Chultan kingdom but, at an unknown point in time, a green hag slaughtered and ate all of the town's citizens. After the population's demise, Myrkul claimed Mbala as his territory and demanded an offering from any passing travelers.

markers.name: Memnonnar
markers.textHtml: Memnonnar is the remains of the city center of Memnon from the time when it was the capital city of the kingdom of Memnonnar under the Great Pasha Memnon, between ca. –6800 and –6000 DR. Millennia after the destruction of the city, the red bricks of the ruins still are hot to the touch. This is because the Great Brass Gate, the original portal through which Memnon came to Toril, still functions deep below the surface. The subterranean ruins contain luxurious palace chambers buried under smoking debris. Near the seeping Great Brass Gate, the tunnels are so hot that they glow, and the air is full of unbreathable smoke. The old mining tunnels below the ruins are inhabited by fire elementals, mephits, salamanders, and other creatures of fire. The Great Brass Gate itself is defended by a powerful and loyal genie named Ufbrann. Defending against the potential horrors of Memnonnar's ruins is one of the key jobs of the troops garrisoned in Memnon. Additionally, Memnon will annually send out a heavily armed contingent of guards and stonecutters to plumb the ruins of Memnonnar, scavenging stone they can use in building repairs or for decorative purposes.

markers.name: Mezro
markers.textHtml: Before its disappearance in the Spellplague, Mezro was the largest civilized population center in Chult, and a holy place to the Tabaxi people, being founded by their god Ubtao. When the Spellplague struck the world, to save the city from destruction, as well as from Ras Nsi ambitions, the Chosen of Ubtao transported their city to a demiplane. They left behind artificial ruins to discourage troublemakers from finding what really happened to the city. For the rest of the world, Mezro was destroyed during the Spellplague. By 1479 DR, the artificial ruins of Mezro are an undead- and monster-infested site.

markers.name: Mhiilamniir
markers.textHtml: Mhiilamniir was a city of the elven realm of Eaerlann. It was destroyed in the demonic uprising from Hellgate Keep, and most of it was overgrown with moss and ivy. The female green dragon Chloracridara claimed the whole city and its environs as her domain. She had lived in the ruins of the temple to Corellon Larethian for six hundred years by 1370 DR. Her rabid paranoia and offspring's hunting made the surrounding area quite dangerous, too hazardous for elven pilgrims to visit any of the temples.

markers.name: Morningstar Hollows
markers.textHtml: Morningstar Hollows was once a small fishing village, but was abandoned due to the continual flooding of the Alaoreum River. After having been taken over by bogs and swamps, the place became home to a tribe of lizardfolk. However, by 1479 DR, the lizardfolk were gone, driven away by a slew of adventurers seeking fame and fortune. The lizardfolk moved on to the Wetwoods while a gang of bandits took over the village, ambushing scar pilgrims.

markers.name: Mussum
markers.textHtml: Mussum rose to become of the most popular ports in the Vilhon Reach, until 472 DR, when it was struck by the Panicked Plague. Over the course of a single night, known as the 'Eve of the Panicked Plague', over three-quarters of the population perished, their skin turned green and disfigured by welts and abscesses. Natural and magical cures could do nothing to stop it. The terrified survivors fled the city so quickly they left all their belongings behind. Mussum had remained plague-ridden, empty, and abandoned ever since. The only inhabitants are those monsters that can resist the plague and who gnaw on the bones of the dead. Few dare step foot inside ruined Mussum, and none lives to tell of it, as the Panicked Plague continues to stalk its streets. Surprisingly, the area of Mussum is home to the House of Holy Suffering, a major temple to Ilmater.

markers.name: Myth Drannor
markers.textHtml:
Founded in -3983 DR, Myth Drannor, formerly known as the city of Song, was the former capital city of Cormanthyr in the forest of Cormanthor and the greatest city in Faerûn. A magical metropolis constructed by elves but home to people of all species, invited into Cormanthor on the establishment of the Dales Compact. It was considered by many to be the pinnacle of shared knowledge, culture, and civilization in Faerûn. But Myth Drannor fell in 711 DR, torn by internal strife, wracked by magical experiments gone awry, and finally invaded by fiends-the Army of Darkness-during the Weeping War, which led to the 'Retreat', the great migration of elves from Faerûn to the isle of Evermeet. For centuries, the elves tried to keep the place sealed, refusing to let any into the ruins.

Until 1374 DR when an Elven Crusade returned from Evermeet to reclaim Myth Drannor and restored the mythal (a powerful magic which extended the lives of the city's inhabitants, controlled the weather, and had other wondrous uses). By the following year, Myth Drannor became a flourishing city-state, on the threshold of a new golden age. But after over a century of tension and conflict, war broke out with the Shadovar and led to the flying city of Thultanthar crashing into the city in 1487. The mythal failed again, and the wreckage of Thultanthar made the ruins of Myth Drannor more dangerous and unpredictable than ever. No one knows whether the mythal can be repaired again.

Nowadays, elves and half elves are rebuilding the place with the help of some dwarves (foundations stage). It's interesting to note there are a lot a cellars under the ruins, some with hidden magic items forgotten.

markers.name: Myth Lharast
markers.textHtml: Founded in the late 4th century DR as a place of worship for zealous Selûnites, Myth Larast was stripped from Faerûn and transferred into a demiplane in the early 8th century. Accounts and descriptions of the city were subsequently lost over time. Only devout Selûnite priests could glean its true location in the Realms. Myth Lharast appeared as a spectral city, featuring splendid walls and seven Star Spires that glistened in the moonlight.

markers.name: Myth Ondath
markers.textHtml: Myth Ondath, formerly known as Yrlaancel and Ondathel, was once a haven of peace in the North, its doors always open to refugees of conflicts and disasters. The people of the city dedicated themselves to the dogma and goals of Eldath, and toiled to make their home a place where all peoples could dwell together, nurture one another, and reap the benefits of a pacifistic life. However, in the 7th century DR, the city was destroyed by the armies of Iyraclea, the Ice Queen of the Great Glacier and the preeminent cleric of Auril in the Realms, in a magical explosion that also consumed the Ice Queen’s legions. The only remnants left of the City of Peace, aside from its rubble and ruins, were a few survivors who were scattered across the North in the years after, including the pacifistic orcs known as the ondonti.

markers.name: Nangalore
markers.textHtml: Ka-Nanji, meaning the 'Hanging Garden of Dreams', was constructed under the rule of Queen Zalkoré of Omu. Once Zalkoré became corrupted and turned into a medusa by bargaining with erinyes, the army made the queen abdicate the throne and exiled her here. The garden became her lair and Ka-Nanji came to be called Nangalore, meaning the 'Garden of Lost Dreams'. The gardens of Nangalore are filled with lush creepers and jungle plants, and stone statues in various states of ruin stand around the vast complex. Three main domed areas lay in the farther parts of the garden, some having collapsed and fallen deeper into the jungles of Chult. Some carvings and statues were defaced when Zalkoré unleashed her anger at the different sculptures throughout the complex.

markers.name: Old Owl Well
markers.textHtml: Originally known as Quesseer, this settlement was built in –2368 DR by the Empire of Netheril as a military outpost and watering hole. Over the millennia, it was claimed and abandoned by various parties, but always remained a strategically important watering hole. In the late 15th century DR, Old Owl Well is nothing but a ruined watchtower that has nothing but a few crumbling walls. However, the well still pumps clean, fresh water, and it is a popular rest stop for caravan traffic, especially between Yartar and Neverwinter.

markers.name: Oreme
markers.textHtml: Oreme was the capital of the Sarrukh empire of Isstosseffifil (the Land of the Lost Sea), remaining after the collapse of the empire in –33,800 DR. Following the destruction of their kingdom, sixty sarrukh retreated into lichdom and would spend the ongoing centuries ruling the ruins of Oreme in turns while the others hibernated. By 1375 DR, mainly ruins and the circular rubble of its great towers remained on the surface, but many of its subterranean structures were intact and some of them were said to be connected with the Underdark. The descendants of the asabi who once loyally served the sarrukh continue to inhabit the ruins and believe it is their duty to protect the lich kings.

markers.name: Orlsfall
markers.textHtml: Orlsfall is a mining town turned ghost town. It grew rapidly in a rush to exploit the mine, and was then abandoned when the mine ran dry. It remained uninhabited until just before 1370 DR, when opportunistic bandits led by Timok the Tusk moved in, conducting raids from there into the nearby Greenfields region.

markers.name: Orogoth
markers.textHtml: Orogoth is a large and sprawling ruined villa. It was built in −2482 DR by the Netherese Orogoth family, and served as their headquarters for their dark magical experiments and efforts to gain control over dragons and to assume dragon shape. It is one of the most dangerous locations in the High Moor and known for the presence of black dragons. In the 14th century, the ruin of became well-known among adventurers, and even children heard tales of it. However, treasure seekers who visited were killed, sent fleeing, or transformed into beasts. As of the late 15th century DR, a black dracolich and his descendants continue to lair in Orogoth.

markers.name: Orolunga
markers.textHtml: Orolunga is a ruined city of ancient Chult. As of the late 15th century DR, all that remains is a single, 60-feet (18 m) tall ziggurat. Its facade is lined with numerous staircases, decorated with symbols of Ubtao and entirely covered by tropical vegetation. The ruined city is home to a number of chwinga spirits.

markers.name: Port Castigliar
markers.textHtml: Port Castigliar is a small settlement tucked away in a rarely visited part of a bay opening into the Shining Sea. Lacking a dock or any place to moor a ship or boat of any size, it is a port in name only, but it is a perfect place to land in Chult without being noticed. The settlement itself consists only of seven tin huts and a well-stocked supply depot along an open beach. Port Castigliar is infamous for its foul hot, and muggy weather with frequent rains. It was overrun by undead in the late 15th century DR, but subsequently resettled.

markers.name: Praskallest
markers.textHtml: Praskallest was the former estate of the Count of Carrelath in the days when Erlkazar was a territory of Tethyr, prior to the mid-14th century DR. As of the 1370s, Praskallest was the estate manor of the Tanistan Barony, who ruled over the Plains of Pehrrifaal and the garrison of Five Spears Hold, and defended the country's southern borders in alliance with the Shieldbreaker ogres from Rivenshield.

markers.name: Pros
markers.textHtml: Pros was a small port town functioning as an unloading point for goods that were moved overland to Elversult. When the Spellplague struck Faerûn in 1384 DR, the waters of the Sea of Fallen Stars receded, and many coastal settlements were left destitute and outright abandoned. Pros was one of those towns; by the 15th century it is hardly worth mentioning except as a memory of the past.

markers.name: Purple Spire
markers.textHtml: An ancient and battered tower that stands over the ancient ruins of an Imaskari complex.

markers.name: Rasilith
markers.textHtml: Rasilith was the capital city of the ancient realm of Thaeravel, a major city of Netheril, and later a settlement of the returned Shadovar. After its abandonment and the coming of Anauroch, the city was partially buried by sand dunes but still stood intact. It has thick gray stone walls, a central fortress ringed with alabaster towers, and a connection to a series of underground caverns and chambers with rivers of cool water, linked to the Underdark. These were inhabited by the phaerimms and their laerti slaves, until the return of Thultanthar in 1372 DR and the following extermination of phaerimms. Rasilith was then once again resettled, this time with Bedine people. They became urbanized and were assimilated into Netherese culture.

markers.name: Ring of Gray Flames
markers.textHtml: The Ring of Gray Flames are a series of ruined towers that dated back to the ancient empire of Raumathar. The five towers of the ring were set in a circular arrangement each of which was topped with corona of gray flame. Most are ruined, but two are intact, and mechanical sounds and grinding noises come from within. From atop all five, gray flames spout. Divine magic doesn't work within or near the towers.

markers.name: Ruin of Ascarle
markers.textHtml: [not Ascarl] Ascarle, the 'Sunken City,' is an underwater elven city swept into the ocean by melting glaciers and destroyed by the drow. The somewhat-ruined buildings and walkways are formed from crystal and red coral. The city is filled with air and was used as headquarters for the Kraken Society as of the 14th century DR.

markers.name: Ruins of Amazandar
markers.textHtml: Amazandar, also known as the City of Gems, was the capital of the Lost Kingdom of Anauria, founded in –339 DR. In 111 DR, pressures from the encroaching desert combined with large raids of goblins caused the collapse of the kingdom. Its survivors melted away into the surrounding kingdoms, melded into nomadic tribes or perished in the harsh conditions. Amazandar lay above rich veins of gems that could be reached by numerous deep shafts and tunnels. Circa 1374 DR, these mines were inhabited by the phaerimm.

markers.name: Ruins of Harpshield Castle
markers.textHtml: Harpshield Castle was once the seat of power for Harpshield, a small human realm that hoped to survive the fall of the Delimbiyran kingdom, but that fell to the drow in the mid-10th century DR. The castle's ruins can still be seen on top of a round hill rising from the southeastern tip of the Ardeep Forest. Only one tower and the walls of the ground floor remain. The ruins have been used as a landmark and campsite for those wandering the wilds, and as a home to a succession of nomads, bandits, and monsters, most recently (in the 1480s) an orc tribe. Below the castle is an underground complex known as the Dungeon of the Shield.

markers.name: Saarlik
markers.textHtml: Saarlik is a haunted town on the Arglander River, part of the ruins of Tathtar and the end of the Tethir Road. It is home to Sala-min Lion Warriors, and to numerous creatures, both magical and undead, placed there by the vampire Shyressa, who made it her home.

markers.name: Sekras
markers.textHtml: Sekras was once the center of worship of Sebek, the Lord of Crocodiles. The city thus became infested with werecrocodiles, prompting paladins of Osiris to eradicate them in 1183 DR. Sekras was reduced to ruins during this process, prompting many of the surviving werecrocodiles to flee to the Adder Swamp and the Lake of Salt. However, by the mid-to-late 14th century DR, the ruins were once again notable for the presence of werecrocodiles.

markers.name: Semberholme
markers.textHtml: Towering trees soar high above the city of Semberholme, creating a cathedral-like retreat for those elves who remain in Cormanthor. Limestone caves beneath the city provide access to Lake Sember. IIsivele Miritar, who led the restoration of Myth Drannor a century ago, and her husband Fflar Melruth, rule Semberholme, but the second fall of Myth Drannor has darkened their spirits. Ilsivele and Fflar have resigned themselves to lingering in Semberholme.

markers.name: Sessrenglade
markers.textHtml: Sessrenglade was the capital of Sessrendale, a realm destroyed centuries ago by Archendale in a brief war. Archendale's mercenaries razed everything. Few travel this devastated land, but that made it all the more appealing to Thraxata, an Adult Red Dragon that lairs in Blood Peak, a mountain overlooking Sessrenglade.

markers.name: Settlestone
markers.textHtml: The village of Settlestone was first inhabited by dwarves, fell into ruins, then was resettled in 1358 DR by the barbarians of Icewind Dale. It is a cold and windy place with mountains surrounding it on three sides. The dwarven village was built above ground, which was an unheard of creation for dwarven crafters at the time. Built to last, the buildings of Settlestone have large stone slabs of stone stacked together. The rebuilt settlement consists of both stone buildings and tents. The center of the village is Hengorot, the mead hall inside a deerskin tent large enough to sit 400 barbarians. The barbarians here live a very prosperous life since they handle the trading of dwarven goods to merchants.

markers.name: Shamph
markers.textHtml: Shamph, also known as the Crossroads City, was a trade city sitting at the junction of the Emerald Way and the Old Road. It was destroyed during the Spellplague and still lays in ruins as of the early 16th century DR.

markers.name: Shandaular (Ashanath)
markers.textHtml: Shandaular, later called the City of Weeping Ghosts, is a ruined city in Thesk, and once capital of the Nar realm of Ashanath. A two-way gate was established between Shandaular and the Council Hills in Shaar in −1064 DR, and thereafter the city expanded through the gate. Its name was shared with the southern portions of the city. Northern Shandaular was destroyed in −946 DR by the forces of the Nentyarch of Tharos, and the refugees fled through the gate and settled in Southern Shandaular. Numerous undead along with at least one nightshade are known to linger within the ruins of Northern Shandaular.

markers.name: Shandaular (Shaar)
markers.textHtml: Shandaular was a city in Ashanath. A two-way gate was established between Shandaular and the Council Hills in −1064 DR, and thereafter the city expanded through the gate. Its name was shared with the southern portions of the city. Northern Shandaular was destroyed in −946 DR, and the refugees fled through the gate and settled in Southern Shandaular, until they had to abandon the city in 211 DR when Mulhorand and Unther invaded the Council Hills. Today the remnants of Southern Shaudaular form buried ruins and tunnels beneath the Council Hills. The tribesmen of the Shaar use the more superficial tunnels and passageways as tombs to honor their dead.

markers.name: Shoonach
markers.textHtml: Shoonach is the former capital-complex of the Shoon Imperium, long fallen to ruin when the Imperium was destroyed in the 5th century DR. It lays lay on the north bank of the River Memnon in the region of Monrativi Teshy Mir, on the border between Tethyr and Calimshan. It is an enormous open-air dungeon that stretches for miles, home to treasures and dangers in equal measure. The ruins of Shoonach are actually a set of many smaller (but still massive) ruins—the cities of Iltakar and the Imperial City, the towns of Mamlukkar, Debukkher, and Agis, the fields and villages of the Fedhiyajar, and the Palace Mount itself.

markers.name: Shade
markers.textHtml: Thultanthar ('Shade' in the Loross language), also known as the Shade Enclave and the City of Shade, was a Netherese flying city situated in the middle of the Anauroch desert. It appeared as a floating inverted mountain of stone topped with harsh black spires and foreboding walls. In 1487 DR, Thultanthar was brought to ground by Elminster. It fell upon Myth Drannor, and was destroyed.

markers.name: The Shattered Tower
markers.textHtml: Long ago, phaerimm magic brought down the cloud castle of a storm giant. The castle is vast and now inhabited by elementals and a blue dragon.

markers.name: Skelem
markers.textHtml: Skelem was originally a small but thriving Netherese city hosting a cabal of powerful wizards allied with the church of Kossuth. It was overcome and left abandoned after the fall of Netheril in –339 DR. By the 14th century DR, the town was in ruins and had been mostly reclaimed by nature. Thieves and scavengers had thoroughly looted the ruins by this time, and viscous monsters had made the ruins into their lairs.

markers.name: Spellgard
markers.textHtml: Spellgard (formerly known as Saharelgard) is the ruin of an ancient castle complex sitting atop its own spring, dating from the Netherese Empire. A few structures remain largely intact, such as the imposing Scepter Tower, and the Fountain Hall, home to a dozen splendid, sculpted waterspouts and plants that grow abundantly under their spray. But most of the complex is a ruin, largely stripped of its riches by time, thieves, and abundant mosses, molds, and fungi that grow in its halls. It is a huge place of towers, crumbling walls, archways, balconies, and mile upon mile of interlinked stairs, galleries, and chambers.

markers.name: Ss'thar'tiss'ssun
markers.textHtml: Now in ruins, Ss'thar'tiss'ssun, or the City of Fountains, was once a sarrukh realm and later the capital city of the ancient serpent empire known as Najara. The ruins of Ss'thar'tiss'ssun are occupied from different kinds of undead and construct guardians left to their own devices, as well as successful species brought over by the different occupations of serpentfolk.

markers.name: Starmantle
markers.textHtml: Starmantle was once a small, coastal city. It was destroyed in 1385 DR by the Spellplague. Following its destruction, the ruins of the city and the surrounding landscape were covered by large, man-sized, gemstone-hard, emerald-colored outcrops that hummed with a single flute-like note. A flickering blue glow passed between the outcrops and obsidian masses slowly drifted across the open ground. However, by the late 15th century DR, Starmantle became a small village that grew around a caravan stop and a tent city in the city's ruins after an inn was built and a humble general store opened next to a warehouse.

markers.name: Sulasspryn
markers.textHtml: In its heyday, Sulasspryn was a major city and a prosperous seaport. In 1307 DR, the city suddenly dropped into a large sinkhole, collapsing in on itself and reducing most of its buildings to rubble within less than ten minutes. 7,500 lives were lost that day. Whether the cause of Sulasspryn's collapse was a drow revenge plot or the subtle machinations of a beholder—or a combination of the two—the city was utterly ruined and would never rise again. The ruins were overtaken by the Drow, their summoned Abyssal servants, various creatures from the Underdark, and enormous quantities of spiders. As of the late 15th century DR, Sulasspryn is still notorious for being haunted by spiders.

markers.name: Sundabar
markers.textHtml: Sundabar, previously known as Citadel Sundbarr, was one of the wealthiest and most militaristic cities within the nation of Luruar, or all of Faerûn for that matter. While it was originally a dwarven citadel, it evolved as its demographics, exports and overall culture changed. One thing remained however, Sundabar continued to serve as the rampart of the north, keeping the realm's dangers beyond civilized lands. Sundabar is a fortress. Its harsh and treeless surface is surrounded by two ringed walls, surrounded by Tarnar's Moat, a water-filled trench that is rumored to be filled with man-eating eels. Within its walls, Sundabar's massive stone buildings are encircled by cobblestone streets, with no flora to be found. Beneath Sundabar is a volcano rift known as the Everfire. In 1484 DR, during the War of the Silver Marches, Sundabar was conquered by an orc army and renamed Hartusk Keep, and the surface population was completely wiped out.

markers.name: Tangled Trees
markers.textHtml: This network of interlocking bridges, wooden platforms, and tree forts surrounded by nets of strong but delicate rope is the settlement of Tangled Trees, which is home to both Cormanthor and Dalefolk scouts. The inhabitants of Tangled Trees patrol the roads, trails, and paths that crisscross through the woods. Every year at least one group travels to Myth Drannor to inspect the borders of the ancient city.

markers.name: Tilverton
markers.textHtml: Tilverton was annexed by Cormyr in 1356 DR and governed as a protectorate, only to be destroyed in 1372 DR during the invasion by the Shade Enclave. By 1373 DR, the survivors of Tilverton had returned and built a new town naming it New Tilverton.

markers.name: Thundertree
markers.textHtml: Thundertree was a small village that supported itself by harvesting timber and shipping it down the Neverwinter River to Neverwinter and beyond. It was considered to be the source of some of the best timber in Neverwinter. However, when Mount Hotenow erupted in 1451 DR, it devastated the small town. In the wake of the disaster, ash zombies drove off any who survived the eruption. During this time, Thundertree lay in overgrown ruins, with many of its stone houses lacking roofs and lying open to the elements.

markers.name: Tower Hlithal
markers.textHtml: Anauria was one of a small number of enclaves that survived the fall of ancient Netheril. It grew into a great kingdom and the wealthiest of the Netherese survivor states before being swallowed up by the growing Anauroch desert in the early 2nd century DR. The kingdom was defended by two fortresses, Tower Hlithal to stop the Araugul goblins to the west, and Tower Ramanath to block monsters from the Hunters' Hills.

markers.name: Tower Ramanath
markers.textHtml: Anauria was one of a small number of enclaves that survived the fall of ancient Netheril. It grew into a great kingdom and the wealthiest of the Netherese survivor states before being swallowed up by the growing Anauroch desert in the early 2nd century DR. The kingdom was defended by two fortresses, Tower Hlithal to stop the Araugul goblins to the west, and Tower Ramanath to block monsters from the Hunters' Hills.

markers.name: Towers of Smoke
markers.textHtml: The Towers of Smoke are Raumathari ruins that derive their name from the plumes of smoke that constantly leak from their tops. The smoke is quite harmless to anyone foolish enough to approach too closely, but the nearby villages give the Towers of Smoke a wide berth, because they are believed to be guarded by a band of iron golems.

markers.name: Old Vaelen
markers.textHtml: [not Vaelen] Old Vaelen, originally known as Vaelen, is the ruined former capital of Veldorn. The city was built within a grouping of hills upon a natural spring that formed from the nearby Liontongue River, northwest of the beauteous Golden Water bay. Following its collapse in the 3rd century DR, Old Vaelen remained as a vast subterranean network beneath the grassy plains where the original city once stood. It is rumored that the ruins of Old Vaelen hold a great wealth of old Durpari riches, but the place is ruled by the Vampire Lord Saed and is home to its undead and lycanthrope minions.

markers.name: Vaerluth
markers.textHtml: Vaerluth is the easternmost goblin realm in the Rauvin Mountains. The goblins of Vaerluth are considered the poorest of the goblins in the mountains. They dwell in huge-spider-infested caverns and foothills, which gives them immunity to the spider venom.

markers.name: Vorpal Tower
markers.textHtml: When the tower was first discovered by a ranger, sometime before the late 14th century DR, the tower was already a ruin. It was distinctly damaged – the tower was diagonally sliced in two, cleanly severing its two upper floors. The cut was what inspired the 'Vorpal Tower' name. The ruin is uninhabited and weathered by nature and time.

markers.name: Well of Dragons
markers.textHtml:
The Well of Dragons is an extinct volcanic caldera that holds deep spiritual and historical significance for draconic kind. Dragons dying of natural causes often travel to this caldera to spend their final hours, and it previously served as a major base for the Cult of the Dragon, though that specific cell has been vanquished.

<%>Despite the fall of the previous base, cult leaders have been spotted recently probing the ruins of the site, suggesting a lingering interest in its secrets.

markers.name: The White Crag
markers.textHtml: The ruined tower of White Crag is so ancient it is said to predate even elven presence in the Dagger Hills. It is rumored that under the tower, vast passages lead to the Underdark.

markers.name: Zelbross
markers.textHtml: Zelbross was a hamlet producing all kinds of pottery of excellent quality. In particular, it was famed for its clay smoking pipes. Zelbross was considered a very old settlement by the mid–14th century DR, and possibly was established here because of the rich clay that could be found in the vicinity. By 1479 DR, Zelbross lay in ruins.

markers.name: Clymph Tower
markers.textHtml: The Nar demonbinder Clymph built this tower in 888 DR as a pen for the demons he had summoned. He made the perimeter of the keep into a magic circle from which the demons could not escape. Clymph dreamt of founding an empire in the Great Dale with his demonic armies, and created a permanent gate to the Abyss from which he drew his forces. In 890 DR he created Tower Threespires, and linked the two by a permanent portal, so as to be able to come and leave as he pleased, while bypassing the magic circle of Clymph Tower. But he was unable to control the demons and was slain by them. The demons were unable to leave the keep as the demon-trap was well made, the portal to Tower Threespires was sealed, and the portal to the Abyss was torn during the Spellplague. In the centuries since, those few who approached the lonesome walled keep reported that its stones glowed red with an inner heat, and the sound of a massive fire was to be heard within.

markers.name: Drakelight
markers.textHtml: The Drakelight was a massive lighthouse, 800 feet (240 meters) tall, located in Threskel during the mid–14th century DR. It was lit whenever pirates were spotted entering the Bay of Chessenta. In order to protect the Drakelight from pirate retaliation, Soorenar, Luthcheq, and Mordulkin each sent a detachment of one hundred soldiers to garrison the lighthouse. The soldiers stationed here were sworn to work together even if their parent cities were at war with each other.

markers.name: Durlag's Tower
markers.textHtml: Durlag's Tower is built entirely of volcanic stone and is in fair condition given the amount of time and neglect involved. It lays atop a volcanic plug that dominates the surrounding lands. This local landmark was the isolated keep of Durlag Trollkiller, a dwarven hero said to have amassed a large quantity of treasure and stored it here. This treasure was protected by all manner of magical wards and mechanical traps. Many adventurers sought his treasure over the decades, with varying degrees of success. Around 1372 DR, rumors said a fiend, maybe a lich, had taken up residence within the remains of the tower.

markers.name: Fang Light
markers.textHtml: The Fang Light is a lighthouse at the northern tip of the Fang, a large, barren, and rocky promontory that juts out from the northern coast of Aglarond. The area is battered by storms almost constantly, and seas are often violent. Reefs surround the coastline.

markers.name: Hawk's Nest
markers.textHtml: Hawk's Nest is a small watchtower near the top of the Silverymoon Pass. It is usually manned by a dozen Knights in Silver during the months when the Silverymoon Pass is in heavy use.

markers.name: Iniarv's Tower
markers.textHtml: Iniarv's Tower is a ruined tower sitting upon an escarpment overlooking the High Road near the Mere of Dead Men. It is a square keep with five foot thick stone walls, and two stone towers standing on the northern and southern corners of the square. Iniarv's Tower originally belonged to Iniarv, the Mage Royal of Uthtower, who later became a lich and mysteriously disappeared sometime before the early 7th century DR. After the tower was abandoned, it fell into ruins and was at various times occupied by bandits, fugitives, orcs, and others. The structure has been repaired and rebuilt many times over the centuries using varying styles and degrees of care, but the tower always seems to fall back into ruin. Many attribute this to a curse upon the building bestowed by Iniarv himself.

markers.name: Irphong Sea Tower
markers.textHtml: The Sea Towers are twin lighthouses on the islands of Irphong and Nemessor to mark Asavir's Channel and the stretch of water known as the Race. Of almost identical design and quality construction, the towers stand atop a high cliff and are 40 feet (12 m) high by 25 feet (7,50 m) in diameter. Their roof is illuminated by a continual light (permanent spell), bright enough to be seen for miles around thanks to their height. Built by the Shoon Empire during Emperor Shoon IV’s reign, the Sea Towers allowed imperial vessels to travel safely through the Race at night. They were likely manned to serve as watch posts, but by the mid-to-late 14th century DR they were left empty, with only rotting wooden furniture marking their former occupation. However, their lights still glowed brightly.

markers.name: The Lonely Tower
markers.textHtml: This mysterious tower, made of white stone, has an incredibly light and frail structure compared to where it stands. The surrounding area is a perfect circle, strewn with volcanic rock and dotted with deep cracks, which differs considerably from the nearby hills, as if the tower had been built elsewhere and then moved to this place, along with all its environment for about one kilometer. In the 14th century DR, the tower was home to Sssessibil Istahvar, a half-fiend yuan-ti archmage who, despite having very few interests in the Silver Marches, protected his dwelling with the help of fearsome elemental guardians and destructive traps.

markers.name: Magis Tor
markers.textHtml: The five towers that comprise the Magis Tor are monolithic structures with no doors or windows. Four towers are placed around the central spire that is elevated 30 feet (9 m) off the ground. The corner towers connect to the central spire by covered archways. In the 14th century DR, Magis Tor served as the base for a group of adventuring mages known as Magis Mir, but they went missing in the mid-14th century. As of the late 14th century DR, the adventuring band had not been seen nor heard from. It is believed that Magis Tor can only be entered by the individuals wearing a special ring.

markers.name: Nemessor Sea Tower
markers.textHtml: The Sea Towers are twin lighthouses on the islands of Irphong and Nemessor to mark Asavir's Channel and the stretch of water known as the Race. Of almost identical design and quality construction, the towers stand atop a high cliff and are 40 feet (12 m) high by 25 feet (7,50 m) in diameter. Their roof is illuminated by a continual light (permanent spell), bright enough to be seen for miles around thanks to their height. Built by the Shoon Empire during Emperor Shoon IV’s reign, the Sea Towers allowed imperial vessels to travel safely through the Race at night. They were likely manned to serve as watch posts, but by the mid-to-late 14th century DR they were left empty, with only rotting wooden furniture marking their former occupation. However, their lights still glowed brightly.

markers.name: Orgoth's Tower
markers.textHtml: This abandoned tower was the home and death place of a powerful wizard named Orgoth the Tainted, who is said to have been slain by three fiends he summoned.

markers.name: Stormkeep
markers.textHtml: Stormkeep is a tower that has the shape of a sharp fang. From the summit of the tower, lightning bolts constantly strike at passing clouds. Along with the slender and tapered walls, there aren’t any visible doors or windows. The entire structure seems carved from a single giant unique piece of obsidian. Countless rumors about this tower circulate in the Fallen Lands. All agree that Stormkeep was built for a terrible purpose, and that all those who ventured there may have been marked in some way in order to be called up again later for an obscure reason.

markers.name: Telkoun's Tower
markers.textHtml: Telkoun's Tower is a wizard stronghold located in the small valley named High Thorog. It consists of one building with two double towers. In the late 14th century, it was protected by monsters serving the evil wizard Telkoun Adranther. Among these monsters were gargoyles and perytons.

markers.name: Tower of Lamentation
markers.textHtml: One of the several monasteries and abbeys devoted to the Triad (Tyr, Ilmater and Torm) that can be found in the Uplands region of Impiltur.

markers.name: Tower Threespires
markers.textHtml: At a distance, this ruined wizard tower looks like a giant three-fingered hand reaching out of the Dunwood towards the sky. The construction would have collapsed many years previously if not held together by a great network of vines wrapping themselves around every inch of the three towers. The Nar demonbinder Clymph built this tower in 890 DR, two years after creating his fortress of Clymph Tower. He linked the two by a permanent portal, making this the only way to enter or leave the sealed Clymph Tower. By the late 14th century DR, the tower was inhabited by a small group of druids, chosen to protect the lands against the demons held in Clymph Tower. Every year on the Feast of the Moon, they performed a ritual to maintain the portal seal. While the druids were no longer sure if any demons still lingered in Clymph Tower, they nonetheless still performed their appointed task.

markers.name: Twin Towers of the Eternal Eclipse (w)
markers.textHtml: Formerly temples to Bane, the Twin Towers of the Eternal Eclipse were seized and rededicated as temples to Cyric in the mid-to-late 14th century DR. The towers are about 5 miles (8 km) apart on each side of the Trade Way, deep in the highest peaks of the Small Teeth Mountains, and almost completely obscured from view, even from above. Each tower is five stories tall and they are connected by a twisting underground passage.

markers.name: Twin Towers of the Eternal Eclipse (e)
markers.textHtml: Formerly temples to Bane, the Twin Towers of the Eternal Eclipse were seized and rededicated as temples to Cyric in the mid-to-late 14th century DR. The towers are about 5 miles (8 km) apart on each side of the Trade Way, deep in the highest peaks of the Small Teeth Mountains, and almost completely obscured from view, even from above. Each tower is five stories tall and they are connected by a twisting underground passage.

markers.name: Xonthal's Tower
markers.textHtml: Xonthal's Tower is an isolated settlement populated by cordial but wary folks that live simple lives as herders, farmers, and fishers. They live under the constant threat of orcs, dangerous serpentfolk that dwell within the nearby Serpent Hills and Marsh of Chelimber. They always offer stark warnings about the enigmatic black tower that lays near their village, surrounded by a magical hedge maze.

markers.name: Abbey of the Just Hammer
markers.textHtml: The Abbey of the Just Hammer is dedicated to Tyr. It was the site of a legendary battle when Deren Eriach, Lord High Justicar of the abbey, slew the ancient green dragon Sirvinhandra with a Holy Avenger. The abbey provides legal education to citizens of Tasseldale, making it one of the few places for advanced education anywhere in the Dalelands. Lawyers and judges who complete their training at the abbey are offered lucrative employment throughout the Heartlands, but many help the poor and unfortunate instead.

markers.name: Aencar's Manor
markers.textHtml:
From the road, the old manor appears inviting, with gardens overlooked by statues of mounted knights. The folk of Battledale use these gardens and other areas around the manor as the site for their regular Shieldmeet festivals, every four years, strictly avoiding the manor's interior.

<%>The interior is haunted by ghosts, specters, wraiths and the restless corpse of Aencar, a death knight. Aencar was killed by a dracolich, so the Cult of the Dragon has long felt entitled to Aencar's property and explores tunnels underneath the manor that connect to the Underdark.

markers.name: Aghazstamn's Lair
markers.textHtml: Aghazstamn was a blue dragon dracolich and a member of the Cult of the Dragon. He was killed by Elminster in 1357 DR.

markers.name: Anathar's Dell
markers.textHtml: Anathar's Dell is an inn which is surprisingly well defended in spite of its size and relative exposure. These defenses give Anathar's Dell a reputation that helps to ward away would-be bandits, a reputation further enhanced by the protection of the Harpers.

markers.name: Bhaerlith
markers.textHtml: Bhaerlith is an artificially-created oasis utilized by the Zhentarim as a way point and military outpost. The oasis is protected by a ring of traps, and rich with a variety of fruiting trees including palms. Within the camp is a single underground stone structure used by the Zhent soldiers.

markers.name: Barrowfield
markers.textHtml: These mounds, called the Barrowfield, conceal the ancient graves of Netherese warrior-wizards buried here after a long-forgotten battle. Wights are common in this region.

markers.name: The Battlescarred Bard
markers.textHtml: The Battlescarred Bard is a one-story inn with a large stable attached.

markers.name: Beorunna's Well
markers.textHtml: The Black Lion tribe of Uthgardt barbarians forsook their nomadic habits and settled in a small, roughly built town around their ancestral mound. They trade timber, furs, and smoked meat, notably with the dwarves of Citadel Felbarr. The town is surrounded by a stockade, and the modest houses are built around the deep pit that once contained the ancestral mound. At the bottom of the pit dwell Underdark monsters that the barbarians fear. Legend says that Beorunna, the father of Uthgar, defeated the demon Zukothoth at the ancestral mound, causing it to sink into the earth.

markers.name: Berun's Hill
markers.textHtml: Berun's Hill is a bare-topped, conical hill that commands a splendid view of the valley of the Dessarin River to the east. The hill is used as a lookout for advancing barbarians and orc hordes from north and east. Bandits sometimes watch for likely victims from this hill. It got its name from a ranger named Berun, who was slain by an orc horde but managed to take 300 of them with him, before they overwhelmed him. In 1351 DR, a group of dwarven priests discovered the dwarven tomb that had long been talked about in legends of the North. They found that this cavernous crypt belonged to none other than Maegar, of the Royal House of the Helm of Gharraghaur, and that it had been plundered long ago by duergar.

markers.name: Bitter Well
markers.textHtml: The Bitter Well is an oasis that was taken over by the Zhentarim in 1360 DR.

markers.name: Cairn Wheel
markers.textHtml: The Cairn Wheel is an ancient druidic stone circle and a place of worship of Silvanus, the Oak-Father. Centuries ago, a druid named Sklaerth Tholone was murdered there by bandits. With his dying breath, the druid evoked Thorncall. The magic erected an impenetrable thorn barrier around the circle. As of the late 14th century, the remains of Sklaerth and his four murderers still laid at the center of the circle. Stories said that the bandits were rich and perished with their wealth beside them, and that Sklaerth possessed magic items sacred to Silvanus. But none risked removing the treasures from the Cairn Wheel in fear of the Oak-Father's wrath.

markers.name: Carlsmere
markers.textHtml: Carlsmere is just one of many abandoned and ruined noble estates found on The Bite area of the Dragon's Head, on the shores of the Dragon's Teeth Lagoon. It was a favored summer home of the royal Tethyrean Royal Family, but was abandoned in the 12th century DR after being the scene of a terrible family massacre. Over the years, the manor and its smaller servants' quarters served as brigand and monster lairs, however, none stayed in the ruins for long thanks to a ghost infestation. Those who venture into Carlsmere report ghostly sounds of weeping children, apparitions wandering the halls, and translucent figures playing in the nursery. Even though many opportunistic treasure seekers thought the Carlsmere hid some things of value, due to the magical sentry at the gates, they failed to report finding anything.

markers.name: Carnath Roadhouse
markers.textHtml: The Carnath Roadhouse is a compound and small supply depot found near the Mere of Dead Men. In the 1480s DR, it was used by the Cult of the Dragon as a waypoint to transfer stolen goods to a secret location in the Mere.

markers.name: Colored Waters
markers.textHtml: Colored Waters is a grand oasis and settlement, home of the Mahwa tribe of the Bedine people. The oasis was formed in a dormant caldera, set within a massive basin covered in silt and ebony-ash. Within the center of the oases is a cylindrical spire of yellow-colored cinder. Around the center of the oasis are five naturally warm, crescent-shaped lakes, each of a different and eye-catching color. According to Bedine legend, Colored Waters is the site where their deities defeated the forces of the djinn N'asr in a mythical battle. It is said that when the gods died, the blood from their dead bodies colored the waters of the lakes.

markers.name: The Dancing Place
markers.textHtml: Eight centuries ago, more than a dozen deities manifested in a hidden valley a few miles northwest of High Castle, an event called the Gathering of the Gods that led to the founding of the Harpers. Ever since, this valley has been a pilgrimage site visited by thousands every year. Temples to the deities honored in the Dancing Place fill the valley, along with hostels and other establishments that cater to pilgrims. A group of druids known as the High Dance guards the valley's borders.

markers.name: Dead Horse Ford
markers.textHtml: Dead Horse Ford is a crossing on the Dessarin River. It is a part of Jundar's Pass, the trail from Beliard to Calling Horns, and drew its name from the battle fought here where the hero Destril Longtracker had three horses slain from underneath him.

markers.name: Deadstone Cleft
markers.textHtml: Deadstone Cleft is a dead-end canyon in the foothills of the Graypeak Mountains, populated by stone giants. They believe the canyon is a sacred site to their god Skoraeus Stonebones. It comprises a 150-foot-tall statue of the god, an ancient temple to him and a magical stalactite, the Steinfang.

markers.name: Delhalls
markers.textHtml: Delhalls is a Dwarven mine that, along with its twin Talagbar, was frozen under the Great Glacier for millennia. The ancient mines were rediscovered in the 13th century DR, and fully exposed by 1372 by melting of the Glacier. As of the late 15th century, mining has fully resumed and the mine is operated by dwarven slaves controlled by the Warlock Knights of Vaasa. Dellhalls is exporting gems and copper, and is estimated to be rich with deposits of bloodstone, emerald, ruby, and iron. Hidden deep within the mines are miles of mazelike tunnels that twist in every direction, home to a colony of oblivion moss. Deep within the tunnels is a vault sacred to Auril, heavily protected with both mechanical and magical traps, and inside which an iceborn clone of the Ice Queen Iyraclea was sealed.

markers.name: Duskwood Dell
markers.textHtml: At this former elven settlement, the River Rimril plunges down Eldath's Mount on the eastern face of the Troll Mountains, creating the Green Goddess Falls that fall for over 400 feet (120 meters) to a series of smaller waterfalls and pools. This place is the most sacred site of Eldath located in Faerûn. Priests of Eldath come for training to Eldathyn, a small tree city found within Duskwood Dell.

markers.name: El Ma'ra
markers.textHtml: El Ma'ra is an oasis and a settlement home to the Qahtan tribe the Bedine people. The oasis is located next to Ma'ra Dat-ur Ojhogo, a sandstone mesa named after the spirit that lives within, translated from midani as 'The tall god who lets men sit upon his head'. The Bedine use the spire as a lookout post to better defend their home, and sacrifice some of their water to the resident spirit. In 1360 DR, the oasis and nearly all of the Qahtari were wiped out by the Zhentarim during their expansion of conquest across Anauroch.

markers.name: El Rahalat
markers.textHtml: El Rahalat is the name given to a lone peak and to the oasis situated at its base. They were named after the Bedine sorceress Rahalat, who made her home here. Bedine legend says that when she was slain, the water of the oasis's spring transformed into blood. For the following decade a mere sip of its waters would kill any being that partook. In the years that followed, any tribe that made camp at El Rahalat made sure to sacrifice a single camel in order for the water to remain drinkable and not turn lethal again. The oasis at Rahalat was the home of the Dakawa tribe of Bedine nomads, then of the Matair Dhafir tribe.

markers.name: Elah'zad
markers.textHtml: Elah'zad, meaning 'House of the Moon' in midani, is an isolated oasis that is considered a sacred place of great power by the nomadic Bedine people. Elah'zad is nestled within a valley surrounded by 1,000-foot-tall (300-meter) mounds of earth dotted with scores of small water springs. Water flows down into a vividly blue lake, 1 mile (1,6 km) in width, that gives life to striking and beautiful plant life. Within the center of the lake is a small, grass-covered isle set in the shape of a half moon. According to Bedine legend, the valley was once home to the moon goddess Elah, before she was driven away by the goddess of the sun, At'ar. At'ar then transformed Elah'zad into a prison for the goddess Eldath, as she was jealous of her beauty.

markers.name: Evermelt
markers.textHtml: The Evermelt is a subterranean hot spring found within a cave at the base of the Reghed Glacier. The warm, tranquil waters of Evermelt appear as a serene turquoise color that glitters like gold. It is considered a holy place by the Reghedmen barbarians of nearby Icewind Dale, and, as of 1485 DR, it is guarded by members of the Tribe of the Bear, at the behest of the Ice Witch Hedrun Arnsfirth.

markers.name: Eye of the All Father
markers.textHtml: The Eye of the All-Father is a secret underground temple to Annam All-Father, the chief giant deity and creator of the giant race. The temple was built in the time of ancient Ostoria. It can be accessed by passing through a mountain pass that begins in the Valley of Khedrun. The complex is accessed by crossing over a 40-foot-wide (12 m) causeway with sheer drops to the valley floor below. The causeway leads to a domed entrance with six columns carved with likenesses of various giant races. Beyond the entryway there are no doors, but there is a 40-foot-wide (12 m) corridor over 300 feet (91 meters) long which leads to a set of stairs to the main temple's massive doors. Only a handful of giants know the location, as do a few ancient dragons.

markers.name: The Five Lions
markers.textHtml: An inn in the realm of Turmish in the Vilhon Reach.

markers.name: Flame Fault
markers.textHtml: The Flame Fault is a deep volcanic crevasse, one of many volcanically active sites on Tuern, with fire and smoke constantly pouring out from it. The Fault is famously home to a trio of red dragons who terrorize Tuern and several of the neighboring islands. The kingdoms on Tuern (and elsewhere within their range) send them gifts of food, slaves, and treasure to placate them. As of the late 15th century DR, the isle's red dragons were effectively the rulers of Tuern and were even worshiped by the island's human inhabitants.

markers.name: Grandfather Tree
markers.textHtml: A colossal, sentient oak tree of mythic proportions. It is a holy site protected by ancient magic and its own guardians. It is the spiritual center of the forest. It is sacred to the Tree Ghost tribe of the Uthgardt barbarians and is a place of gathering for Treants and druids. The tree is a symbol of the forest's enduring life force and acts as a massive 'weird' (a magical ward) that protects the surrounding area from corruption.

markers.name: Grudd Haug
markers.textHtml: Grudd Haug, meaning 'River Mound' in Giant, is the steading of the hill giant Chief Guh as of 1485 DR. She lives here with her minions, one of which is charged with maintaining the river dam next to the steading.

markers.name: Gulf of Storms
markers.textHtml: This deep valley is a sacred site to the servants of Talos.

markers.name: Harvest House
markers.textHtml: A temple to Chauntea.

markers.name: Heart of Ubtao
markers.textHtml: The Heart of Ubtao is an earthmote that hovers over the Aldani Basin. It is a sacred site for worshipers of Ubtao, the deity known as the Creator of Chult. The earthmote earned its name from its shape that somewhat resembles a humanoid heart, with petrified tree roots that duplicate veins and arteries. Rainwater that filters through deposits of iron become red in hue, resembling blood that drips down to the jungle below. Faithful Chultans believe it is the actual heart of their patron deity. While the hallow, petrified tree in the earthmote is naturally home to swarms of bats, the earthmote also houses a number of undead protectors.

markers.name: Heroes' Helm
markers.textHtml: A massive cavern complex in a stone pinnacle, home to an ancient blue dragon named Ghondalaath.

markers.name: Hlaunga
markers.textHtml: Hlaunga is a secret, Zhentarim-manufactured oasis that served as a way point for Zhent caravans traveling via the Black Road in the mid-14th century DR.

markers.name: Huthduth
markers.textHtml: A small fortified Chauntean monastery and keep in the Storm Horns.

markers.name: Firefinger
markers.textHtml: Firefinger is an ancient Chultan signal tower that survived the ages. During the prosperous years of Chultan civilization, it was used to communication from one settlement to another: colored flames were lit at night and colored smoke was created during the day. As of the late 15th century DR, it still stands 300 feet (90 m) tall, with steep walls that are very difficult to climb by hand. A few crumbling ladders are situated around its exterior, though they are quite precarious. Giant spiders and stirges have taken over the tower's lower floors, while pterafolk nest within its upper ones.

markers.name: Five Spears Hold
markers.textHtml: Five Spears Hold is a mining town garrisoned with troops meant to protect the southern borders of Erlkazar. The leader of the barony is also the commander of the garrisons.

markers.name: Flint Rock
markers.textHtml: Flint Rock is the ancestral mound of the Elk tribe of Uthgardt barbarians, used for veneration of the of the Elk beast totem of the god Uthgar. It is a set of concentric hills formed from piles of heaped stones, with a central depression that is shaped like the profile of a leaping elk, set on top of a gnarled outcropping of flint. It is quite a bleak-looking place, being devoid of any vegetation but for mosses and constantly enveloped in fog. The outer ring holds a number of cairns, in which are interred the remains of the Elk tribe's most exceptional champions. The inner ring bears four menhirs of gray stone, between which stand twelve totem poles. At the very center of Flint Rock is a massive stone slab that serves as the altar.

markers.name: Giants' Dance
markers.textHtml: [not Giant's Dance] The Giants' Dance is a strange circle of nine standing stones, seven of which have fallen down. Three of those that fell are cracked apart with marks that imply something clawed and scraped its way out of each one despite the lack of cavities.

markers.name: Growling Hill
markers.textHtml: Originally the site of a wizard's tower, the place fell to ruin and became buried by mud and grass growth, resembling a small irregularly shaped hill. An intermittent growling sound of unknown origin can be heard if one presses their ear to the ground. Local parents claim there is a great beast living under the hill, who will surely eat naughty children.

markers.name: Iymrith's Lair
markers.textHtml: For centuries, this abandoned amphitheater was the home of Iymrith, also known as the Doom of the Desert and the Dragon of the Statues, a powerful female blue dragon sorcerer. After causing much chaos at the storm giant court of Maelstrom, she was eventually slain in her lair ca. 1485 DR by the storm giant King Hekaton.

markers.name: Ironfang Deep
markers.textHtml: Ironfang Deep is the main underground dwarven settlement in the Mountains of the Alaoreum. It is an independent mountain city-kingdom that stretches deep into the heart of the Alaoreum, and one of the very few non-human settlements of the Vilhon Reach. The dwarves engage in a great amount of trade, mostly with Turmish, purchasing Turmish goods and trading it for mined precious metals and various minerals. Ironfang is especially renowned for its great-quality gemstones and gold.

markers.name: Lady's Hand Monastery
markers.textHtml: The Lady's Hand Monastery is a fortified temple dedicated to Loviatar, the evil goddess of agony. Priests reside in the temple and in the caves under the abbey. These monks are able to repel attacks by orcs and other monsters using ancient Netherese magic. They have a bad reputation in the surrounding area, because travelers passing by tend to disappear mysteriously. In 1371 DR, the Monastery was attacked by a dragon from the Blood of Morueme and partly destroyed. Rebuilding efforts started immediately after the attack.

markers.name: Land of the Dead
markers.textHtml: The Land of the Dead, also referred to as the City of the Dead, is a region in Mulhorand dedicated to housing that country's honored dead. Tombs are constantly being erected here, planning for future pharaohs before their deaths. The holy vale hosts many pyramids, obelisks, ziggurats, all decorated with hieroglyphs and divine scenes. The grandest structure is the tomb of Horuseres II that was carved directly from the mountain face. The depths of the city are infested with scores of undead.

markers.name: Lion's Eye Oasis
markers.textHtml: The Lion's Eye is said to be the most lush of the oases found in the Anauroch. Within its center is a small lake encircled by ascending grasslands. A rocky outcropping on the eastern stretch reaches an apex that is referred to as the actual 'Lion's Eye'. As the winds whip over the plains they pass through a hole in this rock formation creating a moaning sound that permeates the landscape. It is said that the lake holds submerged treasures protected by underwater monstrosities. Unlike many oases found in Anauroch, the Lion's Eye is not home to the Bedine people, but rather a community of lamias. They graze upon their verdant stretch of the desert, hunting any intruders that dare pass over their land.

markers.name: Lonely Lake Oasis
markers.textHtml: The Lonely Lake is a small oasis found within the Plains of Purple Dust. It is held and protected by descendants of the mighty wizard Nezram.

markers.name: Ma'atar
markers.textHtml: Ma'atar is an artificially created, Zhentarim-controlled oasis. It is protected by a stone wall and houses a number of stone structures that serve as fortified barracks. It is a bountiful oasis that is rich with vegetation including nut-producing trees. Beneath the oasis are tunnels that store an arsenal of supplies and weapons, and harbor a small mushroom farm containing fine frill-collars used for Zhent alchemy.

markers.name: Magar's Hill
markers.textHtml: Magar's Hill is the site of the Stone of Clans' Parting, a monolith that contains ancient elven writing and is the center of a dead-magic zone. It is also the place where Esmel Torlath slew a dragon in the days before the founding of Amn.

markers.name: Menzoberranzan
markers.textHtml:
Menzoberranzan is a drow city carved from the pillars of a great cavern in the Underdark. It is the center of Lolth's worship in Faerûn. Also known as the City of Spiders, it's known for its culture of deception, manipulation, and the capture and trade of enslaved surface dwellers. Although officially governed by a council of matrons from the eight greatest houses, the city is under the heel of House Baenre and the Matron Mother, who rules in Lolth's name. Over a hundred tunnels link the city to other parts of the Underdark, and drow traders sell poisons, strange mushrooms, beasts of burden, scrolls, wine, and secrets with merchants of the deep.

MAP OF MENZOBERRANZAN

markers.name: Mines of Tethkel
markers.textHtml: A series of deep mines, accompanied by a small town. The mines held a small community of Rashemi miners who are considered among the most undisciplined, wild people of their land. They are a great source of mineral wealth, rich with iron ore. The miners export their goods to the settlements of Urling and Immilmar where they are forged into tools, weapons and other goods.

markers.name: Mines of Tethyamar
markers.textHtml: The mines of Tethyamar were once home to one of the greatest dwarven communities in north Faerûn. However, in 1104 DR, after more than a decade of resistance, Tethyamar was entirely overwhelmed by legion of orcs and goblins. The surviving dwarves of the Iron House fled to the dwarven communities of the Far Hills and Storm Horns of Cormyr. Nevertheless, dwarves in the lands around the Sea of Fallen Stars continue to speak of and work towards the day when 'the king shall take his throne again'.

markers.name: Monastery of the Yellow Rose
markers.textHtml: Also known as the Citadel of the White Worm, it's a monastery dedicated to St. Sollars the Twice-Martyred and Ilmater, the Broken God. It overlooks the Glacier of the White Worm and is the most well known of the facilities belonging to the Disciples of St. Sollars the Twice-Martyred.

markers.name: Monument of the Ancients
markers.textHtml: The Monument of the Ancients is a step-pyramid structure located in an overgrown hollow of the Frozen Forest and towering above the surrounding trees. It is constructed of arcane, stone-like, and metallic materials that are not native to Toril. The properties of these materials render the structure seemingly impervious to the effects of time. It is unknown who originally raised the monument, but ancient myths claim that it dates from the time of the First Flowering, between –24000 and –12000 DR. It is believed to contain a large seal artifact known as the Anchor of Chaos, whose function is to magically protect a region of the world where the boundary between Toril and the Elemental Chaos is weak.

markers.name: Moon Stair
markers.textHtml: A crystal spiral staircase that arises out of Azulduth Lake near its southern end.

markers.name: Morgur's Mound
markers.textHtml: Morgur's Mound is the ancestor mound of the Thunderbeast tribe of the Uthgardt. It is roughly shaped like a creature known as a 'thunderbeast' to the Uthgardt, described by some as a wingless dragon with a long neck. Prior to the late 15th century DR, a thunderbeast skeleton sat atop the mound. In the late 15th century DR, it had been replaced by a row of massive bones protruding along the mound's back from the head to the tail. Enormous cairns encircle the mound, housing the bones of warriors of the Thunderbeast tribe. Throughout the Mound's history, superstitious members of the tribe often buried magic items within it, believing they would be watched over by the spirits of the dead. When the Thunderbeasts visited the mound in the late 15th century DR, they found that the mound had been dug up by the Red Wizards of Thay, and both the thunderbeast skeleton and the treasures within had been stolen. The tribe stopped visiting the mound in the following years, which left it open to looters.

markers.name: Mound of the Sleepless
markers.textHtml: The Aerilpar Mound, also known as the Mound of the Sleepless, is a mysterious mound in the center of the Aerilpar Forest. It is a location that radiates magic. The origins of its magical energy are unknown but no trees grow within the encircling walls and all the predatory animals of the forest keep away.

markers.name: Mountain of Skulls
markers.textHtml: The Mountain of Skulls is a temple of Cyric. It is built within a nearly inaccessible cavern with a skull-shaped opening, 1 mile (1,5 km) into a gaping gorge. The Mountain of Skulls earned its name from the massive mound of skulls that lies within its center. This horrific display is treated as an idol by the local Cyricists. The temple was once dedicated to the god Bane, before being claimed by Cyricists during the Amnian Banedeath in 1362 DR. The temple then endured the decades of languish experienced by the Cyricist faith, and remains standing as of the late 15th century 9 DR.

markers.name: Mucklestones
markers.textHtml: The Mucklestones are a ring of standing stones, etched with nature runes, that border a cleared out, circular bowl on the land. A ring of trees surrounds the stones, whose branches reach out above the hollow bowl. The place is known to shift about from time to time and is watched over by elder druids.
