The Mucklestones served as magical portals, allowing access to places throughout Faerûn, but by 1497 DR, many of these portals have broken down or been rerouted because of the Spellplague.

markers.name: Mystics' Academy
markers.textHtml: [not Mystic's Academy] The Mystics' Academy is a school of magic. In the 13th century DR, it had links with the School of Wonder, in Amn, but greatly outlived its sibling Amnian school. Among its famous students are Gamalon Idogyr, the Count of Spellshire and the Court Sage of Tethyr in the early 14th century DR, and Zallanora Argentresses, Lady Varytha Gheldig's elven apprentice in the mid-14th century.

markers.name: Needle's Bones
markers.textHtml: Needle's Bones is a massive grotto, resembling a sinkhole. The bottom is covered by a shallow pool of murky green water full of skeletal remains. Originally, this landmark served as the lair of the green dragon Ormalagos, commonly known as Needle. At some point the nearby batiri goblins concocted a plan to slay the dragon. They trapped her with nets and pelted her with spears while schools of carnivorous quippers consumed her from below. Ormalagos was slain and the tribe of goblins were nearly completely eradicated.

markers.name: Oasis of Vipers
markers.textHtml: The Oasis of Vipers is an old, well-watered set of three pools that is mostly ignored by the Bedine. The oasis has a large variety of plant life concealing the bones of many dead creatures, including camels and humans. The oasis is home to hundreds of extremely poisonous serpents, which hunt each other and any life that attempts to drink water from the pools.

markers.name: Olomaa
markers.textHtml: Olomaa is an artificially-created oasis utilized by the Zhentarim. The oasis is splayed out over two hills and the valley that lays tween them. The Zhent's fortification consists of a stone house, a small pasture, a fountain for water, and a handful of bathing pools. Olomaa is protected by a trench, a great number of enormous cacti, and a number of man-made traps.

markers.name: One Stone
markers.textHtml: One Stone was originally worshiped as their ancestral mound by members of the Sky Pony, Golden Eagle, and Red Pony tribes of the Uthgardt barbarians, but for reasons unknown the latter two tribes eventually moved on to other places. At the center of cairn rings stands a single massive boulder 20 feet (6.1 meters) across and 12 feet (3.7 meters) high, covered in precisely engraved tracery. Carved steps ascend the south face to the altar top. Legend says a god sealed a passageway from the Nine Hells by thrusting this single rock into the ground.

markers.name: Oryndoll
markers.textHtml: Oryndoll is the holy city of Ilsensine, the creator deity of the illithids (mind flayers) located in the Lowerdark around 26 miles beneath the surface of the Shining Plains. The holy city is home to thousands of mind flayers and their thralls and governed by the Encephalithid, the city's elder brain, as well as the council of nine Creedmasters.

markers.name: Point Iron
markers.textHtml: Point Iron is a small yet profitable iron mine. It was worked by dwarves until around the 1260s DR, but by 1367 DR it had fallen under duergar control. Rumors persisted of a gold vein in the mine, but it was never found.

markers.name: Raven Rock
markers.textHtml: Raven Rock is the ancestral mound of the Black Raven and Gray Wolf tribes of Uthgardt people. The site is so named for the 100-foot-tall (30.5 meter) stone sculpture of a perched raven that stands atop the snow-covered plateaus that compose the tribe's burial mound. Just south of the stone is a wolf-shaped depression that surrounds an altar engraved with the phases of the moon. A number of stone menhirs dot Raven Rock's plateaus. Members of the Gray Wolf tribe are forbidden from the mound's highest plateau. They gather near their altar to the south. The Black Ravens are rumored to have a consistent honor guard of no less than ten barbarians and ten giant ravens guarding the Raven Rock at all times.

markers.name: Shadowtop Cathedral
markers.textHtml: Shadowtop Cathedral is a stand of tall shadowtop trees in the High Forest and an important meeting place for the Emerald Enclave after 1485 DR.

markers.name: Shargrailar's Lair
markers.textHtml: Shargrailar was a great wyrm red dragon and the first successful dracolich created by Sammaster in 902 DR. Shargrailar went on to become the most powerful dracolich in the Cult of the Dragon and a loyal ally of Sammaster. Shargrailar's demise occurred after Shandril destroyed two dracoliches and an archmage of the Cult. Then Shargrailar decided to resolve the matter himself and ambushed the girl and her friends. However, her spellfire proved too powerful and the dracolich was destroyed for good.

markers.name: Shield of the Rathgaunt Hills
markers.textHtml: Prior to the Spellplague in 1385 DR, the Shield of the Rathgaunt Hills was a gnomish abbey. When the Spellplague created the Underchasm, the area surrounding the abbey broke off into a large earthmote. As of 1479 DR the mote is inhabited by only the former gnomish monks who continue to exist as undead creatures.

markers.name: The Shining Falls
markers.textHtml: Shining Falls is a waterfall in the Graypeak Mountains that forms part of the River Delimbiyr. The waters fall in a great torrent around a 120-foot-high (36 m), horseshoe-shaped concave drop-off, making for a breathtaking and beautiful sight. At the top of the waterfall is a tiny rocky island rumored to be the site of a portal. Behind the falls is an entrance to the Royal Caverns of Splendarrmornn, the capital city of the dwarven realm of Ammarindar. The Royal Caverns are a legendary tomb of dwarf royalty, but tomb raiders had cleared them out long before the mid-1300s DR. By then, the caves were more often used as a hideout by bandits working for the Zhentarim. Above the falls are the remnants of an old Eaerlanni portage road. The ruins of a port can also be found. Finally, downriver of the falls is a strongheart halfling village named for it, Shining Falls.

markers.name: Shining White
markers.textHtml: Shining White is the ancestor mound of the Griffon tribe of the Uthgardt. It is an ancient barbarian burial cairn. Its name comes from both the white marble menhirs and the white chalk soil uncovered by the barbarians' cuts into the permafrost layer. It includes a large cavern and tunnel complex. The narrow passes that lead up into the cavern are warded with runes by frost giant Gerti Orelsdottr.

markers.name: Sister of Rains
markers.textHtml: The Sister of Rains is a canyon-oasis nestled in a steep-walled canyon, accessible by a narrow gulch dotted with plant life. Freshwater springs pour out the canyon's walls on its southern side, forming the pools from which the oasis takes its name. The only notable landmark within the Sister of Rains is a ruined tower, built long before the Era of Upheaval by a tribe of Bedine that did not survive to that era. As of the late 14th century DR, the oasis was a refuge for those Bedine who were outcast from their tribes, such as witches, wizards and other spellcasters. It was later abandoned.

markers.name: Skyship's Glade
markers.textHtml: This open glade has long been used as a landing site for both spelljammers and Halruaan skyships—the famous sailing vessels that fly through the air, the most famous wonder to come from the magical, oversea land of Halruaa. Over the 15th century, the glade was used as the site for many a magefair. But by the turn of the 16th century DR, it is continuing to see use as a docking site by Halruaan skyships and the once-hidden site has become widely known for it.

markers.name: Spirit Soaring
markers.textHtml: Following the fall of the Edificant Library in 1362 DR, Cadderly Bonaduce, a Chosen of Deneir, began construction of Spirit Soaring that same year using magic. The building was a large and beautiful cathedral-library complex dedicated to Deneir, with soaring towers and flying buttresses, and great windows of colored glass. The cathedral's main roof was more than a hundred feet from the ground and three of the towers reached more than twice that height. The stone was gray and brown, as if the roots of the cathedral were of the mountain itself. In 1385 DR, Spirit Soaring was destroyed after a battle with the Ghost King. By 1479 DR, the cathedral is home to an unknown threat. Those who enter are rarely seen again.

markers.name: Standing Stone
markers.textHtml: When the elves of Cormanthor and refugees from war-torn lands formed the Dales Compact, they erected the Standing Stone in the forest to commemorate the pact. It is one of the most important monuments in Daleland culture. Many Dalefolk take a pilgrimage to the stone to read its inscriptions. But despite roads that lead through Cormanthor, the journey to the Standing Stone can be perilous, and travelers must be prepared to face bugbears, gnolls, wild animals, ettercaps, and trolls.

markers.name: Storm Giant Aerie
markers.textHtml: The storm giant aerie of Lake Woe mountain is the home of a small tribe of storm giants who played a significant role in the War of the Hart, many centuries ago. The aerie is located at the summit of Lake Woe mountain, which itself grows out of the center of Lake Woe. It is almost always covered in storm clouds; when it is clear, thousands of birds tend to gather there. The aerie consists of a fortress palace of spired buildings interconnected by elevated walkways, a few other structures, and a large garden park. The buildings are of course enormous, beings sized for giants. It is said to be one of the most magnificent palace complexes in all of Faerûn. A mooring bay has been established in the aerie, where visiting cloud giants can anchor their cloud palaces and visit with the giants of the tribe.

markers.name: Stone Stand
markers.textHtml: The Stone Stand is sacred to both the Tree Ghost and Blue Bear tribes of the Uthgardt barbarians. At the heart of the site stands a tall oak tree, grown from a cutting from the Grandfather Tree. It is encircled by two cairn rings, which are each surmounted by a ring of menhirs. Each menhir is capped by lintel pieces that link the cairn rings together into two unbroken rings. These menhir rings predate the arrival of the Uthgardt tribes and are inscribed with the symbols of many nature deities, some too obscured by age to properly make out.

markers.name: The Sunglade
markers.textHtml: The Sunglade is a clearing in Yuirwood. Within the center of the Sunglade is a large circle of stone menhirs with a second, smaller circle of ten menhirs in its center. Both sets of menhirs are covered in elaborate zoomorphic images and ancient Espruar runes, and are magically warded against age, so they seem almost new.

markers.name: Svardborg
markers.textHtml: Svardborg is an isolated frost giant fortress built atop an iceberg in the Sea of Moving Ice. After Ordning of the giants was destroyed in the late 15th century DR, Svardborg came under the control of Jarl Storvald, the ruler of the frost giants. The fortress comprises a number of old frost giant hunting lodges, arranged in a circle around a central peak of ice. The core of the iceberg is hollow and flooded with water from the surrounding sea. This creates a well-guarded dock that houses the frost giants' vessels.

markers.name: Talagbar
markers.textHtml: Talagbar is a Dwarven mine that, along with its twin Delhalls, was frozen under the Great Glacier for millennia. The ancient mines were rediscovered in the 13th century DR, and fully exposed by 1372 by melting of the Glacier. As of the late 15th century, mining has fully resumed and the mine is estimated to be rich with deposits of bloodstone, emerald, ruby, copper, and iron.

markers.name: Tower of Twilight
markers.textHtml: This enchanted emerald tower with two twisted spires rests on an island within a small lake that drains into the woods to the west. It is invisible in the sunlight, but in starlight, the twisting spires twinkle and an emerald-green bridge appears spanning the water to the island. It the 14th century DR, it was home to the wizard Malchor Harpell. During the Spellplague of 1385 DR, the tower and its inhabitants vanished from Neverwinter Wood. It was theorized that the tower was just expelled from the normal timeline. The tower reappeared after the eruption of Mount Hotenow, in 1451 DR. At first, the tower began making infrequent and uncertain returns, however it was uncertain if the lives of those who lived inside had returned with it.

markers.name: Vuerthyl
markers.textHtml: Vuerthyl is an artificial oasis and military outpost created by the Zhentarim, and a stop along the Black Road. The oasis comprises a number of fruit trees that grow atop an underground reservoir. Like many of the Zhentarim-made oases, Vuerthyl was formed using a Decanter of endless water.

markers.name: The Wailing Dwarf
markers.textHtml: The Wailing Dwarf is a 4,000 ft (1,220 m) stone sculpture of a dwarf named for the sound made by wind passing over and through the cavernous ears, eyes, and mouth. According to dwarven oral tradition, the statue is a monument to Karlyn of House Kuldelver who led the dwarves in a victory over the giants around −5350 DR. The statue was carved in his likeness overlooking the eponymous Karlyn's Vale, site of the final battle, and was completed circa −5200 DR. The mountain complex with its colossal entrance was originally known as Fortress Kuldelvon, but it has been known only as the Wailing Dwarf since about the 7th century DR. Inside the edifice and the mountain was a dwarven city that flourished during the age of Shanatar, but was abandoned long before Dalereckoning began. Sometime during the Age of Humanity, trolls gained entry to the underground city and started to form a colony, kicking off a decades-long war with the guardian nagas that patrol the Wailing Dwarf.

markers.name: Warlock's Crypt
markers.textHtml: Warlock's Crypt is home to the ancient lich wizard Larloch the Shadow King, a survivor of the fallen empire of Netheril. The name itself is a corruption of 'Larloch's Crypt'. It is a great city of undead, populated by liches, vampires, wraiths, wights, and legions of lesser undead creatures. Travelers are warned not to even go within sight of Warlock's Crypt, as Larloch almost always prefers to slay trespassers outright. The city is made out of the ruins of a millennia-old Netherese flying enclave called Orbedal or Sanctuary, which had been dedicated to being a haven for peace before it crashed to the ground in the downfall of Netheril. Seen from afar, the soaring towers that comprise the Warlock's Crypt have the semblance of great talons clad in black mail, menacing and grasping at the sky.

markers.name: Well of the Chasm
markers.textHtml: The Well of the Chasm is a vast subterranean valley set in the bedrock beneath the sands of the Anauroch. It is accessible by a narrow passageway followed by a long, winding canyon. A single stream of fresh, opaque water passes beneath a pit set into the sands of the Chasm. Due to this natural source of fresh water, it is also an oasis, home of the Raz'hadi tribe of the Bedine. When the Zhentarim attempted to coerce and enslave the tribes of the Bedine in 1360 DR, they met significant resistance when reaching the Well of the Chasm, and were defeated by the Raz'hadi. This great victory for the Bedine came to be known as the Battle of the Chasm.

markers.name: Well of Cloven Rock
markers.textHtml: This oasis consists of a large, crescent-shaped lake, surrounded by many varieties of trees, within a grass-filled, bowl-shaped valley in the hills. The lake is fed by a powerful spring that gushes form from a cleft in a large rock in the center of the meadow. At the end of the meadow, the lake drains into a tiny stream that circles the perimeter of the valley. A well is also here, from before the spring burst forth. The oasis and well are popular among the Bedine tribes, and also among lions, who live near the oasis when not hunting in the Hills of Scent.

markers.name: Wreck of the Golden Crown
markers.textHtml: The Golden Crown was a galleon from Calimshan. In the early 1330s DR, it was carrying a cargo of treasure from the Calishite mining colony of Berranzo when it was struck by a storm and sunk off the coast of the island. Also on board was the wizard Hoch Miraz of Calimshan. As of 1501 DR, it still lay beneath 70 feet (21 m) of water, and its precious cargo and Hoch Miraz's possessions were still thought to be there.

markers.name: Wreck of the Hvalspyd
markers.textHtml: [not Hvalspid] The Hvalspyd, meaning Whale Spear in the giant language, was a frost giant long ship captained by Drufi. After the Ordning was destroyed, Jarl Storvald tasked Drufi and other frost giant captains with finding the Ice That Never Melts. Drufi and her crew sailed the Hvalspyd down to Chult, arriving around the time the death curse struck Toril.

markers.name: Wreck of the Narwhal
markers.textHtml: [not Narwal] The Narwhal was a stolen galleon from the Cormyrean armada and later repurposed for both piracy and as the centerpiece of the Refuge Bay Trading Company fleet. At some point in the late 14th or the 15th century DR, it ended up upside down in the middle of the Chultan jungle due to a dysfunction in magic.

markers.name: Wreck of the Star Goddess
markers.textHtml: The Star Goddess was a Halruaan skyship that sailed the skies during the 15th century DR. Some time the late 1480s - early 1490s DR, the Star Goddess and its crew took to the skies from Halruaa to explore the nearby jungles of Chult. While flying over the peninsula, it was assailed by a flock of pterafolk. They tore open the vessel's blimp and sent it crashing to the tree canopy of the jungles.

markers.name: Wyvern Tor
markers.textHtml: Wyvern Tor is a crag in the Lost Hills northeast of the Sword Mountains, and is easily seen by any on the Triboar Trail near the vicinity of Conyberry. It was originally inhabited by a nest of wyverns, but after they were driven out, many creatures used the place as their lair, notably orc groups in the late 15th century DR.

markers.name: Yliyl
markers.textHtml: Yliyl is a small oasis comprising a small pool of water surrounded by some palm trees and desert shrubs. Long before the Era of Upheaval, an entire tribe of Bedine perished from some unknown disease and were buried in the sands around the oasis. Over the years, stories about this lost Bedine tribe metamorphized into rumors about great treasures buried beneath the oasis.

markers.name: The Crumbling Stair
markers.textHtml: The Crumbling Stair is the remnant of a mansion named the House of Taeros that was built in the days of Phalorm and the kingdom of Delimbiyran in the 6th century DR. It was established as a school for young women that yearned to master the Art, and many girls were tutored here for more than twenty years. The mansion and much of the ridge it sat upon were destroyed when an unknown group of mages attacked, killing the teacher and most of her apprentices in a battle of powerful magics. All that remains above ground is a grand circular staircase of white marble. Six steps hover above ground level, held aloft by magic, and form a circular staircase rising to nowhere. The Stair continues below ground to what used to be the main floor of a grand hall and then to a set of partially collapsed cellars. Many strangely enchanted objects were removed from these cellars by various adventuring bands, leaving the accessible rooms nearly bare. The site is haunted by phantoms of the past.

markers.name: Crypts of the Deepening Moon
markers.textHtml: The Crypts of the Deepening Moon are a series of vaults that hold the remains of laranlors and laranlas (kings and queens) from the ancient moon elf realm of Ardeep. They are rumored to be guarded by baelnorns and green warders. Legends about the great wealth and powerful magic buried with the elvish royalty attracted expeditions funded by Waterdhavian noble families and independent adventuring bands. Few to none returned with their lives.

markers.name: Delimbiyran
markers.textHtml: The Kingdom of Man, officially called Delimbiyran after its capital city, was a human kingdom formed in 511 DR after the rest of the kingdom of Elembar was wiped out by a horde of orcs. Between 523 and 615, it became a part of the short-lived Phalorm, the Kingdom of the Three Crowns. Delimbiyran eventually collapsed in a civil war in 697 DR and was overrun by orcs the year after. The ruins of the capital still lie north of Delimbiyr Road and north of the Trail Way that leads south to Daggerford.

markers.name: Dungeon of Death
markers.textHtml: The Dungeon of Death was a dwarven gem mine, until the original builders were driven out by medusae, who in turn were killed by trolls. It was under troll rule that the mine came to be known as the 'Dungeon of Death', owing to the trolls' kidnapping of humans for food. By the 1300s DR, a group of nabassu controlled the Dungeon, using it as a staging ground for their schemes across the North. By 1369 DR, only the adolescent nabassu Viscaris remained, hoping to use the dungeon to lure in adventurers for him to feast on. Above-ground, only a ruined blockhouse remains standing, overgrown by the local flora, and surrounded by the debris of fallen fortifications. The area is notably devoid of signs of life, and an eerie stillness surrounds the ruins.

markers.name: Dungeon of the Hark
markers.textHtml: The Dungeon of the Hark is an underground bandit stronghold located beneath the ruined village of Moorsedge. For centuries, the site was the base of operations of a band of wererat brigands whose leader was called 'the Hark'. They were eventually routed by Stedd Rein and the Red Fellowship in 1372 DR. The former village stands on top of a cliff amidst rocky ridges. Its surface ruins are extensive, but very little is left save half-buried, overgrown stones and masonry, and those of the buildings that have been rebuilt and restored to be fit for habitation by the wererat bandits. Tunnels, holes and pits among the ruins lead down into the underground warrens that are the main Dungeon of the Hark. These comprise subterranean spaces constructed while Moorsedge thrived, and later linked by a network of tunnels dug by the wererats and others, including a connection to the Underdark.

markers.name: Dungeon of the Ruins
markers.textHtml: Originally a dwarven hold called Andalbruin, the site was transformed into a human village when a group of Netherese arcanists occupied the ruins in −626 DR and set up a magic school named Selskartur ('Tower of the Star' in Loross). Less than forty years later, one of the arcanists summoned the slaad lord Bazim-Gorag in an effort to protect the village against the troll army of the warlord Harska. When the trolls finally wiped out the arcanists, Bazim-Gorag remained bound and trapped in the ruins. From the 14th century DR, the slaad managed to remotely influence many evil individuals to come serve him. These individuals became a cult, calling themselves the Acolytes of the Hidden Flame, and came to inhabit the dungeon, doing all they could to enact Bazim-Gorag's will.

markers.name: Dungeon of Swords
markers.textHtml: Thardresk Crypt, also known as the Dungeon of Swords, is the supposed family tomb of the royal House Thardresk, ruling family of the ancient kingdom of Anauria. The crypt is only accessible by means of a tunnel that connects to the carved-out pathways of an abandoned gnome mining expedition. It is as heavily guarded as the other tombs in the area, with magical traps and undead. At least one adult deep dragon was also known to dwell within the deepest depths of the crypt before being slain by dragon cultists circa 1371 DR, though its mate survived to drive them out. The cultists’ expedition confirmed that that the place is actually a false crypt, serving as a portal nexus that opens a network of portals between a number of Anaurian tombs, including another false Thardresk family crypt beneath a ruined temple of Jergal in the eastern deserts of Anauroch, and an empty tomb beneath the Evermoors.

markers.name: Endless Caverns
markers.textHtml: The Endless Caverns are a series of deep-reaching cavern and tunnel complexes inside which live many powerful and dangerous creatures, such as Grimnoshtasdrano, the Riddling Dragon, and the lich Aumvor The Undying.

markers.name: Gauntlgrym
markers.textHtml: Gauntlgrym was an ancient dwarven city and the capital of the Delzoun. The city was, at first, occupied only by dwarves, but during its rise to power, it gained in notoriety and developed populations of humans, elves, gnomes, and other races, until its fall in −111 DR. After that, it was resettled briefly by humans until it was overtaken by illithids. After its location was lost for centuries, it was reclaimed and resettled by dwarves under the leadership of King Bruenor Battlehammer in the late 15th century DR. The city is built on a massive scale, with many doorways large enough that even dragons can pass through them. The city is adorned with icons to the dwarven pantheon and other dwarven heroes.

markers.name: Great Worm Cavern
markers.textHtml: The Great Worm Cavern is the traditional ancestor site of the Great Worm Tribe of Uthgardt. It is part of a vast underground complex, with tunnels connecting to other parts of the Underdark. Inside the biggest cavern is the ancestor mound itself, a large altar mound shaped like a great spiraling winged snake, which is the Great Worm.

markers.name: Grimlight's Lair
markers.textHtml: Located near a gorge, this cave was home to Grimlight, a large, clever and vicious behir, well known for his tastes for dwarf flesh and gold. He was defeated in 1374 DR by the reformed Company of the White Star when they entered the cave looking for a telkiira, a powerful and dangerous lore gem worn by the leaders of the elven Houses.

markers.name: Halls of the Hammer
markers.textHtml: The Halls of the Hammer are an ancient dwarven stronghold believed to comprise a large central chamber that is affected by illusions of terrifying violence. By the mid-14th century DR, the Halls had long been abandoned, and were home to a number of watchghosts and helmed horrors.

markers.name: Halls of the Hunting Axe
markers.textHtml: The Halls of the Hunting Axe are halls that were part of the dwarven realm of Besilmer before falling into ruin when the realm fell. The halls are constructed of stone with arched walls, and once contained windows consisting of coloured mosaics. As of 1368 DR, the smashed shards of coloured glass were popular for use in making bottles, but the ruins were occupied by doppelgangers and leucrotta.

markers.name: Haunted Halls
markers.textHtml: The Haunted Halls are a legendary dungeon located on the western side of the Starwater Gorge. They were built by dwarves for the human bandit lord Rivior sometime in the mid-1200s DR. Rivior was then slain by the Warrior-Queen Enchara of Esparin and the Halls were deserted for a time. Soon, the Halls became home to monsters and evil mages, followed quickly by adventurers. The number of violent deaths in the former bandit hold led to a large number of undead and gave the place its name.

markers.name: House of Stone
markers.textHtml: The House of Stone, also known as Stoneturn, is an immense square tower that began construction in 342 DR by the dwarves of Dardath. Originally, it was built to protect Stoneturn Well, a source of drinkable water. In the time of Phalorm, in the 6th century DR, the House was expanded and set up as a massive trap against marauding orcs. After Phalorm's fall in 615 DR, the remaining moon elves of Ardeep Forest kept watch over the House of Stone, until their population declined so much that they could no longer effectively guard the House of Stone. After the House was abandoned in the mid-14th century, it became a beacon to adventuring bands chasing tales of immense treasures and powerful magic that remained undiscovered. However, this maze-like place is riddled with brutal, deadly traps of the crushing and impaling varieties, and overrun with undead. The House is so deadly that carrion birds roost nearby almost year-round, never going hungry for long.

markers.name: Masulk's Tomb
markers.textHtml: Masulk the Magnificent was a Calishite vizar stricken by a terrible and incurable disease. After he died, Masulk's servants dug the tomb as ordered by the master himself, where his flying carpet came to rest. Masulk wanted to be buried with no coins, but with all the magic he'd accumulated during his lifetime.

markers.name: The Nameless Dungeon
markers.textHtml: The underground fortress known as the Nameless Dungeon is located below a stone bluff in the High Forest. At its entrance is a nearly impenetrable, magically protected stone door. It is said that the elves of the ancient kingdom of Eaerlann knew the true name of the site but refused to speak it, and that elven patrols once guarded it against intrusion. In the late 14th century DR, several factions of tanarukks and yuan-ti competed for the control of the site, before the sorceress Sarya Dlardrageth eventually freed the fey’ri (interbreedings between sun elves and tanar'ri demons) who had been imprisoned here for millennia.

markers.name: Ruins of Dekanter
markers.textHtml: Established in –2758 DR, the mines of Dekanter were incredibly successful, triggering the beginning of the Silver Age of Netheril. By −1658 DR, all the minerals had been extracted and it became a sort of laboratory for Netherese mages. It was abandoned with the fall of Netheril in –339 DR. The legacy of Netheril remains within the ruins as the dangerous side effects of powerful spellcasting. Dekanter's main chamber is an amphitheater built to giant-sized scale. Every rainfall causes some tunnels to close down or collapse, while others become open. The ruins of Dekanter are home to beholders, gargoyles, gnolls, and goblinoid communities. The mind flayer known as the Beast Lord was also known to have its lair here. One of his most famous creations was the Dekanter goblins, a variety of large, aggressive, horned goblin.

markers.name: Southkrypt
markers.textHtml: The Southkrypt Garden, also known simply as Southkrypt, is the ruin of a dwarfhold. On the surface, the ruins are surrounded by wild, overgrown and tangled vegetation, the remains of a magnificent garden originally constructed by the dwarven inhabitants. Southkrypt was once part of Haungdannar, a small realm of seafaring dwarves located on the Sword Coast during the Age of the Proud People, and acted as a defense for their eastern borders. At one point before it was left in ruins, Southkrypt was known to be a silver mine. Its interiors are described as being maze-like. Since at least 1358 DR, the upper level of the ruins are known to be inhabited by gibberlings and norkers, while the lower levels are home to storopers, vilstrak, vargouille, xaren, and a vampiric hill giant shaman known as Morg.

markers.name: Stronghold of the Nine
markers.textHtml: Originally a dwarven hold, this underground cavern complex was abandoned and fell into ruin until it was rediscovered and renovated by The Nine, a powerful group of adventurers from the 14th century DR. When Laeral Silverhand brought the Crown of Horns back with her to the Stronghold in 1337 DR, her fellows were gripped with a desire to possess it and their secure home transformed into a battleground as The Nine battled each other. Four members of the group died. Many of the magical defenses in place failed as the last three living spellcasters concentrated on the infighting. Eventually, in 1357 DR, Khelben Arunsun ventured into the hold to find Laeral, quite mad under the influence of the Crown, and took her to convalesce after freeing her from its influence. The stronghold was largely uninhabited a decade later. Shortly after, it was reclaimed by a band of elven adventurers who claimed the same name.

markers.name: Taerymdoom
markers.textHtml: This network of natural caves was named for an elf, Taerym, who was slain by a cloaker while trying to investigate the place. Several other groups of adventurers and explorers lost their lives to these evil tunnels. Those who ventured there say the caves stretch for several miles underground and are guarded by vargouilles.

markers.name: Tomb of Chonis
markers.textHtml: The Tomb of Chonis is an ancient funerary monument that has long been emptied of its contents. Over the centuries, it has been used as a lair by countless creatures and as base for smugglers, bandits and monsters out of the Vast Swamp to assault travelers on the road. It is a long, low barrow mound resembling a hill, running north to south. The barrow tomb was constructed of great stone slabs about 3 feet (1 meter) thick that had been roughly cut and loosely joined together. It was then buried in earth to form the mound. Like the surrounding landscape, it is covered over with grass, heather, and thistle. At the base of the hill on the southern end yawns an open door. A heavy stone lintel hangs over the entrance, with deeply carved letters in the Thorass alphabet giving the name 'Chonis'.

markers.name: Tombs of Deckon Thar
markers.textHtml: Named for Deckon Thar, the first leader of the bandit kingdom known as the Chieftains in Gold, the tombs are legendary. For years, these raiders attacked merchants between Silverymoon and Sundabar and grew fabulously rich. Their ill-gotten gains were hoarded in a great keep and in these seven cairns located in a secret box canyon. At some point prior to the mid–14th century DR, the great wight Vinjarek the Mound King arose in the Tombs and claimed rulership over them. His rule continues into the late 15th century DR, by which time he has become plaguechanged and is a menace to nearby Luruar.

markers.name: Aglarond
markers.textHtml:
Aglarond, formed in 1065 DR by half-elves, is a magocratic state. This land of magic, largely covered by the Yuirwood and only state in Faerûn to possess a significant half-elven population, was ruled by the Olósynne dynasty until the apparent death of the Simbul in 1425 DR, and by the Simbarch Council, formed by twelve mages as of 1497. But many half-elves distrust and question the authority of this Simbarch Council.

Aglarond's population is nowadays mostly made up of humans, though one third are half-elves, and a fair number of elves. The tieflings proportion is increasing continuously due to the large number of refugees fleeing from Thay, Aglarond's greatest rival.

Aglarond's economy is largely self-sufficient. They export copper, gems, grain, lumber and wine. They import glass, iron and textiles. But Aglarond don't seek actively trade with other nations.

markers.name: The Alaor
markers.textHtml: A tharch of Thay. Two islands, Alaoran and Raeldreth, that house Thay's fleet and shipyards and served as a strategical naval stronghold for the magocratic nation.

markers.name: Alaron
markers.textHtml: Home to the largest Ffolk populations and Caer Calidyrr, Alaron is the seat of High King Derid Kendrick.

markers.name: Aleaxtis
markers.textHtml: Aleaxtis, also known as the Kingdom of the Sahuagin, is the major kingdom of the sahuagin people, with the city of Vahaxtyl as its capital. This kingdom lays in the Alamber Sea, though its exact location is a mystery to the surface dwellers of the Old Empires.

markers.name: Altumbel
markers.textHtml: Altumbel is a secluded nation of fishermen and merchant-pirates. By choice, its people became isolationists and have little relations with nearby Aglarond or other neighboring countries. Altumbel is governed by a Ruling Council that meets in the capital city of Spandeliyon. Altumbel's harsh landscape is dotted with temperate, wind-swept valleys that are not suitable for farming. There are no forests in the region. The coastline is shrouded in fog, obscuring a rocky descent into the sea. The cold waters are rich with sea life.

markers.name: Amn
markers.textHtml:
Amn was a tremendously wealthy nation prior to the Spellplague. Post-Spellplague it still controlled important trade routes to Calimshan and Baldur's Gate as well as colonial ports in the Moonshaes and Chult. Its population is mostly human with a large minority population of halflings. The country's natural resources and wealth, combined with its mercantile nature, led Amn to become one of the most important and influential trading centers throughout Faerûn.

Between 100 DR and 212 DR, three cities were founded in the Emirate of Amin of Calimshan: Athkatla, Crimmor, and Murann. During the 7th century, King Brinnaq I established Amn as a nation of powerful merchants and absorbed a lot of territory, including the purchase of a large swathe of Tethyr's northern lands.

Nowadays, Amn is a realm dominated by the five merchant houses whose lords sat on the Council of Five. In Amn, arcane magic is considered illegal.

markers.name: Arnrock
markers.textHtml: The Arnrock, also known as Old Smokey, is one of countless volcanoes laying within the Lake of Steam, but the only one that breaks the surface to form an island. It lays near the lake's center, and is the landmark used by sailors to mark the transition from the relatively safe and stable western half to the more dangerous eastern half. The Arnrock erupted violently in 1386 DR. Prior to the eruption, the caldera was home to the halflings who called themselves the Arn. The eruption completely destroyed the halfling settlement. As late as 1479 DR, the caldera remained filled with magma, completely erasing any signs of the former inhabitants.

markers.name: Ashanath
markers.textHtml: An unsettled region of Thesk with rich soil that once was one of the petty and short-lived kingdoms that rose in the aftermath of the Orcgate Wars.

markers.name: Black Ash Plain
markers.textHtml: Named for the volcanic black ash always falling from the Smoking Mountains to the north. Only sparse grass and twisted shrubs grow on the plain and strange rock formations shift and move around the landscape on their own. Hardly any soil other than the volcanic ash itself exists within this dark land, which is blackened and infertile, and even the Mulhorandi army avoid it.

markers.name: Blade Kingdoms
markers.textHtml: The Blade Kingdoms include six city-states: Lotoli, Lomatra, Kirenzia, Sumbria, located on the fertile shores of Akanamere, Zutria, that lays on the shores and in the Hills of Apion, and, lastly, Colletro, in the Akanapeaks' rich Valley of Umbricci. All cities of the Blade Kingdoms are surrounded by smaller quaint rustic villages, vineyards, and olive tree groves. The city walls were erected on top of Chessentan ruins that predate the Blade Kingdoms by a dozen centuries. The Blade Kingdoms produce salt, olives, and grapes, along with wines and olive oils, and are known for their balls, feasts, and the annual grand Festival of Blades. Politically, the long militaristic traditions of the Blade Kingdoms founders eventually changed to democracy. Free-voting mercenary councils of each of the city-states turned into senates of nobility.

markers.name: Border Kingdoms
markers.textHtml: An area of lush grasslands between the Lake of Steam and the Shaar. This area is divided politically into many smaller principalities and kingdoms. Because of the instability of the Border Kingdoms, no accurate maps exist and the borders move around as rulers and boundaries change. A borderer is the name given to an inhabitant of the Border Kingdoms.

markers.name: Calim Desert
markers.textHtml: The Calim Desert, also known as the Skyfire Wastes, is a rocky desert that formed as the result of an epic magical war between two genies, Calim and Memnon. It is primarily a level and rocky salt flat, yet the magical effects of Calim and Memnon can change that. These often dangerous manifestations of the two genies are called 'Calim's Breath' and 'Memnon's Crackle'. The former is the cause of frequent sandstorms and whirlwinds; the latter the cause of earthquakes or the creation of patches of hot sand. The Trade Way cuts directly across the Calim Desert from Calimport to Memnon. The only reason it has continued to exist at all over centuries of Memnon's crackling is due to the magical minarets erected along the road by the Shoon Imperium.

markers.name: Calimshan
markers.textHtml:
In −7800 DR, a large group of djinn and their slaves and servants, led by the noble djinni Calim, founded the Calim Empire. In −6800 DR, the efreeti Memnon built the country of Memnonnar, north of the river Agis, and the two civilizations became hostile until -6100 DR, when elves bound Calim and Memnon into a gem. By −6060 DR the last of the genies  were driven out by humans and dwarves, and the human nation of Coramshan was founded. In −375 DR, a terrible disease ravaged the country. The pasha and his heirs were killed and the empire fragmented into independent city-states during a period.

After the Spellplague in 1385 DR, the prison of Memnon and Calim shattered, and the released creatures resumed their millennia-old hostilities. Then many of the residents of Calimshan, presumed to be human, revealed themselves to be genasi, and joined the war between the efreet and djinns. In 1450 DR, both Memnon and Calim were banished by an unknown event. The genasi of Calimshan became notorious for their treatment of humans as slaves and their constant fighting across the Calim Desert.

By 1489 DR, the human slaves of Calimshan had overthrown their genasi masters with the aid of a Chosen of Ilmater. Free of their genie masters, the people of Calimshan needed to find new ways to live together and tolerate those genasi who remained. So, nowadays, Calimshan is a predominantly human country with deeply rooted ties to the genies that had long since left the lands. The government of Calimshan is located in Calimport, the capital, and ruled by a pasha.

markers.name: Chessenta
markers.textHtml:
Chessenta is a confederation of city-states bound by common culture, but for most of its history it was not truly an unified nation, and the Chessentan cities were often at war with one another.

Founded as an autonomous province of the Untheric empire, in 929 DR the city states of Chessenta drove the armies of Unther to the mountains, freeing Chessenta from Untheric rule. But the kingdom slowly broke apart around 1117 DR. After the Spellplague, the war hero Ishual Karanok unified Chessenta as a single nation again, but nowadays Chessenta is once again broke apart into squabbling city states. The capital of Chessenta is the city of Luthcheq and, in 1479 DR, the ruler of Chessenta is Shala Karanok.

Chessenta's sizable middle class controlled the country's economy, with a government that encouraged prosperity for all, so tax revenues are high. Chessentans has also a well-earned reputation as fierce warriors, and indeed their nation has prospered through the conflicts between their rival cities. But few nations in all Faerûn are as erudite in philosophy, astronomy, poetry, and history as Chessenta. Chessentans practices slavery (most of their slaves are elves) and developed a deep-seated mistrust of arcane.

markers.name: Chondath
markers.textHtml: Chondath is a country with a fabled past, once the site of a mighty empire ruling over much of the Vilhon Reach and Sembia. Military defeats and numerous plagues led Chondath to shrink in on itself over a millennium. Now, Chondath is but a shell of its former glory. Adventurers flock to Chondath to see what abandoned treasures lie amid the ruined cities. Chondathans are primarily traders, often dealing with far-off lands. They are also a somewhat warlike people and distrust wizards and magic altogether. Waukeen, the goddess of trade, is heavily worshiped here.

markers.name: Chult
markers.textHtml:
Chult is a land of tropical wilderness: dense jungles and snaky rivers ringed by mountains, volcanoes, and sheer escarpments. Walls of mountains to the west, south, and east shield the interior from the sea and from the view of sailors. The rivers are so sluggish that it can be difficult determining which direction is upstream and which is down. The rivers pick up speed only where they thunder down through steep-sided gorges.

MAP OF CHULT

markers.name: Cimarine Isles
markers.textHtml: As of the late 14th century DR, this archipelago is populated by watersoul genasi and trade settlements from Amn. The city of Branestria on the western island, inhabited by watersoul genasi and partially submerged, trades with Baldur's Gate and some smaller settlements.

markers.name: Cinnabar Wastes
markers.textHtml: This region is characterized by badlands, steep-sloped striated rock formations carved by the advancing and retreating of the Great Glacier. The rock of the area is distinctly reddish in color. The whole area is littered with massive boulders and fields of broken rocks and gravel. This region has a reputation for being home to nothing but foul monsters, and indeed, goblinoids and giantkin make their homes in the area.

markers.name: Cormyr
markers.textHtml:
Cormyr, the Land of the Purple Dragon (Cormyr was once the hunting domain of a black dragon so old that its scales had turned purple), is historically one of the most prosperous countries of Faerûn. It survived relatively unharmed to the Spellplague.

Tensions between Cormyr and its neighbors Netheril and Sembia were constant. Between 1484 DR and 1486 DR Cormyr fought a war on two fronts: it mustered troops to defend the Dalelands against Sembia's invasion, and its own borders were invaded by Netheril. King Irvel Obarskyr was killed during the Siege of Suzail and was succeeded by his daughter Queen Raedra Obarskyr. By the end of the war, Cormyr has successfully reclaimed its land from the Sembian and Netherese forces and now turns its focus to recovery.

The ruling monarch has an advisor, the High Wizard, who is in charge of the War Wizards. The nobility of Cormyr is composed of the most wealthy and influential households. The Purple Dragons are the famous military of Cormyr, renowned for their discipline, skill, and loyalty, and considered to be one of the strongest armies in the Realms.

markers.name: The Dalelands
markers.textHtml:
The Dalelands is a loose collection of tiny vales called dales, each with its own territory, governance, and character.

  • Archendale
  • Battledale
  • Daggerdale
  • Deepingdale
  • Featherdale
  • Harrowdale
  • High Dale
  • Mistledale
  • Scardale
  • Shadowdale
  • Tasseldale

Each year at Midwinter, every dale sends a delegate to a selected Dalelands town for the Dales Council. Delegates debate issues affecting the dales, such as defense, border disputes, resistance to Zhentarim or Sembian aggression, trade deals, and matters relating to the great forest of Cormanthor.

markers.name: Archendale
markers.textHtml: The most militant region in the Dalelands is also one of the wealthiest. Archendale is the first stop for trade between the Dalelands and Sembia and a place where traditional Dalelands occupations like farmer and hunter give way to merchant and soldier. The people of Archendale believe mercantile and military strength together keep Archendale safe from longtime rivals Sembia and Tasseldale. Archendale's rulers are the Swords, masked individuals who keep their identities a mystery. The Archenriders are the dale's primary defenders, six hundred well-equipped warriors that patrol the River Arkhen looking for spies, outlaws, or anyone else who needs a lesson in Archendale justice.

markers.name: Battledale
markers.textHtml:
The people of Battledale are peace-loving farmers, shepherds, and foresters of no particular martial skill. Battledale is named for the many pitched battles that have taken place within its borders, much to the dismay of the folk who live here. The most fertile part of Battledale is the Belt, a region of fenced farms and cottages, orchards, and sheep pastures blessed with fish-filled streams and ponds. Stone tables sacred to Chauntea line paths and trails.

A few years ago, a silver-scaled dragonborn named Volsembra led a group of families back to the ruined town of Essembra, where she took the title of Protector of Battledale and began the town's reconstruction, Volsembra commands the Spears ofB attledale, a troop of one hundred Guards who safeguard the town and patrol Rauthauvyr's Road. Battledale has no formal laws; folk tend to their own affairs. Those suspected ofa crime are tried by their neighbors, who mete out whatever justice they think appropriate. Serious problems can be brought to Protector Volsembra, but she has no authority beyond the town in which she dwells.

markers.name: Daggerdale
markers.textHtml:
Daggerdale lies south of the Border Forest, stretching to the Dagger River and beyond. Rolling plains and rocky valleys give this realm a different character than the wooded dales to the south and east. Families of dwarf miners, primarily the Brightblade clan, dwell in Anathar's Dell, along with human and elf farmers. The village of Serpentbridge grew up around an old and unstable span crossing the Dagger River.

A few years ago, the son of a beryl miner from the Dagger Hills, was made Lord Kirkland Morn overnight and now rules unsteadily from Castle Daggerdale. He is idealistic but cautious, quick to listen to advisers but slow to make decisions. But the people of Daggerdale have embraced Kirkland.

markers.name: Deepingdale
markers.textHtml:
Deepingdale was founded by a sorcerer named Imryll Eluarshee. More than any other dale, Deepingdale is dedicated to respecting the forest and living in it without destroying it. Most families in Deepingdale include both humans and elves, and the dale boasts a small community of drow who worship Eilistraee and other good gods. The people of Deepingdale are foresters, hunters, trappers, and rangers; the forest provides everything they need, so long as they don't abuse it or take its gifts for granted.

The high elf Lord Theremen Ulath has ruled Deepingdale for almost two centuries, and his palace—the Tower of the Rising Moon in the town of Highmoon—is a destination for elves throughout the Heartlands. An informal militia called the Wardens of Deepingdale, quick to act and respected throughout the dales, protects the dale when necessary.

markers.name: Featherdale
markers.textHtml: Featherdale encompasses the flat and fertile farmland on either side of the River Ashaba between Blackfeather Bridge and Feather Falls. The people of Featherdale don't believe in government or laws, doing as they please so long as they harm no one. As a result of this free-spirited outlook, Featherdale has no large settlements, no ruler, no standing army, and no capital. Crime is dealt with through vigilante justice, resulting in feuds between families that go back generations. The lack of military defenses throughout Featherdale makes the realm an easy target for invaders, but the people of Featherdale aren't rich, and they have little to arouse the envy of wealthy Sembian nobles. Featherdale is a good neighbor to the other dales.

markers.name: Harrowdale
markers.textHtml: The oldest of the dales, Harrowdale was founded over a thousand years ago. The dale government is the Council of Seven Burghers, made up of the wealthiest individuals in the dale. Harrowdale has laws against chopping down the trees of Cormanthor. Harrowdale farms, orchards, and vineyards are broken up by hedgerows and crumbling stone walls. Harrowdale had a period of tremendous growth when Scardale Town, wracked by invasion and the Shaking Plague, descended into chaos and refugees fled here. Now the dale has returned to the quiet of its former years. The only armed force in Harrowdale is a group of mounted Scouts called the Gray Riders. They are commanded by Ellarian Dawnhorn, a grim elf warrior and spellcaster who has seen generations of Harrowdale folk come and go over her long life. Ellarian is infamously unfriendly to adventurers, considering them troublemakers who put innocent citizens in danger.

markers.name: High Dale
markers.textHtml: High Dale is one of the least-known dales, but that's just how the people of High Dale like it. The dale lies along a mountain pass that leads from Cormyr and the town of Thunderstone to the town of Saerb in Sembia. Many traders take this route, helping to sustain High Dale. High Dale is protected by harsh terrain, a famous company of mercenary archers, and an ancient defense treaty with Cormyr. The political and administrative affairs of High Dale are managed by a high constable chosen by the people of the dale. The current high constable is Cordance Coppercroft, a gnome engineer who led the effort to revive the dale's exhausted copper mines.

markers.name: Mistledale
markers.textHtml: Mistledale is considered one of the most fortunate dales. Its neighbors—Cormyr, Shadowdale, and the elves of Cormanthor—are friendly and powerful, and a meteor that crashed into the land centuries ago made it magically fertile. The thick mist rolls that fill the valley gives the dale its name. Six councilors elected make up a ruling body called the Council of Six. They in turn select another individual to act as grand councilor and command the dale's military forces. The current grand councilor is Honorious Vinleaf. The villages, hamlets, and isolated farms of Mistledale communicate via sets of bells to send messages up and down the valley, an especially useful communication method when the mist is thick.

markers.name: Scardale
markers.textHtml: Historically, Scardale has been one of the most influential of the dales. It benefits from clear geographical boundaries, a large population, and flourishing trade. But Scardale has a foul reputation as a home for would-be conquerors, and it has suffered many setbacks, including a terrible plague. Now, under a new ruler, the tiefling Lord Kharu Aumersair, the people of this realm are eager to reclaim Scardale's status as the wealthiest, most powerful, and most important dale. Merchants from outside the Dalelands travel to Scardale Town to barter with local traders, who then make the laborious journey upstream to sell goods in Chandlerscross. Among Scardale's legacies is the Shaking Plague. This magical sickness killed two-thirds of the population of Scardale Town when it erupted over a century ago, and it still surfaces occasionally in the towns along the river.

markers.name: Shadowdale
markers.textHtml: In Shadowdale, proud and stubborn families work their farms and mind their own business. Meanwhile, the Zhentarim, evil drow from the Underdark, and other malevolent forces gather in the eaves of the forest and in subterranean tunnels, threatening the peace that generations of Dalefolk have purchased with blood and tears. More than one invasion or would-be tyrant has been stopped at Shadowdale. Shadowdale has historically chosen its rulers by popular acclaim, selecting adventurers who've done the people of Shadowdale a great service or otherwise proven their valor and selflessness. The current ruler is Khara Sulwood. Her true claim to the loyalty of her subjects is that she helped repel an assault on the town decades ago. Humans, elves, and orcs live peacefully in Shadowdale, along the River Ashaba.

markers.name: Tasseldale
markers.textHtml: Tasseldale is a land of wide-open spaces. The twelve villages of Tasseldale are connected by the Tasselway, a road that cuts through rolling, mist-covered grassland. The people of Tasseldale are proud farmers, ranchers, and crafters. Tasseldale is famous for the forging of excellent swords. Fifteen years ago, Sembia retreated from Tasseldale, returning the realm to independent rule. The ruler of Tasseldale is Grand Marshair Rora Rudiland, a devotee of Tyr and graduate of the Abbey of the Just Hammer. She is advised by the Elder Council, consisting of one representative from each of the twelve villages. The dale is defended by the Marshairs, a company of sixty mounted warriors who patrol the dale and keep the peace. Tasseldale has a long rivalry with neighboring Archendale.

markers.name: Damara
markers.textHtml:
Damara is home to a sparse population of hardy people, working hard to farm rocky soil or mine the mountains for bloodstone. Winters are harsh and often make many roads impassable. The fortunes of Damara saw their peak between the mid-14th and the mid-15th century during the reign of the Dragonsbane dynasty. But Yarin Frostmantle usurped the throne in 1465 DR, remaining in power through the turn of the 16th century DR. As king, Yarin Frostmantle is considered by many to be a petty, incompetent, and oppressive tyrant. Since the late 15th century, King Frostmantle's reign is characterized by increased threats to Damara's borders, including monsters from the Great Glacier and Frostfell to the north, demons from the Dunwood to the east, and raiders from Narfell to the northeast.

The nations of Damara, Narfell, Sossal, and Vaasa, are collectively known as the Cold Lands.

markers.name: The Dawn Warrior
markers.textHtml: The Dawn Warrior is a lushly overgrown jungle island. When the island is seen from a ship sailing by, its shape is reminiscent of a cone-helmed head and an axe, raised to the 'shoulder'. It is a well-known sailor sea-mark. The island's shores are coated in white rocks that catch the morning sun and radiate a gentle pink glow. Over the centuries, the Dawn Warrior faced several settling attempts, but roaming monsters many dangers of the wild island made sure no group claimed the Warrior.

markers.name: Eastern Shaar
markers.textHtml: An eastern extension of the Shaar, separated by the Landrise. Compared to the fertile grasslands of its western counterpart, the Eastern Shaar is a relative wasteland.

markers.name: Elturgard
markers.textHtml: Elturgard, also called the Kingdom of the Two Suns, is a theocratic realm dedicated to Torm, the Loyal Fury. It has Elturel as capital, and is defended by a paladin knighthood, the Order of the Companion. In the late 1400s DR, Elturel and Elturgard as a whole are ruled by the High Observer of Torm, a priest based in the High Hall in Elturel and enforcing very strict laws. Since 1444 DR, a shining orb known as the Companion or 'Amaunator's Gift' hangs over Elturel, never setting and dominating all Elturgard, making it a land of unending daylight. Creatures of darkness cannot even bear to look at the city. It can be seen from nearly anywhere within Elturgard, though it fades with distance.

markers.name: Erlkazar
markers.textHtml: Erlkazar is a small kingdom that seceded from Tethyr in 1348 DR. In the late 14th century DR, the king of Erlkazar was seduced by a vampire who converted the local bandits into her own personal army. By 1479 DR, during the day, Erlkazar is a peaceful, sleepy land of farmers and hillfolk, but at night, the bandit army raids all of the countries that border the kingdom, most often at midnight. The vampiric nature of the Night Bandits remains a secret outside Erlkazar, for the locals are too frightened of their vampire masters to betray them to outsiders.

markers.name: Th7e Evermoors
markers.textHtml: A region that comprised hills and troughs, partially filled with chilly bogs and rolling landscapes. In addition to the trolls that famously infested its bogs, the Evermoors are home to a variety of other monstrous creatures. Despite this, a few scattered drovers, shepherds and prospectors managed to eke out a life within.

markers.name: Fallen Lands
markers.textHtml: The rolling hills and scrublands of the Fallen Lands appear rather ordinary during the daytime hours. At night, strange and seemingly in inexplicable radiances appear in sporadic locations. The area was once part of the realm of Netheril, and holds scores of ruins that serve as reminders of ancient cities and the battles that brought them down. It is a favored destination for treasure-seekers and other adventurers alike, although it is home to many monsters and beset with dead-magic zones, areas of wild magic and random discharges of witchfire.

markers.name: Fields of the Dead
markers.textHtml: A region of rolling hills that, around the mid–9th century DR, before the founding of Amn, was a favored battleground for control of the lands north of Calimshan. Centuries after the fighting, bones still littered the field and cairns of the dead were everywhere. Now used for farming and ranching, plows occasionally turned up skeletons in rusting armor, weapons, and other detritus.

markers.name: Frozen Sea
markers.textHtml: A region of hard ground of frozen sand. The place is a maze of natural sand sculptures, shaped by the wind and frozen by the cold. The area is void of any water or ice. In springtime, life briefly appears in the Frozen Sea, as low-growing ground plants briefly grow. During this time many varieties of birds will fly here to breed. Outside this narrow time, little life can be found here.

markers.name: The Giant's Plain
markers.textHtml: The Giant's Plain is home to large herds of migratory animals that travel from the Western Heartlands. These beasts includ rothé, deer, and boars, that, in turn, serve as a food source for the Sword Coast dragons, who consider it to be their 'happy hunting ground'.

markers.name: Great Dale
markers.textHtml: Governed by a druidic hierarchy.

markers.name: Green Fields
markers.textHtml: A region of rolling grasslands.

markers.name: Gundarlun
markers.textHtml: Gundarlun is inhabited largely by Northmen. They are excellent fisherfolk and merchants, and are relatively more devout when it came to the gods than most other Northmen. Their longships are adorned with figureheads of fish or predatory birds. Gund warriors preferred swords to axes, and Gund mercenaries are found all down the Sword Coast. Some fought as barbarians, but only a few as berserkers.

markers.name: The Great Vale
markers.textHtml: The Great Vale is considered the breadbasket of the nation of Mulhorand. It included the lands that lie between the Dragonsword Mountains and the Menesankh.

markers.name: Gull Rocks
markers.textHtml: This small cluster of islands is completely barren and covered in guano from the large local seagull population. The islands vary in size, with most being smaller than a ship and the largest being almost 1 mile (1,5 km) across. The tallest point of the Gull Rocks is about 50 feet (15 m) above sea level.

markers.name: Gwynneth
markers.textHtml: The most magical of the Moonshae Isles, Gwynneth's power centers around Myrloch Vale and the fey kingdom of Sarifal.

markers.name: High Moor
markers.textHtml: A large, monster-infested, open moorland, bordered on the east by the Serpent Hills and on the west by the Misty Forest. The High Moor contained numerous ancient ruins and structures, like Dragonspear Castle, Hammer Hall, and Orogoth.

markers.name: Ice Peak
markers.textHtml: The Ice Peak island is mostly made up of a huge volcano. It is at mercy to raging seas and the freezing cold storms all but four months of the year. Thick fog covers the region. The island is mainly populated by Northlanders and Ice Hunters. The bulk of its trade comes from the harvesting of fish, whales, and seals. Its tiny capital Aurilssbarg is controlled by a ruler loyal to Luskan, but the other villages on the island, Bjorn's Hold and Icewolf, often act in support of the Lords' Alliance.

markers.name: Icewind Dale
markers.textHtml:
An arctic tundra known for being the northernmost explored region in all of Faerûn. It earned its name from the harsh winds and icy storms that destroyed buildings and scoured the landscape.

MAP OF ICEWIND DALE

markers.name: Ilighôn
markers.textHtml: Ilighôn is one of two islands, along with Wavecrest, that make up the Eyes of Silvanus in the Sea of Fallen Stars. It benefits from the protection of reefs as well as the magic of the Emerald Enclave. The forested island is accessible via a gap in the reef, known as the Farshore Straits. A unique property of the island is that it naturally negates all arcane magic.

markers.name: Impiltur
markers.textHtml: Impiltur is a wealthy and influential nation of feudal lords, with a long and storied history within the eastern realms. It lost much of its glory and reputation over the course of the 15th century following the Spellplague. However, by the turn of the 16th century, Impiltur has regained much of its former importance, although the Grand Council is facing the presence of demons within the nation's borders. People are beginning to whisper about the return of the Impilturan monarchy and the restoration of Impiltur to the great empire it once was. Impiltur is a nation of humans with some dwarves and halflings among its populace. Its society is formed around the tenets of the Triad—the faiths of Ilmater, Torm and Tyr. This religion places the expectation of devotion, service and sacrifice upon the Impilturan people. Despite its national religion, Impiltur maintains a liberal attitude to the faithful of benevolent deities. Practitioners of the Art are commonly found here, and this region has often been a refuge for those who sought asylum from conflict in nearby lands.

markers.name: Ixinos
markers.textHtml: As of 1370 DR, this island was populated by a matriarchal tribe, comprised almost entirely of women, and ruled by Queen Elissa Tarixon. The island nation refused all visitors, even diplomatic envoys, and would trade only with select ships captained by women. Ixinos was also the home base of the all-women mercenary company known as the She-Wolves.

markers.name: Kingdom of Many-Arrows
markers.textHtml: The Kingdom of Many-Arrows was founded in 1371 DR as a loose coalition of orc tribes in the icy and windswept mountains of the Spine of the World, brought together under the rule of king Obould. It came to be known as the homeland of orcs who valued civilization, and who found as much pride in building their own realm as in tearing down others'. The Many-Arrows orcs were in constant conflict with the Uthgardt of the region but were ostensibly at peace with their neighbors in the Silver Marches. But the peace and stability came to an end in the late 15th century when the kingdom was destroyed in the War of the Silver Marches. It was not until the turn of the 16th century DR that a new effort to establish the realm would arise.

markers.name: Korinn Archipelago
markers.textHtml: The archipelago consists of dozens of small, windswept islands. All of these isles are rocky and hilly or mountainous, with only a few good fields suitable for farming. Although some of them are wooded with a hardy breed of pine, most of the isles are either barren moors, tundras, or simple bedrock. Many of them are inhabited by dragons, griffons, harpies, and wyverns. The human inhabitants of these islands are primarily made up of Northmen, many of whom are shepherd or fishers but will occasionally arm themselves to go pirating or raiding. Primary exports of the Korinn Archipelago's islands include fish, pork, mutton, leather, linen, wool, timber, and raspberries. The isles' dozens of small settlements are each ruled as mini-kingdoms, and are unified by little other than their shared Northlander culture.

markers.name: Kultaakarr
markers.textHtml: Kultaakarr, also called the Throne of the Titan Lords, is a realm of giants founded in the 15th century DR. It is ruled by titan lords from their provisional capital of Baunilorotha on the northern coast of the Sea of Frozen Tears.

markers.name: Lantan
markers.textHtml: An island-nation consisting of three separate islands: Lantan itself, which the Sambar Run separated from the southern island known as Suj and the easternmost, smallest and almost uninhabited Orlil. The islands are rocky and covered in lush jungle with stone pinnacles rising up from the trees. The settlements are built elevated above the ground and jungle cover, with turreted homes connected by 'spiderwebs' of rail-less bridges. Lantan has several coastal fishing communities, and the islands also have prime soil for farming. The inhabitants of Lantan are primarily rock gnomes and humans called the Lantanna. The islands are said to be protected by 'secret weapons'. Whether true or not, the ships of the Lantanna navy indeed have firethrowers and explosive nets, and the island nation exports many items of technological wonder to mainland Faerûn.

markers.name: Lonely Moor
markers.textHtml: An area of boggy land. It is occupied by bands of gnolls and bugbears.

markers.name: Lone Rock
markers.textHtml: A wooded and mountainous island, known as a ship graveyard, surrounded by wrecks of endless naval vessels.

markers.name: Mintarn
markers.textHtml: A a popular safe haven for those fleeing the authorities as well as neutral ground for conflicting parties to come to accord. This beautiful island asked no questions and turned no one away.

markers.name: Moonshae Isles
markers.textHtml:
Populated mainly by humans, split between the Ffolk and the Northlanders, two societies which had been rivals and enemies for centuries, the Ffolk venerating the land and the Northlanders revering the sea. Large settlements are rare, and most Moonshavians lived in isolated villages separated by large stretches of harsh landscapes. Both the Ffolk and the Northlanders are skeptical of arcane magic and suspicious of its practitioners, making wizards fairly rare across the isles.

The six largest islands of the Moonshaes are Alaron, Gwynneth, Moray, Norland, Oman's Isle, and Snowdown.

markers.name: Moray
markers.textHtml: An island overrun by werewolves and other monsters, Moray has become a refuge for evil.

markers.name: Mulhorand
markers.textHtml:
The nation of Mulhorand was found in an arid region in −2135 DR by the gods, as well as the rival nation of Unther decades after. In −1967 DR, war broke out between Mulhorand and Unther, then in −1076 DR, orcs invaded Mulhorand and Unther. After that, Mulhorand began a prolonged decline which gave rise to Murghôm, Semphar, and Thay. The people of Mulhorand were arrogant individuals that consider themselves better than any rival nation.

During the Time of Troubles, in 1357 DR, Mulhorand was ruled by a true mortal king (pharaoh Horustep III) for the first time in its history, after a long line of god-kings. He invaded Unther in 1371 DR after the death of Gilgeam. Horustep III's reign came to an end when Mulhorand was destroyed by the Spellplague in 1385 DR.

During the Second Sundering in 1487 DR, the gods of the Mulhorandi Pantheon returned to Faerûn and focused in rebuilding and ruling the reborn nation of Mulhorand once more. Among other things, they abolished slavery.

markers.name: Najara
markers.textHtml: The ancient realm of Najara comprises parts of the High Moor, the Forest of Wyrms, and the Marsh of Chelimber, with the Serpent Hills lying in the center. Najara is inhabited by a wide variety of nagas, yuan-ti, and lizardfolk. The main deity of this land is Sseth, but with some yuan-ti becoming increasingly disappointed in him, they are turning to the forbidden, worshiping Zehir. Circa 1474 DR, the spirit naga Jarant killed the previous King of Snakes Ebarnaje, triggering a civil war, increasing yuan-ti and lizardfolk activity in the area, and making the neighboring nations of Elturgard and Evereska more concerned with the existence of the kingdom after over a millennium of oblivion. Although Jarant's nefarious motives remain unexplored, the kingdom is now perceived as a growing threat.

markers.name: Narfell
markers.textHtml:
Narfell was once a great human empire that arose thousands of years before the Era of Upheaval in the Cold Lands. Its fiend-worshiping citizens were all but annihilated in a terrific war with their neighboring nation of Raumathar. Narfell is today a savage and inhospitable realm of vast plains that attracts few outside visitors. It is home to migrant tribes of horse-riding humans known as the Nars, scant few permanent settlements, as well as numerous long-abandoned ruins dating back to the fallen empire.

The nations of Damara, Narfell, Sossal, and Vaasa, are collectively known as the Cold Lands.

markers.name: The Nelanther
markers.textHtml: A widely scattered chain of nearly one thousand islands. More than half the islands lacked potable water and are an unfit place to live. The hundreds of islands that could support life are fought over by ruthless pirates who tended to prey upon the shipping lines of Amn, Tethyr, Calimshan, the Sword Coast and the Moonshae Isles.

markers.name: Norheim Isles
markers.textHtml: The islands of Canthrell, Jotunspine, Llorgh, Graygruun, Rotterock, and Dragonshome are collectively known as the Norheim Isles. This grouping also includes many smaller islands. They are considered to be part of the kingdom of Norland.

markers.name: Norland
markers.textHtml: The isle of Norland stands proud as the heart of Norlander culture in the Moonshaes.

markers.name: Northlander Isles
markers.textHtml: Northlanders are a tall, sturdy, war-ready people. The savage and harsh environments in which their societies developed had in turn created a people of fierce, hardy, and barbaric disposition. They are often impolite and blunt in their speech. Men wore their hair and beards long. They are said to often have tattoos on their faces which represented their specific island homes. Northlanders are descended from raiders and settlers originally from Ruathym.

markers.name: Nsi Wastes
markers.textHtml: A stretch of blighted jungle. The plant life found within are diseased and poisonous to any who eats from them.

markers.name: Nykkar, Isle of Memory
markers.textHtml: Nykkar is notable for being the central burial site of Calimshan's elite ever since the Seventh Age. In Calimshan it is traditionally considered impermissible to mark the location of the Isle of Memory on maps. Most Calishites rely upon directions or word of mouth to ascertain its location. The island is primarily inhabited by the Necroppar, clerics of the dead.

markers.name: Oman's Isle
markers.textHtml: The isle itself is peaceful and lovely, but made extremely dangerous by the giants and evil fey who claimed it as their own. Its shores are rocky.

markers.name: Orlumbor
markers.textHtml: Orlumbor is a small rocky barren island city-state. It is closely allied with Baldur's Gate, and also retains its independence thanks to Waterdeep's patronage and protection. Despite its size, the island nation is independent and influential, in part due to its skilled, in-demand shipwrights who produce some of the best quality ocean-faring ships in all Northwest Faerûn. Orlumbor's mainland-facing natural harbor is the main populated area of the island. The settlement's buildings are cave-like structures and complexes connected by a complex chain of tunnels and stair flights.

markers.name: Pirate Isles
markers.textHtml: A collection of rocky islands inhabited by pirates. There are fifteen named islands but around sixty in total.

markers.name: Plain of Standing Stones
markers.textHtml: Bands of human or dwarven outlaws sometimes managed to survive here, and there are also a few barbarian tribes of hill giant, hobgoblin, human, ogre, or verbeeg tribes living in caves within some of the hidden ravines and valleys that supported animal life.

markers.name: Prespur
markers.textHtml: Two islands in the same chain of islands as the Pirate Isles, though not considered part of them.

markers.name: Purple Rocks
markers.textHtml: The Purple Rocks islands appear as rocky outcroppings, but verdant valleys are sheltered from the storms behind their sparse forested peaks. Under stormy skies, the islands acquire a dark, purplish hue: it is this feature they were named for. The islands live at the mercy of far harsher sea storms than those on the mainland, and have a winter spanning nearly eight months a year. When not frozen by the winter winds called the Aurilsbreath, the islands are shrouded in a dense fog that lasts through the day. Here, like in rural communities all over the North, magic use is punishable by death. People of the Purple Rocks appear to worship Tempus (called Tempos), Auril and Umberlee, the usual Northman deities, but their idols of those gods all show many tentacle-like arms. The dragon Galadaeros considers the Purple Rocks its territory, and it is believed that Flamehome Isle, as he calls its lair, is actually one of these islands.

markers.name: Rashemen
markers.textHtml: Fiercely traditional nation ruled by its Iron Lord, Mangan Uruk who speaks for the power behind the throne: the Wychlaran, the society of masked witches.

markers.name: The Ride
markers.textHtml: An open steppe land of cold plains. The Ride is inhabited by tribes of mounted barbarians, proud folk who treated outsiders as fair game. Although they are prone to constant internal struggling, they would quickly unite to face threats from the outside. Known tribes included the Vaegould (aggressive worshipers of the primordials) and the Varm (a more peaceful tribe, practitioners of totem magic and worshipers of primal spirits).

markers.name: Ruathym
markers.textHtml: Homeland of the Northlander peoples, Ruathym is a rocky island with rugged coasts dotted with narrow coves, fjords, and inlets. The island itself is largely barren but with green hills and forested purple mountains. The people of Ruathym are known to be savage and violent, even compared to other Northlanders. As the first of the Northlanders, they are said to believe that it is their birthright to be regarded as the rulers of all other Northlanders. By the early 16th century DR, the island are relatively peaceful, but even so, outsiders are cautioned that the island can rally for war at a moment's notice: the people of Ruathym remain warlike, but mainly focus their aggression on each other, with wars between petty rulers erupting every few years.

markers.name: Ruins of Tathtar
markers.textHtml: Tathtar was a small kingdom founded in 227 DR, occupying territory in the Deepwing Mountains, Cloven Mountains, and the High Peaks. Upper Tathtar was overrun by an orc horde and destroyed in 293 DR. Lower Tathtar survived the conflict but was destroyed in 796 DR by a plague caused by the vampire Shyressa. By as late as 1370 DR, monsters and brigands dwelt in the eastern ruins of the kingdom. The capital of Tathtar is sometimes thought to be Tulhaspyr, at other times Dajaan, and still others think the southern capital to be Saalirk. The remnants of Lower Tathtar became inhabited by Sala-min Lion Warriors, centered on the ruins of Saalirk, which also holds numerous creatures placed there by Shyressa, who made it her home. Wyverns from the Cloven Mountains are also noted to inhabit the crumbling ruins of the region. Upper Tathtar lies mostly in the Deepwing Mountains, most noted for its griffons, hippogriffs, manticores, red dragons, and wyverns.

markers.name: Sembia
markers.textHtml:
The nation Sembia was founded in 913 DR and led by a merchant's council, who elected an Overmaster. This independent collection of city-states of considerable wealth and prosperity, in the path of many trade routes, is indeed governed by its merchants. Natural traders, sembians prefer to pursue mercantile endeavors, with a vague racism, turning away outlanders that 'looked different'. Also, corruption is rampant in Sembian society. The capital and largest city is Selgaunt.

Despite Sembia's aversion to war, a civil war was fought in the 1370s, which weakened Sembia considerably. The Netherese took advantage of the situation and by 1400 DR, the Netherese had fully consolidated Sembia into their empire.
