In 1484 DR, Sembia invaded the Dalelands, who in turn were aided by the nation of Cormyr. But after the fall of Netheril, in 1487 DR, Sembia regained its independence and became again the economic power it once was.

markers.name: Sespech
markers.textHtml: Sespech, also known as The Land-Between-The-Rivers, was a barony of Chondath, and became an independent nation in the early 10th century DR, in the aftermath of the Rotting War. Even after achieving independence, Sespech still name their ruler a baron, who reigns from Ormpetarr with the support of a court. Outside the few established settlements, Sespech mostly consists of grasslands, stretching from one border to the next. As a whole, Sespech is known to export horses, mercenaries, and salt, while importing metal. The nation relies on Mimph to serve as a port for merchants coming from the rest of the Vilhon Reach. The historical conflict between Chondath and Sespech has been a continued threat to the barony's existence, until the destruction of Chondath in the late 14th century DR.

markers.name: The Shaar
markers.textHtml: A vast land of plains in south Faerûn. The lands to the west of the Landrise are generally called the Shaar, while those to the east are the Eastern Shaar. Nomadic humans, centaurs, and gnolls populate the Shaar.

markers.name: Shining Plains
markers.textHtml: A portion of the Vilhon Reach, named after the visual effect of sunlight hitting the plains. The plains themselves are not held by any cohesive force, instead being composed of human-filled city-states, wemic and centaur tribes, and a powerful druidic organization called the Emerald Enclave.

markers.name: Ship of the Gods
markers.textHtml: This volcanic island is considered to be part of the Smoking Mountains chain. It has no ports. Major eruptions happened in the mid-tenth century and the mid-fourteenth century DR, the latter causing part of its upper slopes to collapse.

markers.name: Singing Rocks
markers.textHtml: The Singing Rocks mark the sea border between the realms of Calimshan and Tethyr. Moreover, captains commonly use them as a vital aid to navigation in order to avoid going too far off course to the west, away from the coast. The rocks were named for a strange effect reported by sailors whose ships had ventured close, and claimed to have heard singing from hundreds of feminine voices. They described it as beautiful, elusive, and haunting. However, it apparently had no dangerous charming effects. Moreover, landing parties found that the singing stopped around 150 feet (45 m) from the shores and that there was no living creature, magical effect, or any other source for the sound.

markers.name: Snowdown
markers.textHtml: Rich in mineral resources, Snowdown was briefly conquered by the nation of Amn.

markers.name: Sossal
markers.textHtml:
Sossal is an isolated nation, both in terms of location and diplomacy. From its founding in 331 DR to 1319 DR it was ruled under a system of monarchy. Since the death of its then-leader at that latter date, the nation is ruled by a druid conclave known as the Circle of the Frozen Sun. Sossal has very little contact with the rest of Faerûn outside of trade. The sailors of Sossal are rumored to know of a secret passage through the ice pack northwest from the Yal Tengri to the Trackless Sea, and tales are told of strange ships matching their description sailing as far south as Calimshan.

The nations of Damara, Narfell, Sossal, and Vaasa, are collectively known as the Cold Lands.

markers.name: Stormwreck Isle
markers.textHtml: This small mountainous island of volcanic origin is characterized by basalt cliffs, sea caves, and hot springs. Sharp rocks along the coast were the cause of many shipwrecks, especially along a particularly treacherous shoal to the northwest. White wildflowers grow on the island's northeast, while mushrooms and the occasional violet fungus grow in the caves below ground. This island is a beacon to dragons, and has become suffused with the magic of countless dragons who have died there. It is said that this has left a spiritual wound across the island that can cause unpredictable magical phenomena.

markers.name: Toaridge-at-the-Sun's-Setting
markers.textHtml: This island played an important role in the history of a powerful magic sword known as Taragarth. Aeroth, the hero of the Second Trollwar and wielder of Taragarth, hid the famous sword on this island in the late 10th century DR so his sons would not squabble over it. The sword laid there for centuries until it was discovered by a band of illithids and carried off to their home base on the mainland.

markers.name: The Sword
markers.textHtml: One of the three major regions of Anauroch. It is the hot, sandy region most thought of when someone mentioned the Great Desert. The term 'the Sword' is coined by sages because of the violent Bedine tribes known to live there. The Sword is the part of Anauroch most people thought about and cared about. It had great strategic importance as a route across the desert, and many of the ruins of the fallen empire of Netheril hid beneath its sands.

markers.name: The Teeth
markers.textHtml: As of the late 14th century DR, this tiny cluster of islets fell along the westernmost reaches of the territory of the white dragon Arveiaturace. As she hunted across the open seas, the dragon was known to alight and rest atop Barth, a crag that rises to the highest elevation among the Teeth.

markers.name: Tethyr
markers.textHtml:
Tethyr is bordered by Amn to the north, and Calimshan to the south. It was once a great forest inhabited by elves and dwarves, but over the centuries, monsters of all sorts have ravaged the lands. In –288 DR, Calimshan accedes independence to Tethyr and its people. In 1369 DR, after two decades of civil war, Tethyr was united under the rulership of Queen-Monarch Zaranda and her consort, King Haedrak III. As of 1489 DR, Anais Rhindaun is the Queen of Tethyr.

Tethyr is too big to be ruled easily from Darromar, so dukes and counts have little help from the crown. Remote Velen used this situation as an excuse to secede from Tethyr in 1423 DR.

markers.name: Thar
markers.textHtml: A rocky, broken moor stretching for hundreds of miles with a harsh climate. It is inhabited by wild tribes of humanoids such as orcs and ogres that are constantly at war with one another.

markers.name: Tharsult
markers.textHtml: An independent island nation in the Shining Sea. Its port is Urbeth.

markers.name: Thay
markers.textHtml:
Thay is a huge and terribly powerful magocratic nation ruled by the necromancer lich Szass Tam, along with the Council of Zulkirs, 8 wizards each specialized in a different school of magic. This Council of Zulkirs appoints the tharchions and tharchionesses, the governors of the vast provinces of Thay, known as tharchs. The arrogant Red Wizards, a magocratic noble class, are the upper echelon of Thayan society, nearly only from the Mulan ethnic group. By 1375 DR, they were divided in two factions by the civil war instigated by Szass Tam.

The 11 tharchs are: Alaor (islands), Delhumide, Eltabbar, Gauros, Lapendrar, Priador, Pyarados, Surthay, Thay Mount (host of the Citadel, the capital of Thay) Thazalhar, and Tyraturos.

Thay merchants, a rising class, sell silken textiles, perfumes, scents, wines, jewelry and others luxury goods. They look for lumber and food that their desertic lands lack.

markers.name: Thesk
markers.textHtml: The nation of Thesk is an oligarchy of around 850,000 people, inhabited by humans, gnomes, and orcs. The majority of its inhabitants are either merchants or farmers. The Golden Way, the main trade route to Kara-Tur, runs through Thesk, exposing it to a wide variety of cultures and making its people more tolerant than most Faerûnians.

markers.name: Thordentor
markers.textHtml: White sand beaches line the entire coast of this large island, and many large bays are suitable for landing ships. The inner island only grows low shrubs and bushes, and there are virtually no trees. The Nelanther pirates and most Tethyrians superstitiously avoid this island, and the only widely known inhabited settlement is a Tethyrian outpost on the western bay. Each year, anywhere from 5–30 whales beach themselves on the eastern and southern shores of the island. The island's beaches are often covered by the bleached bones of dead whales—for this reason, the dwarves of the region named the island 'Thordentor', or 'hill of bones'.

markers.name: Thousand Swords
markers.textHtml: The Thousand Swords are also known simply as the Archipelago of Altumbel. The islands are composed of coral and rock, and primarily contain fishing villages. There are numerous wizard towers on the islands, some of which wearere disused and some of which are still in use. Pirates also occupy the archipelago.

markers.name: The Tortured Land
markers.textHtml: Few outsiders went into the Tortured Land, and fewer still lingered there long enough to explore, let alone map it. It is believed to be a cold and harsh wasteland of rampaging monsters and an evil land shunned and little-spoken-of by even the Riders of the Ride. However, in a secluded part of the Tortured Lands there lived the ondontis, a community of pacifist orcs. Fifteen tribes lived in harmony with the land through farming and foraging. In the icy northeast, where the Way of the Powrie crossed the River Pelauvir at the bridge called Maram's Span is the settlement of Frozen Flindyke, the home of gnolls who claimed the tundra from here to the Frozen Forest.

markers.name: Threskel
markers.textHtml: Threskel, also known as the Land of the Bone Wyrm, is an impoverished kingdom ruled over by powerful and evil dragons. Threskel's most famous landmark is Mount Thulbane, the realm's highest peak and an active volcano that continuously fills the nation's skies with smoke and ash. Threskel is a sparsely populated land that comprises wind-buffeted valleys, low-set plains, and harsh stonelands. Scattered cities are dotted among these arid lands that are too often poorly suited for farming. Its people live hard lives that are not made better by the constant struggles that spring up between their draconic overlords.

markers.name: Tuern
markers.textHtml: Inhabited largely by Northmen. Though they worshiped no gods, they held great reverence for the red dragons that dwelt in the mountains. Their longships are big enough to house giants and are decorated with figureheads depicting fierce warriors. The Tuernish despised magic, and its usage is punishable by death. Clerics are treated with disdain, and even Northman shamans found little respect among the Tuernish. Other non-Northmen and non-humans only received respect if their skill in battle is obvious. The volcanic conditions also made Tuern an ideal home for both fire giants and red dragons. Though the fire giants had their own enormous longships, they rarely raided further than Gundarlun.

markers.name: Turmish
markers.textHtml: Turmish is a densely populated nation, famous for its mercenary companies and fair-dealing merchants that trade across all of Faerûn. Turmish is also unique in the Vilhon Reach for outlawing the slave trade. The country itself is peaceful, prosperous, and civilized. Its welcoming people, peaceful nature, beautiful and fertile countryside, and focus on commerce over warfare earned it the nickname of the 'heartland of the Reach'. By the mid-to-late 14th century DR, Turmish was a confederation of three city-states – Alaghôn, Gildenglade, and Nonthal –, each of them a democratic republic. The capital, Alaghôn, was governed by the Assembly of Stars, also known as the Free Council. Members were chosen in free elections. Cities are rare in Turmish because people prefer to live close to the land. There are dozens of towns and hundreds of villages, most too small to be marked on maps. A strongly mercantile realm, trade and commerce dominate matters in Turmish, but this is balanced by the dictates of the druids of the Emerald Enclave.

markers.name: The Tusks
markers.textHtml: The two islands that collectively are called the Tusks are Iyritrastrul and Borammur, separated by the so-called Gullet. Being one of the most dangerous areas of the Sword Coast, this perilous straight full of obtruding sea rocks is rarely navigated through. The islands' main population are wild sheep that survived some ancient shipwrecks along the Gullet. The isle of Borammur once produced copper and held a small village. Many years before the late 14th century DR, the entire settlement and the mines were overrun by mysterious 'dark things of the depths' that slaughtered the inhabitants. Various parties attempted to resettle Borammur and claim its rich copper deposits twice. Both lead to slaughter.

markers.name: Twilight Marsh
markers.textHtml: As the Trank River flows south from the Vanishing Hills, it opens up into a depression in the land, flowing between the small tors and thick vegetation that make up the Twilight Marsh. As the currents formed by the river continue southward, they form an eddy that encircles the archipelago known as the Ringcurrent Islands. The swamp is typically blanketed with heavy rain and a thick fog in the morning. It is considered quite unpleasant and difficult to traverse, with flooded terrain that reeks with the stench of decay. It is home to three clans of lizardfolk, and perhaps the most menacing of the swamp's wildlife are giant toads.

markers.name: Unther
markers.textHtml:
After the foundation of the empire in −2087 DR, the god-kings of the Untheric pantheon ruled the land. Gilgeam ruled Unther since -731 DR until his death in 1358 DR by Tiamat. During this period, the populace of Unther was primarily composed of humans. But Unther declined greatly and eventually Chessenta conquered Unther and ruled it as a vassal state for nearly a century. In the period after, Mulhorand conquered most of Unther, then the Spellplague of 1385 DR ravaged the land and Unther was transported to the world of Abeir. All Unther's territories on Toril became part of Tymanther.

During the Second Sundering, Unther returned to the world of Toril, and a second incarnation of Gilgeam, crowned as the supreme ruler of Unther, was able to reclaim most of the northern lands, the others ones remaining part of Tymanther.

markers.name: Vaasa
markers.textHtml:
Vaasa is an empty and inhospitable land of rolling hills, frozen wastes, meager grasslands, and open tundra. Most folk who settle in Vaasa do so in the Sunderland region in the southeast where they have access to both the gem- and metal-rich Galena Mountains as well as rare workable soil for raising crops and livestock. As a result of the harsh environment, the Vaasan people are known to be a tough and close-knit folk who view themselves as frontierspeople who prioritize adapting and surviving. As of the late 15th century DR, Vaasa is a feudal society in which all power rests with the Warlock Knights. Anyone who is not a member of the Knights is considered a lowly serf or a slave.

The nations of Damara, Narfell, Sossal, and Vaasa, are collectively known as the Cold Lands.

markers.name: The Vast
markers.textHtml: Over its long history, the Vast was home to the orc nation of Vastar for which it was named; the ancient elf city of Ylraphon, an outpost of Cormanthyr; the brief dwarf kingdom of Roldilar, the Realm of Glimmering Swords; and a variety of human city-states, some of which gathered into the nation of Vesperin. The Vast is a land of great open spaces, sparsely settled by diverse and mixed peoples. Once, it was covered in dense forests, but the orcs of Vastar cut these down, leaving only grasslands and isolated woods. This left a green and temperate realm of hunting grounds and rolling farmlands, rich in small-sized caves, rifts and sinkholes, that can be well concealed in apparently empty fields. Such places are a favorite spot for children to hide, for farmers to conceal their savings, or for some to build privies over. There is no overall government or leader in the Vast, and no capital city. Instead, it is divided up by a number of city-states and feudal holdings.

markers.name: Wave Rocks
markers.textHtml: As of the late 14th century DR, this small cluster of islands marked the western boundary of the territory claimed as hunting grounds by the white dragon Arveiaturace. At the same time, the isles served as the northeastern boundary of the seas patrolled by the navy of Evermeet, who hunted the seas for potential threats from pirates and Northmen raiders.

markers.name: Whalebones
markers.textHtml: An archipelago. The islands are called the Whalebones because of the whale skeletons that could be found on the islands' beaches. Petty, warmongering kings ruled over the islands, often skirmishing in battles whose casualties rarely exceeded a few dozen. The larger islands are home to massive rocs that are used as mounts by the storm giants of Maelstrom. The islanders paid tribute to the rocs and giants by leaving them food and treasure. Finback, the tallest island of the Whalebones, held the lair of the crazed bronze dragon, Tauntzoth of the Rocks, prior to her death.

markers.name: Whamite Isles
markers.textHtml: An archipelago. Once the home of a number of warring states, the fun is over when in 1369 DR almost everyone who lived there drowned themselves and became undead.

markers.name: The Wayrock
markers.textHtml: Both an island and a mountain. It is visible from up to 100 miles away. The only man-made structures on it are a ruined keep on the eastern slope and a temple of Cyric, which had been stripped, stolen, and placed upon the Wayrock by the githvyrik sorcerer Vhostym. It later became a temple to Mask, guarded closely by his exarch Drasek Riven.

markers.name: Wavecrest
markers.textHtml: Wavecrest is one of two islands that made up the Eyes of Silvanus on the Sea of Fallen Stars. It benefits from the protection of reefs and also the magic of the Emerald Enclave. The island is known for its untamed jungles full of mysteries and danger.

markers.name: Adhe Wood
markers.textHtml: This forest is old, dense, and overgrown, and the trunks of its trees stand very close together. Most of these are duskwoods and shadowtops, while felsuls and oaks are found on the southern edge. The tallest trees grow over 80 feet (24 m) tall. As a result, the depths of Adhe Wood are kept in eternal darkness. The forest is infested with arachnid monsters. These spiders can be of any size or variety, and most possess bizarre magical powers and mutations. They are exceedingly vicious and constantly hunt both each other and those who venture into the forest. The many mutant spiders are thought to be the result of some magical force active in the heart of Adhe Wood, likely ancient and evil.

markers.name: Ankhwood
markers.textHtml: A forest whose insect, animal and plant populations have become mutated. There were once wild magic and dead magic areas orbiting one another in the forest, but by 1374 DR this effect had subsided.

markers.name: Archwood
markers.textHtml: A forest on the northern border of Archendale. The majority of the forest has never been logged because it contains ruins infested with the rot-minions of Moander, the god of corruption and rot. It is a dark, dense forest full of oak, ash, elm, shadowtop and duskwood trees.

markers.name: Ardeep Forest
markers.textHtml: The Ardeep Forest, also known as the Faraway Forest or Elfwood, is a relatively small but thick woodland. The interior of Ardeep Forest contains many ridges and steep gullies hidden beneath thick vines, dense shrubbery, and cloaking mists that present a danger to casual travelers. In addition, tunnels, cellars, and vaults constructed by dwarves during a time of alliance became pitfalls centuries later. Thanks to the loamy ground, Ardeep Forest's vegetation is thick and healthy and aids the growth of mushrooms that glow faintly. It is a place with sun-speckled paths, parcels of deer, and pleasant verges among blueleaf, duskwood and weirwood trees. Deeper into the forest the vegetation becomes dense with shrubbery, cloaked in vines, shrouded in mists, home for wild boar, wolves, and the occasional owlbear.

markers.name: Arn Forest
markers.textHtml: The Arn Forest is an area of pine forests and swamps, which does not see many visitors. The western area of the forest is very rainy and marshy, unlike the eastern side. It is infested by several bands of kobolds and various ruins can be found inside that date back to the ancient empire of Netheril.

markers.name: Ashenwood
markers.textHtml: A wild evergreen forest known for its large number of fey inhabitants. The forest comprises a number of different varieties of trees, including pines and firs, along with ash and aspen trees. The Rashemaar people consider the Ashenwood a sacred site filled with ancient magic, and refuse to settle within its borders. While there are no humans that dwell within the forest, hunters make regular forays into the woods to go hunting. There are certainly plenty of dangerous beasts within. Mothers in Rashemaar families tell their children of the 'Wood Man', a terrifying specter that haunts the Ashenwood forest.

markers.name: Border Forest
markers.textHtml: A large forest that connects four different regions of Faerûn: the Dalelands to the south, Anauroch to the west, the Tortured Land to the north, and the Moonsea to the east. The Border Forest is a mixture of pines, oaks, and other deciduous trees. The forest undergrowth is relatively thick, making travel difficult. A very large population of wolves inhabits the forest, and early humans in the area based some of their societal norms after the behavior of this community. As the influence of the elves in the forest dwindled, other creatures replaced them, most notably large populations of orcs and trolls. By 1372 DR the Border Forest was inhabited mostly by fey.

markers.name: Brynwood
markers.textHtml: The terrain under Brynwood is tumbled and uneven, going up and down with many outcroppings of rock and ravines with steep sides, and the forest spreads chaotically across this. There are no tracks through it. A number of legends surround Brynwood and monsters are said to dwell there. A story oft-told in the taverns of Impiltur speaks of an ancient castle with many spires lying at the heart of Brynwood. This is said to be the home of the Bryndar. The tale of the Bryndar is unproven but popular and takes many forms, telling of shapechanging women, or creatures who take the form of women, who dwell in the wood and either lure in male victims or travel far to abduct wizards.

markers.name: Channathwood
markers.textHtml: A small forest near the border of Lapaliiya. The Channathwood is the source of the River Channath.

markers.name: Chondalwood
markers.textHtml: Also known as Satyrwood, this massive forest was once home to the green elf realm of Nikerymath. It is rumored to hide the elven city-state of Rucien-Xan. It is a dangerous area filled with wild elves, territorial druids, satyrs, and Ghostwise halflings, a ferocious and telepathic clan that shuns outsiders, all of which have one goal in mind: to prevent human encroachment.

markers.name: Cloakwood
markers.textHtml: An ancient, thickly grown forest in the Sword Coast. Unlike the cliffs to the north, Cloak Wood's shoreline theoretically allows a ship to moor and send a small boat to shore for water and supplies. In practice, only desperate mariners dare to face the wood's dangerous wildlife. Cloak Wood is indeed known to be full of beasts, monsters, and vicious fey, causing most folks to avoid the forest. The sages of nearby Candlekeep assert that Cloak Wood contains portals to several other parts of the cosmos, but as of 1479 DR, these portals are believed to be defunct.

markers.name: Cormanthor
markers.textHtml: The forest of Cormanthor, also known as The Elven Woods and The Northern Forest, was long the place of the elven empire of Cormanthyr. It is a large, dense forest comprising various species of plants, including some trees that can grow over 100 feet deep in the woods. Although Cormanthor can be considered one forest, it incorporates four different forests: the Elven Court, Semberholme, the Tangled Trees, and the Vale of Lost Voices. The first three roughly correspond to the old elven communities of the same names. The forest is also divided roughly according to tree type: the Rimwood is mainly made up of pine trees, runs along the edge of the forest and is the least dense part of it; the Midwood consists chiefly of white ash and beech trees, separates the Rimwood from the Starwood and accounts for roughly half of the total area of the forest; the Starwood is made up mostly of giant oaks and maple and is the densest part of the forest. Light rain serves the forest year-round, and the ground is usually moist, keeping humidity high. Winds cannot penetrate the thick, dense canopy, so the forest is calm at ground level. At the height of the summer, storms can come and go in just an hour. There are often patches of dense fog in the forest, especially near the northern Elvenflow. The fireflies in the forest can cause wild blazes, especially on clear summer nights, when they assemble in huge swarms.

markers.name: Dernall Forest
markers.textHtml: The Dernall Forest is the largest woodland on the island of Alaron. It is a region of deep contrast, serving as both a vital trade corridor and a dangerous haven for monstrous gangs. The forest is primarily dominated by violent gangs of goblins, hobgoblins, and bugbears. These groups frequently prey on small hamlets within the woods, travelers on the road, and even each other. They are known to cooperate with packs of Worgs that roam the forest, occasionally launching raids on the prosperous farms of southern Alaron. In opposition to these dark forces are the forest's guardians, the Swanmays. These are fey creatures capable of transforming into swans that dwell near the Swanmay River. They actively seek out heroic individuals to help them restrain the goblinoid gangs and restore natural harmony to the woodland.

markers.name: Duskwood
markers.textHtml: Of all forests in the Border Kingdoms, the Duskwood is the deepest and most untamed. It has dense undergrowth. Duskwood is home to several species of unique plants and animals. Ash wheat grows in abundance every spring, providing a generous food source for the badger mice and swelling their numbers.

markers.name: Dunwood
markers.textHtml: Dunwood, known as The Rawlinswood prior to 1479 DR, is a large forest dense with firs, oaks, and pines thinning to taiga and muskeg in the north. It is larger than the Forest of Lethyr and together they cover 70,000 square miles. Legend in the Great Dale suggests a dragon cleared the valley between the Duskwood and the Forest of Lethyr with fire. Winters are bitterly cold, while summers are cool with abundant mosquitos when the permafrost thaws. Woodsmen take lumber from the Dunwood for construction in Impiltur and Damara. A diverse assortment of forest creatures, magical beasts, the undead, and demons from the ruins of Dun-Tharos can all be encountered here.

markers.name: Earthwood
markers.textHtml: A thick forest which consists mainly of birch and evergreen trees. Despite the poor appearance of the stony, hilly soil, the fertility of the land is such that it allows trees to grow quickly. The cause of this was dictated by legend as the work of a druid who was attempting to defy the advance of the Great Glacier, which had reached this far south in the past. The forest is thinly inhabited, and some woodcutters choose to live here. The goblins and other inhabitants of the Earthspur Mountains will not enter the forest.

markers.name: Erech Forest
markers.textHtml: A dark and isolated forest that is the domain of malevolent fey creatures and telthors. It is also home to dangerous plants like assassin vines and shambling mounds, as well as fearsome beasts.

markers.name: Far Forest
markers.textHtml: One of a group of three forests named the Triangle of Trees. This was once a relatively peaceful forest much like the brighter parts of the nearby High Forest, but as dark creatures poured out of Hellgate Keep into these woods, the forest itself became dark and twisted. The Far Forest is full of small lakes, some of which have become stinking marshes infested by will-o'-wisps and a few chuul. The heart of the forest is populated by giant spiders and other creatures that are the result of cruel magical experiments conducted by the Netherese.

markers.name: Forest of Amtar
markers.textHtml: This large, lush forest is extremely hot and humid, with temperatures typically above 90F (32C) even during the night, for most of the year. The majority of trees are suthwood which intertwine with each other to form nearly impenetrable barriers in the densest parts of the forest. Interspersed among the suth are tall shadowtops and slightly shorter pine trees, all towering over a thick underbrush made of ivy, climbing vines, and brambles like living, leafy spider webs clawing their way upward. The canopy and clinging vines block most of the sunlight from reaching the ground, creating a green gloom in the understory. The floor of the forest is typically covered with dead leaves to a depth of at least a foot (30 cm) that decompose in the damp dark to form rich but smelly black soil. The eastern part of the forest hides many stone ruins of past cities, built by human-sized inhabitants as well as giant-sized, that make for break-neck drops and break-ankle ditches covered by profuse undergrowth.

markers.name: Forest of Lethyr
markers.textHtml: One of the two forests that comprise the borders of the Great Dale, the other being Duskwood. Lethyr is the southern border and separates it from Thesk (which claims the trees south of the River Flam). The forest is guarded by reclusive druids and rangers as well as a few treants who forbid the exploitation of its resources. The forest was once connected to the Duskwood, and together they were known as the Riildath. The Great Glacier's expansion down a valley within its woods split the forest into two and the trees never regrew after the ice retreated back to the north. The Forest of Lethyr, as it came to be known, saw very little settlement for many centuries afterward, though its southern reaches were extensively logged. It houses the architectural marvel of Yeshelmaar.

markers.name: Forest of Mir
markers.textHtml: A dense forest that covers the southern highlands of Tethyr and continues south over the Marching Mountains and into Calimshan. It was named after the lost elven kingdom of Keltormir and the later human First Kingdom of Mir. The forest has hilly, rough terrain, which makes passage of large groups difficult. Many trails pass through the forest, and except for a few logging roads, the majority of these trails are game trails that lead to ponds or streams and change rapidly. There is plenty of water in the forest: small streams run intermittently throughout, emptying into the Spider Swamp. The forest consists mostly of evergreen trees, growing in dense clusters. These trees are far taller than any others found in the region, and they produce pine cones of legendary size. The forest is stifling hot most of the year, the pine trees doing little to cool the forest from the heat of the sun. The Forest of Mir has always been known as a great hive of monster races, posing continual problems for both Tethyr and Calimshan. Goblins are the most populous inhabitants, followed closely by ogres and gnolls.

markers.name: Forest of Tethyr (Wealdath)
markers.textHtml: The largest forest in the Lands of Intrigue and one of the largest in all of Faerûn. Some scholars are of the opinion that the forest is the 'single most important thing' that sets the culture of Tethyr. The elves also call the forest 'Wealdath', which means 'unspoiled woods' in Elvish. Worship of both Eldath and Silvanus is very popular among those living near and within the borders of the forest, even among the elves. The forest is dark and cool, and receives a lot of rain, especially on the Peninsula. It is full of small lakes, pools, streams, and cascades. The terrain on the Peninsula is rather rocky, unlike the rest of the forest. The trees are very large and old, their wide trunks rising to a great height above. Most of them are evergreens, though there are many other kinds as well, including cedar, duskwood, maple, and oak. The forest is full of a plethora of monsters. Packs of wolves are also common, and the trees are home to an abundant variety of birds and wild game. On the Peninsula, the moist conditions mean that there are many fungi, molds, and slimes to be found.

markers.name: Forest of Wyrms
markers.textHtml: A dark and dangerous forest in the Western Heartlands. It contains many pines and tall redwood trees, some with trunks more than 20 feet (6 m) in diameter. Helmthorn brush grows dense in the forest, but there is not much vegetation on the forest floor and the terrain is mostly smooth. The air in the forest is cool and damp, and mists cover some areas of the ground. The forest's name is derived from the population of green dragons who inhabit the eastern side of the forest. The forest is also home to great many species of poisonous and constrictor snakes.

markers.name: Fogotten Forest
markers.textHtml: A small but mysterious forest of treants and fey creatures found in the Backlands of the Western Heartlands. The forest is made up of overgrown and ancient oak, walnut, and shadowtop trees. The thick foliage of the forest is cast in continual shadow. The forest's shadows contain an easy area of access to the Plane of Shadow. The forest is inhabited by a large population of treants, led by their 'king' Fuorn, indeed believed to be the greatest collection of their kind in the North. Fey creatures are believed to inhabit the forest, among them sprites, korreds, and unicorns.

markers.name: Frozen Forest
markers.textHtml: A vast coniferous expanse, so named for the glittering ice that sheaths the needles and boughs of its close-standing trees. At the forest edges, most of the trees were heavily bowed from the accumulated weight of the ice, but deeper within towards the heart of the wood, the trees stood too closely together to bend, and their uppermost boughs became welded together with ice. The forest had very few natural clearings, and no human-cut roads or trails. Ground fogs are ever-present below the low forest canopy; close to the ground there is an unnatural warmth radiating up from underground magma, which supports the trees and wildlife. At various times over the ages, humans tried to settle within the forest, but persistent hobgoblin attacks decimated their settlements and numbers and forced survivors to flee to the south. They reported finding strange ruins and foundations in the forest from previous settlements.

markers.name: Ganathwood
markers.textHtml: The Ganathwood serves as a border of sorts, as Mulhorand has no towns nor outposts to the east of the Ganathwood or the Fields of Ganath. The Ganathwood's green willow vines are used in creation of wands of sleep, while cedars and beech are logged by Mulhorandi woodcutters for export to Thay. The Ganathwood is the home of an ancient elven society that shares their heritage with the elves of Yuirwood. The edges of the wood holds a group of escaped Thayan and Mulhorandi slaves that over the course of years have become savage, surviving on marauding villages of Murghôm.

markers.name: Glimmerwood
markers.textHtml: Glimmerwood combines what were once called the Moonwood, Cold Wood, Druarwood, and the Night Trees. The Moonwood is known to be the haunt of many lycanthropes, primarily in the northern reaches; its southern section is home to small bands of moon elves. The Druarwood is sparse and narrow, full of pine, spruce firs and conifers, and near mountains the forest gives way to the scrub and heaths that are arid and cold, in which there are looming glaciers and peaks; this part has a bad reputation as a place always being raided by orcs, giants, and other dangerous creatures. The Cold Wood is a wild place, full of pine, birch, and spruce trees, and in the winter time the snow is so deep that the trees are often bent down; most woodcutters and homesteaders avoid it because Uthgardt barbarians hunt in the area, but will not cut any living tree and keep fires to a minimum to protect the forest. The Night Trees are a dark and silent wood shunned by all, as an ancient evil power lurks in the dark under the foliage.

markers.name: Gray Forest
markers.textHtml: The Grey Forest, also spelled the Gray Forest and also called the Tsurlar Forest, was once populated with moon elves. However, when hobgoblins and various other creatures began to encroach on their lands, the elves fought a losing war against them, facing extinction. Thus, their last act was to transform themselves into trees, so that they would never leave. In amongst the ordinary trees of the Gray Forest can be found circles of the grand, gray-barked trees with dark leaves that the moon elves have turned themselves into. Small shrines dedicated to Eilistraee can also be found in the Grey Forest.

markers.name: Gulthmere
markers.textHtml: An overgrown forest of pine and cedar that is sacred to the god Nobanion and his followers. It is an untamed, subtropical expanse of woodland predominantly made up of cedar and pine trees. The terrain of the forest is hill country, with rich mineral deposits and so is coveted by miners. The terrain of the forest also includes at least two dormant volcanoes. Located within the forest south of Starmantle is the Lightless Lake, also known as the Moonmere. For hundreds of years beginning in the 3rd century DR, the Gulthmere Forest along with the Chondalwood were watched over a band of druids, led by one the five Grand Druids of the Realms. Their order defended the forest realm from the expansion of neighboring nations, and eventually formed the expansive druidic organization known as the Emerald Enclave. Some time during the 15th century DR, the forest of Gulthmere transformed into the nation of Gulthandor, a forest realm still governed by the druidic leaders of the region.

markers.name: Hermit's Wood
markers.textHtml: During the life of King Azoun II (1001–1056 DR), a hermit was the first person to dwell in the forest, giving it its name. By 1372 DR the Hullack Forest, the King's Forest, and Hermit's Wood were almost all that remained of the southern reaches of Cormanthor, having been pushed back significantly during the prosperous reign of Azoun IV. The village of Blustich lays at the edge of Hermit's Wood, on the western bank of the Wyvernflow, and the settlement of Ongul's Water lays in a wooded valley on the northwest side of the wood. Some of the braver folk hunt in Hermit's Wood. Hickory trees grow in the forest, and their nuts are gathered by the inhabitants of Blustich. Meanwhile, blueleaf trees grow in large groves on the northern side of Hermit's Wood.

markers.name: High Forest
markers.textHtml: The High Forest, also known as the Great Everwood, is a remnant of the days of old, when all of Faerûn was covered in green and elves, giants and dragons ruled the world. It is recorded as being over 500 miles (800 km) in length from north to south and covers nearly 20% of the lands of what called the 'Savage Frontier', so it is considered the greatest forest in Faerûn. The region of forest within the wide bend of the Heartblood River is called the Dire Wood, where a great massacre once took place. Strange supernatural phenomena originate from this place. At the northern end of the High Forest is an area called Turlang's Wood, a place where the forest is continuing to expand thanks to the tending of a band of treants. The forest is not ruled by any one group, but instead contains many forces and groups, including Fey'ri, nature-worshiping druids, Uthgardt barbarians, and the elven Caerilcarn, often in competition with one another. The most powerful of these are the treants led by the elderly Turlang.

markers.name: Hullack Forest
markers.textHtml: A forest that traditionally marks Cormyr's eastern border with the Dalelands. It was named for the legendary human druid Hullack of Eldath. It was formerly part of the huge forest of Cormanthor and was the oldest forest in all of Cormyr, but by the late 1300s DR, Hullack Forest and the King's Forest were almost all that remained of the southern reaches of Cormanthor in Cormyr, having been cut back significantly by settlers during the prosperous reign of King Azoun IV. As a primeval woodland, the Hullack Forest is known for its dark and hidden valleys, some of which can go decades between visits by people, although goblins and orcs from the Thunder Peaks are known to regularly visit the Hullack.

markers.name: King's Forest
markers.textHtml: The King's Forest was so named because it is the property of the crown. A network of roads pass through this forest that connects many small towns and farms in Cormyr. The flora primarily consists of maple, oak, and rowan trees that create high canopies. There is very little undergrowth. In the 14th century DR there were no major predators within the forest and it was devoid of intelligent monsters. Due to this lack of natural threats it was densely inhabited and a popular spot for hunting, though such activity was forbidden. After the Goblin War ended in 1371 DR, the forest became infested with orcs.

markers.name: Kryptgarden Forest
markers.textHtml: A small but ancient and foreboding forest and one of the so-called the Moonwoods. Kryptgarden is a hilly woodland covered in a thick layer of foul-smelling fog, and contains many ruins of bygone dwarven civilizations. A portion of Kryptgarden south of Phandalin is known as the Crooked Forest because some of the trees there had been bent by ancient creatures that dwelled there to indicate paths through the forest. Small game animals live in the forest, but few larger creatures dwell within, as they were readily eaten by Kryptgarden's most fearsome predator, the dragon Claugiyliamatar. Kryptgarden Forest is reputed to be one of the sources of the evil that lurk in the ruined dwarfhold of Southkrypt, and only the second most powerful of them after the Sword Mountains.

markers.name: Llyrath Forest
markers.textHtml: One of the wildest areas of the kingdom of Corwell, comprising aspen, oak, pine, and evergreen trees. Some types of fungi on Gwynneth can only be found in Llyrath. Having such thick woods right along the sea makes the coast largely inaccessible and useless to the people of Corwell. The forest has few Ffolk human residents save for isolated families of lumberjacks and a few small bands of cutthroat outlaws who have fled death sentences to hide in the woods. Locals who live along the edges of the forest generally regard it with some apprehension. While they have no permanent settlement in the woods, firbolg raiders are known to appear in Llyrath on rare occasions, which is cause to rally the men-at-arms of Corwell. Pirates are also known to make their hideouts in the southern edge of the woods, which in turn makes it dangerous to sail the waters south of Gwynneth.

markers.name: Lurkwood
markers.textHtml: A great, temperate hardwood forest. It is one of the woodlands that are collectively called the Moonwoods by some. In ancient times, the Lurkwood was part of the High Forest, but it and many other woodlands were split off by the conflicts of the Crown Wars. The southern area of Lurkwood is safe and attracts loggers, hunters, and woodsmen from the neighboring communities, but its shadowy depths are largely mysterious, plagued with legends of bottomless bogs and deadly quicksand, and overrun with evil creatures, such as orcs. Several Uthgardt barbarian tribes live within the forest as well.

markers.name: Methwood
markers.textHtml: A large and hot forest filled with cypress, date palms, cedar, juniper, and mulberry trees, with lots of vine growth. The vegetation is thick, making travel difficult. It is home to the common fauna of the Old Empires area, such as elephants, monkeys, and leopards. Although the Methwood is the only significant forest in Unther, Untheran people avoid to chop down its trees because of the numerous monsters that inhabit the area. It is also rumored that an ancient city of the Turami who lived in this region before the foundation of Unther is lost in the forest. However, nobody has ever found the slightest hint about the location of this city.

markers.name: Misty Forest
markers.textHtml: A fog-filled, rainy forest, that is also an elven kingdom. It is full of pine trees and other types of evergreen trees. Red deer are common in the forest, and Owlbears, bears, boars, and wild cows can also be encountered. As of 1489 DR, the Misty Forest is ruled over by King Melandrach, known as the 'King of the Woods'. He has ruled the forest for at least 150 years, and maintains that his realm is one that belongs exclusively to the elves. The elves of the Misty Forest keep bees, and do a brisk trade in mead and honey wine with merchants who pass through The Way Inn, trading for anything that they can't make themselves. Sites of power sacred to Eilistraee dating back to Miyeritar, a pool sacred to Eldath, shrines to Eilistraee, Eldath, Mielikki, and Silvanus, and a temple to Chauntea, can be found within the forest.

markers.name: Misty Vale
markers.textHtml: A hot, dense jungle on a plateau over the River Talar in the Shaar. The Misty Vale is populated by Fair Folk refugees of the Second Crown War and escaped slaves from Ilythiir. It is also home to wild elves and to predatory horrors descended from Ilythiiri sorcerers' castoff experiments.

markers.name: Neverwinter Wood
markers.textHtml: A dense forest with a sense of magic within it. The western region of the Neverwinter Wood is known as the Watcher's Forest, so named for its sacredness amongst the Helmite priests of the region. The Neverwinter River flows down from Mount Hotenow through the Wood. Abundant whitethistle shrubbery grows throughout the Wood. If consumed, the plant is known to give someone the runs. Shederran are a highly valuable species of trees found only within the Wood. They are highly sought after for their use in crafting the highest quality bows and crossbows.

markers.name: Nothdark Wood
markers.textHtml: The northern part of Reaching Woods is called the Northdark Wood, but others name it the Dusk Wood or Reluvethel's Wood after the famed elf ranger Reluvethel who once hunted here. It thins where the Dusk Road runs through the woods, before continuing as a minor sub-branch on the far side of the road. The village of Drawn Swords stood on a lone crag on the eastern side.

markers.name: Nunwoods
markers.textHtml: The nearby cities of Reth and Hlath use the Nunwood as their main source of lumber. Snakes that spit a blinding venom are common in the Nunwood, and as of 1357 DR a tribe of hybsils called the Autunuk were also known to live there.

markers.name: Quivering Forest
markers.textHtml: This forest acts as a barrier to the great gray waste of Thar to the east. It was first planted as a copse of trees at the time Phlan was founded, c. 367 DR, but due to its magical nature, it grew to a great forest in the span of a single human generation. Morcant Burl, a purple-hued hardwood, grows only in the Quivering Forest. Other plants found within this forest include safflowers, pine trees, and treants. In the late-15th century DR, a section of the Quivering Forest was pulled into the Domain of Dread known as Barovia. This section borders Mount Baratok and has an ever-present mist that makes it only dimly lit during the daytime. During the evening, it becomes completely dark. Other changes that occurred within this part of the forest included many animals becoming larger and more threatening. Eventually, monsters from Barovia began to spill out into the Prime Material section of the Quivering Forest where the veil between the worlds was thin.

markers.name: Qurth Forest
markers.textHtml: Various towns and ruins border this forest with the Barony of the Great Oak on the southwest side. The River Scelptar flows southwest from its source in the Qurth Forest into the Inner Sea. Since the Spellplague, the land is slowly turning into a desert, and the forest is a tiny remnant of its former self. A rumor whispered among the Borderers pretends that Bloutarrans trapped the trails and allowed some monsters such as deepspawns to flourish, causing them to disgorge countless stags in the woods.

markers.name: Reaching Woods
markers.textHtml: This large forest is young and dense, populated with deciduous trees like beeches, elms, maples, oaks, and walnuts. Pleasant and picturesque from the outside, the woods give a feeling of peacefulness and serenity. There are a number of tranquil pools to be found amidst the trees. In winter, the Reaching Woods see a lot of snow, freezing temperatures, and clouded skies. In the late 15th century DR, the Reaching Woods are inhabited by humans and elves and by goblins and gnolls, but by 1479 DR, the gnolls dominate the forest and keep the humans and elves as no more than food.

markers.name: Riftwood
markers.textHtml: An isolated forest located in the eastern region of the Shaar. The trees form in a neat circular forest bisected by the Rift River. Unlike the nearby tropical forests, the Riftwood features fewer vines and less undergrowth. Within the forest is a well-protected logging community of gold dwarves.

markers.name: Shaareach Forest
markers.textHtml: A small forest near the border of Lapaliiya. The River Shaar flows through the middles of the Shaareach Forest.

markers.name: Shaarwood
markers.textHtml: A small forest which is the only forested section of the Shaar for hundreds of miles in any direction.

markers.name: Sharawood (Drakewood)
markers.textHtml: A haunted forest overrun with monsters and undead. Watching over the forest jealously is the Everlasting Wyrm, a powerful dracolich.

markers.name: Shilmista Forest
markers.textHtml: This forest, also known as the Forest of Shadows, has been sometimes considered within the territory of Amn or Tethyr, depending on the state of those countries' borders. The majority of the trees in the Forest of Shadows are elm and oak trees, though in the higher altitudes, on the slopes of the Snowflake mountains, one can find pine trees and dusktops. The ancient trees are rumored to be imbued with magic and, if called, will provide aid in battle.

markers.name: Silverwood
markers.textHtml: The Silverwood was once part of the High Forest, but after centuries of logging, the woodcutters took most of the trees on both sides of the Evermoor Way. Bare hills covered with stumps and old fallen trees flank this plain. The Silverwood survived thanks to its dark and tangled thickets and to its steep and dangerous ground. Many creatures from the Evermoors, including trolls, roam these woods.

markers.name: Snakewood
markers.textHtml: A relatively small forest. It was given the name 'Snakewood' because of the great number of black and green snakes that live there. The Snakewood was once part of the great forest of Shantel Othreier, and was split off from it by dragon fires as early as −11700 DR. By −1100 DR, elves had slain the last great dragon from the forest, then known as the Wyrmwood, and they founded a kingdom there, which they called Arundath, the Quiet Wood. The elves were nearly eradicated by humans during the last one hundred years of the Shoon Imperium, the late 350s DR.

markers.name: Southwood
markers.textHtml: A forest within the Triangle of Trees in the Savage Frontier, one of the satellite woodlands that are collectively called the Moonwoods by some local folk. The forest is relatively young, but still dim and foreboding, and contains the same varieties of trees as the High Forest. As of 1479 DR, it is populated by a large tribe of aggressive goblins, and is home to a group of ghosts that hunt spectral boars that appear among its trees on certain nights.

markers.name: Spiderhaunt Woods
markers.textHtml: The North Ride runs just south of the woods, and the Tethyamar Trail runs the whole eastern border of the wood. A single trail, the Arachnid Pathway, leads from the Tethyamar Trail into the heart of the wood. The Spiderhaunt Wood is, of course, inhabited by spiders of all known types, as well as ettercaps and chitines. Spellcasters of all types come to the Spiderhaunt Woods to gather spider-related spell components or to collecting rare venoms. A large number of fey crossings means that it is easy to accidentally pass into the Feywild while deep within Spiderhaunt.

markers.name: Stone Wood
markers.textHtml: This forest contains the only moonwell (a magical pool of water sacred to the Earthmother and her druids) on Oman's Isle. It is located near the Cairn of Thelgaar Ironhand.

markers.name: The Neth Stand
markers.textHtml: A forest containing underground volcanic vents that emit a sulfurous mist. The wood here is valuable, despite the need to treat it to get rid of the sulfur smell. The forest is extremely dense and dark, containing phosphorescent fungi, some of which can move and have intelligence. A range of crumbling rock faces run north to south through the forest. They have no official name, and are peppered with caves and ledges. Wyverns and perytons densely inhabit this area, making it attractive as a food source for dragons from the surrounding areas.

markers.name: Thornwood
markers.textHtml: Several species of dangerous flora grow within this forest, including the thornslinger for which it is named, and several intelligent species of plant life. The Thornwood was once part of the elven nation of Thearnytaar, which fell to the dark elves at the end of the Second Crown War. Rumors of ancient repositories of elven magic and treasure from the time of Thearnytar never died down, though whether they were left by moon elves or dark elves is debated.

markers.name: Turlang's Wood
markers.textHtml: This area is under the direct protection of Turlang the Thoughtful, a Great Treant of immense power. This is where the High Forest is actually growing. Under Turlang's leadership, the Treants are pushing the boundaries of the forest outward to reclaim lost lands. It is a place of deep natural magic where the trees themselves might move to block the path of those who mean the forest harm.

markers.name: Trollback Forest
markers.textHtml: A forest largely devoid of birds and small woodland creatures, having been wiped out by the forest's monstrous inhabitants. It is also home to a variety of monstrous trees, both carnivorous and poisonous. Mundane varieties of trees that can be found here include twisted oaks, black ashes, and scrub pines. Due to the forest's thorny foliage, and the presence of poisonous snakes, it is seen as a hunting ground for monsters that are resistant to such toxins. The forest is teeming with bogs and underbrush, making it a perfect environment for its troll inhabitants. These trolls live in an uneasy co-existence with chokers, who form highways of vines as well as toppled and intertwined branches.

markers.name: Urlingwood
markers.textHtml: A wild, untamed forest that is considered to be of great importance to the Wychlaran, the order of witches that holds great influence in the nation of the east. Anyone not of their order, or members of the Vremyonni, is prohibited from entering its borders, an act that is punishable by death. Patrols of Rashemaar warriors march around the border to warn off those who are lost or ignorant to local customs.

markers.name: Velarwood
markers.textHtml: A forest in the Dalelands. The north of the wood contains a temple to Eilistraee, which is guarded by a number of drow and other elves. There are also a number of cloakers, stirges, trolls, and werewolves in the area.

markers.name: Vercy Wood
markers.textHtml: Flowing northwest from the Galenas, Telstang Brook passes west through the forest before emptying into Lake Longreach. The Ilinver Trail also passes through Vercy Wood, linking Ilinver with the settlement of Avang to the east.

markers.name: Vordrorn Forest
markers.textHtml: A small forest at the base of the Ice Mountains, comprising mainly pine trees and muskeg bogs, usually shrouded in chilly mist. It is sometimes referred to as the Ghostwood, owing to the large number of incorporeal undead that seem bound to it. The forest lays on top of caverns and dungeons dating back to ancient Illusk.

markers.name: Westwood
markers.textHtml: A forest on the Sword Coast North, part of a group of forests that were referred to by some as the Other Woods and others as the Moonwoods. Some sages claimed that Westwood was once as vast as the High Forest and stretched as far north as Yartar and as far south as the Delimbiyr Route, and that it had been shrinking after the destruction of Netheril. The closest settlement to Westwood is Kheldell, which sits on the northwestern edge of the wood.

markers.name: Weathercote Wood
markers.textHtml: A forest of the Triangle of Trees, located in the great expanse known as the Savage Frontier. According to the elves, Weathercote wood is full of gates to other worlds, as well as wizards that guard them from casual use. Blue mists and strange lights are often seen in the woods, and those that enter are never heard from again. Weathercote Wood has existed from before the rise of Netheril and continues to endure despite the advancing of the desert of Anauroch.

markers.name: Winterwood
markers.textHtml: A vast lush woodland filled with pines and oaks, but the thick foliage from these tall trees stop sunlight passing through the canopy, making it the darkest forest in the region. A cool, light, white mist is commonly seen floating along the ground. The white mist is so dense it looks like newly fallen snow: this is the source of the forest's name. The Winterwood's secluded nature enabled a tribe of mold men to make a home in the northern reaches of the forest. The mold men's territorial claims put them in conflict with the local orcs. The green elves also fight them for land. This in turn makes the forest a hazardous locale for visitors. The lands south of the Wintercloak river are much more hospitable, however.

markers.name: Aerilpar Forest (Wood of Dark Trees)
markers.textHtml: The Aerilpar Forest, also known as the Wood of Dark Trees, both for its animated jungle and the dangerous beasts that prowled it, is located in Veldorn in the Shining South. Among its more notable features is the Aerilpar Mound, also known as the Mound of the Sleepless. True to its name, the trees of this steamy forest are evil. It is widely rumored that the trees are some kind of animated monster created by a foul wizard.

markers.name: Wood of Sharp Teeth (Werewoods)
markers.textHtml: The Wood of Sharp Teeth was formerly called the Glimmerwood, and was known as the Werewoods for a brief period in the 15th century DR. It is a wilderness except for Durlag's Tower on the forest's southern edge, and the site of the ruined city of Vehlarr within the forest. Two types of common medicinal herbs can be found growing within the forest: maiden's milk that is used to suppress cough, and bellows root that cause vomiting. The forest is home to satyrs, dryads, and a small number of werewolves, and some believe wolfweres also dwell within.

markers.name: Yuirwood
markers.textHtml: An ancient forest that once spanned the entirety of the Aglarondan peninsula, but the human settlers of Aglarond caused its boundaries to recede to the center of the peninsula. The forest was named after the Yuir, an ancient nation of star elves and wild elves who once dwelled in the forest. The ruins of their civilization dot the woods, the most prominent of which being the menhir circles. Two of them mark the Sunglade, a meadow-like clearing in the center of the Yuirwood, with the stones of the outermost menhir circle standing nearly 20 feet high. The Yuirwood is infused with both ancient and new magic. It is populated by primarily by half-elves, rumored to be the descendants of Yuir themselves, and said to be warm and fiercely loyal companions. However, they are highly insular and suspicious of strangers, with little regard for what happens outside the borders of their forest.

markers.name: Akanapeaks
markers.textHtml: Iron is mined in the safer parts of the Akanapeaks, and supplies the nearby city of Reth and other parts of Chessenta. Precious metals and gems are mined by the inhabitants of Akanûl. Another notable trade good from Akanapeaks is delph, a spice with an extremely long shelf-life.

markers.name: Alimir Mountains
markers.textHtml: The caverns of the Alimirs are full of all kinds of monsters, the slaves of the beholders who used to reign over Calimshan and still thrived in the darkness in the depths of the mountains. Dragons occasionally seek out lairs in the southern reaches of the mountains, but with the beholder presence-a massive hive of nearly fifty-these rarely becomes more than temporary hoards.

markers.name: Aphrun Mountains
markers.textHtml: [or Aphrunn] The gold dragon Frizzerfraz made his home in these mountains. A tribe of kobolds called the Scything Claw resides in the Aphrunn and will occasionally raid into Turmish.

markers.name: Armridge Mountains
markers.textHtml: The Sossrim settlements of Uldfest and Shannath lie between the mountains' eastern edges and the Angsaas Chain, while the settlement of Dharvil lies on the southeastern side. A road heading east from Dharvil connects these settlements to Sundice and then beyond to Naupau on the coast, while a trail known as 'The Reach' heading south from Uldfast connects them to the Great Glacier and then beyond to far off Vaasa.

markers.name: Azirrhat
markers.textHtml: Some native Bedine people spoke of finding deposits of gold within the clefts found between the peaks but the caverns and chasms beneath Azirrhat are home to a large population of asabis, nocturnal lizard-folk hunters that threatened any creatures who dared pass close by the peaks at night.

markers.name: Battle of Bones
markers.textHtml: As of 1089 DR, a charismatic goblin commander named Izac Uthor rallied his fellow goblinoids and their orc and kobold allies, to attack human settlements to the north and west of their lands. Armies were sent from all over the Western Heartlands and even as far as Waterdeep, Baldur's Gate, Silverymoon, and its neighboring dwarf holds to meet Uthor's horde at Torgor's Triangle. It was said that the battle lasted a total of two days and two-and-a-half nights. Almost four hundred thousand men, elves, dwarves, orcs, kobolds, goblins and hobgoblins were dead by the time the fighting ceased, and Torgor's Triangle would be known thereafter as the Battle of Bones. Nowadays, skeletons and zombies commonly inhabite the Battle of Bones.

markers.name: Cloud Peaks
markers.textHtml: The mountains are thoroughly mined for iron, gems, and precious metals. The nearby town of Crimmor process a good portion of the ore and gems coming from the Peaks before shipping them to Athkatla. Tribes of ogres and hill giants live in the mountains. White dragons inhabit the mountains as well. One such white dragon named Icehaupt lives on Mount Speartop but preferes to never leave his lair.

markers.name: Cloven Mountains
markers.textHtml: The range runs from east to west and is split in two by the Wintercloak River, which is how the mountains received their name. The tallest peak is was 10,000 feet (3000 m). The Cloven Mountains are inhabited by a handful of warring goblinoid and orcish tribes.

markers.name: Council Hills
markers.textHtml: A clump of hills where the territories of several Shaaran tribes meet. The hills are home to the long-destroyed city of Shandaular. Beneath the hills, however, are the remains of the old city, a series of underground tunnels. The tribesmen of the Shaar use the more superficial tunnels and passageways as tombs to honor their dead.

markers.name: The Crags
markers.textHtml: As of 1451 DR, the discovery of the lost city of Gauntlgrym and the activation of the legendary forges within awakened the primordial Maegera within her prison beneath Mount Hotenow. In a fit of rage and fury, the once-slumbering primordial released a burst of energy that caused a massive volcanic eruption and subsequent earthquake. Nearby cities such as Neverwinter were left in a near-ruined state. Among the races that can be found throughout the crags are humans, dwarves, goblins, bugbears, hobgoblins, as well as gnolls, orcs, and ogres.

markers.name: Dagger Hills
markers.textHtml: In the mid–14th century DR, the sparse human settlements in the Dagger Hills, mostly small farms, dwindled during the Zhent occupation of Daggerdale and the hills became covered with dense thickets and thorn fields, resistant to fire and human intrusion. Many monsters hunted in these hills. After the Spellplague, by 1479 DR, these hills were infested mainly by murderous lycanthropes (wereboars and werewolves) and their thralls. Despite the threat of the lycanthropes, small villages, protected by stockades, and farms still existe in the Dagger Hills prosperity, as they are a famous source for emeralds, aquamarines, and other gems.

markers.name: Deepwing Mountains
markers.textHtml: The Deepwing Mountains are a small mountain range in the Vilhon Reach. At the western end of the range, the mountains gave way to rough, rocky hills known as the Rushing Hills, while the southwestern mountains are known as the Spines of Surkh, which encircled Surkh and were home to many tombs and burial sites of the lizardfolk who dwellt there. The mountains are populated with a number of large, flying monsters, including manticores, griffons and hippogriffs, red dragons, and wyverns.

markers.name: Dessarin Hills
markers.textHtml: The River Dessarin wind its way through these grouping of rugged hills, which dominated the landscape on either side. They contain few settlements. A number of ancient dwarven ruins dotted the landscape of the hills. In addition to the Uthgardt barbarians that claim the hills as their land, they are home to savage humanoids, such as orcs and ogres. Beyond these are hill giants and manticores.

markers.name: Desertsmouth Mountains
markers.textHtml: In the mid–14th century, the depths of the mountains became the site of a full-scale war between the native orcs and dwarves of Tethyamar. Now the mountain area is infested by orcs, ogres, goblins, hobgoblins, along with giants. They are even a number of dragons, including the great wyrm Nexus, a number of copper dragons, along with a pair of gold dragons.

markers.name: Dun Hills (Dalelands)
markers.textHtml: A series of hills separating Tasseldale from Deepingdale to the west and Battledale to the north. Before the followers of Tyr established the Abbey of the Just Hammer and the Sun Soul monks settled within, the Dun Hills had been assaulted by monsters and human raiders. The presence of these spiritual-martial groups has greatly improved the hills' safety. As of 1372 DR, the hills were generally considered to be safe, containing wild ponies.

markers.name: Dun Hills ((Lapaliiya)
markers.textHtml: The Dun Hills are a series of low hills that form the western edge of the Channath Vale. The Cliffs of Talar and the River Talar run east to west through the range. The western slopes support little vegetation bar some scrub and the occasional olive grove. The eastern slopes are thickly blanketed in mists and trees, as they descend into the Misty Vale. Some goat-herding tribes dwell in the hills. The wild elves of the Misty Vale do not venture very far west into the hills.

markers.name: Dragonjaw Mountains
markers.textHtml: Les monts Mâchoires-de-Dragon sont habités par des gnomes et des dragons de cuivre. Les gnomes se rendent fréquemment à Milvarune, la cité humaine, où ils vendent leurs créations artisanales.

markers.name: Dragonspine Mountains
markers.textHtml: The Dragonspines are a series of rough crags that are extremely difficult to traverse. They are wracked with frigid temperatures that turn away all but the most experienced travelers. Those that dare to brave the peaks could find themselves with great rewards however, as they are rich with natural resources, including a wealth ore, such as black granite. The eastern slopes of the Dragonspines hold a passage to the Underdark, that facilitates slave trade between the Zhentarim and the drow. As of the 14th century, red, white, and at least one pair of copper dragons were known to lair in the higher peaks, along with other aerial creatures such as dire hawks, and hippogriffs. Goblins, orcs, and giants fill the lower areas and valleys, that latter of which are occasionally enslaved by the dragons of the peaks.

markers.name: Dragonsword Mountains
markers.textHtml: The Dragonsword Mountains are considered impassable by the people of Mulhorand, and as a result remain largely unexplored. They protect the populated regions of the nation from the storms of the Plains of Purple Dust. The inhabitants of the Dragonsword Mountains long plague the nation of Mulhorand, and include a number of dracosphinxes. This mountain range is also known to contain the lair of the blue dragon Gestaniius.

markers.name: Giant's Run Mountains
markers.textHtml: According to dwarven legend, the mountains were named after a battle known as the Fall of Karlyn's Vale, in which over 5,000 giants fell at the hands of dwarves from Shanatar. As well as goblins and ogres, the emerald dragon Behrshimmer makes its lair in the range. Some mines in the mountains were abandoned when tunnels breached the Underdark, releasing drow, illithids and worse into the area.

markers.name: Giantspire Mountains
markers.textHtml: The mountains gained their name from the tribes of giants that occupied them. As of 1479 DR, a three-way conflict brewed in the mountains between powerful goblinoid tribes: the Blood Mountain tribe, the Razor Heart tribe, and the Black Wolf clan. Neither the Nar tribes nor the people from Damara occupy these lands. The peaks are held by hobgoblins, bugbears, and evil cloud giants, and are home to wandering tribes of stone and hill giants as well as ogres.

markers.name: Glacier of the White Worm
markers.textHtml: Once a part of the Great Glacier, the Glacier of the White Worm survives at a much lower latitude than its altitude allows, suggesting that magic or some other force might be involved. Most notably, it is home to a variety of pale, albino remorhaz. These 'white worms' gave the glacier its name. The remorhazes are said to be led by a giant-sized 'king' worm that laires in the very center of the ice. Some mountain barbarian tribes also live around the area of the glacier, in particularly the White Worm tribe. Silver dragons laire in the mountains surrounded the glacier, and red dragons are also sighted in the area.

markers.name: Graypeak Mountains
markers.textHtml: These mountains are a rugged and desolate mountain range. The perilous peaks are famous for being exceptionally hard to traverse, home to many fearsome beasts that inspire terror in wary travelers. For all their danger, the Greypeaks once housed great deposits of mineral wealth, much if not all of which was recovered. The Greypeaks Mountains were named after the grey-skinned stone giants that live amongst the mountains. But many others monsters inhabite the range including goblins that dwell in keeps along with their allied barghest chieftains that commanded large packs of worgs, stone giants, goliaths, orcs, galeb duhr, a hive of beholders, and many young dragons, including at least one family of red dragons. The easternmost peaks of the mountains are said to house the greatest known population of griffons in all the Realms. Giant owls are also known to make their nests within the tallest of the Greypeak's mountains.

markers.name: Great Glacier
markers.textHtml: Also called Pelvuria, it's a large mass of frozen land. It hosted several distinct societies. In addition, white dragons and frost giants are known to live there.

markers.name: Great Rift
markers.textHtml: The Great Rift formed around –8000 DR when one part of the underground dwarven kingdom of Bhaerynden collapsed. The Great Rift was the traditional home of Faerûn's gold dwarves, with a population of 1,300,000 dwarves, gnomes, halflings, and humans. When the Spellplague hit in 1385 DR, the western half of the Great Rift collapsed into the Underdark, forming an enormous canyon called the Underchasm. What remained of the Great Rift became known as East Rift. The city of Underhome was destroyed in the cataclysm and the capital was moved to Eartheart.

markers.name: Greycloak Hills
markers.textHtml: The ridges formed by these tall hills are covered with weeds, grass, and scrub, with an occasional copse of small trees. The region was known as the Tomb Hills because it is riddled with old tombs from the Fallen Kingdom. The moon elves of Evereska claimed the hills for settlement in 1335 DR in the “Evereska Charter”, and caused the hills to be shrouded in constant magical mists. These mists and the gray clothes that the residents wore gave the locale its new name. Ever since the Evereska Charter was declared, there have been rumors and speculation about why the elves claimed the area. The hills are defended by regular, well-armed archer patrols, and travelers passing close to the Hills reported seeing human skeletons rotting in iron cages and presumed they were left as a warning to would-be tomb robbers.

markers.name: Earthfast Mountains
markers.textHtml: Despite being somewhat smaller than the Earthspur Mountains, the Earthfast Mountains are jagged and only slightly less harsh, and just as hard to cross or climb. The mountains had rocky peaks and steep slopes, and are separated by abrupt gorges. There are deposits of iron, silver, and copper ores to be found within the Earthfast Mountains. In the western end, there are dwarven stone quarries. The highest peaks of the Earthfast Mountains are dominated by giant eagles. Their depths, meanwhile, are occupied by large populations of orcs and ogres. Other parts are inhabited by goblins and bugbears, as well as hobgoblins from the Gray Forest. Finally, drow could be found within and beneath the mountains. There are also barbarians dwelling in the Earthfast Mountains, as well as dwarves.

markers.name: Earthspur Mountains
markers.textHtml: These mountains are home to dwarves, such as the Ludwakazar clan. There are many hardy human and dwarven miners and prospectors working the mountains, as well as bandits who prey on them. Indeed, mining are lucrative here but conditions are harsh and dangerous. Miners endure a lot of hardship to pursue their wealth, and the mining communities suffer high death rates. Dunfee in Impiltur and the Arcatan towns of Sudrav and Tomrav existe thanks to the mines here. On the other side of the mountains, King's Reach in the Vast grows wealthy off trade and smelting of the metals that came out, and by supplying the prospectors who found them. They go via secret routes to protect their claims and hide their finds. The Earthspurs are inhabited by orcs, goblins, hobgoblins, and bugbears, while drow could also be found. Cave trolls are common in the mountains west of Impiltur, as are other monsters.

markers.name: East Galena Mountains
markers.textHtml: The mountains are rich in valuable minerals, and mining operations are among the most productive on Toril. The Galenas are best known for bloodstone, iron and silver. Despite the danger from hostile inhabitants, people come to the mountains in the hope of mining their way to riches, especially the dwarves, who had set up the Bloodstone mines, Hillsafar Hall and Ironspur in the area in order to cultivate the ores. The mountains are home to many dwarfholds, including duergar, and its peaks are the domain of many goblinoids, orcs, and even giants. Within the Underdark beneath the mountains is a city of the drow called V'elddrinnsshar, and a svirfneblin realm known as Deepearth.

markers.name: Fairheight Range
markers.textHtml:
The Fairheights are a rugged but relatively low mountain range that separate the southern lands of the Ffolk from the northern kingdom of Gnarhelm belonging to the Northmen. They are mined by both peoples and contained rich deposits of iron and precious metals including gold and silver as well as copper. The tunnels beneath the mountains connect to Dwarvenhome, and from there, to the island of Gwynneth. The mountains fill within Callidyrr's county of Fairheight, and most of the Ffolk inhabitants are miners. On the northern side, the Northlander settlers are also miners, and both groups hire dwarves to oversee their operations. The highlands of the Fairheights are infested with trolls that attack those who were foolish enough to journey over the passes alone or in small numbers. Orcs and goblins from the mountains sometimes carry out raids against the mining towns along the foothills.

As of the late 15th century DR, a shadow dragon has come to lead the orcs and trolls, as well as a number of shadow creatures, which it directs in a war to exterminate the dwarves and establish a new realm for it and its followers.

markers.name: Far Hills
markers.textHtml: A region of rocky hills, hidden valleys and thick strands of trees. The Far Hills form the eastern border of Sunset Vale. Darkhold's control over the region is all but absolute.

markers.name: Firecap Mountains
markers.textHtml: A small mountain range, part of the larger Ice Spires South, overlooking Lake Fief. This range is largely dominated by a trio of semi-active volcanoes, known as the Three Sisters, whose foothills are adjacent to a small dale that they keep warm.

markers.name: Firesteap Mountains
markers.textHtml: The mountains do not have many monsters but are claimed by the Shaaryan tribes. They serve as home to the ancient tombs of the Shaaryans, who kill or capture anyone that do not have traditional rights. The northern mountains has a couple of bands of thieves, bandits as well as ogres and hobgoblins that struck at caravans that pass through.

markers.name: Firward Mountains
markers.textHtml: [not Fireward]

markers.name: Forlorn Hills
markers.textHtml: These hills were once the center of the ancient shield dwarven kingdom of Dardath, one of the three kingdoms in Phalorm, the Realm of Three Crowns. After the triumvirate collapsed in 615 DR, the hills became known as the Fallen Hills or, more commonly, the Forlorn Hills. They were of the great forest that included Ardeep Forest, but dwarves cleared portions of it. By 1373 DR, there were a number of dwarf clans making their home in the Forlorn Hills. The Forlorn Hills are prowled by leucrotta and wyverns. There are also hill giants and ettins that manage to eke out a living. The highest peaks are usually inhabited by copper dragons that rummage around in abandoned dwarf holds.

markers.name: Frost Hills
markers.textHtml: The Frost Hills are the barren region that hosts Mithral Hall. There also dwell several Uthgardt tribes, orcs, frost giants, white dragons and the lich-lord who lays buried between its labyrinthine ruins under the ice.

markers.name: The High Ice
markers.textHtml: A desolate monster-infested glacier, that had been extended from its original size by the magic of the phaerimm.

markers.name: Hills of Maerth
