markers.textHtml: The Hills of Maerth, also known as the Maerthwatch, are a range of hills, only rising to mountains in their westernmost stretch beside the Chondalwood. The hills are low yet rugged and craggy, and hold deposits of rare minerals and gems that are predominantly found here, such as beljurils, jacinths, and luspeel. Many long-worked mines, some quite ancient, are scattered across the Hills of Maerth. The isolationist, nomadic giants known as geriviars wander the lands between the Maerthwatch, Chondalwood, and the Black Ash Plain.

markers.name: Ice Mountains
markers.textHtml: Humans are rarely encountered among these mountains, other than members of the Red Tiger tribe and merchants traveling from Sundabar. Dwarves often travel through these mountains in search of the lost dwarf strongholds of Delzoun and would treat strangers with suspicion, fearing they may be treasure hunters. In terms of giants, this mountain range is inhabited by frost giants and fog giants. White dragons commonly vie for dominion in this region.

markers.name: Ice Spires
markers.textHtml: Many races of giant and giant-kin inhabit the Ice Spires. Most notably the eponymous Ice Spire ogres dwell there. White dragons are also known to frequently vie for dominion over the region.

markers.name: Icerim Mountains
markers.textHtml: These mountains were once part of the Great Glacier, but a recession of the ice over the centuries had separated the Icerim. Many tribes live within the Icerim Mountains, including the Tark, Chif, and Dagranost.

markers.name: Icespire Peak
markers.textHtml: The tallest of the Sword Mountains, famous for its frozen waterfalls and for being the lair of the white dragon Cryovain.

markers.name: Jotunhammer Range
markers.textHtml: These mountains were once part of the giant kingdom of Ostoria and remain a hub for cloud and stone giants.

markers.name: Kelvin's Cairn
markers.textHtml: A mountain of giant boulders that serves as the highest point in Icewind Dale. It is the central landmark from which distances in the region are measured, though its slopes are dangerous due to yetis and wild beasts.

markers.name: Kuldin Peaks
markers.textHtml:
The Kuldin Peaks are largely unexplored and no major road passes through them. However, small trails blazed by the dwarves of Shanatar are used by brave travelers and patrols or shepherds from Carrelath. Shield dwarves are known to live in these mountains, as are ogres, gnolls, and trolls. The Starrock goblin tribe lives there also. The red dragon Charvekannathor laires in the Kuldin Peaks and keeps them free from orcs.

<%>Kartak Spellseer, a powerful lich, lives in the Kuldin Peaks within an abandoned dwarven mine.

markers.name: Laughing Hollow
markers.textHtml: Within the Laughing Hollow are a number of welcoming glades and small pools of water, but the dense trees and shade from the nearby elevations create a near-continuous atmosphere that resemble a day's twilight. The forests of the hollow are inhabited by a band of wild elves led by an individual known as the King of the Woods.

markers.name: Lost Peaks
markers.textHtml: The Lost Peaks, also known as the Lost Mountains, are a pair of isolated mountains full of forests, said to be as old as Toril itself. The Dessarin River originates from these peaks. Many fey—notably satyrs—live near them. A large tribe of centaurs also call the peaks home and keeps out any hunters that come from Olostin's Hold. A dwarven fortress once laid under the Lost Peaks, but the dwarven population was exterminated thousand years prior to the 14th century DR by some curse or an epidemic.

markers.name: Lugsaas Chain
markers.textHtml: During the 15th century DR, the retreat of the Great Glacier has freed the Lugsaas Chain from the ice north of Vaasa and Damara but the western reaches of the Chain north of the Moonsea and known as the Titan Peaks, remained frozen. Wild packs of kupuk roam the lower elevations of the Lugsaas Chain and the peaks are swarming with remorhaz, living in tunnels underneath the ice of the mountains.

markers.name: Maerthwatch Mountains
markers.textHtml: The mountains hold deposits of rare minerals and gems that are predominantly found here. Isolationist, nomadic giants wander between the Maerthwatch and Chondalwood.

markers.name: Marching Mountains
markers.textHtml: The Marching Mountains received their current name when dwarves marched into the plains north of the mountains in what later became the kingdom of Tethyr. The remains of ancient dwarven bridges from the days still exist, but the only passage across such places in later times require paying tolls to the ogres who guard rope bridges. To descend into the valleys and bypass the toll bridges is to risk landslides, monsters, or getting lost. The mountains are also known to be home to orcs, goblins, kobolds, and sylvan folk. Fomorian giants are among the more deadly monsters known to exist in these mountains.

markers.name: Mistcliff Mountains
markers.textHtml: As of 1372 DR, the Mistcliffs were home to one of only four major colonies of aarakocra in all of Faerûn. By the late 15th century DR, the aarakocra population has grown several large settlements across the coastal Mistcliff. Pteranodons and quetzalcoatluses are also known to make their home within the range's jagged peaks.

markers.name: Nether Mountains
markers.textHtml: Many travelers avoid these peaks, because several orc tribes lair in the region, such as the Thousand Fists and the Ripped Gut tribes who fought ferociously over the large gorge that split the mountains into its eastern and western ranges. But the greatest danger within these mountains are an entire clan of blue dragons known as the Morueme which resides on the high slope of the mountain peak aptly named Dragondoom. The dragons' threat is only enhanced by the servitude of a large tribe of hobgoblins called the Red Flayers, who maintain the Doomspire, a citadel carved into the rock, and protect their masters with an almost deific reverence. The peaks are also home to dozens of other dangerous monster species making them one of the most inhospitable wild regions in all Faerûn. For those daring enough to brave the dangers, gold, silver, and copper could be mined in quantity. Finally, many adventurers hunt the mountains in search of the legends of lost caches of Netherese magic.

markers.name: Novularond
markers.textHtml: The area is a range of large mountains, some of which reach 20,000 feet (6 km) in height. The mountains are surprisingly free of ice, unlike the surrounding areas. There are many tunnels and passages throughout the mountains, which lead to scattered veins of gold, gems, and other valuable minerals, including the spark stones so greatly valued across the Great Glacier. The overall range radiates a faint magical aura, but it is too weak to identify or trace to a source. Besides the dragons of the high peaks, the area is home to small clans of Frost giants. The clans are highly territorial and despised all other races, especially those they hold to be trespassers.

markers.name: Omlarandin Mountains
markers.textHtml: [not Omlarandan] The Omlarandin Mountains are a cluster of lightly forested mountains and the most populated mountains in Tethyr. They are most famous for being the only known source of omlar gems in Faerûn-rare turquoise stones that are exceptional for crafting magic items-though only a few are ever discovered in a given century. Bleucorundum, a very old male sapphire dragon, guard in a secret location in the heart of the mountains. The mountains are also inhabited by giants, wyverns, and displacer beasts.

markers.name: Orsraun Mountains
markers.textHtml: The Orsraun Mountains are the largest range of mountains south of the Spine of the World. They make up the majority of Turmish's northwestern border. The northeastern mountains are known as the Mountains of the Alaoreum and are separated from the rest of the range by Treefall Pass. The Alaoreum are ostensibly the demesne of the dwarves of Ironfang Deep though duergar, as well as the evil inhabitants of the wider Orsrauns, repeatedly pressed them. The Orbrekh are a thin part of the range separated by a river valley that are controlled by around thirty to thirty-five hill giants.

markers.name: Purple Hills
markers.textHtml: The Purple Hills are a range of hills on the coast of Tethyr, lying in County Vintor in the duchy of the Purple Marches, inhabited almost exclusively by halflings famed as the best winemakers in Tethyr. The Purple Hills is a lush and fertile land of green farmlands overlooking the Sea of Swords. The farmlands produce cattle like sheep and wool that are produced in quality fabrics in Tethyr, as well as various fruits, vegetables, and other foodstuffs that feed the city of Zazesspur in the north and Myratma in the south. The coastal area of the Purple Hills is called the Purple Cliffs – a virtually deserted land as it was not suitable for farming. The coastline is so named due to an abundance of creeping, purple flowers that hang over its edge, and the rich quartz deposits found throughout the rocks.

markers.name: Reghed Glacier
markers.textHtml: This glacial wasteland is so desolate and isolated that few travelers dare to cross near its eastern border, let alone venture atop its icy facade. The Reghedman barbarians of Icewind Dale took their name from this glacier, and claim the giants of the Dawn Age created the glacier to mark the edge of their territory. Beyond the Reghed Glacier is the enormous Endless Ice Sea glacier, with which it is sometimes confused.

markers.name: Riders to the Sky Mountains
markers.textHtml: The Riders to the Sky are composed mostly of hills, ranging between 3,000 and 5,000 feet (900 and 1500 m) in height, with cliffs on the southern end. They are rich with mineral veins. During the last stages of the Second Sundering, a great portion of the lands of the Tymantheran side of the Riders to the Sky was transposed to Abeir with the return of Unther. Under the leadership of Gilgeam, the Untherans quickly regained control of their former lands in the vicinity of the Riders to the Sky. Bandits and outlaw tribes live on the eastern, Untheric slopes, using the hills as a base for raids on both Unther and Chessenta. The western, Chessentan side along the Winding River is much wilder. Here, the range is inhabited by trolls who live in villages on the surface and gray dwarves who dwelled in caverns below ground. The range is also home to various chromatic dragons, who occasionally claim the riches dug out of the mines.

markers.name: Running Rocks
markers.textHtml: A succession of solid granite peaks intercut by a series of chasms and canyons. The River Rasha originates from among the peaks, before flowing south to Lake Tirulag. A small number of copper and iron mines have been established within the mountains, though none was active as of the mid–14th century DR. The mountains are thought to be a place of mysticism that is revered by the people of Rashemen. They are mostly left uninhabited by the Rashemaar people, save for the secluded caves that house the Vremyonni. Their hidden domains are protected by powerful magic and great stone doors. Malevolent drow and wicked bheur hags also make their home in the Running Rocks, along with some more monstrous humanoids like trolls.

markers.name: Rushing Hills
markers.textHtml: A rough and rocky range, consisting of natural outcroppings of rock which hinder planting. The Krakk't tribe of thri-kreen make their home here, as well as in the plains to the southwest. The tribe only numbers about fifty or so members and is nomadic. They rarely attacked travelers in the area, but the chances of an attack rise if there are elves in the group (as elf-meat is a delicacy for thri-kreen). The fire drakes of the Deepwing Mountains hunt for food in the Rushing Hills, and occasionally attack the thri-kreen there.

markers.name: Scimitar Spires
markers.textHtml: A range of shadowy, sword-like mountains. During the time of Netheril, prior to the Anauroch, it was known as the Lodestar Mountains. Until −403 DR, Netherese mines in the mountains were a productive source of metal ores including platinum, copper, brass, and steel. They were abandoned due to frequent monster attacks as the Netherese fought the Phaerimm. After the fall of Netheril the mountains were infested with monsters that the Netherese survivor states were not able to dislodge.

markers.name: Serpent Hills
markers.textHtml: As the name says, the hills are full of innumerable snakes. Also, beneath the red-clay hills is a system called the Serpent Tunnels, partially consisting of tombs from around the time of Netheril's fall. Even deeper beneath the hills, in a region of the Underdark, is the grimlock city of Reeshov. Many types of dragons live in the hills, such as copper, red and silver dragons. Bands of yuan-ti are also known to live in the area.

markers.name: The Shaeradim
markers.textHtml: The Shaeradim is the name given to a ring of twelve mountains that surround the elven settlement of Evereska. The mountains stand nearly 1800m high for much of their length and are surrounded for 24km by lesser hills and narrow vales. They are considered largely impassable for those unknowledgeable about how to navigate within. There is a pass that winds through the hills known to the elves of Evereska. Any outsider brought along this path by the elves has to be blindfolded during their journey.

markers.name: The Small Teeth
markers.textHtml: The Small Teeth are a relatively small cluster of mountains. The average peak height was 8,000 feet (2500 m). They were lightly forested, except for on the tallest peaks. The southern foothills are a good area for growing grapes. A large amount of snow fall in the mountains. The Teeth are home to hill giants and to the blue dragon Iryklathagra.

markers.name: Smoking Mountains
markers.textHtml: The Smoking Mountains, a chain of rough hills and active volcanoes, are divided into two branches. The eastern branch is full of dormant volcanes, while the western branch has active volcanic activity, with two volcanoes in particular, Mount Fussel and Mount Temmikant, erupting frequently.

markers.name: Snowflake Mountains
markers.textHtml:
The Snowflake Mountains form the borders between Tethyr, Amn, and what was once the barony of Impresk within Erlkazar. They include some of the tallest mountains in Faerûn.

<%>The lich Priamon Rakesk maintains a lair in a tower hidden among the mountains.

markers.name: Spine of the World
markers.textHtml: The Spine of the World is a mountain range that stretched from the Cold Run near the Sea of Moving Ice, to the Ice Spires north of the Silver Marches. Folks all across the lands of the Savage Frontier and the Silver Marches, particularly those in Scornubel, refer to the Spine as 'the Wall'. On the winter days when the white snow cover faces of the peaks met the iron-gray of the sunless winter sky, it appeared as though the peaks were holding up sky. To others, it simply marked the end of the world as they see it. It is home to frost giants, white dragons, and yetis.

markers.name: Starmetal Hills
markers.textHtml: The dwarf worshipers of Marthammor Duin use Berun's Hill to worship their deity on dark, moonless nights. They also watch over the merchants and travelers traversing the Long Road. Their vigilance and quick aided small dwarven holds to rebuild trade with human settlements up and down the Long Road and River Dessarin. The bronze dragon Felgolos has a den (one of many) in a cave above a river that ran through the western Starmetal Hills.

markers.name: Star Mounts
markers.textHtml: A massive, ancient mountain range located in the very heart of the High Forest. These peaks are often shrouded in perpetual mist and clouds. They are home to powerful creatures like ancient dragons and giant eagles. The mountains also serve as the headwaters for several major rivers, including the Unicorn Run. Because they are so difficult to reach, they remain one of the most mysterious and unexplored parts of Faerûn.

markers.name: Starspire Mountains
markers.textHtml: Despite the lower altitude, these mountains contain many treacherous passes. The Trade Way passes through the mountains, but beyond that, there are few navigable trails. A tribe of dwarves, Clan Ghalmrin, lives under the eastern peaks of the Starspires and trades with the city of Zazesspur in the west. In addition to the dwarves, other races made their homes in the mountains and hills, including svirfneblin, ogres, goblinoids, and werewolves. Orcs used to live in the Starspires, but by the 1300s DR, they were extinct from the region. The great red dragon Balagos keeps a lair in the Starspires, and other dragons live there as well, in the western mountains.

markers.name: The Storm Horns
markers.textHtml: As its name suggests, the Storm Horns are battered with horrific thunderstorms all year round. The highest peaks receive snowfall much of the year. The peaks are inhabited by dragons. Aarakocras also made their home in the Storm Horns. Monstrous humanoids such as bugbears, and orcs make their home in the mountains, as do several species of giants, including hill and stone giants, and among its tallest peaks, cloud and storm giants. Even the malevolent fomorian giants are known to lair deep within abandoned mines.

markers.name: Sunrise Mountains
markers.textHtml: Regarded as the eastern edge of Faerûn, these 15,000-foot peaks (4500 m) are littered with the ruins of ancient empires and are nearly impassable. The peaks extend north into the neighboring country of Rashemen. The few passes over the mountains are exceedingly dangerous, and many lethal creatures lair in deep caves and subterranean passages.

markers.name: Sunset Mountains
markers.textHtml: The Sunset Mountains export black veined marble. These mountains are home to a large number of tribes and humanoids. Some dwarven clans also live in this region. Many of these dwarves trade in the town of Easting. The other humanoids are yeti, gnoll, ogre, and drow. A white dragon known as Ciraxis is known to dwell around these mountains.

markers.name: Surbrin Hills
markers.textHtml: In addition to the Uthgardt barbarians, the Surbrin Hills house orcs, hill giants and other monstrous beings.

markers.name: Surykyk Range
markers.textHtml: [not Surkyk] The slopes of the Surykyk Range are icy and barren, and the wind and snow quickly cover any tracks. Some of the peaks conceal networks of tunnels and caverns beneath them, which connect to the surface via deep fissures and crevasses. Mount Akka is up to 1,000 feet (300 m) in elevation.

markers.name: Sword Mountains
markers.textHtml: The western slopes of these mountains lead to the Trackless Sea in rolling foothills. The mountains themselves are steep and craggy, with snow-capped peaks. Gold and platinum are found in the foothills of the Sword Mountains. They are known to be home to several tribes of orcs and goblinoids.

markers.name: Taurax Mountains (Sunrise Mountains)
markers.textHtml: The Taurax Mountains lay between the High Ice of the Anauroch to the west and the Great Glacier to the east. It is the furthest north mountain range in the region.

markers.name: Tejarn Hills
markers.textHtml: A grouping of hills that, at some times in history, served as a border between Amn and Tethyr. Off the road, the hills are exceptionally rough and difficult to traverse. The region supplies root crops, livestock such as cattle, horses, and sheep, wines, beers, and ales, wild mushrooms and rare wildflowers to the rest of the country. Amnian humans and halflings make up the 'civilized' farmers and soldiers of the hills, but there are also significant numbers of hill giants, hobgoblins, goblins, and gnolls. They are joined by deserters from the hillforts, who find a life of brigandage superior to suffering under military discipline.

markers.name: Thaymount
markers.textHtml: Some of the volcanoes of Thaymount is still active, with earthquakes and eruptions of ash being commonplace. The peaks that are not smoldering, are shaped like fangs, darting up into ridges and walls of basalt. The mountains house Thayvian gold mines located south of the Citadel, volcanic forges, magical workshops, barracks of Thay's monstrous armies, and the private fortresses of many Red Wizards. The most notable of them is the Citadel, an ancient fortress carved into one of the highest peaks. Under the Citadel, deep underneath Thaymount, lay the Paths of the Doomed – a dangerous labyrinthine cave system and defense chambers for the Citadel's inner sanctums. The Thaymount hides tunnels that connected it to the Underdark.

markers.name: Thesk Mountains
markers.textHtml: The Thesk Mountains are bare, inhospitable mountains that reached as high as 7,000 feet (2,100 m). They feature extremely difficult terrain, and offer a limited amount of iron ore which are enough to supply the nation and provide extra for export.

markers.name: Thunder Peaks
markers.textHtml: The entire range is subject to extremely severe and sudden storms all year round. They are home to tribes of orcs and goblins that launch raids in nearby Cormyr, Sembia, and the Dalelands, and are also home to the dracolich Aurgloroasa, the Sibilant Shade, adn to goliaths. The Thunder Peaks once hosted the dwarven settlement of Thunderholme.

markers.name: Trielta Hills
markers.textHtml: The Trielta Hills is an area of pastoral and idyllic rolling hills, spotted with rocky outcroppings, tiny farming settlements, shallow close-to-the-surface 'scratch mines', and an occasional fortified hold. They are home to many communities of rock gnomes and a few halflings, as well as three communities of orcs by 1492 DR. The entire Trielta Hills region is not menaced by raiders or warlords, dragons, or more sinister villains. There are not much to rob, pillage, or conquer. Despite their cruel culture, the orcs are seemingly uninvolved in any warfare, occupying at least one fortress in the region. Those who live in the hills prefere calm and seclusion. Because of the small volume of mining discoveries, no nation or trade consortium cared to claim the land or set up large-scale mining operations. Sometimes dangerous creatures from the nearby Forest of Wyrms are on the prowl in the hills.

markers.name: Troll Mountains
markers.textHtml: Centuries before humans settled the lands of Amn, the Troll Mountains were home to an actual civilization of trolls. The humans eventually slaughtered the trolls nearly to extinction, but they were unsuccessful in wiping them out entirely. The majority of the trolls' treasures, including magical items, were never taken and remain deep in the mountains. The trolls from the Troll Mountains have historically been a problem for Amn. In the northern reaches of the mountains, the trolls are sometimes joined by goblins and orcs. The infamous ancient red dragon Balagos also makes his lair in these mountains, in the Smokespire volcano.

markers.name: The Trollclaws
markers.textHtml: Many caravans and other travelers pass through the Trollclaws by virtue of the Coast Way passing through the region. Caravans typically use around twenty armed guards when making the trip. The Trollclaws are home to a seemingly inexhaustible population of trolls. Repeated attempts by mercenaries and adventuring bands to clear the area seem to have little effect.

markers.name: Toadsquat Mountains
markers.textHtml: The Toadsquat Mountains, or the Little Mountains, form the northern border of the small halfling kingdom of Luiren, in the Shining South. This mountain range is infested with ogres. Attracted by the mountain's rich silver deposits, dwarves help clear some of the ogre tribes in the east to gain access to mines. Rage drakes and Criosphinxes are also sometimes encountered in the region.

markers.name: Trollclaw Range
markers.textHtml: A rocky wasteland, the tallest peaks of the Trollclaw Range rarely exceed 4,000 feet (1200 m), but the many sheer cliffs, loose rocks, narrow ravines, and deep lakes make traversing the region very dangerous. The mountains are impassable by horses. Dwarves historically made their homes beneath the Trollclaws while the mountains themselves were populated with tribes of orcs and goblins as well as giants and giant-kin. During the 15th century DR, the region's coast also came to be home to Northlanders. As of the turn of the 16th century DR, the inhabitants of nearby Farview have begun to reopen old mines in these mountains.

markers.name: Turnback Mountains
markers.textHtml: The Turnback Mountains are a quite frigid and windswept range, with only the topmost peaks getting consistent snow caps. To the south of the range is Turnback Mountain, a distinctively tall peak. A very cold river, the Alaphaer Run, flows north out of the western part of the range and into the nearby Frozen Forest. The mountains are also known as the Abbey Mountains because of the presence of the Abbey of the Moon, formerly a magnificent temple to Selûne, now a toppled ruin. For centuries the ruins have been used for the caching of treasure, the entombment of barbarian chieftains from the Ride, and as natural funnel hunting traps by local gnolls. Recent expeditions reported that there were two dragon lairs and three shattered but extensive ruins, possibly fallen Netherese flying cities, hidden away in the deep and rarely visited valleys at the heart of the range.

markers.name: Uthangol Mountains
markers.textHtml: The Uthangol Mountains are a range of mountains between southern Unther and the Eastern Shaar. After the Spellplague, the mountains became part of the dragonborn nation of Tymanther. Uthangol Pass, found in the center of the mountain range, allows for passage through the peaks.

markers.name: Wall of Fallen Djinn
markers.textHtml: A range of mountains appearing as a literal wall of brown rock earth that rises above the desert floor. The foothills are intercut by brush-covered ravines and the slopes aremottled with copses of qassis and acacia trees. An ancient battle between the Bedine tribespeople and a group of djinn was the source of the mountains' unique name. According to Bedine lore, every member of the tribe died while fighting off the djinn.

markers.name: West Galena Mountains
markers.textHtml: The mountains are rich in valuable minerals, and mining operations are among the most productive on Toril. The Galenas are best known for bloodstone, iron and silver. Despite the danger from hostile inhabitants, people come to the mountains in the hope of mining their way to riches, especially the dwarves, who had set up the Bloodstone mines, Hillsafar Hall and Ironspur in the area in order to cultivate the ores. While the western Galenas are just as rich as the rest of the mountains to the east, they are largely ignored by outsiders for quite some time. Only local miners and the native dwarves of the region know of these additional sources of wealth. The mountains are home to many dwarfholds, including duergar, and its peaks are the domain of many goblinoids, orcs, and even giants. Within the Underdark beneath the mountains is a city of the drow called V'elddrinnsshar, and a svirfneblin realm known as Deepearth.

markers.name: White Peaks
markers.textHtml: The mountains divid the barren, desolate Tortured Lands in the north, from the grassy steppes of the Ride to the south. The most notable mountain of the White Peaks range are Whitehorn, the peak that lent its name to a local mining town at its base, and Kossuth's Ire, the long-dormant volcano.

markers.name: The Wyrmbones
markers.textHtml: The Wyrmbones is a small mountain range in the Channath Vale that got its name from one of the tribes of the Shaar who believed that the range were bones of a massive slumbering skeletal dragon. The Wyrmbones are home to a population of rage drakes that prey on Shiertalaran trade caravans that move on land and along the Talar River. Roaming bands of marauding gnoll raiders have their homes in the Wyrmbones as well as in the nearby Dun Hills.

markers.name: Adder swamp
markers.textHtml: A dangerous expanse of lycanthrope-infested marshland, home to both werecrocodiles and wererats (which are in frequent conflict with each other), the Adder Swamp is also known for its large snake population. It is also one of the rare places along the entire coast of Chessenta where sailors will not moor their boats.

markers.name: The Akanamere
markers.textHtml: The Akanamere is rich with fish, while its shores are some of the richest farmlands in Faerûn. The collection of city-states known as the Blade Kingdoms sits on the lake's north-western shores. The southeastern shores are home to the small trading villages of Oslin and Maerduuth. River Akax connects the Akanamere to the Bay of Chessenta and offers a waterway navigatable by small and medium-sized naval vessels.

markers.name: Alamber Sea
markers.textHtml: The Alamber Sea, also known as the Sahuagin Sea, is the primary trade avenue between the nations of Mulhorand, Unther, Thay, and Tymanther (and for a time, High Imaskar as well). As a result, its waters are considered neutral territory. In the late 15th century DR, the northernmost island of Alaor served as the center of Thayvian naval power in the region, and despite the status of the Alamber Sea as neutral territory, merchant vessels and other ships found there were usually Thayvian in origin.

markers.name: Aldani Basin
markers.textHtml: This upland basin is surrounded on all sides by high plateaus and has multiple secluded lakes where its waters gathered. The central region of the basin is swampland, surrounded by thick foliage. Unhealthy fumes come from the swampy basin and are carried on the winds miles from place. Many Chultans have been told stories of the Aldani, the 'lobsterfolk' that live in the rivers of the basin, but by the late 15th century DR, no witnesses have spotted any for decades, and it is believed that the species is extinct.

markers.name: Ashane (Lake of Tears)
markers.textHtml: Lake Ashane, also known as the Lake of Tears, is a large, glacial lake, and it forms the western border of Rashemen. It is surrounded by mountains covered in dense pine forests and deciduous trees bordering those forests. The lake is somewhat boomerang-shaped and cradled around Immil Vale and Ashenwood. It is fed from glaciers in the Icerim Mountains far to the north and the Sunrise Mountains and Lake Tirulag to the east. The southern part of the lake narrows before it opens up into Lake Mulsantir, which forms part of the border between Rashemen and Thay. Many battles have been fought on the shores of Lake Ashane, thus earning it the nickname of the 'Lake of Tears'.

markers.name: Azulduth (Lake of Salt)
markers.textHtml: The ruined tower of the powerful wizard Nezram lay on the lake's far northeastern shore. Deep beneath the lake is a large, complex system of pumps called the Pumps of Pyraddin, believed to be named for a dwarven engineer responsible for their construction. These pumps continually drained the welling springs around the lake and filtered their water through lakes of charcoal and chalk, as well as air jet bubble-rakes. Finally, it pumped the water down into the Deep Lands where it formed a river that eventually flowed up into the Aerilpar Forest.

markers.name: Bay of Chessenta
markers.textHtml: In the late 15th century DR, it was the preferred means of travel in Chessenta as the Chessentan roads were poorly maintained. Oar-powered Chessentan triremes plied the waters of the often windless bay and there were many small harbors for ships to pull into.

markers.name: Bay of Chult
markers.textHtml: The Bay of Chult is fed by the Rivers Soshenstar and Tiryki. Port Nyanzaru is located on this bay. For centuries, the dragon turtle Aremag inhabited the bay, demanding treasure from ships passing through.

markers.name: Bloodlake
markers.textHtml: The Bloodlake is a small lake flowing from the haunted Sharawood forest.

markers.name: Bluefang Water
markers.textHtml: Bluefang Water sits downstream where the River Icehilt join the Great Imphras River. Bluefang Water, along with sister lake Old Water further downstream, are collectively referred to as Traders Bay.

markers.name: Bottomless bogs
markers.textHtml: This swampland was left behind as the Great Glacier retreated northwards. The Bogs are fairly navigable so long as temperatures are low, but in the heat of the northern summer, the whole area becomes a sludgy soup, and summer also brings on the 'bottomless bogs' from which the region earned its name: insidious pits of sucking mud that can entrap the unwary. Many explorers lost their lives looking for mythical hot springs deep within the Bogs. The waters of these springs are said to rejuvenate and enhance the body, or even to grant wishes.

markers.name: Deepwash
markers.textHtml: The Deepwash is a freshwater lake. The northwestern stretch is known as Shalane Lake in Erlkazar. Deepwash lake is almost as deep as lake Esmel. It is filled by tributaries from the Cloven Mountains and the Deepwing Mountains, and by Shalane River flowing from the icecold Impresk Lake. The lake's main outlet is the Wintercloak River. The Deepwash is known to be filled with fish.

markers.name: Dragon Reach
markers.textHtml: The Dragon Reach is a northwestern fjord of the Sea of Fallen Stars that stretches between the Vast and the Dalelands.

markers.name: Easting Reach
markers.textHtml: The Easting Reach is a wide expanse of water at the northeast edge of the Sea of Fallen Stars. It is calm and predictable, and therefore well suited to trade. The city of Sarshel lays at its mouth.

markers.name: Evenstar Lake
markers.textHtml: Fed by the Alaoreum River, Evenstar Lake is surrounded by dense forests and is a popular meeting place for lovers on the night of the local Feast of the Moon celebration.

markers.name: Farsea Marshes
markers.textHtml: This wetland is inhabited by the Marsh Drovers, a welcoming people whose ancestors hailed from ancient kingdom of Anauria. They dwell in small villages and live off the land, herding groups of domesticated catoblepas. The Tun River originates in the marsh, flows south towards the Dragonmere. The marsh floods during heavy rains, which occur often during the spring months, while in summer the land grows dry and even hardens over, and freezing temperatures in the winter cause the water to freeze over and become largely unnavigable. The region was once home to an ancient civilization lost to time. The remnants of their astonishingly resilient structures are buried beneath the wetlands.

markers.name: Fire Marshes
markers.textHtml: A spread of hazardous marshlands and peat bogs lit with everburning flames that make them into dangerous, inhospitable areas. Once, the fire marshes were nothing but just regular wetlands. But in the 13th century, one man's attempt to exterminate a local population of trolls resulted in the peat being lit aflame with either permanent spells or magic items of some sort. Some creatures are not bothered by the swamp's flammability and are found in the fire marshes, offering even more danger to those traversing them. These creatures adapted to the flames, granting them an innate resistance to fire.

markers.name: Flooded Forest
markers.textHtml: The Flooded Forest is a section of the great forest of Cormanthor laying in a low bowl that filled with water, becoming a swamp surrounded by a ring of boggy woodland. The swamp is stinking and foul and the marshes around the southern edges tend to be wreathed in mists. Maple and oak trees made up much of the forest. Although the trees had lasted centuries, most died when it turned to swampland; some are still standing a century later, but are so fragile they can fall at a touch. Fallen trees are a common hazard to explorers. Lizardfolk and black dragons made their homes in the Flooded Forest.

markers.name: Frostfenn
markers.textHtml: This large, frozen marshland is a dominating feature of inner Norland, covering approximately 2,600 km² of the island. The marsh is known to be home to creatures native to ice and snow. Norfen Lake sits along the northeast edge of the Frostfenn.

markers.name: Glaun bog
markers.textHtml: The Glaun Bog is a fetid swamp home to perytons, trolls, will-O-wisps, wyverns, and countless goblins. Pits of quicksand make travel perilous, and at the center of the bog lie ancient barrows inhabited by wights. But the bog is rich in iron deposits, and swordsmiths of Tasseldale covet this iron for their work.

markers.name: Highstar Lake
markers.textHtml: Highstar Lake is the subject of a number of tales, legends, mystifying appearances and mysterious disappearances. Highstar is quite famous for its clear, mirror-like surface. It sits in a bowl formed by granite in the north and west, as well as marble and limestone in the south and east. In the western reaches of granite is a gap that the lake drained out of, forming a waterfall and series of rapids along the granite cliffs that culminate in the hark river, the southernmost tributary of the River Shining.

markers.name: Huta Lake
markers.textHtml: Huta Lake is one of the smaller lakes forming the Teardrops in Narfell. It is the only lake of the Teardrops not connected to the other lakes by small rivers.

markers.name: The Ice Lakes
markers.textHtml: A group of lakes. Some of the larger lakes are named: Derthym (the northernmost named lake, also known as Longknife Lake), Belardym (also known as Tynkur's Fist), Rarghraum (the largest lake, also known as Ondran's Grave), Loroloth (a lake that bears several islands, also known as Daern's Copper Camp) and Antaerth (the southernmost lake, also known as Saeriphahra's Mirror). The kobold tribe known as the Selgryn inhabits the area.

markers.name: Icelace Lake
markers.textHtml: Icelace Lake is a frigid lake in Narfell that was created from runoff from the Great Glacier. The body of Icelace Lake is long and thin, almost resembling a bloated river. It stretches across much of northern Narfell, acting as a kind of barrier against the Great Glacier. Various tribes of Nar brave the frigid waters of the lake in deerskin canoes for the rich salmon and trout teeming in the water. Bears are also drawn to the lake because of the fish.

markers.name: Impresk Lake
markers.textHtml: Impresk is a lake in Erlkazar. Its water is ice cold. It is fed by a large number of rivers and streams, some flowing off from the Snowflake Mountains and others from the Shining Plains. Impresk Lake drains into the Deepwash by means of the short Shalane River.

markers.name: Lake of Dragons (Dragonmere)
markers.textHtml: The Dragonmere (also known as the Lake of Dragons) is a lake on the Dragon Coast. Cormyr and Sembia are on the northern part of this lake, with the Dragon Coast along the south. The Dragonmere is almost 100 miles from north to south at its widest point.

markers.name: Lake of Drifting Stars
markers.textHtml: The Lake of Drifting Stars is a popular rendezvous spot for lovers who seek a romantic encounter during the Feast of the Moon, or the Festival of Lovers, a grand revelry for the inhabitants of Turmish. Its shores are grass-covered hills that overlook the calm lake. The shores of the lake became commonly patrolled after the Time of Troubles and the increased activities of the dreaded Cult of Malar.

markers.name: Lake Esmel
markers.textHtml: Lake Esmel is the largest freshwater lake in the Lands of Intrigue. It is directly fed by four rivers from three sides and indirectly by the waters of the Esmelflow. The waters of the lake are dark blue, except in the western shallows, where mineral springs give it a sea green color. It is unusually deep, so much so that its depth had never been successfully measured. The waters of the eastern half of the lake are especially cold, but the western shallows are fed by several hot mineral springs. The lake is suitable for boating, swimming, and fishing, and the region around it is popular as a resort area. Because of the wide variety of temperatures in the lake, its waters are full of a wide variety of fishes.

markers.name: Lake Icemelt
markers.textHtml: Lake Icemelt is found high in the peaks of the Earthspur Mountains in southern Damara. The River Icehilt flowed from the lake's southern shore into Impiltur.

markers.name: Lake Lhespen
markers.textHtml: Lake Lhespen is a medium-sized lake. It sits several hundred miles inland from the Shining Sea, marking the rough midpoint between Sheirtalar and the Great Rift. The River Shaar flows into the lake on its northeastern rim and exits on the southern end to eventually join the River Talar to the south.

markers.name: Lake of the Long Arm
markers.textHtml: [also Long Arm Lake] A vast lake found in the Dragon Coast region of the Eastern Heartlands. The lake was fed by the Wet River that delivered water from the Wet Wood at the western base of the Orsraun Mountains.

markers.name: Lake Longreach
markers.textHtml: Lake Longreach is a closed lake surrounded by three great mountain ranges, the White Peaks, the Dragonspines, and the West Galenas. A portion of the Ilinver Trail runs parallel with the lake's southern banks. The lake is fed from three streams that flow down from the nearby mountains, the Telstang Brook, Naed Creek, and Razack's Tears. Overflow from the lake flow south by Castle Vathar and empty out into the Glumpen Swamp in the land of Thar.

markers.name: Lake Mereth
markers.textHtml: Lake Mereth [not Merith] is the largest and westernmost of the lakes forming the Teardrops in Narfell. It is flanked by the great mountain of Hark's Finger to the west, and connected to the other parts of the Teardrops by a long river to Muirenon Lake, which the Cold Road crossed some 30 miles (50 km) east from Lake Mereth. The Abordabe tribe lives in the area between Hark's Finger and the western coast of Muirenon Lake, fishing in the waters of Lake Mereth.

markers.name: Lake Mogador
markers.textHtml: Lake Mogador is a crucial central location in Damara for both commercial and political reasons. Both the Goliad River and Icelace River empty into the lake, making it the convergence of both of these rivers as well as of their major tributaries, the Beaumaris River and the Galena Snake respectively. The lake empties into the Great Imphras River, which flows south into Impiltur. Given the lake's position as the convergence point for most of Damara's waterways, nearly all goods leaving Damara by water pass through the lake at some point. To protect their interests, the Damaran baronies of Morov, Ostel and Polten all maintain heavily armed vessels on the lake in addition to their regular commercial ships.

markers.name: Lake Muirenon
markers.textHtml: Muirenon Lake is the center-most and longest of the lakes forming the Teardrops. It is connected to the lakes Mereth and Muskar by small rivers. The Ragnor tribe lives along the northern Teardrop lakes of Muirenon and Muskar.

markers.name: Lake Mulsantir
markers.textHtml: Lake Mulsantir is a freshwater lake connected to Lake Ashane via the wide River Mulsantir. Together they make up the western and southern borders of Rashemen. Three rivers drain into the lake from the south: River Sur, River Thay, and River Gauros. The waters of the lake itself move north through the River Mulsantir to Lake Ashane. Its waters are dangerously turbulent and murky, but house great numbers of fish, since it is warmer than the neighboring waters of Lake Ashane. Rashemi witchboats piloted by hathrans patrol the lake.

markers.name: Lake Muskar
markers.textHtml: Lake Muskar is the easternmost lake of the Teardrops, connected to the Muirenon Lake by a winding river, and fed by waters flowing westward from the Icerim Mountains. The Ragnor tribe lives along the northern Teardrop lakes of Muirenon and Muskar.

markers.name: Lake Luo
markers.textHtml: Lake Luo formed upon a literal hot bed of geothermal activity. It is kept warm by subterranean volcanic vents and receives a steady stream of lava from the surrounding valley. Due to nearby volcanic activity the waters of Lake Luo are excessively alkaline and cannot support aquatic life forms. The nearby landscape is similarly affected and resembles a barren wasteland. The water that flows from the lake continues into the River Olung, though by that point, its acidity and alkaline levels reach more balanced levels.

markers.name: Lake Sember
markers.textHtml: Lake Sember is a medium sized freshwater lake in the southwest of Cormanthyr. It is drained by the Semberflow to the east. The lake is sacred to the elves of the forest and guards from Semberholme protect the lake. Few outsiders are permitted to visit.

markers.name: Lake of the Snake
markers.textHtml: The Lake of the Snake is a small lake in the Dragon Coast. The river Reddan feeds the lake. The town of Wyrm's Hamlet sits on the northern shore.

markers.name: Lake of Steam
markers.textHtml: The Lake of Steam, with the Shaar on the east, separates the Shining South from the rest of Faerûn. On its shores is a collection of free cities, mainly on the northern and western sides. The southern side, by contrast, is a collection of kingdoms collectively named the Border Kingdoms. At the center of the lake is the Arnrock, a volcanic island.

markers.name: Lake Sunder
markers.textHtml: This freshwater lake is one of the larger lakes found in the kingdom. The Farfair River enters the lake on its southwest shore and exits to the sea on the northeast shore.

markers.name: Lake Thaylambar
markers.textHtml: Lake Thaylambar is the largest freshwater lake in all of Thay. Its waters are vast, deep and ice-cold, even during the summer months of the year. The frigid waters are fed by the River Eltar, which flow down from Thaymount and the eastern Surag River, down the slopes of the Sunrise Mountains. The lake feeds the waters of the northward River Thay, which proceed to open up into Lake Mulsantir. Rich with a wide variety of sea life, Lake Thaylambar is a blessing to Thayan fishermen. However, the deep waters of Lake Thaylambar are infested with dragon turtles, monstrous sea creatures that regularly snack upon Thayan fishing vessels.

markers.name: Lake Tirulag
markers.textHtml: Lake Tirulag is an ice-cold lake found in Rashemen that provides great natural resources and cultural significance for the nation of witches and berserkers. Those Rashemaar who want to prove their courage (as well as their foolishness) often compete with one another by leaping naked into the lake and swim around for as long as possible. Fed by the River Tir coming down from the High Country, the lake is located in the center of Rashemen's most populated region, within a number of major settlements. Its waters continue west through the River Rasha to the great River Mulsantir.

markers.name: Lake Umber
markers.textHtml: Lake Umber is a freshwater lake. The river plummets from the escarpment and widens, shaping the slow-moving narrow lake. From there, it flows through the Lower Falls and forms the main wider body of Lake Umber. Lake Umber was a part of the River Umber, which begins in the mountain glaciers of Thaymount and ends in the Umber Marshes, feeding Aglarond's farms. The area surrounding the lake is sparsely populated. It is claimed by both Thay and Aglarond, and only a few fishermen families live on its banks.

markers.name: Lake Weng
markers.textHtml: Lake Weng is a freshwater lake sitting at an altitude of 1,500 feet (460 m). It is fed by the Amnurflow and Wengwater and emptied by the Alandor River. The lake is surrounded by more than half a dozen tiny villages. The people living along the water survive by fishing in the lake and growing root crops.

markers.name: Lake Woe
markers.textHtml: Lake Woe is a large lake in the North. The lake is full of ice and covered in a thick mist that drifts down from the massive Mount Woe, one of the tallest mountains in northern Faerûn. The lake feeds the Clear Whirl River.

markers.name: Lizard Marsh
markers.textHtml: The Delimbiyr river plunged into the bogs and scattered waterways of the swamp delta. The Marsh is filled with cold and humid hanging moss-filled mangroves. The marshes does not freeze over in the winter, turning into slush instead. Sages are not sure of the reasons why that is the case.

markers.name: Lhespenbog
markers.textHtml: This wetland is known for its sprawling reed-fields. One of the species of these reeds was study enough to be used by an eastern weapon maker, Meng Aiedo, to create enchanted staves found as far north as the city of Neverwinter.

markers.name: Lashloch
markers.textHtml: The Lashloch [or Llashloc] formed in a large glacial rift, said to resemble a bent human arm, that had been hollowed out by hot springs. This steaming lake is teeming with wind-sculpted ice floes, the hot springs unable to completely remove the ice.

markers.name: Marsh of Chelimber
markers.textHtml: The marsh had pungent sulfurous steam pools.

markers.name: Marshes of Tun
markers.textHtml: The region is home to many creatures both magical and mundane. It is home to various lizardfolk tribes, and two known black dragons. Other creatures that could be found within are basilisks, catoblepas, trolls, and wights.

markers.name: Mere of Dead Men
markers.textHtml: A cold saltwater swamp. It is a desolate place full of monsters and few members of the civilized races. The swamp constantly grew over time, swallowing homes and property located close to its boundaries. The Mere itself is full of trees, vines, quicksand, and hidden islands, and it is generally covered in fog, making visibility very poor. The bones of fallen creatures are clearly visible throughout the Mere. The water is deep enough that it could be navigated on a flat-bottomed boat, but the dark water and hidden obstructions made that choice dangerous. Inside the Mere are the flooded ruins of a number of castles and settlements. Many of the castles had strange wards that would either strengthen certain magic or negate it.

markers.name: Methmere
markers.textHtml: The Methmere, also called Lake Meth, is long and narrow. It is composed of freshwater and has a winding coastline. The Winding River flows into it, and the River of Metals flows from the Methmere into the Alamber Sea. The Methmere has a few volcanoes under its waters. By the late years of the 15th century DR, those volcanoes were active and the temperatures in the northern shore made the waters release clouds of steam that covered the peaks of the Riders to the Sky.

markers.name: Mirror Lake
markers.textHtml: Mirror Lake is an icemelt lake fed by the River Pelauvir and the source of the Beaumaris River.

markers.name: Moon Sea
markers.textHtml: The Moonsea, originally known as the Dragon Sea, is a deep natural lake just north of Cormanthor. The Moonsea gives its name to the whole Moonsea region, as the name Moonsea applies to both the lake and the lands around the lake.

markers.name: Nagawater
markers.textHtml: The Nagawater is a large, deep freshwater lake. The Nagaflow river feeds it, bringing waters from the depths of the Chondalwood. The Nagawater is connected to the Deepwash lake and eventually with the Vilhon Reach via the Wintercloak River. It is named after a large population of water nagas that lives in the southern part the lake. A line of buoys lit with continual flame spells marks the boundary between the north half of the lake that humans inhabit and the southern part left for the nagas.

markers.name: Nebulae Pool
markers.textHtml: A peaceful forest lake named after Naebrulae Athtrilan, a human explorer who first (apart from the elves) discovered this natural beauty. Its beauty was the reason why it was the only one of many lakes ever to be marked on maps of the great forest. The lake's calm waters reflect the sky and stars like a perfect mirror, making it a spectacularly beautiful location. To accentuate the Nebulae's natural beauty, when the stars are bright in the sky, ancient female elven ghosts appear on the pool's waters, dancing in elegant sways. During the daytime, the lake's floor of rocky crystals reflects the sun's rays back and outwards from the Nebulae Pool. The reflected light flashes brightly until the sun hides behind the forest's trees. The lake's waters are cold, pure, and taste sweet.

markers.name: Old Water
markers.textHtml: Old Water, sometimes called Cold Water, is a large lake sits downstream beyond Bluefang Water along Great Imphras River. This waterway is collectively referred to as Traders Bay, and Old Water is the last step on its way to the Easting Reach.

markers.name: Pool of Yeven
markers.textHtml: The Pool of Yeven is a small lake found within the Yevenwood in Battledale. The lake is fed by the mighty River Ashaba as well as the Semberflow and Glaemril.

markers.name: Redwater Lake
markers.textHtml: The Redwater is a lake found in the Dragon Coast region of the Eastern Heartlands. It is fed by the Lake of the Snake by means of the River Reddan, while another waterway empties out northeast into the western waters of the Sea of Fallen Stars.

markers.name: Riftlake
markers.textHtml: The Riftlake is a large body of water that sits in the middle of the Great Rift. The lake is fed from the north by the River Shaar and from the south by the Rift River.

markers.name: Sea of Dlurg
markers.textHtml: The Sea of Dlurg is a calm body of water, sheltered from storms of the Sea of Fallen Stars. The River Umber, which runs across the nation of the Red Wizards, empties into the Sea of Dlurg on its eastern side. The other shores are marked by magnificent, steep, and high Dragonjaw Mountains that stretch into Cape Dragonfang to the Sea of Dlurg's northwest.

markers.name: Sea of Fallen Stars
markers.textHtml: The Sea of Fallen Stars, also known as the Inner Sea, is the largest inland body of water in Faerûn, large enough to feel tidal effects. The vast majority of it is saltwater. It mixes with freshwater to become brackish near many of the coastal shallows, especially at inlets where rivers drained into the sea. The sea serves as a crucial trade way for the powers of central and northern Faerûn and beyond. There is a recognized territorial limit of ten miles offshore among all the nations on the Sea of Fallen Stars. Areas of sea further than ten miles from the nearest land are considered neutral territory. Any attacks on shipping within a country's waters are considered to be acts of war.

markers.name: Sea of the Frozen Tears
markers.textHtml: The Sea of Frozen Tears, also known as the Sea of Tears or the Lake of Tears, is a lake on the border between Alpuk and Vaasa. The Utaak Stream flows east out of the lake, connecting it to the Lugalpgotak Sea and feeding Taak Lake. Prior to the mid–14th century DR, the Sea of Frozen Tears was almost completely frozen beneath the Great Glacier. Over the course of the 15th century, the Great Glacier continued to melt and retreat, and in the mid-to-late 15th century DR, the Sea of Frozen Tears was a long lake, much of which was covered in platform ice.

markers.name: Sea of Moonshae
markers.textHtml: The Sea of Moonshae is the part of the Sea of Swords that is surrounded by the collective islands of the Moonshae Isles: the Norheim Isles to the north, Gnarhelm and Alaron to the east, Oman and Gwynneth to the south, Moray to the southwest, and Norland to the west. The sea is approximately 125 miles (200 km) wide from east to west, and 250 miles (400 km) in length from north to south.

markers.name: Sea of Moving Ice
markers.textHtml: The Sea of Moving Ice, also referred to as the Floating Land by the Reghedmen barbarians, is a collection of icebergs and frost-covered isles that floats in the waters of northwest Faerûn. Almost completely uncharted, the ice masses set wide enough apart for a ship to pass. The ice of the sea changes so that all but the most experienced seafarers can be caught off guard. Proof of such are a collection of ancient ice-locked ships.

markers.name: Sea of Swords
markers.textHtml: The Sea of Swords is a region of the Trackless Sea that separates the western coast of Faerûn (known as the Sword Coast) from the Nelanther Isles and the Moonshae Isles. Prevailing winds in the northern parts of the Sea of Swords tend to blow west-to-east. A major current known as the Southern Drift moves south along the Sword Coast before turning west toward Maztica once it has passed the Moonshae Isles. A northward current flows from the coast of Alaron of the Moonshae Isles toward Waterdeep.

markers.name: Shining Sea
markers.textHtml: The Shining Sea is an arm of the Trackless Sea that is one of the great seas of Faerûn. The sea stretches along the coastline of southwest Faerûn, with the Chultan Peninsula to the south and the shoreline of Calimshan to the north. The island nation of Tharsult is located in the sea's eastern reaches. In the northeast was a massive enclosed bay known as the Lake of Steam, owing to its high volcanic activity.

markers.name: Spider Swamp
markers.textHtml: A hot, fetid swamp that was formed when a great marid was slain.

markers.name: Stump Bog
markers.textHtml: The foul mire known as Stump Bog was named after the many rotting stumps rising out of the green algae-covered waters. Over the many years of bloodshed in the North a great deal of bodies were dumped into this dark bog, and many wounded victims of such battles drowned here. Over time it became a popular and convenient site for killers, thieves, and dishonest Waterdhavians to dispose of bodies. There are many rumors of buried treasure in its murky waters, but fortune hunters need to beware the many creatures that haunt the dark marsh: giant beasts, catoblepas, gas spores, shambling mounds, will-o'-wisps, mudmen, and various undead.

markers.name: Surmarsh
markers.textHtml: A swamp fed by both the River Sur and the River Thay and opening up into Lake Mulsantir. It is among the favored grounds of the nobles of Thay who partake in trophy hunting. The Surmarsh is a foul place, with a fetid smell that drifts well beyond its borders, swarms of disease-carrying insects, and occasional small hills that hold isolated shelters. The Surmarsh is populated with tribes of lizardfolk that occasionally raid the outlying lands. Some escaped slaves and other Thayan criminals attempt to find refuge within the swamp and eke out some sort of lives for themselves.

markers.name: Trackless Sea
markers.textHtml: The Trackless Sea is the ocean due west of the continent of Faerûn. It runs from the Sea of Swords in the east to Maztica, where it is known as Taylola or the Eastern Ocean, and to Anchorome in the west. The sea also spans north to the Sea of Moving Ice and south to the peninsula of Chult.

markers.name: Umber Marshes
markers.textHtml: The name of these large boggy wetlands comes from their rust-brown color – the result of abundant iron deposits underneath. The seemingly endless swamps and mudflats, with many small springs and streams, hide the ruins of Dloemen, a city abandoned by its inhabitants long ago. The bog's humidity caused many undead left behind by Thayan-Aglarondan wars to rot more quickly. This left many of the Thayan undead soldiers to be stuck in Umber Marshes' muck. Umber Marshes are also full of giant vermin, and starving clouds of blood-sucking insects.

markers.name: Vast Swamp
markers.textHtml: An area of swampland. It is widely thought of as the most dangerous stretch of wilderness in the land. Ignored by surrounding nations, it had no proper name; 'vast swamp' appears to be a nickname that stuck.

markers.name: Vilhon Reach
markers.textHtml: The Vilhon Reach is a part of the Sea of Fallen Stars. It got its current shape from the use of Elven High Magic which destroyed two empires, Jhaamdath and Aryselmalyr, by summoning an enormous, destructive tsunami that utterly transformed the region.

markers.name: Wetwoods
markers.textHtml: The Wetwoods, sometimes called the Flooded Forest, is an area of wooded and grazing land. Two-thirds of the southern area of the Wetwoods is a misty bog with fungus-coated trees.

markers.name: Wizards' Reach
markers.textHtml: The Wizards' Reach is the name given to the stretch of the Inner Sea lying between Chessenta and Aglarond. The coast is full of extensive tidal marshes and barrier islands.

markers.name: Wyvernwater
markers.textHtml: The Wyvernwater is a star-shaped freshwater lake. It has abundant crab, eel, and fish stocks. Each morning, the lake is covered in a thick mist, and those who fish it early in the morning are therefore known as 'mistfishers'. The lake is fed by the Sword River to the northwest, the Immerflow to the northeast, and the Thunder River to the east, and it flows into the Dragonmere to the south, via the Wyvernflow.

markers.name: Yal Tengri, The Great Ice Sea
markers.textHtml: Yal Tengri, also known as the Great Ice Sea, is an arm of the ocean that covers the north pole of Toril. The sea is open for about six months of the year. Fall brings rapidly dropping temperatures and the water gradually freezes over. In the winter Yal Tengri is basically a sheet of ice, broken only by a few dangerous shifting ribbons of open water. As spring dawns, icebergs calve off the permanent ice cap and the melting begins.
